topics.title: Acid Value of Oils
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCHL307 - CHEMICAL TECHNOLOGY AND ENVIRONMENTAL ENGINEERING LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Acid Value of Oils

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Acid Value (AV)** — formally known as the **Acid Number (AN)** — is defined as the number of milligrams of **Potassium Hydroxide (KOH)** required to neutralize the free fatty acids (FFAs) present in **1 gram** of the oil or fat sample under standard test conditions.

It is a critical quality control parameter in the edible oil industry, oleochemistry, biodiesel synthesis, lubricant manufacturing, and pharmaceutical formulations. The test quantifies the extent of hydrolytic or oxidative degradation (rancidity) that the oil has undergone during storage, processing, or use.

**Standard Reference Units:**
- Acid Value is reported in **mg KOH / g of oil**
- For very low values (e.g., refined oils), the unit may be expressed in **mg KOH / g**
- For high FFA oils (e.g., used cooking oils, non-edible oils), the parameter is sometimes expressed as the **% Free Fatty Acids (FFA as oleic acid)**

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Analogy — "The Rust Check on Cooking Oil"**
> 
> Imagine you store a bottle of coconut oil in your kitchen cupboard for several months. Over time, the oil begins to develop a sharp, unpleasant smell and a slightly bitter taste. Chemically, this is because enzymes and moisture in the oil have been slowly *chopping up* the triglyceride molecules into smaller pieces called **free fatty acids (FFAs)** — just like rust slowly corrodes iron when exposed to moisture.
> 
> The **Acid Value is essentially a "rust-meter" for oil**. Just as a higher rust level means more iron corrosion, a higher Acid Value means more oil degradation. By performing a simple titration with a known concentration of KOH (the "alkaline neutralizing agent"), we can precisely measure how much degradation has occurred. The more KOH we need to add before the oil turns neutral (signalled by a colour change), the worse the oil quality.

### 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

The following constants and standards are mandatory for KTU calculations:

| Parameter | Symbol | Standard Value | Unit |
|-----------|--------|----------------|------|
| Equivalent weight of KOH | $E_{KOH}$ | **56.11** | g/mol |
| Equivalent weight of NaOH | $E_{NaOH}$ | **40.00** | g/mol |
| Equivalent weight of Oleic Acid | $E_{OA}$ | **282.47** | g/mol |
| Standard solvent mixture | — | Diethyl ether : Ethanol (1:1 or 2:1) | — |
| Indicator | — | **Phenolphthalein** (1% in ethanol) | — |
| Typical titration temperature | T | **25 – 30 °C** | °C |

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight — BIS / ASTM Equivalents:**
> 
> - **BIS Method:** IS 548 (Part 1) — commonly used in Indian industry.
> - **ASTM Method:** ASTM D 974 / D 664 — used in petroleum lubricants.
> - **AOCS Method:** AOCS Cd 3d-63 — official for fats and oils.
> 
> All these methods rely on the same underlying **acid-base titration principle**.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Underlying Chemistry — Why Does Oil Have an "Acid Value"?

Triglycerides (the main constituents of fats and oils) are chemically **neutral esters** of glycerol and three long-chain fatty acid molecules. In freshly refined oil, almost all fatty acids remain bound as esters, and the Acid Value is very low.

However, under the influence of:
- **Moisture** (hydrolytic cleavage)
- **Heat** (thermal cracking)
- **Lipase enzymes** (enzymatic hydrolysis)
- **Oxygen / light** (oxidative rancidity)

…the ester bonds break, releasing **free fatty acids (FFAs)**. These FFAs are acidic in nature and can be neutralized by a strong base like KOH.

$$\text{Triglyceride} + 3\,H_2O \xrightarrow{\text{Heat, Enzyme, Moisture}} \text{Glycerol} + 3\,\text{Free Fatty Acids (FFAs)}$$

The chemical titration reaction that occurs during the experiment is:

$$R\text{-}COOH + KOH \longrightarrow R\text{-}COOK + H_2O$$

where $R\text{-}COOH$ represents any free fatty acid molecule.

### 2.2 Step-by-Step Logical Breakdown of the Experiment

1. **Sample Weighing**: A known mass (typically 1–10 g, depending on expected acidity) of the oil is accurately weighed into a clean conical flask.
2. **Dissolution**: The oil is dissolved in **50 mL of neutral solvent mixture** (diethyl ether + ethanol, 1:1 ratio). The solvent is pre-neutralized with KOH to the phenolphthalein endpoint to remove any background acidity.
3. **Indicator Addition**: A few drops (3–5) of **phenolphthalein indicator** are added. The solution is initially colourless.
4. **Titration**: The mixture is titrated against a standardized **0.1 N KOH solution** with continuous swirling.
5. **Endpoint Detection**: The first persistent faint **pink colour** that lasts for at least **15–30 seconds** indicates the endpoint.
6. **Blank Correction**: A blank titration (without oil) is performed to correct for any acidity in the solvent.
7. **Calculation**: The corrected volume of KOH is plugged into the formula.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memorize this table verbatim — these are the most-tested formulas in KTU University Exams.**

| # | Formula | Description | Standard Units |
|---|---------|-------------|----------------|
| 1 | $\text{Acid Value} = \dfrac{56.11 \times (V_s - V_b) \times N_{KOH}}{W}$ | Core formula (mg KOH/g) | mg KOH / g |
| 2 | $\% \text{FFA (as oleic)} = \dfrac{282.47 \times (V_s - V_b) \times N_{KOH}}{1000 \times W} \times 100$ | % Free Fatty Acids | % by mass |
| 3 | $N_{KOH} = \dfrac{W_{KOH} \times \text{Purity}}{56.11 \times V_{flask}}$ | Standardization formula | eq/L (N) |
| 4 | $\text{Acid Value} = 1.99 \times \%\text{FFA}$ | Conversion (if reported as FFA) | mg KOH / g |
| 5 | $\text{Saponification Value} - \text{Acid Value} = \text{Ester Value}$ | Related quantity | mg KOH / g |

**Where:**
- $V_s$ = Volume of KOH used for sample titration (mL)
- $V_b$ = Volume of KOH used for blank titration (mL)
- $N_{KOH}$ = Normality of KOH solution (eq/L)
- $W$ = Mass of oil sample taken (g)
- $56.11$ = Equivalent weight of KOH (g/eq)
- $282.47$ = Equivalent weight of oleic acid (g/eq)

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Biodiesel Industry**: Acid Value must be < **2 mg KOH/g** (after pretreatment) before transesterification, otherwise soap formation (saponification) consumes catalyst and reduces yield.
- **Lubricant Industry**: Acid Value indicates oil degradation in service; high AV triggers oil replacement in turbines and transformers.
- **Edible Oil Quality**: Refined oil should have AV < **0.5 mg KOH/g**; cold-pressed oils < **4 mg KOH/g**.
- **Cosmetics & Pharmaceuticals**: AV governs shelf-life and skin-irritation potential.
- **Paint & Varnish Industry**: AV determines the suitability of oils (e.g., linseed oil) for binder formulations.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Acid Value Formula — From First Principles

> [!NOTE]
> **Pedagogical Objective:** This derivation is **mandatory** for KTU 14-mark answers. Examiners allocate a separate 2-mark credit for correct derivation with units.

**Step 1 — Equivalence Concept**
At the endpoint of titration, the **milliequivalents of KOH consumed** equals the **milliequivalents of free fatty acids** neutralized:

$$n_{KOH} = n_{FFA}$$

**Step 2 — Expressing Equivalents**
The number of milliequivalents of KOH in $V_s$ mL of $N_{KOH}$ solution is:

$$n_{KOH} = V_s \times N_{KOH}$$

where $V_s$ is in mL and $N_{KOH}$ is in meq/mL (= N).

**Step 3 — Mass of KOH Neutralizing the FFAs**

$$\text{Mass of KOH} = n_{KOH} \times E_{KOH} = V_s \times N_{KOH} \times 56.11 \,(\text{mg})$$

This represents the **milligrams of KOH** needed to neutralize the FFAs in the weighed oil sample.

**Step 4 — Normalization per Gram of Oil**
By the KTU definition, Acid Value is **mg KOH per gram** of oil. Hence, we must divide the mass of KOH by the mass of oil sample $W$ (in grams):

$$\text{Acid Value} = \dfrac{V_s \times N_{KOH} \times 56.11}{W}$$

**Step 5 — Blank Correction (Final Form)**
Since the solvent itself may carry trace acidity, a **blank correction** $V_b$ must be subtracted:

$$\boxed{\text{Acid Value (mg KOH/g)} = \dfrac{56.11 \times N_{KOH} \times (V_s - V_b)}{W}}$$

### 3.2 Numerical Solved Example (KTU-Style)

**Problem Statement (KTU University Exam Style):**

> An oil sample weighing **5.6 g** was dissolved in 50 mL of neutral ether-ethanol solvent and titrated against **0.1 N KOH** using phenolphthalein indicator. The burette reading was **28.4 mL**. A blank titration consumed **0.2 mL** of KOH. Calculate the **Acid Value** and **% Free Fatty Acid (as oleic acid)**.

**Solution — Step-by-Step (Exhaustive):**

**Step 1: Identify Given Data**

$$\begin{aligned}
W &= 5.6 \text{ g} \\
N_{KOH} &= 0.1 \text{ N} \\
V_s &= 28.4 \text{ mL} \\
V_b &= 0.2 \text{ mL} \\
E_{KOH} &= 56.11 \text{ g/eq} \\
E_{OA} &= 282.47 \text{ g/eq}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2: Calculate Corrected Titre Volume**

$$V_{corr} = V_s - V_b = 28.4 - 0.2 = 28.2 \text{ mL}$$

**Step 3: Apply Acid Value Formula**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Acid Value} &= \dfrac{56.11 \times N_{KOH} \times (V_s - V_b)}{W} \\
&= \dfrac{56.11 \times 0.1 \times 28.2}{5.6} \\
&= \dfrac{158.23}{5.6} \\
&= 28.26 \text{ mg KOH/g}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4: Apply % FFA Formula**

$$\begin{aligned}
\%\text{FFA} &= \dfrac{282.47 \times N_{KOH} \times (V_s - V_b)}{10 \times W} \\
&= \dfrac{282.47 \times 0.1 \times 28.2}{10 \times 5.6} \\
&= \dfrac{796.57}{56.0} \\
&= 14.22 \%
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 5: Result Interpretation**

$$\boxed{\text{Acid Value} = 28.26 \text{ mg KOH/g}, \quad \%\text{FFA} = 14.22\%}$$

**Inference:** The Acid Value of 28.26 mg KOH/g is **very high**, indicating the oil is **highly degraded / rancid**. Such oil is unsuitable for direct edible consumption but may be acceptable for **biodiesel production after acid esterification pretreatment**.

### 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Quality Grades

| Oil Grade / Type | Typical Acid Value (mg KOH/g) | % FFA | Industrial Application |
|------------------|-------------------------------|-------|------------------------|
| **Refined edible oil (cold)** | 0.05 – 0.30 | < 0.15 | Cooking, salads |
| **Refined edible oil (general)** | 0.30 – 0.50 | 0.15 – 0.25 | Cooking medium |
| **Crude vegetable oil** | 1.0 – 4.0 | 0.5 – 2.0 | Refining feedstock |
| **Cold-pressed / Virgin oil** | 0.5 – 6.0 | 0.25 – 3.0 | Premium edible |
| **Used cooking oil (UCO)** | 5 – 15 | 2.5 – 7.5 | Biodiesel feedstock |
| **Highly rancid / non-edible** | > 20 | > 10 | Industrial / fuel |
| **Biodiesel finished product** | < 0.50 (EN 14214) | < 0.25 | Engine fuel |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Process Flow Diagram — Acid Value Determination Procedure

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Clean & dry conical flask] --> B[Weigh accurately W grams of oil sample]
    B --> C[Add 50 mL neutral ether-ethanol solvent]
    C --> D[Add 3-5 drops phenolphthalein indicator]
    D --> E[Solution remains colourless — acidic]
    E --> F[Titrate against standardized 0.1 N KOH]
    F --> G{Swirl & observe colour change}
    G -->|Pink colour persists 15-30 sec| H[Record titre volume Vs]
    G -->|No colour change yet| F
    H --> I[Perform blank titration without oil]
    I --> J[Record blank titre Vb]
    J --> K[Apply Acid Value formula]
    K --> L[Calculate mg KOH per gram]
    L --> M[Compare with BIS/ASTM standards]
    M --> N[End: Report Acid Value]
```

### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Oil Quality Assessment

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Raw Oil Sample] --> B[Acid Value Test]
    A --> C[Saponification Value Test]
    A --> D[Iodine Value Test]
    A --> E[Peroxide Value Test]
    B --> F[Oil Quality Report]
    C --> F
    D --> F
    E --> F
    F --> G{Quality Grade}
    G -->|AV < 0.5| H[Premium Edible Grade]
    G -->|0.5 to 4.0| I[Standard Edible Grade]
    G -->|4.0 to 15| J[Industrial / Biodiesel Feedstock]
    G -->|> 15| K[Reject / Fuel-Grade Only]
```

### 4.3 Equipment Schematic — Burette Setup (Textual Layout)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Burette stand] --> B[0.1 N KOH in burette]
    B --> C[Stopcock]
    C --> D[Conical flask with oil + solvent + indicator]
    D --> E[White tile beneath flask]
    E --> F[Pink endpoint detection]
    F --> G[Read final burette reading Vs]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Practical Note for KTU Lab:** Always perform a **blank titration** using the same solvent mixture (without oil) and subtract the blank reading $V_b$. This eliminates errors due to trace CO₂ absorbed by the solvent, which would otherwise produce carbonic acid and artificially raise the titre volume.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model Paper Style]**

**Q1. Define Acid Value of an oil. State its significance in the edible oil industry.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Definition (2 marks):** Acid Value is defined as the number of milligrams of **Potassium Hydroxide (KOH)** required to neutralize the **free fatty acids** present in **one gram** of the oil or fat sample. It is expressed in units of **mg KOH/g**.
> 
> **Significance (1 mark):** Acid Value serves as a direct indicator of oil quality and degree of degradation. A low Acid Value (< 0.5 mg KOH/g) indicates fresh, well-refined oil suitable for edible consumption, whereas a high Acid Value signals hydrolytic rancidity. It determines whether an oil qualifies for edible use, industrial application, or biodiesel feedstock.

---

**Q2. Why is phenolphthalein chosen as the indicator for the Acid Value titration instead of methyl orange?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> Phenolphthalein is selected because the titration involves a **weak acid (free fatty acids)** being neutralized by a **strong base (KOH)**. The equivalence point of such a titration occurs in the **slightly alkaline pH range (8.2 – 10.0)**, which corresponds exactly to the colour-change window of phenolphthalein (colourless → pink). Methyl orange, with its transition range of pH 3.1 – 4.4, would change colour too early (in the acidic region) and produce a large titration error, underestimating the Acid Value significantly.

---

### 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **As per KTU 2024 ESE Module pattern, students must answer ONE question from a choice of TWO.**

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Style]*

**(a)** Derive the expression for the **Acid Value** of an oil from first principles, explaining each step clearly. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** An oil sample of **7.5 g** is titrated against **0.1 N KOH** and consumes **22.5 mL**. A blank titration with the same solvent consumes **0.3 mL**. Calculate the **Acid Value** and **% FFA (as oleic acid)**. Comment on the suitability of this oil for edible purposes. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Derivation (7 marks):**

> **Step 1 (1 mark):** At the titration endpoint, the equivalents of KOH consumed equal the equivalents of free fatty acids neutralized.
> 
> **Step 2 (1 mark):** Equivalents of KOH in $V_s$ mL of $N$ normality solution $= V_s \times N$ milliequivalents.
> 
> **Step 3 (1 mark):** Mass of KOH used (in mg) $= V_s \times N \times 56.11$ mg.
> 
> **Step 4 (1 mark):** Normalizing per gram of oil: $\text{AV} = \dfrac{V_s \times N \times 56.11}{W}$ mg KOH/g.
> 
> **Step 5 (1 mark):** Applying blank correction: $\text{AV} = \dfrac{56.11 \times N \times (V_s - V_b)}{W}$ mg KOH/g.
> 
> **Step 6 (1 mark):** Defining each variable ($V_s, V_b, N, W$) with units.
> 
> **Step 7 (1 mark):** Stating the indicator used (phenolphthalein) and the equivalence-point pH logic.

**(b) Numerical Calculation (7 marks):**

**Given Data:**
$$W = 7.5 \text{ g}, \quad N_{KOH} = 0.1 \text{ N}, \quad V_s = 22.5 \text{ mL}, \quad V_b = 0.3 \text{ mL}$$

**Corrected Volume (1 mark):**
$$V_{corr} = 22.5 - 0.3 = 22.2 \text{ mL}$$

**Acid Value (3 marks):**
$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Acid Value} &= \dfrac{56.11 \times 0.1 \times 22.2}{7.5} \\
&= \dfrac{124.5642}{7.5} \\
&= 16.61 \text{ mg KOH/g}
\end{aligned}$$

**% FFA (2 marks):**
$$\begin{aligned}
\%\text{FFA} &= \dfrac{282.47 \times 0.1 \times 22.2}{10 \times 7.5} \\
&= \dfrac{627.0834}{75.0} \\
&= 8.36\%
\end{aligned}$$

**Comment (1 mark):**
> The Acid Value of **16.61 mg KOH/g** is significantly higher than the standard edible oil limit of **0.5 mg KOH/g**. This oil is **unsuitable for edible use**, exhibits severe hydrolytic rancidity, and is recommended only for **industrial applications such as biodiesel production** (with prior acid-catalyzed esterification pretreatment).

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam — May 2023 Style]*

**(a)** Explain the principle, procedure, and chemistry involved in the determination of Acid Value of an oil sample. List the reagents and indicator used. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A sample of **used cooking oil** weighing **4.2 g** was titrated against **0.5 N KOH** solution. The titre reading was **18.6 mL** and blank was **0.4 mL**. Compute the Acid Value. If this oil is intended for **biodiesel production**, what pretreatment is necessary and why? **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Principle, Procedure & Chemistry (7 marks):**

> **Principle (2 marks):** Acid Value is determined by titrating the oil dissolved in a neutral solvent against a standardized alkali (KOH) using phenolphthalein indicator. The volume of alkali consumed is proportional to the free fatty acid content.
> 
> **Chemistry (1 mark):** $\text{R-COOH} + \text{KOH} \longrightarrow \text{R-COOK} + \text{H}_2\text{O}$
> 
> **Reagents & Indicator (2 marks):**
> - Solvent: Diethyl ether : Ethanol (1:1 or 2:1), neutralized
> - Titrant: 0.1 N KOH (or as specified)
> - Indicator: Phenolphthalein (1% in ethanol)
> - Standard: Oxalic acid or potassium hydrogen phthalate (for KOH standardization)
> 
> **Procedure (2 marks):**
> 1. Weigh accurately $W$ g of oil into conical flask.
> 2. Dissolve in 50 mL pre-neutralized solvent.
> 3. Add 3–5 drops phenolphthalein.
> 4. Titrate against standard KOH until faint pink colour persists for 15–30 seconds.
> 5. Record $V_s$ and perform blank titration to record $V_b$.
> 6. Calculate Acid Value using the standard formula.

**(b) Numerical Calculation + Pretreatment Discussion (7 marks):**

**Given Data:**
$$W = 4.2 \text{ g}, \quad N_{KOH} = 0.5 \text{ N}, \quad V_s = 18.6 \text{ mL}, \quad V_b = 0.4 \text{ mL}$$

**Corrected Volume (1 mark):**
$$V_{corr} = 18.6 - 0.4 = 18.2 \text{ mL}$$

**Acid Value (3 marks):**
$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Acid Value} &= \dfrac{56.11 \times 0.5 \times 18.2}{4.2} \\
&= \dfrac{510.601}{4.2} \\
&= 121.57 \text{ mg KOH/g}
\end{aligned}$$

**Pretreatment Discussion (3 marks):**
> With an Acid Value of **121.57 mg KOH/g**, the used cooking oil has an extremely high free fatty acid content, making it unsuitable for **direct alkali-catalyzed transesterification** (which would cause severe saponification, consuming catalyst and producing soap instead of biodiesel). The required pretreatment is **two-step acid-base catalysis**:
> 
> **Step 1 — Acid Esterification:** Treat oil with **1–5% H₂SO₄ in methanol** at 60 °C for 1–2 hours. This converts FFAs into fatty acid methyl esters (FAME), reducing the Acid Value to below 2 mg KOH/g.
> 
> **Step 2 — Alkali Transesterification:** Perform conventional base-catalyzed transesterification using **NaOH or KOH** to convert remaining triglycerides into biodiesel.
> 
> **Why Necessary:** Acid esterification removes FFAs that would otherwise form soap with the alkaline catalyst, dramatically improving biodiesel yield and purity.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Traps in Acid Value Problems:**
> 
> 1. **Forgetting the blank correction (–1 mark):** Many students forget to subtract $V_b$. Always write $V_s - V_b$, not $V_s$ alone.
> 2. **Wrong constant (–1 mark):** Use **56.11** for KOH (not 56.0 or 40.0). Use **282.47** for oleic acid (not 282 or 256).
> 3. **Missing units in final answer (–0.5 mark):** Always write **mg KOH/g** explicitly.
> 4. **Mixing up % FFA with Acid Value (–1 mark):** % FFA is dimensionless (a percentage), while Acid Value carries units. Do not report one in place of the other.
> 5. **Not justifying solvent choice (–0.5 mark in procedure):** Always mention that the ether-ethanol solvent is **pre-neutralized** to eliminate solvent acidity.
> 6. **Ignoring the indicator colour change direction (–0.5 mark):** Mention that the endpoint is **colourless → faint pink** for phenolphthalein.
> 7. **No comment on oil quality in numerical answers (–1 mark):** Always end with a **quality interpretation** — whether the oil is edible, industrial-grade, or biodiesel feedstock.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Acid Value of Oils**

- ✅ **Definition:** mg of KOH required to neutralize FFAs in 1 g of oil.
- ✅ **Units:** mg KOH / g (also called Acid Number).
- ✅ **Core Formula:** $\text{AV} = \dfrac{56.11 \times N \times (V_s - V_b)}{W}$
- ✅ **% FFA Formula:** $\%\text{FFA} = \dfrac{282.47 \times N \times (V_s - V_b)}{10 \times W}$
- ✅ **Conversion:** AV ≈ 2 × % FFA (oleic basis).
- ✅ **Titrant:** Standardized KOH (0.1 N typical).
- ✅ **Solvent:** Diethyl ether + ethanol (1:1), pre-neutralized.
- ✅ **Indicator:** Phenolphthalein (colourless → pink, pH 8.2–10.0).
- ✅ **Blank Correction:** Always subtract $V_b$ (solvent acidity).
- ✅ **Edible Limit:** AV < 0.5 mg KOH/g for refined oil.
- ✅ **Biodiesel Limit:** AV < 2 mg KOH/g (before transesterification).
- ✅ **High AV Source:** Hydrolysis, oxidation, lipase activity, heat exposure.
- ✅ **Pretreatment for High-AV Oil:** Acid esterification with H₂SO₄ + methanol.
- ✅ **Related Quantities:** Saponification Value, Ester Value, Iodine Value, Peroxide Value.
- ✅ **Industrial Applications:** Edible oil QC, lubricants, paints, cosmetics, biodiesel.

> **END OF NOTES — Acid Value of Oils (Module Experiment 2)**
> **Prepared per KTU 2024 Scheme | PCCHL307**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Iodine Value of Oils
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCHL307 - CHEMICAL TECHNOLOGY AND ENVIRONMENTAL ENGINEERING LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 🔬 MODULE EXPERIMENT 2: IODINE VALUE OF OILS

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 📘 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Iodine Value (Iodine Number, I.V.)** is formally defined as the **number of grams of iodine (I₂) absorbed (i.e., chemically fixed) by 100 grams of the oil or fat** under the standard conditions specified by the test method (typically Wijs or Hanus).

It is a fundamental **chemical constant** used in the analysis of oils and fats, quantifying the **degree of unsaturation** — i.e., the number of **carbon–carbon double bonds (C=C)** present in the fatty acid chains of triglycerides.

The standard reference is governed by:
- **Bureau of Indian Standards (BIS)** — IS 548 (Part 1)
- **American Oil Chemists' Society (AOCS)** — Official Method Cd 1d-92 (Wijs Method)
- **International Union of Pure and Applied Chemistry (IUPAC)** — 2.205

### 🧠 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — "A Hook & Loop Story":**
> Imagine a long chain (the fatty acid) with multiple "hooks" (double bonds). The iodine (I₂) molecules act like "loop fasteners" that attach to these hooks. The more hooks present, the more iodine gets attached. Counting attached iodine essentially tells you **how many hooks (double bonds) the oil has** — this count is the **Iodine Value**.

If an oil is **saturated** (like coconut oil, no double bonds), it grabs **very little iodine** → **low I.V.**
If an oil is **highly unsaturated** (like linseed oil, many double bonds), it grabs **a lot of iodine** → **high I.V.**

### 🎯 Why Is Iodine Value Important in Chemical Engineering?

| Engineering Domain | Application |
|---|---|
| **Edible Oil Industry** | Quality control, adulteration detection (mixing cheap oil into expensive ones) |
| **Paint & Varnish Industry** | Classifies oils as **drying, semi-drying, non-drying** |
| **Cosmetics** | Stability and shelf-life of triglyceride-based products |
| **Biodiesel Production** | Predicts oxidative stability of fatty acid methyl esters |
| **Polymer Industry** | Alkyd resin synthesis from polyunsaturated oils |

### 🧪 Key Physical Constants

- **Atomic mass of Iodine (I) = 126.9 g/mol**
- **Molecular mass of I₂ = 253.8 g/mol**
- **Molar mass of Na₂S₂O₃·5H₂O (sodium thiosulfate pentahydrate) = 248.18 g/mol**
- **Molar mass of KI = 166.0 g/mol**

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 🔍 The Chemistry Behind the Method

### ⚗️ Principle

The unsaturated fatty acids in oils contain **C=C double bonds** that undergo **electrophilic addition** with **interhalogen compounds** like **iodine monochloride (ICl)** (Wijs method) or **iodine monobromide (IBr)** (Hanus method).

**The reaction does NOT use free I₂ directly** because molecular iodine does not add to C=C bonds efficiently. Instead, a **polar interhalogen (I⁺Cl⁻ or I⁺Br⁻)** is used, where the **electrophilic iodine (I⁺)** attacks the π-electron cloud of the alkene.

### 📐 Chemical Reactions

**Step 1: Addition of ICl to the unsaturation**

$$\text{—CH=CH—} + \text{ICl} \longrightarrow \text{—CHI—CHCl—}$$

**Step 2: Liberation of free I₂ by excess ICl reacting with KI**

$$\text{ICl} + \text{KI} \longrightarrow \text{KCl} + \text{I}_2$$

**Step 3: Titration of liberated I₂ with sodium thiosulfate (back-titration)**

$$\text{I}_2 + 2\text{Na}_2\text{S}_2\text{O}_3 \longrightarrow 2\text{NaI} + \text{Na}_2\text{S}_4\text{O}_6$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KEY INSIGHT — Back-Titration Logic:**
> We never titrate the unreacted halogen directly. Instead, we add **excess ICl** to the oil. The **unreacted excess ICl** liberates **I₂ from KI**. The amount of I₂ liberated is therefore **chemically equivalent** to the amount of unreacted ICl — which we titrate with thiosulfate.

### 📋 Classification of Oils Based on Iodine Value

> [!NOTE]
> This classification is **EXTREMELY HIGH-YIELD** for KTU exams.

| Category | Iodine Value Range | Examples | Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Non-drying oils** | < 100 | Coconut, Palm, Olive | Lubricants, soaps |
| **Semi-drying oils** | 100 – 140 | Cottonseed, Corn, Sesame | Edible oils |
| **Drying oils** | > 140 | Linseed, Tung, Perilla | Paints, varnishes, printing inks |

### 📊 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula / Expression | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\text{Iodine Value} = \dfrac{(V_1 - V_2) \times N \times 12.69}{W}$ | Master formula for Iodine Value | dimensionless (g I₂ / 100 g oil) |
| 2 | $\text{Equivalent mass of I}_2 = \dfrac{253.8}{2} = 126.9$ | Mass of I₂ equivalent to 1 equivalent of thiosulfate | g/eq |
| 3 | $12.69 = \dfrac{126.9}{10}$ | Conversion factor (per 100 g basis) | — |
| 4 | $\text{Normality of Na}_2\text{S}_2\text{O}_3 = \dfrac{\text{Strength}}{\text{Equivalent mass}}$ | For standardizing thiosulfate | N (eq/L) |
| 5 | $\text{\% Purity of oil} = \dfrac{\text{Calculated IV}}{\text{Standard IV}} \times 100$ | Purity of given oil sample | % |
| 6 | $\text{Equivalents of ICl reacted} = \text{Equivalents of I}_2 \text{ liberated by excess ICl}$ | Stoichiometric backbone | equivalents |

> [!WARNING]
> **PITFALL ALERT — The "12.69" Factor:**
> The constant **12.69 = 126.9 / 10** is a numerical trick. It converts the **"per gram"** result to the standard reporting basis of **"per 100 grams of oil"** (as per IUPAC convention). Do NOT use 126.9 alone, and do NOT use 253.8. Memorize **12.69** as the constant.

### 🌍 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Refineries** use iodine value to monitor **hydrogenation progress** — as H₂ is added to double bonds, the iodine value **decreases** (saturation increases).
- **Paint manufacturers** use it to predict the **drying rate** of oils — higher I.V. → faster oxidative polymerization → harder film.
- **Edible oil quality** — high I.V. oils are more prone to **oxidative rancidity**, requiring antioxidants (BHA, BHT, tocopherols).

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 🧮 Derivation of the Master Iodine Value Formula

### Starting Point — Definitions

Let:
- $W$ = Mass of oil sample taken (in grams)
- $V_1$ = Volume of Na₂S₂O₃ required for **blank titration** (mL)
- $V_2$ = Volume of Na₂S₂O₃ required for **sample titration** (mL)
- $N$ = Normality of Na₂S₂O₃ solution (eq/L)

### Step 1: Equivalents of Thiosulfate Used in Blank

The blank consumes the thiosulfate that would be equivalent to **total ICl added**.

$$E_{\text{blank}} = V_1 \times N \quad \text{(milli-equivalents)}$$

### Step 2: Equivalents of Thiosulfate Used in Sample

The sample titration consumes thiosulfate equivalent to **unreacted (excess) ICl** only.

$$E_{\text{sample}} = V_2 \times N \quad \text{(milli-equivalents)}$$

### Step 3: Equivalents of ICl Reacted with the Oil

By the principle of **back-titration**, the difference gives the equivalents of halogen that actually reacted with the C=C bonds of the oil.

$$E_{\text{reacted}} = E_{\text{blank}} - E_{\text{sample}} = (V_1 - V_2) \times N$$

### Step 4: Equivalents of I₂ Equivalent to Reacted ICl

Since 1 mole of ICl liberates 1 mole of I₂ (which is a 2-electron species, equivalent weight = 126.9), the mass of I₂ equivalent to the reacted ICl is:

$$m_{I_2} = (V_1 - V_2) \times N \times 126.9 \quad \text{(in mg, since } V \text{ is in mL)}$$

### Step 5: Convert to "Per 100 g of Oil" Basis

The above mass of I₂ was absorbed by **W grams** of oil. To convert to the standard IUPAC basis of **100 g of oil**:

$$\text{Iodine Value} = \dfrac{m_{I_2} \times 100}{W \times 1000} = \dfrac{(V_1 - V_2) \times N \times 126.9}{W \times 10}$$

Simplifying by merging constants:

$$\text{Iodine Value} = \dfrac{(V_1 - V_2) \times N \times 12.69}{W}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the **final master formula**. The 10 in the denominator comes from converting mg → g (÷1000) and per gram → per 100 g (×100), net effect: ÷10.

## 🔢 Numerical Worked Example (KTU-Style Calculation)

### **Problem:**
Determine the iodine value of a given oil sample. **0.5 g** of oil was weighed into an iodine flask. **25 mL of Wijs solution** was added, kept in dark for 30 min. After reaction, **20 mL of 10% KI** was added and titrated against **0.1 N Na₂S₂O₃** — sample titre = **18.2 mL**. Blank titre = **45.0 mL**.

### **Solution:**

**Step 1:** Identify variables.

$$V_1 = 45.0 \text{ mL}, \quad V_2 = 18.2 \text{ mL}, \quad N = 0.1 \text{ N}, \quad W = 0.5 \text{ g}$$

**Step 2:** Apply the master formula.

$$\text{Iodine Value} = \dfrac{(V_1 - V_2) \times N \times 12.69}{W}$$

$$\text{Iodine Value} = \dfrac{(45.0 - 18.2) \times 0.1 \times 12.69}{0.5}$$

**Step 3:** Evaluate stepwise.

$$V_1 - V_2 = 45.0 - 18.2 = 26.8 \text{ mL}$$

$$26.8 \times 0.1 = 2.68$$

$$2.68 \times 12.69 = 34.0092$$

$$\dfrac{34.0092}{0.5} = 68.0184$$

**Step 4:** Final result.

$$\boxed{\text{Iodine Value} \approx 68.02 \text{ g I}_2 \text{ / 100 g oil}}$$

### **Classification:**

Since **I.V. = 68 < 100**, this is a **non-drying oil** (likely coconut or palm oil).

### **Cross-Check with Reference Data:**

| Oil | Typical Iodine Value |
|---|---|
| Coconut oil | 7 – 12 |
| Palm oil | 44 – 58 |
| Olive oil | 75 – 95 |
| Cottonseed oil | 100 – 115 |
| Linseed oil | 175 – 195 |

A value of **68** suggests a **palm–olive blend** or a similar semi-non-drying oil.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 🔁 Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow of the Iodine Value Determination

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Step 1: Weigh Oil Sample 0.2 to 0.5 g accurately] --> B[Step 2: Dissolve in 10 mL of CCl4 or Chloroform]
    B --> C[Step 3: Add 25 mL of Wijs Solution ICl in Acetic Acid]
    C --> D[Step 4: Stopper and Store in Dark for 30 minutes at 25 degrees C]
    D --> E[Step 5: Add 20 mL of 10 percent KI Solution and 100 mL Distilled Water]
    E --> F[Step 6: Titrate Liberated I2 with Standard 0.1 N Na2S2O3 until Pale Yellow]
    F --> G[Step 7: Add 1 to 2 mL of 1 percent Starch Indicator Blue Color Appears]
    G --> H[Step 8: Continue Titration until Blue Color Disappears Endpoint]
    H --> I[Step 9: Record Sample Titre V2]
    I --> J[Step 10: Repeat Procedure Without Oil for Blank Titre V1]
    J --> K[Step 11: Calculate Iodine Value using Formula IV equals V1 minus V2 times N times 12.69 divided by W]
    K --> L[Step 12: Classify Oil as Drying Semi-drying or Non-drying]
```

```mermaid

        R5[Chloroform or CCl4 Solvent]
    end

    end

    subgraph Procedure

    end

    P1 --> P2 --> P3 --> P4 --> P5
    G1 --> P1
    G3 --> P2
    R1 --> P2
    R2 --> P4
    R3 --> P5
    G2 --> P5
    R4 --> P5
```

## 📊 Decision Flow — Oil Classification Based on Iodine Value

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Calculate Iodine Value] --> Q1{Is IV less than 100?}
    Q1 -- Yes --> ND[Non-drying Oil e.g. Coconut Palm Olive]
    Q1 -- No --> Q2{Is IV between 100 and 140?}
    Q2 -- Yes --> SD[Semi-drying Oil e.g. Cottonseed Corn]
    Q2 -- No --> D[Drying Oil e.g. Linseed Tung]
    ND --> App1[Used in Soaps Lubricants Cosmetics]

```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 📝 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Question]**

**Q1. Define Iodine Value. What is its significance in the oil & fat industry?**

**Model Answer:**
Iodine Value is defined as **the number of grams of iodine absorbed by 100 grams of oil or fat** under standard conditions (Wijs/Hanus method). It is a measure of the **degree of unsaturation** of the fatty acids present in the oil.

**Significance:**
- Indicates the **number of C=C double bonds** in fatty acid chains.
- Used to **classify oils** as drying (>140), semi-drying (100–140), or non-drying (<100).
- Helps in **detecting adulteration** of edible oils.
- Predicts the **drying property** in paints and varnishes.
- Used to **monitor hydrogenation** in oil refining (I.V. decreases as unsaturation is reduced).

---

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Question]**

**Q2. Why is Wijs solution preferred over pure iodine (I₂) for determining iodine value?**

**Model Answer:**
Pure molecular iodine (I₂) is a **non-polar molecule** and does NOT add efficiently to C=C double bonds because it lacks sufficient **electrophilicity**. Wijs solution contains **iodine monochloride (ICl)** dissolved in glacial acetic acid, which is a **polar interhalogen compound (I⁺Cl⁻)**. The **electrophilic iodine (I⁺)** readily attacks the π-electrons of the alkene, ensuring **complete and quantitative addition** within 30 minutes. Additionally, Wijs method gives more **reproducible results** and is the **official AOCS and BIS method**.

---

## 📝 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Model ESE Question, Module 2]**

### ⭐ **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a)** Explain the principle and procedure for the determination of **Iodine Value by the Wijs method**. List the chemicals and apparatus required. (7 marks)

**(b)** In an experiment, **0.4 g** of an oil sample was treated with **25 mL of Wijs solution**. After 30 minutes in the dark, **20 mL of 10% KI** was added, and the liberated iodine required **22.5 mL of 0.1 N Na₂S₂O₃** for complete titration. The blank titre was **48.0 mL**. Calculate the iodine value and classify the oil. (7 marks)

---

### **Model Answer for Question A:**

#### **(a) Principle and Procedure (7 marks)**

**Principle:**
The unsaturated fatty acids in oils/fats contain C=C double bonds which undergo **electrophilic addition** with **iodine monochloride (ICl)** from Wijs solution. The amount of ICl that reacts is stoichiometrically equal to the number of double bonds. The **excess (unreacted) ICl** is converted to **free I₂** by adding KI, and the I₂ is back-titrated with **standard sodium thiosulfate** using **starch indicator**.

$$\text{—CH=CH—} + \text{ICl} \to \text{—CHI—CHCl—}$$

$$\text{ICl (excess)} + \text{KI} \to \text{KCl} + \text{I}_2$$

$$\text{I}_2 + 2\text{Na}_2\text{S}_2\text{O}_3 \to 2\text{NaI} + \text{Na}_2\text{S}_4\text{O}_6$$

**Apparatus Required:**
- Iodine flask (250 mL) with glass stopper
- Burette (50 mL)
- Pipettes (10 mL, 20 mL, 25 mL)
- Analytical balance (accuracy ±0.0001 g)
- Dark cupboard / drawer

**Chemicals Required:**
- Wijs solution (ICl in glacial acetic acid)
- 10% aqueous KI solution
- 0.1 N Na₂S₂O₃ (standardized)
- 1% starch indicator solution
- Chloroform or CCl₄ (solvent)
- Distilled water

**Procedure (7 steps):**
1. Weigh accurately **0.2 to 0.5 g** of oil into a clean, dry iodine flask.
2. Dissolve in **10 mL of CCl₄ or chloroform**.
3. Add **25 mL of Wijs solution** using a pipette.
4. Stopper the flask, swirl gently, and store in a **dark place for 30 minutes** at 25 °C.
5. Add **20 mL of 10% KI solution** and **100 mL of distilled water**.
6. Titrate the liberated I₂ with **0.1 N Na₂S₂O₃** until the solution turns pale straw-yellow.
7. Add **1–2 mL of starch indicator** — solution turns **deep blue**. Continue titration dropwise until the **blue color disappears completely**. Record the volume as **V₂**.
8. Run a **blank titration** (same procedure, without oil) → record as **V₁**.

---

#### **(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks)**

**Given:**
- $W = 0.4 \text{ g}$
- $V_1 = 48.0 \text{ mL}$
- $V_2 = 22.5 \text{ mL}$
- $N = 0.1 \text{ N}$

**Step 1: Difference in titres**

$$V_1 - V_2 = 48.0 - 22.5 = 25.5 \text{ mL}$$

**Step 2: Apply the master formula**

$$\text{Iodine Value} = \dfrac{(V_1 - V_2) \times N \times 12.69}{W}$$

$$\text{Iodine Value} = \dfrac{25.5 \times 0.1 \times 12.69}{0.4}$$

**Step 3: Evaluate**

$$25.5 \times 0.1 = 2.55$$

$$2.55 \times 12.69 = 32.3595$$

$$\dfrac{32.3595}{0.4} = 80.90$$

$$\boxed{\text{Iodine Value} \approx 80.90 \text{ g I}_2 \text{ / 100 g oil}}$$

**Step 4: Classification**

Since **80.90 < 100**, the oil is classified as a **non-drying oil** (similar to **olive oil**, which has I.V. in the range 75–95). It is **not suitable for paint/varnish** applications but is excellent as an **edible/cooking oil or cosmetic base**.

---

### ⭐ **Question B — ALTERNATIVE CHOICE (14 Marks)**

**(a)** Compare the **Wijs method** and **Hanus method** for iodine value determination. State the merits and demerits of each. (7 marks)

**(b)** An unknown oil sample weighing **0.3 g** consumed **20.4 mL of 0.1 N Na₂S₂O₃** (sample titre) against a blank of **45.0 mL**. Calculate the iodine value. If the oil is suspected to be **linseed oil adulterated with coconut oil**, comment on the result. The standard I.V. of pure linseed oil is **180** and of coconut oil is **10**. (7 marks)

---

### **Model Answer for Question B:**

#### **(a) Comparison: Wijs vs Hanus Method (7 marks)**

| Parameter | Wijs Method | Hanus Method |
|---|---|---|
| **Active reagent** | Iodine monochloride (ICl) | Iodine monobromide (IBr) |
| **Addition rate** | Fast (quantitative in 30 min) | Slower (requires 30–60 min) |
| **Stability of reagent** | Highly stable (months) | Less stable, must be freshly prepared |
| **Accuracy** | More accurate & reproducible | Slightly less precise |
| **Official status** | AOCS, BIS, IUPAC preferred | Older method, still in textbooks |
| **Best suited for** | All oils, including highly unsaturated (linseed) | Less unsaturated oils |
| **Substitution reaction risk** | Very low | Higher (can overestimate I.V.) |
| **Cost** | Higher (ICl expensive) | Lower (IBr cheaper) |

**Merits of Wijs:** Higher accuracy, stable reagent, official method, less side reactions.
**Demerits of Wijs:** Expensive ICl, requires careful handling (corrosive).
**Merits of Hanus:** Inexpensive, simpler to prepare.
**Demerits of Hanus:** Less stable reagent, prone to substitution (Br adds to saturated chains), so I.V. can be **overestimated by 1–2%**.

---

#### **(b) Numerical Solution + Adulteration Analysis (7 marks)**

**Given:**
- $W = 0.3 \text{ g}$
- $V_1 = 45.0 \text{ mL}$
- $V_2 = 20.4 \text{ mL}$
- $N = 0.1 \text{ N}$

**Step 1: Difference**

$$V_1 - V_2 = 45.0 - 20.4 = 24.6 \text{ mL}$$

**Step 2: Iodine Value**

$$\text{Iodine Value} = \dfrac{24.6 \times 0.1 \times 12.69}{0.3} = \dfrac{31.2174}{0.3} = 104.06$$

$$\boxed{\text{Iodine Value} \approx 104.06 \text{ g I}_2 \text{ / 100 g oil}}$$

**Step 3: Adulteration Analysis**

- Pure linseed oil I.V. = **180**
- Pure coconut oil I.V. = **10**
- Calculated I.V. of sample = **104.06**

The sample's I.V. is **far below** that of pure linseed oil (180) and **far above** that of pure coconut oil (10). The **drastic reduction** from 180 → 104 confirms that the sample is **adulterated**, i.e., linseed oil has been mixed with a significant proportion of coconut oil (a non-drying oil).

**Approximate estimation using linear mixing rule:**

$$\text{If } x \text{ is fraction of linseed, } (1-x) \text{ is fraction of coconut}$$

$$180x + 10(1-x) = 104.06$$

$$170x = 94.06 \implies x \approx 0.553 \text{ (i.e., ~55% linseed + 45% coconut)}$$

**Conclusion:** The sample contains approximately **55% linseed oil and 45% coconut oil** — confirming deliberate **adulteration** for economic gain (coconut oil is much cheaper).

---

> [!WARNING]
> **🚨 KTU EXAMINER'S VALUATION PITFALLS — Common Mark-Loss Areas**
> 1. **Forgetting the 12.69 factor** — using 126.9 instead of 12.69 will give I.V. values 10× too high. Examiners deduct **full marks** for this.
> 2. **Swapping V₁ and V₂** — sample titre is ALWAYS smaller (or equal) than blank. If V₂ > V₁, the experiment is invalid; check for spillage.
> 3. **No dark incubation** — Wijs reaction must be done in **darkness** to prevent photochemical side reactions.
> 4. **Adding starch too early** — starch forms an insoluble complex with I₂ at high concentrations. Add it only when the solution is **pale yellow**.
> 5. **Reporting units** — always report as **"g I₂ / 100 g oil"** (a dimensionless ratio), not just a number.
> 6. **Not classifying the oil** after calculation — KTU expects the **category** (drying/semi-drying/non-drying) as part of the answer.

---

## ✅ Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- 📌 **Iodine Value = g of I₂ absorbed by 100 g of oil** — the standard IUPAC reporting basis.
- 📌 **Master formula:** $\text{IV} = \dfrac{(V_1 - V_2) \times N \times 12.69}{W}$ — memorize verbatim.
- 📌 **The constant 12.69** = (126.9 / 10) — combines equivalent mass of I₂ with the per-100-g conversion.
- 📌 **Wijs reagent (ICl in acetic acid)** is the preferred interhalogen — more stable and accurate than IBr (Hanus) or I₂ (Hübl).
- 📌 **Dark incubation for 30 min** is essential to avoid photochemical side reactions.
- 📌 **Back-titration principle** — we never measure absorbed ICl directly; we measure the **unreacted excess** by converting to I₂ → titrating with thiosulfate.
- 📌 **Starch indicator** is added near the endpoint (pale yellow stage) — never at the start.
- 📌 **Classification:** Non-drying (<100) | Semi-drying (100–140) | Drying (>140).
- 📌 **Higher I.V. = more unsaturation = more reactive** (drying in air, prone to rancidity).
- 📌 **Adulteration detection** — comparing measured I.V. with standard values of pure oils reveals mixing.
- 📌 **Hydrogenation monitoring** — I.V. drops as H₂ is added to C=C bonds; full hydrogenation gives I.V. ≈ 0.
- 📌 **End point of titration** — disappearance of the **blue starch-iodine complex** (colorless solution).
- 📌 **KI is added AFTER** the dark incubation, not before, to liberate only the unreacted excess ICl.
- 📌 **Normality of Na₂S₂O₃** should always be **standardized** (using K₂Cr₂O₇ or KIO₃) before the experiment.
- 📌 **Glass stopper must be used** — ICl and I₂ are volatile; an ordinary cork/cap will allow losses.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Saponification Value of Oils
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCHL307 - CHEMICAL TECHNOLOGY AND ENVIRONMENTAL ENGINEERING LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Saponification Value of Oils

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Aligned)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Saponification Value (SV) of an oil or fat** is defined as the number of **milligrams of potassium hydroxide (KOH)** required to completely saponify **1 gram of the oil/fat sample**.

It is a key quality parameter that quantifies the average molecular weight (chain length) of the fatty acids present in a triglyceride sample. Mathematically, it is the inverse measure of the **mean molecular weight of the fatty acid constituents** of the oil.

$$SV = \frac{\text{mg of KOH consumed}}{\text{gram of oil}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why KTU Tests This Concept:** The saponification value is an industrial specification used in soap manufacturing, edible oil refining, and cosmetics formulation. BIS (Bureau of Indian Standards) specifies SV ranges for commercial oils (e.g., coconut, groundnut, linseed).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Soap Recipe" Intuition

Imagine a **construction crew** demolishing an old brick wall:
- Each brick represents **one fatty acid molecule**.
- The demolition crew's workers represent **KOH molecules**.
- The **Saponification Value** is essentially the *"number of workers needed to dismantle 1 kg of the wall"*.

If the wall is built of **small bricks** (short-chain fatty acids, e.g., coconut oil), **more mortar joints** exist per kilogram, so **more KOH workers are needed** → **Higher SV**.
If the wall is built of **large bricks** (long-chain fatty acids, e.g., castor oil), **fewer joints** exist, so **fewer KOH workers are needed** → **Lower SV**.

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Trick:** *"Short and stout, saponify a lot"*. Shorter fatty acid chains → More KOH consumed → Higher Saponification Value.

## 1.3 Underlying Chemical Reaction

The reaction of a triglyceride (oil/fat) with alcoholic KOH:

$$\text{Triglyceride (R-COO)}_3\text{C}_3\text{H}_5 + 3\,\text{KOH} \xrightarrow{\Delta} \text{C}_3\text{H}_5(\text{OH})_3 + 3\,\text{R-COOK}$$

The products are:
- **Glycerol** (C₃H₅(OH)₃) — a polyhydric alcohol
- **Potassium salts of fatty acids (R-COOK)** — these ARE the soap molecules

The leftover (unreacted) KOH is quantified via **back-titration with standard HCl** using phenolphthalein indicator.

## 1.4 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Standard Value | Purpose |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Equivalent weight of KOH | **56.1 g/eq** | Stoichiometric conversion factor |
| Standard alkali for saponification | **0.5 N alcoholic KOH** | Reflux reagent |
| Back-titrant | **0.5 N HCl** | Quantifies excess KOH |
| Indicator | **Phenolphthalein** | Pink → Colourless endpoint |
| Reflux time | **30–60 minutes** | Ensures complete saponification |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Logic Behind the Saponification Process — Step by Step

The experiment is a **back-titration**, not a direct titration, because:
- Oils are **immiscible** with aqueous KOH at room temperature.
- **Alcoholic KOH** is used to dissolve the oil.
- **Direct titration endpoint** is hard to detect (oily medium + slow reaction).
- Therefore, a **known excess** of KOH is added, and the **unreacted excess** is titrated against standard acid.

### Operational Logic Flow:

- **Step 1 — Weighing:** A precise mass (W, usually 1–2 g) of oil is weighed into a clean, dry conical flask.
- **Step 2 — Alkali Addition:** A measured excess volume (V_KOH = 25 mL) of standardized alcoholic KOH is added.
- **Step 3 — Saponification (Reflux):** The mixture is heated under reflux for 30–60 minutes. This drives the triglyceride + KOH reaction to completion, producing glycerol + soap.
- **Step 4 — Cooling & Indicator:** The flask is cooled; 2–3 drops of phenolphthalein are added. The solution turns **pink** (alkaline, due to excess KOH).
- **Step 5 — Back-Titration:** Standard HCl (N_HCl) is run from the burette until the pink colour just **disappears**. Note this volume = **V_sample**.
- **Step 6 — Blank Titration:** Repeat Steps 2–5 **without oil** (or with an inert, non-saponifiable substitute). Note this volume = **V_blank**.
- **Step 7 — Calculation:** The difference (V_blank − V_sample) corresponds to KOH consumed **by the oil** alone.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why is the blank titer always higher than the sample titer?**
> In the blank, **all 25 mL of KOH** must be neutralized by HCl → large titre (V_blank).
> In the sample, **part of the 25 mL KOH** is consumed by the oil → less KOH remains → small titre (V_sample).
> Therefore, **V_blank > V_sample** is the **expected experimental outcome**. A reversed result indicates a procedural error.

## 2.2 Master Formula Sheet (KTU Exam-Ready)

> [!NOTE]
> The following table is **verbatim** what you should write in the KTU answer sheet. No vertical bar symbol `|` is used inside the math expressions; we use `\vert` for absolute value.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Notes |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | **Saponification Value (SV)** | $SV = \dfrac{(V_b - V_s) \times N_{HCl} \times 56.1}{W}$ | mg KOH / g oil | Primary result |
| 2 | KOH consumed by oil | $n_{KOH} = (V_b - V_s) \times N_{HCl}$ | milli-equivalents | Stoichiometric basis |
| 3 | Mean Mol. Wt. of Triglyceride | $M_{tri} = \dfrac{3 \times 56.1 \times 1000}{SV}$ | g/mol | Derived relationship |
| 4 | Mean Mol. Wt. of Fatty Acid | $M_{FA} = \dfrac{56.1 \times 1000}{SV} - 12.67$ | g/mol | Approx. relationship |
| 5 | Moles of triglyceride in W g | $n_{tri} = \dfrac{W}{M_{tri}}$ | mol | Theoretical basis |

**Where the symbols denote:**
- $V_b$ = Volume of HCl used in **blank** titration (mL)
- $V_s$ = Volume of HCl used in **sample** titration (mL)
- $N_{HCl}$ = Normality of standard HCl
- $W$ = Mass of oil sample taken (g)
- $56.1$ = Equivalent weight of KOH (g/eq)
- The difference $(V_b - V_s)$ has units of mL, multiplied by N (meq/mL) → meq of KOH consumed.

## 2.3 Engineering & Industrial Utility

- **Soap Industry:** Direct input for calculating the alkali (NaOH/KOH) requirement for batch saponification.
- **Edible Oil Quality:** Detects adulteration (e.g., castor oil mixed with coconut oil gives abnormal SV).
- **Cosmetics:** Determines the emulsifier/soap-to-oil ratio in lotions and shampoos.
- **Biodiesel Production:** Indicates the transesterification stoichiometry (FAME yield depends on fatty acid MW).
- **Lubricant Formulation:** Higher SV oils (shorter chains) are more biodegradable but less stable.

## 2.4 Typical Saponification Values of Common Oils (BIS Reference)

> [!TIP]
> **Mnemonic for SV ranges (descending):** *"Coconut Palm Linseed Olive Groundnut Cotton Castor"*

| Oil / Fat | Typical SV Range (mg KOH/g) | Fatty Acid Character |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Coconut oil | **250 – 264** | Short-chain (C8–C14 dominant) |
| Palm kernel oil | **245 – 255** | Short-chain |
| Cottonseed oil | **190 – 200** | Medium-chain (C16, C18) |
| Groundnut oil | **188 – 196** | Medium-chain |
| Linseed oil | **188 – 195** | Unsaturated long-chain |
| Olive oil | **185 – 196** | Long-chain (oleic dominant) |
| Sunflower oil | **188 – 194** | Long-chain |
| Castor oil | **176 – 187** | Very long-chain (ricinoleic) |

> A **higher-than-expected SV** indicates shorter fatty acid chains; a **lower-than-expected SV** indicates longer chains or presence of unsaponifiable matter.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Saponification Value Formula

### 3.1.1 Stoichiometric Starting Point

The saponification reaction for a generic triglyceride:

$$(\text{R-COO})_3 \text{C}_3\text{H}_5 + 3\,\text{KOH} \rightarrow \text{C}_3\text{H}_5(\text{OH})_3 + 3\,\text{R-COOK}$$

From the balanced equation, the **molar ratio** is:
- 1 mole of triglyceride reacts with **exactly 3 moles of KOH**.

### 3.1.2 Linking Moles to Mass

Let:
- $W$ = mass of oil sample (grams)
- $M_{tri}$ = molecular weight of triglyceride (g/mol)

Then the number of moles of triglyceride saponified is:

$$n_{tri} = \frac{W}{M_{tri}}$$

The number of moles of KOH that reacted (by stoichiometry) is:

$$n_{KOH} = 3 \times n_{tri} = \frac{3W}{M_{tri}}$$

The mass of KOH consumed (in grams) is:

$$m_{KOH} = n_{KOH} \times 56.1 = \frac{3W \times 56.1}{M_{tri}}$$

The mass of KOH consumed (in **milligrams**) is:

$$m_{KOH}(\text{mg}) = \frac{3W \times 56.1 \times 1000}{M_{tri}}$$

### 3.1.3 Definition of SV (per gram of oil)

By definition, SV = mg KOH per **1 g** of oil. So divide by W:

$$SV = \frac{m_{KOH}(\text{mg})}{W} = \frac{3 \times 56.1 \times 1000}{M_{tri}}$$

Rearranging gives the **inverse relationship** between SV and triglyceride molecular weight:

$$\boxed{M_{tri} = \frac{3 \times 56.1 \times 1000}{SV} = \frac{168300}{SV}}$$

### 3.1.4 Conversion to the Experimental Formula (Back-Titration)

The KOH consumed by the oil is determined experimentally by back-titration with HCl.

**Reaction in titration:**
$$\text{KOH (excess)} + \text{HCl} \rightarrow \text{KCl} + \text{H}_2\text{O}$$

**Equivalents of HCl used (sample titration):**
$$\text{meq}_{HCl}^{sample} = V_s \times N_{HCl}$$

**Equivalents of HCl used (blank titration):**
$$\text{meq}_{HCl}^{blank} = V_b \times N_{HCl}$$

**Equivalents of KOH actually consumed by oil:**
$$\text{meq}_{KOH}^{oil} = (V_b - V_s) \times N_{HCl}$$

**Mass of KOH consumed (mg):**
$$m_{KOH}^{oil} = (V_b - V_s) \times N_{HCl} \times 56.1 \text{ mg}$$

**Mass of KOH consumed per gram of oil (the Saponification Value):**
$$SV = \frac{m_{KOH}^{oil}}{W} = \frac{(V_b - V_s) \times N_{HCl} \times 56.1}{W}$$

$$\boxed{SV = \frac{(V_b - V_s) \times N_{HCl} \times 56.1}{W} \;\; \frac{\text{mg KOH}}{\text{g oil}}}$$

This is the **canonical formula** examiners expect.

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (Full Step-by-Step)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Problem Statement (Typical KTU-style):**
> In an experiment to determine the saponification value of a refined oil sample, 1.5 g of the oil was refluxed with 25.0 mL of 0.5 N alcoholic KOH for 45 minutes. The excess KOH required 18.4 mL of 0.5 N HCl for neutralization (sample titre). A blank titration (without oil) consumed 38.6 mL of 0.5 N HCl. Calculate the saponification value of the oil and identify the likely source.

### Given Data

$$W = 1.5\;\text{g}, \quad V_s = 18.4\;\text{mL}, \quad V_b = 38.6\;\text{mL}, \quad N_{HCl} = 0.5\;\text{N}$$

### Step 1 — Identify the volume difference

$$\Delta V = V_b - V_s = 38.6 - 18.4 = 20.2\;\text{mL}$$

### Step 2 — Compute the KOH consumed (in milli-equivalents)

$$\text{meq}_{KOH}^{oil} = \Delta V \times N_{HCl} = 20.2 \times 0.5 = 10.1\;\text{meq}$$

### Step 3 — Convert meq of KOH to mass (mg)

$$m_{KOH} = 10.1 \times 56.1 = 566.61\;\text{mg}$$

### Step 4 — Apply the SV formula

$$SV = \frac{m_{KOH}}{W} = \frac{566.61}{1.5}$$

$$\boxed{SV = 377.74\;\text{mg KOH/g}}$$

### Step 5 — Verify with mean triglyceride molecular weight

$$M_{tri} = \frac{168300}{SV} = \frac{168300}{377.74} = 445.6\;\text{g/mol}$$

> A molecular weight of ~445 g/mol is **abnormally low** (typical triglycerides have M ≈ 800–900 g/mol). The high SV suggests a **highly saponifiable, low-molecular-weight oil** — characteristic of a **synthetic short-chain ester or a contaminated/adulterated sample** (pure coconut oil tops out around 264). In a teaching context, a value > 300 should prompt re-checking the normality of HCl and the weighing accuracy.

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix (Quick Recognition)

| Observation | Inference |
| :--- | :--- |
| $V_b > V_s$ by a **large margin** | High KOH consumption → High SV → Short-chain oil |
| $V_b \approx V_s$ (very small difference) | Negligible saponification → Unsaponifiable matter dominant |
| $V_s > V_b$ (negative difference) | **Procedural error** — re-check indicator, normality, blank |
| SV = 250–265 | Likely **coconut** or **palm kernel** oil |
| SV = 188–196 | Likely **groundnut / olive / cottonseed** oil |
| SV = 176–187 | Likely **castor** oil |
| SV > 300 | Reagent/weighing error OR short-chain synthetic ester |

## 3.4 Comprehensive List of Sources of Error (For 14-Mark Answers)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often lose 2–3 marks for NOT distinguishing between **systematic** and **random** errors, and for missing the word **"alcoholic"** KOH.

| Error Type | Specific Cause | Effect on SV |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Systematic — Reagent** | Use of aqueous (not alcoholic) KOH — incomplete saponification | Lower SV |
| **Systematic — Reflux** | Insufficient heating time (< 30 min) | Lower SV |
| **Systematic — Blank** | Not running a parallel blank titration | Cannot compute (V_b − V_s) |
| **Random — Weighing** | Oil sticking to flask walls | Lower W → **Higher** SV |
| **Random — Titration** | Overshooting the endpoint (excess HCl) | Higher V_s → **Lower** SV |
| **Random — Indicator** | Adding too much phenolphthalein (intense pink) | Fades slowly → Larger V_s → Lower SV |

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of the Saponification Experiment

The following Mermaid diagram maps the **functional flow** of the experiment, from sample preparation through titration to calculation. It serves as a substitute for the physical apparatus drawing (reflux condenser + water bath).

> **Mermaid Compilation Note:** All node IDs are alphanumeric and prefixed with letters to avoid reserved-keyword conflicts.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[Step 1: Clean and dry a 250 mL conical flask] --> A2[Step 2: Accurately weigh 1 to 2 g of oil sample W grams]
    A2 --> A3[Step 3: Pipette 25 mL of standardized 0.5 N alcoholic KOH]
    A3 --> A4[Step 4: Attach a reflux air condenser to the flask]
    A4 --> A5[Step 5: Heat on a boiling water bath for 30 to 60 minutes with swirling]
    A5 --> A6[Step 6: Cool to room temperature under tap water]
    A6 --> A7[Step 7: Add 2 to 3 drops of phenolphthalein indicator]
    A7 --> A8[Step 8: Pink colour appears confirming excess KOH]
    A8 --> A9[Step 9: Titrate the excess KOH against 0.5 N HCl until pink disappears]
    A9 --> A10[Step 10: Record burette reading as Vs in mL]
    A10 --> A11[Step 11: Repeat steps 3 to 10 without oil to obtain blank titre Vb]
    A11 --> A12[Step 12: Apply formula SV equals Vb minus Vs times N HCl times 56.1 divided by W]
    A12 --> A13[Step 13: Report SV in mg KOH per g and identify oil from standard table]

    classDef stepBox fill:#E0F2FE,stroke:#0369A1,color:#0C4A6E,stroke-width:1px
    classDef critBox fill:#FEF3C7,stroke:#B45309,color:#78350F,stroke-width:2px
    classDef endBox fill:#DCFCE7,stroke:#15803D,color:#14532D,stroke-width:2px

    class A1,A2,A3,A4,A5,A6,A7,A8,A9,A10,A11 stepBox
    class A12 critBox
    class A13 endBox
```

## 4.2 Process-Phase Schematic — Why Back-Titration is Necessary

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Oil Sample W grams] --> P2[Add KNOWN EXCESS of alcoholic KOH V1 mL of N1]
    P2 --> P3[Reflux: Complete saponification occurs]
    P3 --> P4[Remaining UNREACTED KOH equals V1 minus V_consumed]
    P4 --> P5[Titrate unreacted KOH with standard HCl]
    P5 --> P6[Volume of HCl used equals Vb minus Vs in blank minus sample]
    P6 --> P7[Calculate KOH that reacted with oil and convert to mg per g]

    classDef phsBox fill:#FCE7F3,stroke:#9D174D,color:#831843,stroke-width:1px
    class P1,P2,P3,P4,P5,P6,P7 phsBox
```

## 4.3 Concept Map — Saponification Value and Its Inverse Relationships

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    C1[Saponification Value SV] --> C2[High SV greater than 250]
    C1 --> C3[Low SV less than 190]
    C1 --> C4[Moderate SV 190 to 250]

    C2 --> C5[Short chain fatty acids C8 to C12]
    C2 --> C6[Low molecular weight triglyceride]
    C2 --> C7[Examples: Coconut oil Palm kernel oil]

    C3 --> C8[Long chain fatty acids C18 and above]
    C3 --> C9[High molecular weight triglyceride]
    C3 --> C10[Examples: Castor oil Rapeseed oil]

    C4 --> C11[Medium chain C14 to C18]
    C4 --> C12[Examples: Groundnut Sunflower Olive]

    C1 --> C13[SV inversely proportional to mean molecular weight of triglyceride]
    C13 --> C14[Formula: Mtri equals 168300 divided by SV]

    classDef highBox fill:#FEE2E2,stroke:#B91C1C,color:#7F1D1D,stroke-width:1px
    classDef lowBox fill:#DBEAFE,stroke:#1D4ED8,color:#1E3A8A,stroke-width:1px
```
