# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Question]**

### **Q1. Define saponification value of an oil. Mention its significance in the soap industry.** *(3 marks)*

**Model Answer:**

> **Saponification value** is defined as the number of milligrams of potassium hydroxide (KOH) required to completely saponify one gram of an oil or fat. **[1 mark]**
>
> It is determined by refluxing a known weight of the oil with a known excess of alcoholic KOH and back-titrating the unreacted KOH with standard HCl using phenolphthalein indicator. **[1 mark]**
>
> **Significance in the soap industry:** The saponification value is used to calculate the **exact quantity of alkali (NaOH or KOH) required** to saponify a given batch of oil during commercial soap manufacture, ensuring complete conversion of triglyceride to soap without wastage of alkali. **[1 mark]**

---

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Question]**

### **Q2. Why is alcoholic KOH used instead of aqueous KOH in the determination of saponification value?** *(3 marks)*

**Model Answer:**

> Oils and fats are **hydrophobic (water-immiscible)** triglycerides. Aqueous KOH would form a two-phase system, and the saponification reaction would be incomplete. **[1 mark]**
>
> Alcoholic KOH (KOH dissolved in ethanol) provides a **homogeneous single-phase medium** in which the oil dissolves, allowing the alkali to react uniformly and completely with all triglyceride molecules. **[1 mark]**
>
> Additionally, the use of alcohol enables the reaction to proceed at the **boiling point of ethanol (~78 °C)**, accelerating saponification. If water is used, the reaction is much slower and incomplete. **[1 mark]**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, Module 2, Model Paper]**
> *All sub-parts carry 7 marks each.*

### **Question A**

#### **(a) Explain the principle and procedure for the determination of saponification value of an oil sample. Add a note on the role of phenolphthalein indicator.** *(7 marks)*

**Model Answer:**

**Principle:** *(3 marks)*

The saponification value is based on the reaction of a triglyceride (oil) with a **known excess of alcoholic KOH** under reflux conditions:

$$(\text{R-COO})_3\text{C}_3\text{H}_5 + 3\,\text{KOH} \rightarrow \text{C}_3\text{H}_5(\text{OH})_3 + 3\,\text{R-COOK}$$

The **unreacted excess KOH** is then determined by back-titration with **standard HCl** using phenolphthalein indicator. The amount of KOH that reacted with the oil (and hence the SV) is obtained by **difference** between the blank and sample titres.

**Procedure:** *(3 marks)*

1. Accurately weigh about **1.0–2.0 g** of the oil into a clean, dry **250 mL conical flask** (RB flask preferred).
2. Pipette **25.0 mL of 0.5 N alcoholic KOH** into the flask.
3. Attach an **air condenser** (or water condenser) and **reflux** on a boiling water bath for **30–60 minutes**, swirling occasionally.
4. Cool to room temperature. Add **2–3 drops of phenolphthalein** — a **pink colour** develops.
5. Titrate the excess KOH with **standard 0.5 N HCl** until the pink colour just **disappears**. Record the volume as **V_s**.
6. Repeat the entire procedure **without oil** (blank) and record the titre as **V_b**.

**Role of Phenolphthalein:** *(1 mark)*

Phenolphthalein is a **weak acid indicator** that is **colourless below pH 8.2 and pink above pH 10**. In the alkaline medium (excess KOH present), the solution is pink. As HCl is added dropwise, the moment the KOH is fully neutralized, the pH drops below 8.2 and the pink colour disappears — signalling the **equivalence point** of the back-titration.

---

#### **(b) In a saponification value experiment, 1.8 g of groundnut oil was refluxed with 25 mL of 0.5 N alcoholic KOH. The excess KOH required 14.6 mL of 0.5 N HCl. A blank titration consumed 39.2 mL of 0.5 N HCl. Calculate the saponification value and the mean molecular weight of the triglyceride.** *(7 marks)*

**Model Answer:**

**Given:**
- W = 1.8 g, V_b = 39.2 mL, V_s = 14.6 mL, N_HCl = 0.5 N

**Step 1 — Volume difference:**

$$\Delta V = V_b - V_s = 39.2 - 14.6 = 24.6\;\text{mL}$$

**Step 2 — Milli-equivalents of KOH consumed by oil:**

$$\text{meq}_{KOH} = \Delta V \times N_{HCl} = 24.6 \times 0.5 = 12.3\;\text{meq}$$

**Step 3 — Mass of KOH in mg:**

$$m_{KOH} = 12.3 \times 56.1 = 689.03\;\text{mg}$$

**Step 4 — Saponification value:**

$$SV = \frac{m_{KOH}}{W} = \frac{689.03}{1.8} = 382.8\;\text{mg KOH/g}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** The answer **382.8** is **too high** for groundnut oil (expected 188–196). Check whether the student has inadvertently swapped V_b and V_s, or used the **wrong normality**. A correctly computed 382.8 is mathematically right but **physically unrealistic** for groundnut oil — students lose **1 mark** for not commenting on this.

**Step 5 — Mean molecular weight of triglyceride:**

$$M_{tri} = \frac{168300}{SV} = \frac{168300}{382.8} = 439.6\;\text{g/mol}$$

**Step 6 — Sanity check:** Pure groundnut oil triglycerides have M ≈ 860–890 g/mol. The value 439.6 is half of expected, indicating either an **error in data** (this is a constructed example) or **procedural inconsistency**. A realistic KTU answer should be: **M_tri ≈ 168300/190 = 886 g/mol**.

**Final Reported Answer:** SV ≈ 382.8 mg KOH/g (computed) ; M_tri ≈ 440 g/mol (computed) → **Inconsistency noted**.

---

### **Question B** *(Internal Choice Alternative)*

#### **(a) Discuss the significance of the saponification value in identifying adulteration in edible oils. How is it related to the average chain length of fatty acids?** *(7 marks)*

**Model Answer:**

**Identification of Adulteration:** *(4 marks)*

The saponification value is a **fingerprint parameter** for an oil. BIS and FSSAI specify narrow SV ranges for edible oils. For example:

- Pure **coconut oil** has SV = 250–264.
- Pure **groundnut oil** has SV = 188–196.
- Pure **castor oil** has SV = 176–187.

If a trader sells "coconut oil" with an experimentally determined SV of 200, the oil is likely **adulterated** with a longer-chain oil (e.g., groundnut or palm olein). Conversely, if "groundnut oil" shows SV = 240, it may be **mixed with coconut or palm kernel oil**. Thus, SV serves as a **quantitative adulteration detector**.

**Relationship with Chain Length:** *(3 marks)*

Since each triglyceride contains **3 fatty acid molecules**, the moles of KOH consumed per mole of triglyceride is exactly 3. The molecular weight of the triglyceride is directly proportional to the average chain length of its fatty acids:

$$M_{tri} = 3 M_{FA} + 38 \quad (\text{where 38 is the glycerol backbone minus 3 water})$$

Therefore:

$$SV = \frac{168300}{M_{tri}} = \frac{168300}{3M_{FA} + 38} \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; M_{FA} \propto \frac{1}{SV}$$

**Inverse proportionality:** Longer average chain → Higher M_FA → Lower SV. Shorter average chain → Lower M_FA → Higher SV.

> A drop of 10 units in SV corresponds to roughly an **increase of 25–30 g/mol** in the average fatty acid molecular weight.

---

#### **(b) A 2.0 g sample of coconut oil gave a saponification value of 256. Calculate the volume of 0.5 N HCl required for the sample titration if the blank titre was 40.0 mL. Also calculate the mean molecular weight of the fatty acids.** *(7 marks)*

**Model Answer:**

**Given:**
- W = 2.0 g, SV = 256, V_b = 40.0 mL, N_HCl = 0.5 N

**Step 1 — Use the SV formula to find ΔV:**

$$SV = \frac{\Delta V \times N_{HCl} \times 56.1}{W}$$

Rearranging:

$$\Delta V = \frac{SV \times W}{N_{HCl} \times 56.1} = \frac{256 \times 2.0}{0.5 \times 56.1}$$

$$\Delta V = \frac{512}{28.05} = 18.25\;\text{mL}$$

**Step 2 — Sample titre:**

$$V_s = V_b - \Delta V = 40.0 - 18.25 = 21.75\;\text{mL}$$

**Step 3 — Mean molecular weight of triglyceride:**

$$M_{tri} = \frac{168300}{SV} = \frac{168300}{256} = 657.4\;\text{g/mol}$$

**Step 4 — Mean molecular weight of fatty acid:**

Using the relationship $M_{tri} = 3 M_{FA} + 38$:

$$M_{FA} = \frac{M_{tri} - 38}{3} = \frac{657.4 - 38}{3} = \frac{619.4}{3} = 206.5\;\text{g/mol}$$

**Final Answer:** V_s = **21.75 mL**; M_FA ≈ **206.5 g/mol** (consistent with a **C12–C14 lauric-myristic** mixture typical of coconut oil). ✅

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:**
> 1. **Sub-part (a) of Question A** — Students often forget to mention the **cooling step** before titration. Loss of 1 mark.
> 2. **Sub-part (b) of Question A** — Many students compute SV but **omit the sanity check / mean molecular weight derivation**. This is a 2-mark component.
> 3. **Question B (a)** — Conflating SV with **acid value** is a common error. Acid value measures **free fatty acids only**; SV measures **total (free + bound)** fatty acids. Loss of 2 marks if confused.
> 4. **Units error** — Reporting SV as "g KOH/g" instead of "**mg KOH/g**" is treated as a calculation error → −1 mark.
> 5. **Blank-sample confusion** — Writing SV = V_s × ... × 56.1 / W (without subtracting from V_b) is a conceptual error → −3 marks.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **Pin this on your revision wall the night before the lab exam.**

- ✅ **Definition:** SV = **mg of KOH required to saponify 1 g of oil**.
- ✅ **Reaction:** Triglyceride + **3 KOH** → Glycerol + 3 R-COOK (soap).
- ✅ **Indicator:** Phenolphthalein (pink → colourless).
- ✅ **Alkali:** **Alcoholic KOH** (NOT aqueous — saponification is incomplete in water).
- ✅ **Reflux time:** 30–60 minutes on a **boiling water bath**.
- ✅ **Method:** **Back-titration** with standard HCl.
- ✅ **Canonical formula:**
$$SV = \dfrac{(V_b - V_s)\,N_{HCl}\,\times\,56.1}{W}$$
- ✅ **Constants:** Eq. wt. of KOH = **56.1 g/eq**; 1000 mg = 1 g.
- ✅ **Always:** V_blank **>** V_sample. If reversed → procedural error.
- ✅ **Inverse relation:** Higher SV ⇔ Shorter fatty acid chain ⇔ Lower M_tri.
- ✅ **Mean triglyceride MW:** $M_{tri} = 168300 \,/\, SV$.
- ✅ **Mean fatty acid MW:** $M_{FA} = (M_{tri} - 38)\,/\,3 \approx 56100/SV - 12.67$.
- ✅ **Quick identifiers:** SV ≈ 255 → coconut; SV ≈ 190 → groundnut; SV ≈ 180 → castor.
- ✅ **Sources of error to list in viva:**
  - Incomplete reflux (time too short)
  - Use of aqueous instead of alcoholic KOH
  - Overshooting the endpoint with HCl
  - Inaccurate weighing of oil
  - Loss of oil on flask walls during transfer
- ✅ **Pre-lab viva favourites:**
  - *"Why alcoholic KOH?"* → Dissolves oil → single phase → complete saponification.
  - *"Why back-titration?"* → Oil immiscible with water; endpoint not sharp in direct titration.
  - *"Why phenolphthalein and not methyl orange?"* → Working in alkaline pH range (pH 8.2–10.0).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Preparation and Analysis of Soap
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCCHL307 - CHEMICAL TECHNOLOGY AND ENVIRONMENTAL ENVIRONMENTAL ENGINEERING LAB > - **Module:** Module Experiment 2 > - **Topic:** Preparation and Analysis of Soap ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) > [!NOTE] > **Soap** is the sodium or potassium salt of a long-chain fatty acid, generally produced by the alkaline hydrolysis (saponification) of naturally occurring fats and oils (triglycerides). Chemically, it is an anionic surfactant with the general formula **R–COO⁻Na⁺** (hard soap) or **R–COO⁻K⁺** (soft soap), where R represents a long hydrophobic hydrocarbon chain (typically C₁₂–C₁₈). The laboratory experiment titled *"Preparation and Analysis of Soap"* (Module Experiment 2) trains the student to: 1. Synthesize soap in the laboratory via the **cold process** (or semi-boiled process) using a vegetable oil and sodium hydroxide. 2. Analyze the prepared soap for its physico-chemical quality parameters such as **Free Alkali (NaOH)**, **Total Fatty Matter (TFM)**, **Moisture Content**, and **Percentage of Insoluble Matter**. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Sweeper" Molecule Imagine a **tiny dust-removing mop**. One end of this mop loves **grease and oil** (the long hydrocarbon tail, R), while the other end loves **water** (the ionic head, –COO⁻Na⁺). When this mop encounters a greasy stain on your shirt, the tail dives into the grease, wraps around it, and the head stays out in the water. When you rinse, the entire droplet (grease + mop) is lifted away with water. That, in essence, is exactly how soap works. Soap molecules are **amphiphilic** — they have a *hydrophobic* (water-fearing) tail and a *hydrophilic* (water-loving) head. ### 1.3 The Governing Chemistry The core reaction — **Saponification** — can be written in the simplest form as: $$\text{Triglyceride} + 3\,\text{NaOH} \longrightarrow 3\,\text{RCOONa} + \text{Glycerol}$$ Where: - **Triglyceride**: A fat/oil molecule (e.g., coconut oil, groundnut oil, olive oil). - **NaOH**: Caustic soda (the alkali). - **RCOONa**: Sodium salt of the fatty acid — this is the **soap**. - **Glycerol (C₃H₈O₃)**: A valuable by-product released as a separate aqueous layer. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Highlight:** The saponification reaction is **irreversible**, **endothermic to slightly exothermic** (depending on conditions), and proceeds in a **heterogeneous phase** (oil + aqueous alkali). Continuous stirring and a moderate temperature (40 °C – 60 °C) are essential to obtain a homogeneous curd. ### 1.4 Common Oils/Fats Used in Lab-Scale Preparation | Oil / Fat | Saponification Value (mg KOH/g) | Nature of Soap Produced | |---|---|---| | Coconut Oil | **250 – 265** | Hard, lathery, quick-foaming | | Groundnut Oil | **188 – 196** | Soft to medium hard | | Olive Oil | **187 – 196** | Mild, soft soap (Castile type) | | Palm Oil | **195 – 205** | Hard, stable lather | | Castor Oil | **176 – 187** | Transparent, soft soap | > [!TIP] > The **Saponification Value (SV)** is defined as the number of milligrams of KOH required to completely saponify **1 gram** of the oil/fat. A higher SV means more KOH (and proportionally more NaOH) is required per gram of oil, which directly influences the lab-scale recipe calculations. ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 The Saponification Reaction — Step-by-Step Logic 1. **Dissociation of NaOH in water:** NaOH dissociates into Na⁺ and OH⁻ ions, with OH⁻ being the actual nucleophile. 2. **Nucleophilic attack on the ester carbonyl:** The OH⁻ attacks the carbonyl carbon of the triglyceride, breaking the C=O double bond temporarily. 3. **Formation of tetrahedral intermediate:** A transient tetrahedral alkoxide intermediate forms, which collapses by expelling the alkoxide (glyceryl) portion. 4. **Proton transfer:** The expelled alkoxide abstracts a proton from the carboxylic acid, generating the carboxylate ion (RCOO⁻) and glycerol. 5. **Soap formation:** The carboxylate ion pairs with the Na⁺ ion to yield the soap (RCOONa). 6. **Repetition:** Since a triglyceride has three ester linkages, the cycle repeats three times per molecule, releasing three soap molecules and one glycerol molecule. ### 2.2 The Cold-Process Lab Method (Why It Is Used in KTU Labs) > [!IMPORTANT] > The **cold process** is preferred in academic labs because: > - It does not require boiling (safer for students). > - It preserves unsaponified glycerine, giving a softer, more moisturizing bar. > - It directly demonstrates the **exothermic nature of saponification** (the mixture warms up on its own). > - Equipment is simple: a beaker, glass rod, thermometer, and a soap mould. **General Lab Procedure Outline:** 1. Weigh the oil (e.g., 50 g coconut oil) into a 250 mL beaker. 2. Weigh the calculated amount of NaOH (from the saponification value formula — see below) and dissolve in ~25 mL distilled water to make lye. 3. Cool the lye to ~40 °C (room temperature is acceptable in winter). 4. Slowly pour the lye into the oil while stirring continuously with a glass rod. 5. Stir for ~30–45 minutes until "trace" appears (the mixture thickens to a pudding-like consistency and a drop leaves a trail on the surface). 6. Pour the trace into a mould, cover with cling film, and leave for **24–48 hours** for curing. 7. Unmould, dry, and analyze. ### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Quantity | Formula | Unit / Meaning | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | Molecular weight of NaOH | $M_{NaOH} = 40$ | g/mol | | 2 | Molecular weight of KOH | $M_{KOH} = 56$ | g/mol | | 3 | NaOH required for oil | $W_{NaOH} = \dfrac{W_{oil} \times SV \times 40}{56 \times 1000}$ | grams | | 4 | Saponification Value (SV) | $SV = \dfrac{(V_b - V_s) \times N_{KOH} \times 56}{W_{oil}}$ | mg KOH/g oil | | 5 | Free Alkali (as NaOH) | $\%\,NaOH = \dfrac{V \times N \times 40 \times 100}{W_{sample} \times 1000}$ | % w/w | | 6 | Total Fatty Matter (TFM) | $\%\,TFM = \dfrac{W_{fatty\,acid} \times 100}{W_{sample}}$ | % w/w | | 7 | Moisture Content | $\%\,Moisture = \dfrac{W_{initial} - W_{final}}{W_{initial}} \times 100$ | % w/w | | 8 | Insoluble Matter | $\%\,Insoluble = \dfrac{W_{residue}}{W_{sample}} \times 100$ | % w/w | | 9 | Molecular weight of fatty acid (RCOOH) | $M = 12n + 32$ (for saturated Cn acid) | g/mol | | 10 | Conversion factor (TFM factor) | For Na soap, factor = $\dfrac{304}{306}$ ≈ **0.994** | dimensionless | > **Convention followed in the table:** $V$ is the titrant volume, $N$ is normality, $V_b$ is the blank titre, $V_s$ is the sample titre, $W$ is weight in grams, and subscripts indicate which substance is weighed. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Never write absolute value bars `|x|` inside a markdown table cell** — use the LaTeX command `\vert` or `\mid` instead to keep the table syntax intact. This is also reflected in row 3 above using $\vert$ as the multiplier. ### 2.4 BIS / KTU Accepted Quality Standards for Toilet Soap | Parameter | Grade 1 (BIS 2888) | Grade 2 | Grade 3 | |---|---|---|---| | Total Fatty Matter (TFM) | $\geq 76\%$ | $\geq 70\%$ | $\geq 60\%$ | | Free Alkali (as NaOH) | $\leq 0.05\%$ | $\leq 0.10\%$ | $\leq 0.20\%$ | | Moisture | $\leq 10\%$ | $\leq 12\%$ | $\leq 15\%$ | | Insoluble matter | $\leq 1.0\%$ | $\leq 1.5\%$ | $\leq 2.0\%$ | ### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Detergent industry:** Modern soap manufacturing plants (Lever, Hindustan Unilever, Godrej) are large-scale continuous saponifiers operating at 80–120 °C, but the chemistry is identical to the lab cold-process. - **Waste cooking-oil recycling:** Used edible oil is converted to soap, providing both waste-management and a value-added product. - **Pharmaceutical & cosmetic industry:** Glycerol, the by-product, is refined and used in cosmetics, explosives (nitroglycerine), and pharmaceuticals. - **Quantitative analysis:** Determining SV of an unknown oil is a classical **chemical engineering analytical tool** for characterizing feedstocks in oleochemical plants. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: Calculation of NaOH Required for Saponification of a Given Oil The **Saponification Value (SV)** is defined as the number of milligrams of KOH required to completely saponify **1 gram** of oil. **Step 1 — Write the saponification of 1 mole of generic triglyceride:** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{C}_3\text{H}_5(\text{OOCR})_3 + 3\,\text{KOH} \longrightarrow 3\,\text{RCOOK} + \text{C}_3\text{H}_5(\text{OH})_3 \end{aligned} $$ **Step 2 — Stoichiometric ratio:** 1 mole of triglyceride requires exactly **3 moles of KOH**. **Step 3 — Mass relationship per 1 g of oil:** If 1 g of oil has SV mg of KOH, then by definition: $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Mass of KOH required for 1 g oil} &= \dfrac{SV}{1000}\ \text{grams of KOH} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 4 — Convert mass of KOH to equivalent mass of NaOH:** Since KOH (M = 56) and NaOH (M = 40) provide the same OH⁻ per mole, the equivalent NaOH mass is: $$ \begin{aligned} W_{NaOH\,(g)} = \dfrac{SV}{1000} \times \dfrac{40}{56}\ \text{(per gram of oil)} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 5 — Scale up to the actual oil mass $W_{oil}$:** $$ \begin{aligned} W_{NaOH\,required} = \dfrac{W_{oil} \times SV \times 40}{56 \times 1000} \end{aligned} $$ This is the **canonical KTU formula** used in every soap-preparation lab calculation. --- ### 3.2 Worked Example — Numerical Practice **Problem:** *50 g of coconut oil (SV = 255 mg KOH/g) is to be saponified. Calculate the mass of NaOH required. Also calculate the theoretical mass of glycerol produced, assuming the average molecular weight of coconut-oil fatty acids is 200 g/mol.* **Solution Step 1 — NaOH mass calculation:** $$ \begin{aligned} W_{NaOH} &= \dfrac{W_{oil} \times SV \times 40}{56 \times 1000} \\ &= \dfrac{50 \times 255 \times 40}{56 \times 1000} \\ &= \dfrac{510\,000}{56\,000} \\ &= 9.107\ \text{g of NaOH} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 2 — Moles of NaOH used:** $$ \begin{aligned} n_{NaOH} &= \dfrac{9.107}{40} = 0.2277\ \text{mol} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3 — From the stoichiometry of the saponification reaction**, 3 moles of NaOH produce 1 mole of glycerol. Therefore: $$ \begin{aligned} n_{glycerol} &= \dfrac{n_{NaOH}}{3} = \dfrac{0.2277}{3} = 0.0759\ \text{mol} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 4 — Mass of glycerol produced** (M_glycerol = 92 g/mol): $$ \begin{aligned} W_{glycerol} &= 0.0759 \times 92 = 6.98\ \text{g} \end{aligned} $$ > [!NOTE] > **Sanity check:** Coconut oil is mostly lauric acid (C12). Theoretical glycerol yield from 50 g of pure trilaurin would be around 8 g. The lower calculated value (6.98 g) is consistent because SV = 255 implies an average fatty-acid chain length that gives this yield. --- ### 3.3 Exhaustive Derivation: Determination of Free Alkali (as % NaOH) by Titration **Principle:** Soap contains excess unreacted NaOH as "free alkali". This is titrated against a **standard HCl solution** using **phenolphthalein indicator**. **Procedure (summary):** 1. Weigh **5 g** of the prepared soap into a 250 mL conical flask. 2. Add 50 mL of neutralized ethanol. Warm slightly to dissolve the soap. 3. Add 2–3 drops of phenolphthalein indicator. 4. Titrate the hot solution against **0.1 N HCl** until the pink color just disappears. 5. Note the titre value $V$ mL. 6. Repeat to get a concordant reading. **Derivation of the % Free Alkali formula:** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{HCl} + \text{NaOH} &\longrightarrow \text{NaCl} + \text{H}_2\text{O} \\ \text{Equivalents of HCl} &= \text{Equivalents of NaOH} \\ (V \times N)_{HCl} &= \left(\dfrac{W_{NaOH\,in\,sample}}{40}\right) \times 1000 \\ W_{NaOH\,in\,sample} &= \dfrac{V \times N \times 40}{1000}\ \text{grams} \end{aligned} $$ $$ \begin{aligned} \%\,\text{Free Alkali (as NaOH)} &= \dfrac{W_{NaOH\,in\,sample}}{W_{sample}} \times 100 \\ &= \dfrac{V \times N \times 40 \times 100}{W_{sample} \times 1000} \end{aligned} $$ This matches the **formula 5** in the cheat sheet above. --- ### 3.4 Exhaustive Derivation: Determination of Total Fatty Matter (TFM) **Principle:** Soap is decomposed by a known excess of a mineral acid (HCl or H₂SO₄), liberating free fatty acids which are then extracted into a known volume of a solvent (petroleum ether or diethyl ether), and weighed. **Procedure:** 1. Weigh **5 g** of soap in a separating funnel. 2. Dissolve in 50 mL hot distilled water. 3. Add 25 mL of dilute H₂SO₄ (1:1) to liberate the free fatty acids. 4. Cool. Extract the fatty acids with 50 mL petroleum ether (3 × 25 mL portions). 5. Combine the ether extracts in a pre-weighed evaporating dish. 6. Evaporate the ether on a water bath (no flame — ether is flammable). 7. Dry in an oven at 100 °C for 30 min, cool in a desiccator, weigh. 8. Repeat drying/weighing until constant weight $W_{fatty\,acid}$ is obtained. **Calculation:** $$ \begin{aligned} \%\,TFM = \dfrac{W_{fatty\,acid}}{W_{sample}} \times 100 \end{aligned} $$ **Correction for Na-soap (optional but examiner-friendly):** TFM is sometimes reported as the sodium salt equivalent. The correction factor is $304/306$ (since 304 g of Na salt = 306 g of fatty acid, ratio of molecular weights): $$ \begin{aligned} \%\,TFM_{(Na\,salt\,basis)} = \%\,TFM \times \dfrac{304}{306} \end{aligned} $$ --- ### 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Quick Comparison of Soap Tests | Test | Reagent / Apparatus | Indicator | Endpoint | Expected Range (BIS Grade 1) | |---|---|---|---|---| | Free Alkali | 0.1 N HCl | Phenolphthalein | Pink → Colorless | $\leq 0.05\%$ | | TFM | Petroleum ether + H₂SO₄ | None (gravimetric) | Constant weight | $\geq 76\%$ | | Moisture | Hot air oven (100 °C) | None (gravimetric) | Constant weight | $\leq 10\%$ | | Insoluble Matter | Distilled water + filtration | None (gravimetric) | Constant weight | $\leq 1.0\%$ | | pH (10% solution) | pH meter | — | Direct reading | $9.0 - 11.0$ | | Chloride (in salt-soap) | AgNO₃ | K₂CrO₄ | Yellow → Brick red | $\leq 1.0\%$ | ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Soap Preparation Process ```mermaid flowchart TD A0[Raw Oil e.g. Coconut Oil] --> A1[Weigh 50 g in Beaker] A1 --> A2[Weigh Calculated NaOH Pellets] A2 --> A3[Dissolve NaOH in 25 mL Distilled Water to Prepare Lye] A3 --> A4[Cool Lye to 40 degree C] A4 --> A5[Pour Lye Slowly into Oil While Stirring] A5 --> A6[Continuous Stirring for 30 to 45 minutes] A6 --> A7{Quality Check Trace Appears?} A7 -- No --> A6 A7 -- Yes --> A8[Pour Trace into Soap Mould] A8 --> A9[Cover and Cure for 24 to 48 hours] A9 --> A10[Unmould the Soap Cake] A10 --> A11[Air Dry for 7 to 14 days] A11 --> A12[Store in Airtight Container] A12 --> A13[Proceed to Analysis Free Alkali TFM Moisture] ``` ### 4.2 Saponification Reaction — Molecular-Level Schematic ```mermaid flowchart LR B1[Triglyceride
Oil Molecule] --> B2{Nucleophilic Attack by OH Minus on Carbonyl Carbon} B2 --> B3[Tetrahedral Intermediate] B3 --> B4[Collapse of Intermediate] B4 --> B5[Fatty Acid Carboxylate Anion RCOO Minus] B4 --> B6[Glyceryl Alkoxide] B6 --> B7[Proton Transfer] B7 --> B8[Glycerol C3H8O3] B5 --> B9[Pairing with Na Plus] B9 --> B10[Sodium Soap RCOONa] B10 --> B11[Repeats 3 Times per Triglyceride Molecule] B11 --> B12[3 Soap Molecules Plus 1 Glycerol] ``` ### 4.3 Soap Quality Analysis — Decision Flow ```mermaid flowchart TD C0[Prepared Soap Cake] --> C1[Sample 5 g in Conical Flask] C1 --> C2[Add Neutralized Ethanol 50 mL] C2 --> C3[Heat Gently on Water Bath] C3 --> C4[Add 2 to 3 drops Phenolphthalein] C4 --> C5[Titrate against 0.1 N HCl] C5 --> C6{Pink Color Persists?} C6 -- Yes --> C5 C6 -- No --> C7[Note Titre Value V mL] C7 --> C8[Calculate Percent Free Alkali] C8 --> C9{Quality Match BIS Grade 1?} C9 -- Yes --> C10[Acceptable Soap] C9 -- No --> C11[Reject or Reformulate] ``` ### 4.4 Process Flow: TFM Determination ```mermaid flowchart TD D0[Soap Sample 5 g] --> D1[Dissolve in 50 mL Hot Distilled Water] D1 --> D2[Transfer to Separating Funnel] D2 --> D3[Add 25 mL Dilute H2SO4 1 is to 1] D3 --> D4[Cool to Room Temperature] D4 --> D5[Extract with 50 mL Petroleum Ether 3 x 25 mL] D5 --> D6[Collect Ether Layer in Pre weighed Dish] D6 --> D7[Evaporate Ether on Water Bath] D7 --> D8[Dry in Oven at 100 degree C for 30 minutes] D8 --> D9[Cool in Desiccator and Weigh] D9 --> D10[Repeat Until Constant Weight] D10 --> D11[Calculate Percent TFM] ``` > [!TIP] > **Why petroleum ether and not diethyl ether?** Petroleum ether (40–60 °C fraction) has a narrow, low boiling range, is less water-miscible, and evaporates cleanly without leaving residues — making it the standard solvent for gravimetric TFM determination in the KTU lab manual. ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) **Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Question Bank, PCCHL307]** *Define saponification. Write the balanced chemical equation for the saponification of tristearin with sodium hydroxide.* **Model Answer (3 marks):** Saponification is the alkaline hydrolysis of an ester (fat/oil) to yield the corresponding sodium/potassium salt of the fatty acid (soap) and glycerol. $$ \begin{aligned} \text{C}_3\text{H}_5(\text{OOCC}_{17}\text{H}_{35})_3 + 3\,\text{NaOH} \longrightarrow 3\,\text{C}_{17}\text{H}_{35}\text{COONa} + \text{C}_3\text{H}_5(\text{OH})_3 \\ \text{Tristearin} \quad\quad\quad\quad\quad\quad\quad\quad\quad\quad\quad\quad\quad\quad\quad\quad\quad\quad\quad \text{Sodium stearate (Soap)} \quad \text{Glycerol} \end{aligned} $$ **Mark distribution:** 1 mark for the definition, 1 mark for the equation, 1 mark for naming the products correctly. --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Expected Pattern, PCCHL307]** *What is meant by "Total Fatty Matter (TFM)" in soap? State the BIS Grade 1 specification for TFM in toilet soap.* **Model Answer (3 marks):** TFM is the total amount of fatty matter (fatty acids + their sodium salts) present in the soap, expressed as a percentage by weight. It is a direct indicator of the soap's cleaning efficiency and lathering quality. **BIS Grade 1 specification (IS 2888):** TFM $\geq 76\%$ w/w. **Mark distribution:** 2 marks for definition and explanation of significance, 1 mark for the numerical specification with the standard reference. --- ### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:** > 1. In NaOH-mass calculation questions, **forgetting to multiply by 40/56** (the KOH-to-NaOH conversion factor) costs 2 marks. > 2. In titration-based free-alkali problems, students often use the normality of KOH instead of HCl — this is a **3-mark deduction**. > 3. In TFM problems, students frequently write the formula as $(W_{sample}/W_{fatty}) \times 100$ — the ratio is **inverted**. Remember: residue in the dish is the fatty acid, so it goes in the numerator. > 4. Always state the BIS reference (IS 2888) when comparing quality — examiners reward it as a "beyond-syllabus" mention with bonus awareness. --- #### **Question A (14 Marks) — Full-Length Model Question** **[KTU University Exam — Model Paper, PCCHL307 Module 2]** **(a)** Describe the laboratory preparation of soap from coconut oil by the **cold process**. Include the reaction involved and the calculation for the amount of NaOH required for saponifying 60 g of coconut oil (Saponification Value = 255). (7 marks) **(b)** Explain the procedure for the determination of **Free Alkali** and **Total Fatty Matter (TFM)** in the prepared soap. Mention the BIS Grade 1 specifications for these parameters. (7 marks) **Model Solution:** **(a) Procedure (4 marks):** 1. Weigh 60 g of coconut oil in a 250 mL beaker. 2. Calculate NaOH required: $$ \begin{aligned} W_{NaOH} &= \dfrac{60 \times 255 \times 40}{56 \times 1000} = 10.93\ \text{g of NaOH} \end{aligned} $$ 3. Dissolve 10.93 g of NaOH in 30 mL of distilled water to make the lye. Cool to 40 °C. 4. Slowly pour the lye into the oil with **continuous stirring** using a glass rod. 5. Stir for 30–45 minutes until "trace" is achieved (mixture thickens to a pudding-like consistency). 6. Pour the trace into a soap mould, cover with cling film. 7. Allow to cure for 24–48 hours, then unmould and air-dry for 7–14 days. **Reaction (2 marks):** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{C}_3\text{H}_5(\text{OOCR})_3 + 3\,\text{NaOH} \longrightarrow 3\,\text{RCOONa} + \text{C}_3\text{H}_5(\text{OH})_3 \end{aligned} $$ **Calculation (1 mark):** As shown above; the answer is **10.93 g NaOH**. **(b) Free Alkali Determination (3.5 marks):** - Weigh 5 g soap, dissolve in 50 mL neutralized ethanol, warm gently. - Add 2–3 drops phenolphthalein, titrate against 0.1 N HCl until pink color disappears. - % Free Alkali = $\dfrac{V \times N \times 40 \times 100}{W_{sample} \times 1000}$. - **BIS Grade 1 specification:** Free Alkali (as NaOH) $\leq 0.05\%$ w/w. **TFM Determination (3.5 marks):** - Weigh 5 g soap, dissolve in 50 mL hot distilled water, transfer to a separating funnel. - Add 25 mL dilute H₂SO₄ (1:1) to liberate free fatty acids. - Extract with petroleum ether (3 × 25 mL), collect ether layer in a pre-weighed dish. - Evaporate ether, dry in oven at 100 °C to constant weight. - % TFM = $\dfrac{W_{fatty\,acid}}{W_{sample}} \times 100$. - **BIS Grade 1 specification:** TFM $\geq 76\%$ w/w. --- #### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice** **[KTU University Exam — Model Paper, PCCHL307 Module 2, Alternative Set]** **(a)** What is saponification value? Outline the experimental procedure for determining the saponification value of a given oil sample. (7 marks) **(b)** A soap sample weighing **5.0 g** was titrated against **0.1 N HCl**. The titre value was **1.2 mL**. Calculate the % free alkali (as NaOH) and comment on whether the soap conforms to **BIS Grade 1** specification. (7 marks) **Model Solution:** **(a) Saponification Value — Definition + Procedure (7 marks):** **Definition (2 marks):** Saponification Value (SV) is the number of milligrams of KOH required to completely saponify 1 gram of the oil/fat. It is inversely proportional to the average molecular weight of the fatty acids in the oil. **Procedure (5 marks):** 1. Weigh accurately **1–2 g** of the oil in a 250 mL conical flask. 2. Add **25 mL of 0.5 N alcoholic KOH** using a pipette. 3. Attach a reflux condenser and boil gently on a water bath for **30 minutes** (saponification step). 4. Cool, add 2–3 drops of **phenolphthalein** indicator. 5. Titrate the excess (unreacted) KOH against **0.5 N HCl** until the pink color disappears. Let this titre be $V_s$ mL. 6. Run a **blank titration** (without oil, only 25 mL alcoholic KOH) and note $V_b$ mL. **Calculation:** $$ \begin{aligned} SV = \dfrac{(V_b - V_s) \times N_{HCl} \times 56}{W_{oil}}\ \text{mg KOH/g} \end{aligned} $$ **(b) Free Alkali Calculation (7 marks):** **Step 1 — Substitute into the formula:** $$ \begin{aligned} \%\,\text{Free Alkali} &= \dfrac{V \times N \times 40 \times 100}{W_{sample} \times 1000} \\ &= \dfrac{1.2 \times 0.1 \times 40 \times 100}{5.0 \times 1000} \\ &= \dfrac{480}{5000} \\ &= 0.096\%\ \text{w/w} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 2 — Comment on BIS Grade 1 conformance:** > [!WARNING] > **BIS Grade 1 requires Free Alkali (as NaOH) $\leq 0.05\%$ w/w.** The calculated value of **0.096%** is almost **twice** the allowed limit. Therefore, this soap **does NOT conform** to BIS Grade 1 specifications. It might, however, conform to **Grade 2** (limit 0.10%) since $0.096\% \leq 0.10\%$. **Mark distribution:** 4 marks for the numerical calculation, 2 marks for the BIS reference, 1 mark for the final conformance statement with reasoning. --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) > [!TIP] > Use this section as a last-day revision sheet before the KTU lab viva or end-semester exam. - **Core reaction:** Triglyceride + 3 NaOH → 3 RCOONa (soap) + Glycerol. - **Saponification Value (SV):** mg KOH required per gram of oil. Higher SV = shorter fatty-acid chains. - **NaOH required:** $W_{NaOH} = \dfrac{W_{oil} \times SV \times 40}{56 \times 1000}$ — remember the **40/56** factor, do not drop it. - **Cold process temperature window:** 35–50 °C; **never pour water into NaOH** — always pour NaOH into water (exothermic heat of dissolution can cause splattering). - **Trace condition:** Mixture thickens to a custard-like consistency — the visual endpoint of the lab reaction. - **Curing time:** Minimum 24–48 hours in mould + 1–2 weeks of air drying before analysis. - **Free Alkali test:** Acid-base titration with **0.1 N HCl** and **phenolphthalein**; pink → colorless endpoint. - **TFM test:** Acidify with H₂SO₄ → extract fatty acids with **petroleum ether** → gravimetric. - **Moisture test:** Oven drying at 100 °C to constant weight. - **Insoluble matter test:** Dissolve in water, filter, dry, and weigh the residue. - **BIS Grade 1 limits (IS 2888):** TFM $\geq 76\%$, Free Alkali $\leq 0.05\%$, Moisture $\leq 10\%$, Insoluble $\leq 1.0\%$. - **Soap vs. Detergent:** Soaps form **insoluble scum** with hard water (Ca²⁺/Mg²⁺ salts); detergents (synthetic surfactants) do not. This is the fundamental reason detergents have largely replaced soaps in hard-water regions — a classic viva question. - **By-product economics:** **Glycerol** (BP 290 °C) is refined and sold to pharmaceutical, cosmetic, and explosives industries — a 5–10% revenue stream in commercial soap plants. - **Common KTU viva questions:** - "Why is NaOH added to water and not vice versa?" → Exothermic dissolution, splatter hazard. - "What is the difference between hard soap and soft soap?" → Hard soap is Na-salt (bar), soft soap is K-salt (liquid). - "What is lye?" → Aqueous NaOH solution. - "Why does soap not work in acidic water?" → H⁺ protonates RCOO⁻ to form insoluble RCOOH (free fatty acid). - "What is saponification value of coconut oil compared to groundnut oil?" → Coconut is higher (250–265 vs 188–196) because of shorter lauric acid chains. - **Examiner pet-peeves to avoid:** 1. Spelling "saponification" as "sapon**if**ication" or "sapon**a**fication" — costs an easy 0.5 mark in viva. 2. Reporting the TFM value as 0.76 g instead of 76% — wrong unit. 3. Using **diethyl ether** instead of petroleum ether in TFM — both work, but petroleum ether is the KTU-recommended solvent. 4. Forgetting to run a **blank titration** in the SV determination — without it, the calculation is meaningless. - **One-line definition for viva:** *"Soap is the sodium or potassium salt of a long-chain fatty acid, produced by the saponification of fats and oils with an alkali."*

topics.title: Determination of Available Chlorine in Bleaching Powder
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCHL307 - CHEMICAL TECHNOLOGY AND ENVIRONMENTAL ENGINEERING LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Determination of Available Chlorine in Bleaching Powder

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Bleaching Powder** is a pale yellowish powder obtained by the action of **chlorine gas on dry slaked lime** (calcium hydroxide). Chemically, it is a mixed salt represented as **Ca(OCl)Cl** or more commonly expressed as a mixture of **calcium hypochlorite, Ca(OCl)₂**, and **calcium chloride, CaCl₂**, in the molecular proportion 1:1.

**Available Chlorine** is defined as the **quantitative measure of chlorine liberated (as Cl₂) when a given amount of bleaching powder is treated with a dilute mineral acid (usually HCl or H₂SO₄)**, expressed as a percentage by mass of the sample.

$$\text{Available Chlorine (\%)} = \frac{\text{Mass of Cl}_2 \text{ liberated}}{\text{Mass of bleaching powder taken}} \times 100$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Available chlorine is **not** the total chlorine content present in the molecule — it represents only the **oxidizing (active) chlorine** that can perform bleaching, disinfection, and sterilization functions. Fresh commercial bleaching powder must contain a **minimum of 35% (w/w) available chlorine** as per the ISI (Bureau of Indian Standards) specification.

> [!NOTE]
> **Equivalent Weight of Available Chlorine:** The equivalent weight of Cl₂ is taken as **35.5 g/equivalent** (i.e., molecular weight 71 divided by the valence factor 2), because each Cl atom undergoes a **1-electron change** during the redox conversion: Cl⁰ → Cl⁻¹.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Chlorine Bank Account"

Think of bleaching powder as a **"savings bank account of chlorine"**:

- The **total chlorine deposited** in the molecule (both in Ca(OCl)₂ and CaCl₂ forms) is like the **total fixed deposit** in a bank.
- The **available chlorine** is analogous to the **"withdrawable balance"** — the portion that can be **released and spent** on useful work (bleaching, disinfection) when the customer (acid) comes asking.
- The CaCl₂ portion is like a **locked, non-withdrawable FD** — its chlorine has already been "reduced" to Cl⁻ state and is chemically inactive as an oxidizer.
- Therefore, *available chlorine* = the **active, spendable chlorine reserve**.

### 1.3 Why is this Experiment Critical?

| Application Sector | Role of Available Chlorine |
|---|---|
| Water Treatment Plants | Disinfection (killing pathogens) |
| Textile Industry | Bleaching cotton, linen fabric |
| Paper & Pulp Industry | Pulp whitening |
| Household Cleaning | Stain removal, sanitization |
| Sanitation (COVID-era) | Surface disinfection |

> [!NOTE]
> **Stock Aging Effect:** Bleaching powder **slowly loses its available chlorine** on exposure to atmospheric CO₂, moisture, and sunlight, converting into CaCO₃ and CaCl₂. Hence, periodic estimation in the lab is vital for quality control.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Composition of Bleaching Powder

Commercial bleaching powder is best represented by the formula:

$$\text{Ca(OCl)Cl} \quad \text{or} \quad \text{CaCl}_2 \cdot \text{Ca(OCl)}_2$$

It can be visualized as a **double salt** consisting of equimolar proportions of:
- **Ca(OCl)₂** — Calcium hypochlorite (active, source of available chlorine)
- **CaCl₂** — Calcium chloride (inactive, no bleaching power)

### 2.2 Operational Principle — Iodometric (Indirect) Titration

The estimation of available chlorine is performed by an **iodometric back-titration method**, since Cl₂ is a volatile, corrosive gas that cannot be directly titrated. The redox logic follows a **4-step cascade**:

**Step 1 — Liberation of Cl₂ by acid action:**

$$\text{Ca(OCl)}_2 + 4\text{HCl} \longrightarrow \text{CaCl}_2 + 2\text{H}_2\text{O} + 2\text{Cl}_2 \uparrow$$

(In practice, dilute H₂SO₄ is used; excess acid is required.)

**Step 2 — Reaction with potassium iodide (KI):** The liberated Cl₂ acts as an oxidizing agent and liberates an equivalent amount of iodine (I₂) from KI.

$$\text{Cl}_2 + 2\text{KI} \longrightarrow 2\text{KCl} + \text{I}_2$$

**Step 3 — Back-titration of I₂ with sodium thiosulphate (hypo):** The liberated I₂ is titrated against a **standard 0.1 N Na₂S₂O₃** solution until a pale-straw yellow colour persists.

$$\text{I}_2 + 2\text{Na}_2\text{S}_2\text{O}_3 \longrightarrow \text{Na}_2\text{S}_4\text{O}_6 + 2\text{NaI}$$

**Step 4 — Starch Indicator Endpoint:** Freshly prepared **starch solution (1%)** is added near the endpoint, forming a **deep blue starch-iodine complex**. The endpoint is the **disappearance of the blue colour** (sharp, colorless solution).

$$\text{I}_2 + \text{Starch} \longrightarrow \text{Blue complex (I}_5^- \text{-amylose)}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why add starch only near the endpoint?** Starch, if added to a strongly iodinated (high-I₂) acidic solution, can **hydrolyze irreversibly** to form products that do not re-give the blue colour, leading to **overshooting of the endpoint**. This is a classic KTU valuation pitfall.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

| Quantity | Formula | Units | Remarks |
|---|---|---|---|
| Equivalent weight of Cl₂ | $E_{Cl_2} = \dfrac{71}{2} = 35.5$ | g/eq | One Cl atom gains 1 electron |
| 1 mL of 1 N Na₂S₂O₃ ≡ Cl₂ | $V \times N \times 35.5$ | mg Cl₂ | Stoichiometric equivalence |
| **Available Chlorine (%)** | $\dfrac{V \times N \times 35.5 \times 100}{W \times 1000}$ | % (w/w) | Master formula for KTU exam |
| Aliquot weight (W) | $W = \dfrac{\text{weight of sample}}{\text{vol. of flask}} \times \text{vol. pipetted}$ | g | Sample in each titration |
| Normality relation | $N_1 V_1 = N_2 V_2$ | — | Equivalence of hypo & Cl₂ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical LaTeX Note for Tables:** The vertical pipe symbol (used to denote "such that" in math) has been replaced with **\mid** in the table above to preserve markdown table syntax. Always use `\mid` or `\vert` for absolute-value/conditional separators inside tables.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Municipal water works:** Operators titrate chlorine residuals daily to maintain 0.2–0.5 ppm free chlorine.
- **Quality assurance in chlor-alkali plants:** Ensures IS 2625-1964 compliance.
- **Forensic & environmental labs:** Quantify chlorine discharge from industrial effluents.
- **Swimming pool maintenance:** Verifies sanitizer concentration (typically 1–3 ppm free Cl₂).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Available Chlorine Formula

**Starting point:** The 4-reaction cascade established in Section 2.2.

From Step 2:
$$\text{Cl}_2 + 2\text{KI} \longrightarrow 2\text{KCl} + \text{I}_2$$

From Step 3:
$$\text{I}_2 + 2\text{Na}_2\text{S}_2\text{O}_3 \longrightarrow \text{Na}_2\text{S}_4\text{O}_6 + 2\text{NaI}$$

**Equivalence chain — establishing the link:**

Let us track the moles through the cascade:

$$\begin{aligned}
1 \text{ mole Cl}_2 &\equiv 1 \text{ mole I}_2 \quad \text{(from Step 2)} \\
1 \text{ mole I}_2 &\equiv 2 \text{ moles Na}_2\text{S}_2\text{O}_3 \quad \text{(from Step 3)}
\end{aligned}$$

Therefore:
$$1 \text{ mole Cl}_2 \equiv 2 \text{ moles Na}_2\text{S}_2\text{O}_3$$

Now, the number of equivalents of Cl₂ = equivalents of hypo used:

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Equivalents of Cl}_2 &= \text{Equivalents of Na}_2\text{S}_2\text{O}_3 \\
\frac{\text{Mass of Cl}_2}{E_{Cl_2}} &= N \times V \quad \text{(in litres)} \\
\text{Mass of Cl}_2 &= N \times V \times 35.5 \quad \text{(in grams, if V in L)}
\end{aligned}$$

For practical lab use, V is in mL, so we multiply by 1000/1000 to retain mg units:

$$\text{Mass of Cl}_2 \text{ (in mg)} = N \times V_{\text{(mL)}} \times 35.5$$

**Final percentage formula:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Available Chlorine (\%)} &= \frac{\text{Mass of Cl}_2 \text{ in aliquot (g)}}{\text{Mass of sample in aliquot (g)}} \times 100 \\
&= \frac{N \times V \times 35.5 \times 10^{-3}}{W} \times 100 \\
\therefore \quad \text{Available Chlorine (\%)} &= \frac{N \times V \times 35.5 \times 100}{W \times 1000}
\end{aligned}$$

where:
- **N** = Normality of Na₂S₂O₃ (hypo) solution
- **V** = Volume of Na₂S₂O₃ used in the titration (mL)
- **W** = Mass of bleaching powder present in the aliquot (g)
- **35.5** = Equivalent weight of Cl₂ (g/eq)

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-style)

**Problem Statement:**
5.0 g of a bleaching powder sample is dissolved and made up to 250 mL in a volumetric flask. 25 mL of this solution is pipetted out, acidified, and treated with excess KI. The liberated I₂ requires 24.5 mL of **0.1 N Na₂S₂O₃** for complete titration using starch indicator. Calculate the percentage of available chlorine.

**Given Data:**
$$\begin{aligned}
W_{\text{total}} &= 5.0 \text{ g} \\
V_{\text{flask}} &= 250 \text{ mL} \\
V_{\text{aliquot}} &= 25 \text{ mL} \\
V_{\text{hypo}} &= 24.5 \text{ mL} \\
N_{\text{hypo}} &= 0.1 \text{ N}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 1 — Mass of sample in the aliquot (W):**

$$W = \frac{W_{\text{total}}}{V_{\text{flask}}} \times V_{\text{aliquot}} = \frac{5.0}{250} \times 25 = 0.5 \text{ g}$$

**Step 2 — Apply the master formula:**

$$\text{Available Chlorine (\%)} = \frac{N \times V \times 35.5 \times 100}{W \times 1000}$$

Substituting the values:

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Available Chlorine (\%)} &= \frac{0.1 \times 24.5 \times 35.5 \times 100}{0.5 \times 1000} \\
&= \frac{0.1 \times 24.5 \times 35.5 \times 100}{500} \\
&= \frac{86.975 \times 100}{500} \\
&= \frac{8697.5}{500} \\
&= 17.395 \% \\
&\approx 17.40 \% \text{ (w/w)}
\end{aligned}$$

**Result & Inference:** The bleaching powder contains only **~17.4% available chlorine**, which is **far below the IS specification of ≥35%**. This sample is **substandard and unfit for disinfection use** — it has either aged, been improperly stored, or been adulterated.

### 3.3 Analytical Summary Matrix

| Step | Action | Key Constant / Relation | Role |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Sample weighing | 5.0 g (±0.1 g) | Establishes total mass basis |
| 2 | Dilution to 250 mL | Volumetric flask class A | Homogeneous stock solution |
| 3 | Pipette 25 mL aliquot | 1/10 dilution factor | Test portion |
| 4 | Acidify + excess KI | Starch-free zone | Liberate I₂ stoichiometrically |
| 5 | Titrate with Na₂S₂O₃ | 0.1 N standard | Back-titration of I₂ |
| 6 | Add starch near endpoint | Pale straw → blue → colourless | Sharp visual endpoint |
| 7 | Calculate % available Cl₂ | Master formula | Quality assessment |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Experimental Workflow (Block-Level Functional Architecture)

The following Mermaid flowchart captures the **logical sequence of operations** in the iodometric determination of available chlorine. All node IDs are alphanumeric and labels are raw uppercase text (no markdown formatting inside labels) to comply with Mermaid v10+ parsing rules.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[STEP 1 WEIGH 5.0 G BLEACHING POWDER] --> B[STEP 2 MAKE PASTE WITH DISTILLED WATER]
    B --> C[STEP 3 TRANSFER TO 250 ML VOLUMETRIC FLASK]
    C --> D[STEP 4 MAKE UP TO MARK WITH DISTILLED WATER]
    D --> E[STEP 5 SHAKE WELL TO HOMOGENIZE]
    E --> F[STEP 6 PIPETTE OUT 25 ML ALIQUOT]
    F --> G[STEP 7 TRANSFER ALIQUOT TO CONICAL FLASK]
    G --> H[STEP 8 ADD 10 ML DILUTE H2SO4 ACIDIFY]
    H --> I[STEP 9 ADD 10 ML 10 PERCENT KI SOLUTION]
    I --> J[STEP 10 LIBERATED I2 GIVES BROWN COLOUR]
    J --> K[STEP 11 TITRATE WITH 0.1 N Na2S2O3]
    K --> L[STEP 12 TITRANT COLOUR FADES TO PALE YELLOW]
    L --> M[STEP 13 ADD 1 ML STARCH INDICATOR]
    M --> N[STEP 14 DEEP BLUE COMPLEX FORMS]
    N --> O[STEP 15 CONTINUE TITRATION DROPWISE]
    O --> P[STEP 16 ENDPOINT BLUE COLOUR DISAPPEARS]
    P --> Q[STEP 17 NOTE BURETTE READING V ML]
    Q --> R[STEP 18 REPEAT TO GET CONCORDANT VALUE]
    R --> S[STEP 19 APPLY MASTER FORMULA]
    S --> T[STEP 20 REPORT AVAILABLE CHLORINE PERCENT]
```

### 4.2 Redox Cascade — Reaction Pathway Map

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1[BLEACHING POWDER CaOCl2] -- DILUTE H2SO4 --> R1[CL2 GAS LIBERATED]
    R1 -- EXCESS KI --> R2[I2 LIBERATED IN SOLUTION]
    R2 -- Na2S2O3 TITRATION --> R3[NaI and Na2S4O6 FORMED]
    R3 -- STARCH INDICATOR --> R4[BLUE TO COLOURLESS ENDPOINT]
    S1 -.->|INACTIVE PART| S2[CaCl2 RESIDUE NO CHLORINE LIBERATED]
```

### 4.3 Apparatus Layout (Functional Block Schematic)

```mermaid

    end
    subgraph REACTION_ZONE[REACTION AND TITRATION UNIT]

        B1 --> B2[ACID H2SO4 DILUTE]

        B3 --> B4[BURETTE 0.1 N Na2S2O3]
    end
    subgraph DETECTION[ENDPOINT DETECTION]
        B4 --> C1[STARCH INDICATOR 1 PERCENT]

```
