> **Mermaid Compilation Note:** All node identifiers (e.g., `A1`, `B3`, `SAMPLE_PREP`) are purely alphanumeric. No reserved keywords (`end`, `graph`, `subgraph`, `style`) are used as standalone node IDs. Labels are kept as raw uppercase text without any markdown bold/italic formatting to ensure clean rendering across Mermaid versions.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Q1.** `[KTU University Exam — June 2023]`
**Define the term "Available Chlorine" in bleaching powder. Why is it important to estimate it?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Available chlorine is defined as the **percentage by mass of chlorine gas (Cl₂) liberated** when a given quantity of bleaching powder is treated with a dilute mineral acid such as HCl or H₂SO₄. **(1 mark)**

It serves as the **key quality parameter** because it directly determines the **bleaching, disinfecting, and sterilizing power** of the powder. Commercial-grade bleaching powder must contain a **minimum of 35% available chlorine** as per IS specifications. **(1 mark)**

In storage, available chlorine gradually decreases due to reaction with atmospheric CO₂, moisture, and heat, so regular estimation ensures the material is fit for use in water treatment, textile bleaching, and sanitation. **(1 mark)**

---

#### **Q2.** `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]`
**Why is starch indicator added only near the endpoint in the iodometric titration of available chlorine, and not at the beginning?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Starch forms a **deep blue complex with free I₂ (tri-iodide)** in solution. **(1 mark)**

If starch is added at the beginning when the I₂ concentration is very high, the starch-iodine complex can undergo **irreversible hydrolysis and coagulation**, particularly in the acidic medium, forming products that **do not re-give the blue colour** even if free I₂ is still present. **(1 mark)**

This leads to an **erroneous, overshot endpoint** (excess Na₂S₂O₃ consumed) and falsely low available chlorine values. Adding starch only when the I₂ colour fades to **pale yellow** ensures the indicator works in a low-I₂ environment, giving a **sharp, reversible, and accurate endpoint**. **(1 mark)**

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

> **KTU 2024 Module Rule:** Every Part B question carries **internal choice** — students must answer either the full Question A or the full Question B, including both sub-parts.

---

#### **Question A (14 marks)** `[KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model Paper]`

**(a)** Explain the principle and chemistry involved in the estimation of available chlorine in bleaching powder by the iodometric method. Derive the formula used for the calculation. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** In a laboratory experiment, 5.0 g of bleaching powder was dissolved and made up to 250 mL. 25 mL of this solution, on treatment with KI and acid, liberated I₂ which required 22.0 mL of 0.1 N Na₂S₂O₃. Calculate the percentage of available chlorine and comment on the quality of the sample. **(7 marks)**

##### Model Solution

**(a) Principle and Chemistry (7 marks):**

The determination is based on the **iodometric (indirect titration) method**. The Cl₂ liberated from bleaching powder (on acid treatment) is allowed to react with excess **potassium iodide (KI)** to release an equivalent amount of **iodine (I₂)**, which is then titrated against a **standard sodium thiosulphate (Na₂S₂O₃)** solution using **starch as an indicator**. **(2 marks)**

**Step-wise reactions:** **(3 marks)**

$$\text{Ca(OCl)}_2 + \text{H}_2\text{SO}_4 \longrightarrow \text{CaSO}_4 + \text{H}_2\text{O} + \text{Cl}_2$$

$$\text{Cl}_2 + 2\text{KI} \longrightarrow 2\text{KCl} + \text{I}_2$$

$$\text{I}_2 + 2\text{Na}_2\text{S}_2\text{O}_3 \longrightarrow \text{Na}_2\text{S}_4\text{O}_6 + 2\text{NaI}$$

**Derivation of the master formula:** **(2 marks)**

Since 1 mole Cl₂ ≡ 1 mole I₂ ≡ 2 moles Na₂S₂O₃, by the law of equivalence:

$$N_1 V_1 = N_2 V_2 \implies \frac{\text{Mass of Cl}_2}{35.5} = N \times V \times 10^{-3}$$

$$\therefore \text{Available Chlorine (\%)} = \frac{N \times V \times 35.5 \times 100}{W \times 1000}$$

where W is the mass of sample in the aliquot (g).

---

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**Given:**
$$W_{\text{total}} = 5.0 \text{ g}, \quad V_{\text{flask}} = 250 \text{ mL}, \quad V_{\text{aliquot}} = 25 \text{ mL},$$
$$V_{\text{hypo}} = 22.0 \text{ mL}, \quad N_{\text{hypo}} = 0.1 \text{ N}$$

**Step 1:** Mass of sample in the 25 mL aliquot: **(2 marks)**

$$W = \frac{5.0}{250} \times 25 = 0.5 \text{ g}$$

**Step 2:** Apply the master formula: **(3 marks)**

$$\text{Available Chlorine (\%)} = \frac{0.1 \times 22.0 \times 35.5 \times 100}{0.5 \times 1000}$$

$$= \frac{78.1 \times 100}{500} = \frac{7810}{500} = 15.62\%$$

**Step 3:** Quality comment: **(2 marks)**

The sample contains only **15.62% available chlorine**, which is **far below the IS specification of ≥35%**. The bleaching powder is **substandard**, indicating significant degradation during storage (likely due to exposure to moisture/CO₂). It is **not recommended for use in drinking water disinfection or commercial bleaching applications** without replenishment or replacement.

---

#### **Question B (14 marks — ALTERNATIVE)** `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model Paper]`

**(a)** With the help of a neat labelled schematic, describe the laboratory procedure for the determination of available chlorine in bleaching powder. List the chemicals and apparatus required. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** 4.0 g of bleaching powder was made up to 500 mL. 50 mL of this solution required 18.0 mL of 0.05 N Na₂S₂O₃. Calculate the available chlorine percentage. If the same sample had been titrated using 0.1 N hypo, what volume would have been required? **(7 marks)**

##### Model Solution

**(a) Procedure and Apparatus (7 marks):**

**Apparatus required:** 250 mL volumetric flask, 25 mL pipette, 50 mL burette, conical flasks, glass rod, analytical balance, wash bottle, white tile. **(1 mark)**

**Chemicals required:** Bleaching powder (sample), dilute H₂SO₄ (1:1), 10% w/v KI solution, 0.1 N standard Na₂S₂O₃ solution, freshly prepared 1% starch indicator. **(1 mark)**

**Procedure:** **(5 marks)**

1. Weigh accurately about **5.0 g** of the bleaching powder sample and transfer it to a **250 mL volumetric flask**.
2. Make a smooth paste with a small quantity of distilled water, then dilute and make up to the mark. Shake thoroughly to ensure homogeneity.
3. Pipette out **25 mL** of the well-mixed solution into a clean conical flask.
4. Add **10 mL of dilute H₂SO₄** to acidify, followed by **10 mL of 10% KI solution** (in excess).
5. The liberated I₂ imparts a **brown colour** to the solution.
6. Titrate the liberated I₂ against **0.1 N Na₂S₂O₃** from a burette with constant swirling.
7. As the brown colour fades to **pale yellow** (near the endpoint), add **1 mL of 1% starch indicator** — a **deep blue colour** appears.
8. Continue titration **dropwise** until the blue colour just **disappears**, giving a sharp **colourless endpoint**.
9. Note the burette reading. Repeat to obtain **at least two concordant readings** (±0.1 mL).

---

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**Given:**
$$W_{\text{total}} = 4.0 \text{ g}, \quad V_{\text{flask}} = 500 \text{ mL}, \quad V_{\text{aliquot}} = 50 \text{ mL},$$
$$V_1 = 18.0 \text{ mL}, \quad N_1 = 0.05 \text{ N}, \quad N_2 = 0.1 \text{ N (second case)}$$

**Step 1:** Mass of sample in the 50 mL aliquot: **(2 marks)**

$$W = \frac{4.0}{500} \times 50 = 0.4 \text{ g}$$

**Step 2:** Available chlorine calculation: **(3 marks)**

$$\text{Available Chlorine (\%)} = \frac{0.05 \times 18.0 \times 35.5 \times 100}{0.4 \times 1000}$$

$$= \frac{31.95 \times 100}{400} = \frac{3195}{400} = 7.9875\% \approx 7.99\%$$

**Step 3:** Volume required with 0.1 N hypo: **(2 marks)**

Using the equivalence equation $N_1 V_1 = N_2 V_2$:

$$0.05 \times 18.0 = 0.1 \times V_2$$

$$V_2 = \frac{0.05 \times 18.0}{0.1} = 9.0 \text{ mL}$$

**Inference:** The very low available chlorine (~8%) indicates a **highly aged or adulterated sample**.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls in KTU Valuation:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the aliquot factor:** Many students incorrectly use the total sample weight (e.g., 5.0 g) as W in the master formula. Always calculate the **mass in the aliquot** first: $W = W_{\text{total}} \times (V_{\text{aliquot}} / V_{\text{flask}})$. **[Lose 1 mark]**
>
> 2. **Confusing 35.5 with 71:** The equivalent weight of Cl₂ is **35.5 g/eq** (not 71). Use 35.5; using 71 will halve the answer. **[Lose 1 mark]**
>
> 3. **Unit mismatch in 1000 factor:** The factor 1000 in the denominator converts mL → L. Forgetting it gives a 1000× inflated or 1000× suppressed answer. **[Lose 1 mark]**
>
> 4. **Starch added at the start:** Mentioning that starch indicator is added at the beginning is a **red-flag error**. Examiners specifically look for the correct procedural statement. **[Lose 1 mark]**
>
> 5. **No concordant reading mention:** Always state "repeat the titration to obtain at least two concordant readings (±0.1 mL)." Skipping this loses a procedural mark. **[Lose 0.5–1 mark]**
>
> 6. **No quality comment:** For numerical questions, the final step **must include a comment on sample quality** relative to IS ≥35% specification. Skipping this is a 1-mark loss.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Bleaching powder formula:** Ca(OCl)Cl or CaCl₂·Ca(OCl)₂ — a mixed salt of calcium hypochlorite and calcium chloride.
- **Available chlorine** = the oxidizing chlorine liberated as Cl₂ on acid treatment, expressed as % w/w.
- **IS specification** for fresh bleaching powder: **≥ 35%** available chlorine.
- **Method used:** Iodometric (indirect) titration — back-titration of liberated I₂ with standard **Na₂S₂O₃ (0.1 N)**.
- **Indicator:** Freshly prepared **1% starch solution** added **only near the endpoint**.
- **Endpoint:** Disappearance of the deep blue starch-iodine complex → **colourless solution**.
- **Master formula:**
  $$\% \text{ Available Cl}_2 = \frac{N \times V \times 35.5 \times 100}{W \times 1000}$$
- **Equivalents link:** 1 mole Cl₂ ≡ 1 mole I₂ ≡ 2 moles Na₂S₂O₃.
- **Equivalent weight of Cl₂ = 35.5 g/eq.**
- **Common errors to avoid:** wrong aliquot weight, using 71 instead of 35.5, missing starch-near-endpoint logic, no concordant reading statement, no quality comment.
- **Sample degradation rule of thumb:** ~1% available chlorine is lost per month under improper storage; airtight, dark, cool storage is essential.
- **Starch must be freshly prepared** — old starch solutions hydrolyze and give sluggish/wrong endpoints.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Determination of Flash and Fire Point and Viscosity of Lubricating Oil
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCHL307 - CHEMICAL TECHNOLOGY AND ENVIRONMENTAL ENGINEERING LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Determination of Flash and Fire Point and Viscosity of Lubricating Oil

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Flash Point — The "Whisper of Ignition"

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Flash Point (Open Cup Method — Cleveland):** The lowest temperature at which the application of a standard test flame causes the **vapours of the lubricant** to ignite (flash) momentarily, when heated under specified conditions, but the vapours do **NOT** sustain combustion.

**In Simple Words (Analogy):** Imagine heating milk on a gas stove. Just before it actually starts to boil, you can smell the vapour rising. If you hold a burning matchstick near the surface, you might see a tiny blue flame flicker and go out instantly — that is the **flash point**. The milk (lubricant) is *almost* ready to catch fire but isn't actually burning yet.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction:** Flash point is **NOT** the temperature at which the oil catches fire. It is only the temperature at which enough vapour has been generated to momentarily ignite.

---

### 1.2 Fire Point — The "Sustained Burn"

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Fire Point:** The lowest temperature at which the lubricant vapour, once ignited by a flame, **continues to burn for at least 5 seconds** under standard test conditions.

**In Simple Words (Analogy):** Continuing the milk example — if you keep heating even after the flash moment, the vapour concentration becomes so high that when the matchstick ignites it, the flame **stays on the surface for several seconds** rather than just flickering. That is the **fire point**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Practical Rule:** For any given lubricating oil, **Fire Point > Flash Point**, typically by **10 °C to 30 °C**.

---

### 1.3 Viscosity — The "Internal Stickiness"

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Dynamic Viscosity (Absolute Viscosity), μ:** The internal resistance of a fluid to flow, defined as the tangential force per unit area required to maintain a unit velocity gradient between two parallel planes.

$$\mu = \frac{\tau}{\frac{dv}{dy}}$$

where **τ** is the shear stress (N/m²) and **dv/dy** is the velocity gradient (s⁻¹). The SI unit is **Pa·s**, and the CGS unit is **poise (P)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Kinematic Viscosity, ν:** The ratio of dynamic viscosity to the density of the fluid at the same temperature.

$$\nu = \frac{\mu}{\rho}$$

The SI unit is **m²/s**, and the CGS unit is **stokes (St)** or **centistokes (cSt)**.

**In Simple Words (Analogy):** Pouring honey out of a bottle feels "thick" and slow — that thickness is its **high viscosity**. Pouring water feels easy and fast — that is **low viscosity**. Lubricating oil sits in between; we need to know *exactly* how thick it is so it can coat machine parts properly.

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Lab Insight:** Viscosity **decreases** as temperature **increases** for lubricating oils. That is why we always record the temperature at which viscosity is measured (commonly 40 °C and 100 °C).

---

### 1.4 The Three Properties at a Glance

| Property | What it Tells Us | Practical Significance |
|---|---|---|
| **Flash Point** | Fire safety / Volatility | Storage, transport, fire hazard classification |
| **Fire Point** | Sustained combustion tendency | Fire-fighting strategy |
| **Viscosity** | Flow behaviour | Lubrication film strength, oil selection |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Theory of Flash & Fire Point

**Why does the flash point exist at all?**
Every liquid has a *vapour pressure* that depends on temperature. As oil is heated, more and more molecules escape from the surface into the surrounding air, forming a **flammable vapour-air mixture**. The flash point is reached when this mixture's composition crosses the **lower flammability limit (LFL)** — the minimum fuel concentration in air that can propagate a flame.

**Why is Fire Point > Flash Point?**
At the fire point, the vapour generation rate is high enough that even *after* the test flame is removed, fresh vapour continues to feed the flame for at least 5 seconds. This requires a higher temperature than the flash point.

---

### 2.2 Two Standard Methods of Flash/Fire Point Determination

| Method | Apparatus | Cup Type | When Used |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Open Cup** | Cleveland Open Cup (COC) | Open to atmosphere | Industrial oils, bitumen |
| **Closed Cup** | Pensky-Martens (PMCC) / Abel | Sealed with lid + stirrer | Petroleum products, kerosene |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Open Cup Flash Point is always 5–15 °C HIGHER than Closed Cup Flash Point**, because in the closed cup, vapour accumulates faster.

---

### 2.3 Theory of Viscosity Measurement (Redwood Viscometer)

The **Redwood Viscometer No. 1** is used for oils with flow time **> 2000 seconds** would mean Redwood No. 2 is used instead. The oil is held in a standard cup at a fixed temperature. A ball valve is opened, and **50 mL** of oil is collected in a **Kohlrausch flask**. The time (in seconds) for the 50 mL to drain is the **Redwood time (t seconds)**.

**Why does this measure viscosity?**
The flow through the orifice obeys **Poiseuille's Law**:

$$Q = \frac{\pi r^{4} \Delta P}{8 \mu L}$$

More viscous oil → smaller Q → longer drain time. The Redwood time is therefore directly proportional to kinematic viscosity.

---

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The following table is the EXACT set of formulas you must memorize for KTU exams.**

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units / Constants |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Dynamic Viscosity | $\mu = \tau / (dv/dy)$ | Pa·s or poise |
| 2 | Kinematic Viscosity | $\nu = \mu / \rho$ | m²/s or stokes |
| 3 | Kinematic Viscosity (Redwood No. 1) | $\nu = 0.0026\,t - \dfrac{1.72}{t}$ | cSt, valid for $34 \le t \le 100$ s |
| 4 | Kinematic Viscosity (Redwood No. 2) | $\nu = 0.024\,t - \dfrac{1.0}{t}$ | cSt, valid for $t > 100$ s |
| 5 | Redwood No. 1 Viscosity (Absolute) | $\mu = \rho \left(0.0026\,t - \dfrac{1.72}{t}\right)$ | poise, with $\rho$ in g/cm³ |
| 6 | Saybolt Universal → cSt | $\nu_{cSt} = 0.226\,t_{Say} - \dfrac{0.81}{t_{Say}}$ | For $t_{Say} > 100$ s (lower) |
| 7 | Engler Degrees → cSt | $\nu_{cSt} = 7.6\,^{\circ}E - \dfrac{6.0}{^{\circ}E}$ | For $1 < ^{\circ}E < 3$ (low range) |
| 8 | Kinematic Viscosity to Dynamic | $\mu = \nu \cdot \rho$ | $\nu$ in cSt, $\rho$ in g/cm³, $\mu$ in cP |
| 9 | Viscosity Index (VI) | $VI = \dfrac{L - U}{L - H} \times 100$ | L, U = viscosities of reference oils at 40 °C |
| 10 | Fire Point Estimate | $FP \approx Flash\ Point + (10\ to\ 30\ ^{\circ}C)$ | Empirical rule |

> [!NOTE]
> **Conversions worth remembering:**
> - $1\ \text{St} = 100\ \text{cSt} = 10^{-4}\ \text{m}^{2}/\text{s}$
> - $1\ \text{poise} = 100\ \text{centipoise}\ (\text{cP}) = 0.1\ \text{Pa·s}$
> - $1\ \text{cP} = 1\ \text{cSt}$ (when density = 1 g/cm³, e.g., water at 20 °C)

---

### 2.5 Engineering Utility

| Test | Real-World Engineering Use |
|---|---|
| **Flash Point** | Determines the **fire hazard class** of oil; critical for storage, transport (railway tankers), and choice of lubricant in high-temperature zones like turbines. |
| **Fire Point** | Used in **fire-fighting engineering** — minimum temperature for sustained combustion; helps design relief valves. |
| **Viscosity** | Selects the **right grade of oil** (SAE 10W, 20W, 30, 40, 50) for engines, gearboxes, hydraulic systems. A too-thin oil will not protect bearings; a too-thick oil will starve the engine. |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Redwood Viscosity Equation

The Redwood viscometer measures the time `t` (in seconds) for 50 mL of oil to flow through a standard orifice. Through calibration, this is correlated to kinematic viscosity in centistokes.

**Starting Point (Empirical Calibration):**
The kinematic viscosity in centistokes is empirically related to the Redwood time as:

$$\nu = A\,t - \frac{B}{t}$$

**Substituting Standard Redwood No. 1 Constants (ASTM D88):**
For Redwood Viscometer No. 1 (orifice diameter 1.62 mm):

$$A = 0.0026,\quad B = 1.72$$

**Therefore, the working equation is:**

$$\nu = 0.0026\,t - \frac{1.72}{t}\quad \text{[cSt]}$$

This is **valid when the flow time lies between 34 s and 100 s**.

**Substituting Standard Redwood No. 2 Constants (for heavy oils):**
For Redwood Viscometer No. 2 (orifice diameter 3.55 mm, used when `t > 2000 s` in No. 1):

$$A = 0.024,\quad B = 1.0$$

**Therefore, the working equation becomes:**

$$\nu = 0.024\,t - \frac{1.0}{t}\quad \text{[cSt]}$$

**Conversion to Dynamic Viscosity (Absolute):**
Starting from the definition:

$$\mu = \nu \cdot \rho$$

For Redwood No. 1:

$$\mu = \rho \left( 0.0026\,t - \frac{1.72}{t} \right)\quad \text{[poise]}$$

where $\rho$ must be in **g/cm³** and $\mu$ will be in **poise**.

---

### 3.2 Detailed Worked Numerical — Viscosity Calculation

> [!NOTE]
> **Sample Problem (typical KTU style):** A lubricating oil of density 0.88 g/cm³ has a Redwood No. 1 time of 75 s at 60 °C. Calculate the (a) kinematic viscosity in cSt, (b) dynamic viscosity in cP and Pa·s.

**Part (a): Kinematic Viscosity**

Step 1 — Identify the right formula. Since t = 75 s lies between 34 s and 100 s, we use Redwood No. 1:

$$\nu = 0.0026\,t - \frac{1.72}{t}$$

Step 2 — Substitute t = 75 s:

$$\nu = 0.0026 \times 75 - \frac{1.72}{75}$$

Step 3 — Compute each term:

$$\nu = 0.195 - 0.02293$$

Step 4 — Final answer:

$$\boxed{\nu = 0.1721\ \text{cSt} \approx 0.172\ \text{cSt}}$$

**Part (b): Dynamic Viscosity**

Step 1 — Apply the relation $\mu = \nu \cdot \rho$:

$$\mu = 0.1721\ \text{cSt} \times 0.88\ \frac{\text{g}}{\text{cm}^{3}}$$

Step 2 — Multiply (note: 1 cP = 1 cSt × (g/cm³)):

$$\mu = 0.1514\ \text{cP}$$

Step 3 — Convert to Pa·s (1 cP = 0.001 Pa·s):

$$\mu = 0.1514 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{Pa·s} = 1.514 \times 10^{-4}\ \text{Pa·s}$$

$$\boxed{\mu = 0.1514\ \text{cP} = 1.514 \times 10^{-4}\ \text{Pa·s}}$$

---

### 3.3 Detailed Worked Numerical — Flash & Fire Point Identification

> [!NOTE]
> **Sample Problem:** During a Cleveland open cup test on a lubricant, the following observations are noted. Identify the flash point and fire point.

| Time (min) | Temperature (°C) | Observation |
|---|---|---|
| 0 | 28 | Initial oil temperature |
| 4 | 145 | No flash on test flame application |
| 6 | 178 | A slight flicker (flash) appears — does not sustain |
| 8 | 195 | Flame on test flame application continues for 6 seconds |

**Solution:**

Step 1 — The **Flash Point** is the lowest temperature at which the vapour flashes momentarily but does not sustain. This occurred at **178 °C** (slight flicker, not continuous).

Step 2 — The **Fire Point** is the lowest temperature at which the vapour burns for at least 5 seconds. This occurred at **195 °C** (flame continued 6 s).

Step 3 — Confirm the rule Fire Point > Flash Point: $195 > 178$ ✓

$$\boxed{\text{Flash Point} = 178\ ^{\circ}\text{C},\quad \text{Fire Point} = 195\ ^{\circ}\text{C}}$$

Step 4 — Note the difference: $195 - 178 = 17\ ^{\circ}\text{C}$, well within the typical 10–30 °C range.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Choosing the Right Viscometer

| Redwood Time (s) | Viscometer to Use | Formula |
|---|---|---|
| $t < 30$ | Instrument unreliable | — |
| $34 \le t \le 100$ | Redwood No. 1 | $\nu = 0.0026t - 1.72/t$ |
| $100 < t \le 2000$ | Redwood No. 1 (extended) | $\nu = 0.0026t - 1.72/t$ (approx) |
| $t > 2000$ | **Redwood No. 2** | $\nu = 0.024t - 1.0/t$ |

---

### 3.5 Error Analysis — Sources of Deviation

| Source | Effect on Result |
|---|---|
| Inaccurate temperature control | Viscosity changes ~10% per °C near room temp |
| Air bubbles in oil | Cause premature flow, lowers t |
| Dirty orifice | Increases t, falsely high viscosity |
| Parallax in measuring flask | Reading error of ~1 s |
| Insufficient cooling between runs | Carry-over heat error |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram — Cleveland Open Cup Apparatus

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Heat Source Bunsen Burner] --> B[Heating Plate]
    B --> C[Cleveland Open Cup with Oil Sample]
    C --> D[Thermometer Probe 0-360C]
    E[Test Flame Application Device] --> C
    F[Stopwatch Operator] --> G[Record Temperature vs Flame Behaviour]
    C --> G
    D --> G
    E --> G
    G --> H{Flash Observed?}
    H -- Yes --> I[Note Temperature as Flash Point]
    H -- No --> J[Increase Heat and Retry]
    J --> E
    I --> K{Flame Sustained 5s?}
    K -- Yes --> L[Note Temperature as Fire Point]
    K -- No --> J
```

### 4.2 Block Diagram — Redwood Viscometer No. 1

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Outer Water Bath for Temperature Control] --> B[Inner Oil Cup]
    B --> C[Orifice with Ball Valve at Bottom]
    C --> D[Kohlrausch Flask 50 mL]
    E[Thermometer in Oil] --> B
    F[Stirrer] --> A
    G[Fill Oil up to Mark] --> B
    H[Heat to Desired Temperature] --> B
    I[Open Ball Valve] --> C
    C --> D
    D --> J[Start Stopwatch]
    J --> K[Stop When 50 mL Collected]
    K --> L[Record Time t in Seconds]
    L --> M[Calculate Kinematic Viscosity]
```

### 4.3 Comparative Process Flow — The Two Tests

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph FlashFireTest [Flash and Fire Point Test]
        direction TB
        S1[Sample in Cup] --> S2[Heat at 5 to 6 C per min]
        S2 --> S3[Apply Test Flame Every 2 C]
        S3 --> S4{Any Flash?}
        S4 -- No --> S2
        S4 -- Yes --> S5[Record Flash Point Temperature]
        S5 --> S6{Flame Lasts 5s?}
        S6 -- No --> S2
        S6 -- Yes --> S7[Record Fire Point Temperature]
    end
    subgraph ViscTest [Viscosity Test]
        direction TB
        V1[Fill Redwood Cup with Oil] --> V2[Thermostat to Test Temperature]
        V2 --> V3[Open Orifice]
        V3 --> V4[Collect 50 mL in Kohlrausch Flask]
        V4 --> V5[Note Drain Time t]
        V5 --> V6[Apply Redwood Equation to Get Nu in cSt]
    end
```

### 4.4 Decision Matrix — Why Each Property Matters in Lubricant Selection

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Choose Lubricant for Machine] --> Q1{High Operating Temperature?}
    Q1 -- Above 200 C --> R1[Pick Oil with Flash Point 50 C Above Operating Temp]
    Q1 -- Below 200 C --> R2[Flash Point is Less Critical]
    R1 --> Q2{Need Sustained Fire Resistance?}
    Q2 -- Yes --> R3[Check Fire Point Also]
    Q2 -- No --> R4[Skip Fire Point Check]
    R3 --> Q3{High Shear or Heavy Load?}
    Q4[Lubricant Selected] --> Q3
    R2 --> Q3
    R4 --> Q3
    Q3 -- Yes --> S1[Pick High Viscosity Grade SAE 40 or 50]
    Q3 -- No --> S2[Pick Low Viscosity Grade SAE 10 or 20]
    S1 --> End[Final Lubricant Grade Selected]
    S2 --> End
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper 1]**
> **Define flash point and fire point. How are they different from each other?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Flash Point (1 mark):** The lowest temperature at which the lubricant gives off sufficient vapour to produce a momentary flash (ignition) on application of a standard test flame under prescribed conditions, **but the vapour does not continue to burn**.

**Fire Point (1 mark):** The lowest temperature at which the lubricant vapour, once ignited, **continues to burn for at least 5 seconds** under the same test conditions.

**Difference (1 mark):** At flash point, only momentary ignition occurs. At fire point, sustained combustion occurs. The fire point is always **higher** than the flash point, typically by **10–30 °C**.

---

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model Paper 1]**
> **What is kinematic viscosity? How is it related to absolute viscosity?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1.5 marks):** Kinematic viscosity is the ratio of the dynamic (absolute) viscosity of a fluid to its density. It is a measure of resistance to flow under the influence of gravity.

$$\nu = \frac{\mu}{\rho}$$

**Relation (1.5 marks):** Dynamic viscosity $\mu$ (Pa·s or poise) is the product of kinematic viscosity $\nu$ (m²/s or stokes) and density $\rho$ (kg/m³ or g/cm³).

$$\mu = \nu \cdot \rho$$

In CGS units, **1 cP = 1 cSt × (1 g/cm³)**, i.e., for water at 20 °C, numerical values of cP and cSt are equal.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **Question A. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Adapted]**
>
> **(a) [7 Marks]** Describe the procedure for the determination of flash point and fire point of a given lubricating oil using the **Cleveland Open Cup** apparatus with a neat labelled diagram.
>
> **(b) [7 Marks]** The Redwood No. 1 viscometer is used to measure the viscosity of an oil of density 0.92 g/cm³. The time taken for 50 mL of oil at 80 °C is **90 seconds**. Calculate:
> 1. Kinematic viscosity in cSt
> 2. Dynamic viscosity in cP
> 3. Dynamic viscosity in Pa·s
> 4. Whether Redwood No. 2 should have been used.

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Procedure for Cleveland Open Cup (7 marks):**

1. **Setup (1 mark):** Clean the Cleveland cup, fill with the lubricating oil sample up to the marked level. Place it on the heating plate. Insert the thermometer with its bulb immersed in the oil but not touching the bottom.

2. **Heating (1 mark):** Heat the oil at a uniform rate of approximately **5–6 °C per minute**. Stir gently to ensure uniform temperature.

3. **Test Flame Application (2 marks):** At every **2 °C rise in temperature**, pass the **test flame** (a small gas flame of standard size) horizontally across the cup at a distance of about **10 mm above the oil surface**. The time taken to traverse should be about **1 second**.

4. **Flash Point Detection (1 mark):** Note the temperature at which a **distinct flicker (flash)** appears on the oil surface for the first time. This is the **Flash Point**.

5. **Fire Point Detection (1 mark):** Continue heating and testing. The **Fire Point** is the temperature at which the oil vapour **continues to burn for at least 5 seconds** after the test flame is applied.

6. **Precautions (1 mark):** The test should be conducted in a **fume hood / draught-free area**, no open flames nearby, thermometer should be accurate, and the test flame size should be standard.

**Labelled Diagram (Cleveland Open Cup):**
A vertical cylindrical brass cup (≈ 65 mm depth), heated from below by a Bunsen burner via an asbestos pad. A thermometer is suspended through a clip with its bulb in the oil. The cup has a filling mark and a slightly convex oil surface. A test flame is swept across from one side to the other.

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**Given:**
- Density of oil, $\rho = 0.92\ \text{g/cm}^3$
- Redwood No. 1 time, $t = 90\ \text{s}$
- Temperature = 80 °C

**1. Kinematic Viscosity (2 marks):**
Since t = 90 s lies between 34 s and 100 s, use Redwood No. 1 formula:

$$\nu = 0.0026\,t - \frac{1.72}{t}$$

Substituting t = 90 s:

$$\nu = 0.0026 \times 90 - \frac{1.72}{90}$$

$$\nu = 0.234 - 0.01911$$

$$\boxed{\nu = 0.2149\ \text{cSt} \approx 0.215\ \text{cSt}}$$

**2. Dynamic Viscosity in cP (2 marks):**
Using $\mu = \nu \cdot \rho$ (in CGS-compatible units):

$$\mu = 0.2149\ \text{cSt} \times 0.92\ \frac{\text{g}}{\text{cm}^3}$$

$$\boxed{\mu = 0.1977\ \text{cP}}$$

**3. Dynamic Viscosity in Pa·s (1.5 marks):**
Conversion: 1 cP = 0.001 Pa·s:

$$\mu = 0.1977 \times 0.001$$

$$\boxed{\mu = 1.977 \times 10^{-4}\ \text{Pa·s}}$$

**4. Should Redwood No. 2 be used? (1.5 marks):**
Redwood No. 2 is used when the time in No. 1 exceeds **2000 s**. Here t = 90 s, which is far less than 2000 s, so **Redwood No. 1 is the correct choice**. No, Redwood No. 2 is **NOT** required.

---

> **Question B. [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Adapted — Internal Choice Alternative]**
>
> **(a) [7 Marks]** With a neat sketch, describe the **Redwood Viscometer No. 1** and the experimental procedure to determine the viscosity of a lubricating oil.
>
> **(b) [7 Marks]** Two lubricating oil samples give the following observations in a Cleveland Open Cup test. Identify flash and fire points for each, and comment on the relative safety of the two oils.

| Time (min) | Oil A Temperature (°C) | Oil A Observation | Oil B Temperature (°C) | Oil B Observation |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | 30 | Initial | 30 | Initial |
| 5 | 160 | No flash | 150 | No flash |
| 8 | 188 | Brief flash | 175 | Brief flash |
| 11 | 210 | Burns 6 s | 192 | Burns 7 s |
| 14 | 230 | Burns strongly | 205 | Burns strongly |

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Redwood Viscometer No. 1 (7 marks):**

**Construction (3 marks):**
The Redwood Viscometer No. 1 consists of:
- A **brass oil cup** (capacity ~ 85 mL) suspended inside an **outer water bath** to maintain a constant temperature.
- A **ball valve** at the bottom of the oil cup that seals a standard **jet/orifice** of diameter **1.62 mm** and length 10 mm.
- A **stirrer** in the water bath and a **thermometer** to read oil temperature.
- A **Kohlrausch flask** (50 mL) placed below the orifice to collect the oil.

**Procedure (3 marks):**
1. Clean the oil cup, close the ball valve, and pour the oil up to the pointed gauge mark.
2. Place the cup in the water bath. Heat and stir until the oil reaches the desired test temperature (e.g., 40 °C, 60 °C, 80 °C). Hold for 5 minutes for thermal equilibrium.
3. Place a clean, dry Kohlrausch flask directly below the orifice.
4. Lift the ball valve and simultaneously **start the stopwatch**.
5. **Stop the stopwatch** when the flask collects exactly **50 mL** of oil. Note the time `t` in seconds.
6. Repeat the experiment 2–3 times and take the **mean time**.
7. Calculate $\nu$ using $\nu = 0.0026t - 1.72/t$ in cSt.
8. Convert to dynamic viscosity using $\mu = \nu \cdot \rho$.

**Working formula (1 mark):**

$$\nu = 0.0026\,t - \frac{1.72}{t}\quad \text{[cSt]}$$

**(b) Identification of Flash and Fire Points (7 marks):**

**For Oil A (2.5 marks):**
- Flash Point = **188 °C** (first brief flash observed at this temperature)
- Fire Point = **210 °C** (first instance of burning for ≥ 5 s)
- Difference = 210 − 188 = **22 °C** (within the typical 10–30 °C range ✓)

**For Oil B (2.5 marks):**
- Flash Point = **175 °C**
- Fire Point = **192 °C**
- Difference = 192 − 175 = **17 °C** (within range ✓)

**Comparison and Safety Comment (2 marks):**

| Property | Oil A | Oil B |
|---|---|---|
| Flash Point | 188 °C | **175 °C** (lower) |
| Fire Point | 210 °C | **192 °C** (lower) |
| Relative Fire Hazard | **Lower** | **Higher** |

**Conclusion:** **Oil B is more hazardous** than Oil A because both its flash and fire points are significantly lower. Oil B will ignite earlier in storage or in case of leakage near a hot surface, making it less safe to handle. **Oil A is the preferred choice** where high-temperature operation or fire safety is critical.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Valuation:**
>
> 1. **Wrong formula applied** — Many students use Redwood No. 2 formula ($0.024t - 1.0/t$) for all problems. Always check whether the time is between **34 and 100 s** (No. 1) or **above 100 s** (No. 2 may apply).
>
> 2. **Unit confusion** — Do not mix cSt with cP. They are numerically equal **only when density = 1 g/cm³**. For oil with $\rho = 0.88$, they differ by 12%.
>
> 3. **Forgetting the temperature** — A viscosity number without temperature is meaningless. Always mention the test temperature.
>
> 4. **Confusing Flash Point and Fire Point definitions** — "Flash" = momentary flash; "Fire" = sustained burn for ≥ 5 s. Examiners specifically check this distinction.
>
> 5. **Not writing units in final answers** — KTU valuation often deducts 0.5 marks for missing units. Always write **cSt**, **cP**, **Pa·s**, or **°C** as appropriate.
>
> 6. **Skipping the diagram** — In the 7-mark procedure question, a labelled diagram is compulsory. Without it, expect a **2 to 3 mark** deduction.
>
> 7. **Ignoring significant figures** — Use 3–4 significant figures consistent with given data.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- [x] **Flash Point** = lowest T for **momentary** ignition of vapour (Cleveland = Open Cup method).
- [x] **Fire Point** = lowest T for **sustained** burn (≥ 5 s). Always **Fire Point > Flash Point**.
- [x] **Cleveland Open Cup flash > Pensky-Martens Closed Cup flash** by 5–15 °C.
- [x] **Dynamic Viscosity** $\mu$ = shear stress / velocity gradient; units = **poise or Pa·s**.
- [x] **Kinematic Viscosity** $\nu$ = $\mu / \rho$; units = **stokes or m²/s**.
- [x] **1 cP = 1 cSt × 1 g/cm³** (numerical equivalence only at $\rho$ = 1).
- [x] **Redwood No. 1 formula** (for 34 ≤ t ≤ 100 s): $\nu = 0.0026t - 1.72/t$ in cSt.
- [x] **Redwood No. 2 formula** (for t > 100 s, usually > 2000 s): $\nu = 0.024t - 1.0/t$ in cSt.
- [x] **Procedure for Cleveland:** Heat at 5–6 °C/min, sweep test flame every 2 °C.
- [x] **Procedure for Redwood:** Thermostat the oil, open valve, time 50 mL flow.
- [x] **Viscosity Index (VI)** is the third important property not covered numerically but conceptually — higher VI = less change in viscosity with temperature.
- [x] Always record **temperature** alongside viscosity. Always write **units**.
- [x] Three properties together define a lubricant's **safety + performance profile**: Flash/Fire Point = safety, Viscosity = performance.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Determination of Hardness of Water
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCHL307 - CHEMICAL TECHNOLOGY AND ENVIRONMENTAL ENGINEERING LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Determination of Hardness of Water

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Hardness of Water** is defined as the *soap-destroying power* of water, expressed quantitatively as the total concentration of divalent cations — primarily **Ca²⁺** (calcium) and **Mg²⁺** (magnesium) — and secondarily Fe²⁺, Mn²⁺, Al³⁺, Sr²⁺, Zn²⁺ — reported in terms of equivalent **calcium carbonate (CaCO₃)** in **mg/L** or **ppm**.

Mathematically, total hardness (TH) is the sum of **calcium hardness (CH)** and **magnesium hardness (MH)**:

$$
TH = CH + MH
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Convention:** All hardness values MUST be reported as **mg/L of CaCO₃** even when the actual cation is Mg²⁺, Fe²⁺, or others. This is because CaCO₃ has a molecular weight of **100.09 g/mol** and acts as the universally accepted reference standard.

## 1.2 Classification of Hardness (KTU High-Yield)

| Type | Cause | Removed by | Key Ions |
|------|-------|------------|----------|
| **Temporary Hardness (Carbonate Hardness, CH)** | Bicarbonates of Ca²⁺ and Mg²⁺ | **Boiling** (precipitates as carbonate) | Ca(HCO₃)₂, Mg(HCO₃)₂ |
| **Permanent Hardness (Non-Carbonate Hardness, NCH)** | Chlorides, sulphates, nitrates of Ca²⁺ and Mg²⁺ | **Lime–Soda process / Ion exchange / EDTA** | CaCl₂, CaSO₄, MgCl₂, MgSO₄ |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Soap-Scum" Intuition

Imagine washing a greasy pan with soap in **seawater** versus **soft rainwater**.

- In **soft water**, soap (sodium stearate) lathers freely and cleans the surface.
- In **hard water**, the Ca²⁺ and Mg²⁺ ions act like **hungry magnets** that grab the soap molecules to form a sticky, insoluble white scum (calcium stearate) that floats uselessly on the surface.

$$
2\,C_{17}H_{35}COONa + Ca^{2+} \rightarrow (C_{17}H_{35}COO)_2Ca\downarrow + 2\,Na^+
$$

This scum is the *visible evidence* of hardness. The "magnetic attraction" between Ca²⁺/Mg²⁺ and soap is what we *quantify* in the lab using **EDTA titration**.

## 1.4 Units and Conversions (Exam-Ready)

| Unit | Symbol | Conversion to mg/L as CaCO₃ |
|------|--------|------------------------------|
| Parts per million | **ppm** | 1 ppm = 1 mg/L |
| Milligrams per litre | **mg/L** | 1 mg/L = 1 ppm |
| Clark's degree | **°Cl** | 1 °Cl = 14.3 mg/L |
| French degree | **°Fr** | 1 °Fr = 10 mg/L |
| German degree | **°dH** | 1 °dH = 17.9 mg/L |

> [!TIP]
> **Acceptable Limits (BIS 10500:2012 / WHO):**
> - Drinking water: **≤ 200 mg/L as CaCO₃** (desirable), **600 mg/L** (maximum permissible)
> - Industrial boiler feed: **≤ 1 mg/L** (high-pressure boilers)
> - Distinction: **0–60 mg/L = soft**, **61–120 mg/L = moderately hard**, **121–180 mg/L = hard**, **> 180 mg/L = very hard**.

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The EDTA Complexometric Titration Principle

**Ethylenediaminetetraacetic acid (EDTA)** is a **hexadentate ligand** that wraps around metal ions like an "octopus" using its two nitrogen atoms and four carboxylate oxygen atoms — forming a stable 1:1 complex regardless of the metal's charge.

### 2.1.1 Why EDTA?
- Forms a **single, well-defined 1:1 stoichiometric complex** with Ca²⁺ and Mg²⁺.
- The formation constant is very high ($K_f \approx 10^{10.6}$ for Ca-EDTA) — meaning the reaction goes essentially to completion.
- Allows **direct titration** at controlled pH.

### 2.1.2 The Indicator Chemistry (KTU Favourite)

| Indicator | Used For | Colour Change | pH |
|-----------|----------|---------------|-----|
| **Eriochrome Black T (EBT)** | Total Hardness (Ca²⁺ + Mg²⁺) | Wine-red → Sky-blue | **10.0 ± 0.1** |
| **Murexide (Ammonium Purpurate)** | Calcium Hardness only | Pink → Violet | **12.0 ± 0.2** |
| **Calmagite** | Total Hardness (alternative to EBT) | Red → Blue | 10.0 |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why pH 10 buffer (NH₄Cl + NH₄OH)?**
> At pH 10, the **Y⁴⁻** form of EDTA dominates, which binds Ca²⁺ and Mg²⁺ strongly. If pH drops below 8, the complex doesn't form. If pH rises above 12, Mg²⁺ precipitates as Mg(OH)₂ — this property is exploited to determine Ca²⁺ **separately** (murexide at pH 12).

### 2.1.3 The Sequential Determination Strategy

1. **Total Hardness (TH):** Titrate sample at pH 10 with EBT indicator → volume $V_1$.
2. **Calcium Hardness (CH):** Titrate a separate aliquot at pH 12 with murexide (Mg²⁺ masked as hydroxide) → volume $V_2$.
3. **Magnesium Hardness (MH):** Computed as

$$
MH = TH - CH
$$

## 2.2 Reactions Involved (Step-by-Step Logic)

### 2.2.1 At pH 10 (EBT indicator)
The indicator (HIn²⁻) first complexes with Mg²⁺ (since Ca-EBT is too weak):

$$
Mg^{2+} + HIn^{2-} \rightarrow MgIn^- + H^+ \quad (\text{wine-red})
$$

EDTA (Y⁴⁻) then strips Mg²⁺ from the indicator:

$$
MgIn^- + Y^{4-} \rightarrow MgY^{2-} + In^{3-} \quad (\text{sky-blue})
$$

The sharp colour change from wine-red to pure blue marks the **endpoint**.

### 2.2.2 Net titration reaction

$$
Ca^{2+} + Mg^{2+} + 2\,Y^{4-} \rightarrow CaY^{2-} + MgY^{2-}
$$

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Master this table — these four equations cover ~90% of KTU numerical problems on hardness.**

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units |
|---|----------|---------|-------|
| 1 | Total Hardness (as CaCO₃) | $TH = \dfrac{V_{EDTA} \times M_{EDTA} \times 100.09 \times 1000}{V_{sample}}$ | mg/L |
| 2 | Total Hardness (using Normality) | $TH = \dfrac{V_{EDTA} \times N_{EDTA} \times 50 \times 1000}{V_{sample}}$ | mg/L |
| 3 | 1 mL of 0.01 M EDTA | $\equiv 1.0009$ mg of CaCO₃ | mg |
| 4 | Calcium Hardness | $CH = \dfrac{V_{Ca} \times M_{EDTA} \times 100.09 \times 1000}{V_{sample}}$ | mg/L |
| 5 | Magnesium Hardness | $MH = \dfrac{(V_1 - V_2) \times M_{EDTA} \times 100.09 \times 1000}{V_{sample}}$ | mg/L |
| 6 | Mg²⁺ (as CaCO₃) conversion to mg/L Mg²⁺ | $mg/L\,Mg^{2+} = MH \times \dfrac{24.3}{100.09}$ | mg/L |
| 7 | Ca²⁺ (as CaCO₃) conversion to mg/L Ca²⁺ | $mg/L\,Ca^{2+} = CH \times \dfrac{40.08}{100.09}$ | mg/L |
| 8 | Temporary Hardness (via boiling + HCl titration) | $Temp.\,H = \dfrac{V_{HCl} \times N_{HCl} \times 50 \times 1000}{V_{sample}}$ | mg/L |
| 9 | Permanent Hardness | $Perm.\,H = TH - Temp.\,H$ | mg/L |
| 10 | EDTA Standard Molarity (with CaCO₃ primary standard) | $M_{EDTA} = \dfrac{W_{CaCO_3} \times 1000}{100.09 \times V_{EDTA}}$ | mol/L |

**where:**
- $V_{EDTA}$ = volume of EDTA used in titration (mL)
- $M_{EDTA}$ = molarity of EDTA solution (mol/L)
- $N_{EDTA}$ = normality of EDTA (eq/L)
- $V_{sample}$ = volume of water sample taken (mL)
- **50** = equivalent weight of CaCO₃ (g/eq)
- **100.09** = molecular weight of CaCO₃ (g/mol)
- $W_{CaCO_3}$ = mass of CaCO₃ weighed for standardisation (g)
- $V_1$ = EDTA volume for total hardness, $V_2$ = EDTA volume for Ca²⁺ only

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Significance

| Industry | Consequence of Hardness | Required Limit |
|----------|------------------------|----------------|
| **Boilers / Power plants** | Scale formation → tube overheating, explosion | < 1 mg/L (high pressure) |
| **Textile dyeing** | Precipitates dyes → uneven shade | < 50 mg/L |
| **Pharmaceutical** | Interferes with formulations, injectables | Ultra-pure (RO + EDI) |
| **Dairy / Beverages** | Curdling, off-flavour, scaling in pasteurisers | < 100 mg/L |
| **Domestic washing** | Soap wastage, fabric stiffening | < 200 mg/L |
| **Cooling towers** | Biofouling + scale hybrid | < 500 mg/L (with cycles) |

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Master Hardness Formula

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why is the formula multiplied by 50 (or 100.09)?** This section derives the formula from first principles so you can answer *any* variant question in the KTU exam — not just memorise the result.

### 3.1.1 Step 1: Start with the definition of Normality

Normality ($N$) is defined as the number of **equivalents** of solute per litre of solution:

$$
N = \dfrac{\text{Number of equivalents of solute}}{\text{Volume of solution in litres}}
$$

For a divalent cation like Ca²⁺ reacting with EDTA in 1:1 stoichiometry, **1 mole of Ca²⁺ = 2 equivalents** because each Ca²⁺ neutralises 2 negative charges.

### 3.1.2 Step 2: Apply the titration principle — Law of Equivalents

At the **equivalence point** of any titration, the number of equivalents of the titrant equals the number of equivalents of the analyte:

$$
\text{Equivalents of EDTA} = \text{Equivalents of Ca}^{2+} + \text{Mg}^{2+}
$$

Expressed mathematically:

$$
N_{EDTA} \times V_{EDTA}\,(\text{mL}) = N_{hardness} \times V_{sample}\,(\text{mL})
$$

### 3.1.3 Step 3: Express hardness in terms of CaCO₃ equivalents

We *choose* to report hardness **as if it were all CaCO₃**. The equivalent weight of CaCO₃ is:

$$
E_{CaCO_3} = \dfrac{\text{Molecular weight}}{\text{Valency}} = \dfrac{100.09}{2} = 50.045 \approx 50
$$

The number of equivalents of hardness in $V_{sample}$ mL is:

$$
\text{Equivalents of hardness} = \dfrac{\text{Mass of CaCO}_3\,(g)}{50} = \dfrac{W}{50}
$$

### 3.1.4 Step 4: Convert mass to concentration (mg/L)

If $W$ grams of CaCO₃ are dissolved in $V_{sample}$ mL, the concentration in **mg/L** is:

$$
\text{Concentration} = \dfrac{W\,(g) \times 10^3\,(mg/g) \times 10^3\,(mL/L)}{V_{sample}\,(mL)} = \dfrac{W \times 10^6}{V_{sample}}
$$

### 3.1.5 Step 5: Combine the two expressions

From Step 2:

$$
N_{EDTA} \times V_{EDTA} = \dfrac{W}{50} \quad (\text{where } V \text{ is in litres, but mg/L formula uses mL, so a 1000 factor applies})
$$

Solving for the mass of CaCO₃ in the sample:

$$
W = N_{EDTA} \times V_{EDTA} \times 50
$$

Converting to mg/L (multiply by $10^6 / V_{sample}$):

$$
\boxed{TH\,(mg/L) = \dfrac{N_{EDTA} \times V_{EDTA}\,(mL) \times 50 \times 1000}{V_{sample}\,(mL)}}
$$

### 3.1.6 Step 6: Equivalent molarity-based form

Since $N = M \times n$ where $n$ is the n-factor (= 2 for CaCO₃ reference, but = 1 for EDTA in 1:1 reaction with Ca²⁺), we get:

$$
TH = \dfrac{M_{EDTA} \times V_{EDTA} \times 100.09 \times 1000}{V_{sample}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> The **50** in the normality form and the **100.09** in the molarity form are mathematically equivalent because EDTA's n-factor is 1 (1 mole EDTA = 1 equivalent in this reaction), while CaCO₃'s n-factor is 2 (1 mole CaCO₃ = 2 equivalents).

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Style)

> [!EXAMPLE]
> **Problem:** 50 mL of a water sample required 18.0 mL of 0.02 M EDTA solution for total hardness titration. A separate 50 mL aliquot required 12.0 mL of the same EDTA at pH 12 with murexide indicator. Calculate total hardness, calcium hardness, magnesium hardness, and classify the water.

**Given:**
- $V_{sample} = 50$ mL
- $M_{EDTA} = 0.02$ M
- $V_1$ (total) = 18.0 mL
- $V_2$ (Ca²⁺ only) = 12.0 mL

**Solution:**

### (i) Total Hardness

$$
TH = \dfrac{V_1 \times M_{EDTA} \times 100.09 \times 1000}{V_{sample}}
$$

$$
TH = \dfrac{18.0 \times 0.02 \times 100.09 \times 1000}{50}
$$

$$
TH = \dfrac{18.0 \times 0.02 \times 100090}{50} = \dfrac{36032.4}{50} = 720.65\,mg/L
$$

### (ii) Calcium Hardness

$$
CH = \dfrac{12.0 \times 0.02 \times 100.09 \times 1000}{50} = \dfrac{12.0 \times 0.02 \times 100090}{50} = 480.43\,mg/L
$$

### (iii) Magnesium Hardness

$$
MH = TH - CH = 720.65 - 480.43 = 240.22\,mg/L
$$

### (iv) Actual mg/L of Ca²⁺ and Mg²⁺

$$
mg/L\,Ca^{2+} = CH \times \dfrac{40.08}{100.09} = 480.43 \times 0.4004 = 192.36\,mg/L
$$

$$
mg/L\,Mg^{2+} = MH \times \dfrac{24.3}{100.09} = 240.22 \times 0.2428 = 58.32\,mg/L
$$

### (v) Classification

Since $TH = 720.65\,mg/L > 180\,mg/L$, the water is classified as **VERY HARD**, and **unsuitable for domestic use** without softening.

## 3.3 Standardisation of EDTA — Step-by-Step (Often Asked in Viva)

1. **Primary standard:** Anhydrous CaCO₃ (AR grade, dried at 110 °C for 1 hour).
2. **Stock solution (0.01 M CaCO₃):** Weigh 1.000 g CaCO₃ → dissolve in minimum HCl (1:1) → dilute to 1 litre.
3. **Working solution (0.001 M):** Dilute 100 mL → 1 litre.
4. **Titration:** Pipette 50 mL of CaCO₃ solution + 1 mL NH₄Cl/NH₄OH buffer + EBT indicator → titrate with EDTA until sky-blue endpoint.
5. **Calculation of actual EDTA molarity:**

$$
M_{EDTA} = \dfrac{50 \times 0.001 \times 100.09 \times 1000}{V_{EDTA} \times 1000} = \dfrac{5.0045}{V_{EDTA}}
$$

## 3.4 Sources of Error & Precautions (KTU Viva Hot-List)

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Experimental Errors:**
> 1. **Incorrect buffer pH** → sluggish endpoint or no sharp colour change.
> 2. **Heavy metal interference** (Fe³⁺, Cu²⁺, Zn²⁺) → use **1 mL of 5% Na₂S** or **triethanolamine** to mask.
> 3. **Burette parallax error** → read at eye level, meniscus at bottom.
> 4. **Using wet pipette / conical flask** → rinse with the solution to be measured.
> 5. **Over-titration** → persistent blue-violet tint indicates the endpoint has been passed.

## 4.1 Experimental Procedure — Mermaid Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Collect water sample in clean 250 mL bottle] --> B[Pipette 50 mL sample into 250 mL conical flask]
    B --> C[Add 1 to 2 mL of NH4Cl-NH4OH buffer pH 10]

    D --> E[Solution turns wine red if Ca2+ or Mg2+ present]
    E --> F[Titrate against standardised 0.01 M EDTA from burette]
    F --> G{Endpoint reached?}
    G -->|No: still red| F
    G -->|Yes: sky blue with no reddish tint| H[Record V1 = EDTA volume for TH]
    H --> I[Take another 50 mL aliquot in a fresh flask]
    I --> J[Add 1 mL of 1 N NaOH to raise pH to 12]
    J --> K[Add 50 mg of murexide indicator]
    K --> L[Pink colour appears]
    L --> M[Titrate with same EDTA solution]
    M --> N{Pink to violet endpoint?}
    N -->|No| M
    N -->|Yes| O[Record V2 = EDTA volume for Ca2+ only]
    O --> P[Calculate TH, CH, MH using formulas]
    P --> Q[Report in mg per L as CaCO3]
    Q --> R[End]
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Question]**

### **Q1. Define hardness of water. Distinguish between temporary and permanent hardness.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Hardness of water is the measure of the concentration of dissolved calcium and magnesium salts expressed as equivalent CaCO₃ in mg/L.

| Feature | Temporary Hardness | Permanent Hardness |
|---------|-------------------|-------------------|
| **Causing salts** | Ca(HCO₃)₂, Mg(HCO₃)₂ | CaCl₂, CaSO₄, MgCl₂, MgSO₄ |
| **Removed by** | Boiling | Cannot be removed by boiling |
| **Precipitate on boiling** | CaCO₃ / MgCO₃ | None |
| **Also called** | Carbonate hardness | Non-carbonate hardness |
| **Significance** | Causes scale in kettles, geysers | Causes chronic scaling in boilers |

> *[Mark split: 1 mark definition + 2 marks comparison table.]*

---

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Question]**

### **Q2. Why is NH₄Cl–NH₄OH buffer used in EDTA titration of total hardness? What is the role of Eriochrome Black T indicator?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **NH₄Cl–NH₄OH buffer maintains the pH at 10.0 ± 0.1**, which is essential because:
- At pH 10, the **Y⁴⁻ form of EDTA** dominates, which forms stable 1:1 complexes with both Ca²⁺ and Mg²⁺.
- Below pH 8, the complex does not form; above pH 12, Mg²⁺ precipitates as Mg(OH)₂.

**Eriochrome Black T (EBT)** acts as a **metallochromic indicator**:
- It forms a **wine-red complex** with Mg²⁺ (and weakly with Ca²⁺).
- EDTA, being a stronger complexing agent, **liberates the indicator** at the endpoint, producing a sharp **sky-blue colour**.
- The colour change signals the completion of the titration.

> *[Mark split: 1.5 marks buffer + 1.5 marks indicator role.]*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Module Level, 2024 Scheme Pattern]**
> *Each question has sub-parts (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks. Solve ANY ONE full question.*

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

> **[KTU University Exam – July 2024]**

#### **(a)** Explain the principle and procedure for the determination of **total hardness** of a water sample by EDTA titration method. (7 marks)

**Model Answer:**

**Principle (3 marks):**

Water hardness is determined by titrating Ca²⁺ and Mg²⁺ ions with a standard solution of the disodium salt of EDTA (Na₂H₂Y·2H₂O) at pH 10 using Eriochrome Black T as the indicator. EDTA forms a stable, water-soluble 1:1 chelate complex with both Ca²⁺ and Mg²⁺. The indicator forms a weaker wine-red complex with Mg²⁺; at the endpoint, EDTA displaces Mg²⁺ from the indicator, freeing the indicator in its sky-blue form.

The reactions are:

$$
M^{2+} + H_2Y^{2-} \rightarrow MY^{2-} + 2\,H^+ \quad (M = Ca \text{ or } Mg)
$$

$$
MgIn^- + H_2Y^{2-} \rightarrow MgY^{2-} + HIn^{2-} \quad (\text{wine-red} \rightarrow \text{sky-blue})
$$

**Reagents Required (1 mark):**
1. **Standard EDTA solution (0.01 M):** Prepared by dissolving 3.72 g of Na₂EDTA·2H₂O per litre; standardised against primary standard CaCO₃.
2. **Buffer solution (pH 10.0):** Dissolve 67.5 g NH₄Cl in 570 mL of concentrated NH₄OH and dilute to 1 litre.
3. **Eriochrome Black T indicator:** 0.5 g in 100 mL of triethanolamine (or solid dilution with NaCl).
4. **Triethanolamine / Na₂S solution** to mask interfering Fe³⁺, Cu²⁺, Zn²⁺.

**Procedure (3 marks):**

1. Rinse a 50 mL burette with standard EDTA solution; fill and adjust to zero.
2. Pipette **50 mL** of the clear water sample into a 250 mL conical flask.
3. Add **1–2 mL** of pH 10 buffer solution and swirl.
4. Add **30–50 mg of EBT indicator** — solution turns **wine-red**.
5. Titrate slowly with EDTA until the colour changes from **wine-red → purple → sky-blue** with no reddish tinge.
6. Note the burette reading; repeat to get **concordant values (within ± 0.1 mL)**.
7. Calculate TH using:

$$
TH\,(mg/L) = \dfrac{V_{EDTA} \times M_{EDTA} \times 100.09 \times 1000}{V_{sample}}
$$

> *[Mark split: 3 principle + 1 reagents + 3 procedure.]*

#### **(b)** 50 mL of a water sample required 16.4 mL of 0.02 M EDTA for total hardness, and 10.8 mL of the same EDTA for calcium hardness (murexide titration). Calculate TH, CH, MH, and the actual Ca²⁺ and Mg²⁺ concentrations in mg/L. (7 marks)

**Model Answer:**

**Given:** $V_{sample} = 50$ mL, $M_{EDTA} = 0.02$ M, $V_1 = 16.4$ mL, $V_2 = 10.8$ mL

**Step 1: Total Hardness (TH)**

$$
TH = \dfrac{16.4 \times 0.02 \times 100.09 \times 1000}{50}
$$

$$
TH = \dfrac{16.4 \times 0.02 \times 100090}{50} = \dfrac{32829.52}{50} = 656.59\,mg/L
$$

**Step 2: Calcium Hardness (CH)**

$$
CH = \dfrac{10.8 \times 0.02 \times 100.09 \times 1000}{50} = \dfrac{21619.44}{50} = 432.39\,mg/L
$$

**Step 3: Magnesium Hardness (MH)**

$$
MH = TH - CH = 656.59 - 432.39 = 224.20\,mg/L
$$

**Step 4: Actual Ca²⁺ concentration**

$$
mg/L\,Ca^{2+} = CH \times \dfrac{40.08}{100.09} = 432.39 \times 0.4004 = 173.12\,mg/L
$$

**Step 5: Actual Mg²⁺ concentration**

$$
mg/L\,Mg^{2+} = MH \times \dfrac{24.3}{100.09} = 224.20 \times 0.2428 = 54.44\,mg/L
$$

**Result:** The water is **very hard** (TH > 180 mg/L) and **unsuitable for domestic use** without softening.

> *[Mark split: 2 TH + 2 CH + 1 MH + 1 each Ca²⁺/Mg²⁺ + 1 comment on classification.]*

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

#### **(a)** Discuss in detail the **causes and consequences** of water hardness. Add a note on the **units** used to express hardness. (7 marks)

**Model Answer:**

**Causes (2 marks):**

Hardness is caused by the dissolution of **geological minerals** as water percolates through soil and rock strata. The primary ions responsible are:

- **Calcium (Ca²⁺):** From limestone (CaCO₃), gypsum (CaSO₄·2H₂O), dolomite (CaMg(CO₃)₂).
- **Magnesium (Mg²⁺):** From dolomite, magnesite (MgCO₃), epsomite (MgSO₄·7H₂O).
- **Minor contributors:** Fe²⁺, Mn²⁺, Al³⁺, Sr²⁺, Zn²⁺, Ba²⁺.

**Consequences (3 marks):**

| Domain | Effect |
|--------|--------|
| **Domestic** | Soap wastage, scum in baths, scaling in geysers, dull laundry |
| **Industrial (boilers)** | CaCO₃/MgCO₃ scale reduces heat transfer; 1 mm scale → 5–8 % fuel loss; tube failure |
| **Dairy/Food** | Curdling, off-taste, scaling in pasteurisers and evaporators |
| **Textile** | Uneven dyeing, harsh fabric finish |
| **Pharmaceutical** | Interferes with formulations; injectable water must be hardness-free |
| **Concrete** | Reduces setting strength if aggregates are reactive |

**Units (2 marks):**

| Unit | Conversion to mg/L as CaCO₃ |
|------|------------------------------|
| mg/L or ppm | 1 : 1 |
| Clark's degree (°Cl) | 1 °Cl = 14.3 mg/L |
| French degree (°Fr) | 1 °Fr = 10 mg/L |
| German degree (°dH) | 1 °dH = 17.9 mg/L |

> *[Mark split: 2 causes + 3 consequences + 2 units.]*

#### **(b)** A standard 0.01 M EDTA solution is to be prepared using 3.722 g of Na₂EDTA·2H₂O (Mol. wt. = 372.24 g/mol) per litre. 50 mL of standard CaCO₃ solution (1.00 g/L) was titrated with this EDTA, requiring 9.95 mL. Calculate (i) the exact molarity of EDTA, and (ii) the total hardness of a sample that consumed 12.5 mL of this EDTA per 50 mL. (7 marks)

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1: Molarity of standard CaCO₃**

$$
M_{CaCO_3} = \dfrac{1.00\,g/L}{100.09\,g/mol} = 0.00999\,M \approx 0.01\,M
$$

**Step 2: Molarity of EDTA (using 1:1 stoichiometry)**

$$
M_{EDTA} \times V_{EDTA} = M_{CaCO_3} \times V_{CaCO_3}
$$

$$
M_{EDTA} \times 9.95 = 0.00999 \times 50
$$

$$
M_{EDTA} = \dfrac{0.00999 \times 50}{9.95} = \dfrac{0.4995}{9.95} = 0.05020\,M
$$

**Step 3: Total Hardness of the sample**

$$
TH = \dfrac{12.5 \times 0.05020 \times 100.09 \times 1000}{50}
$$

$$
TH = \dfrac{12.5 \times 0.05020 \times 100090}{50} = \dfrac{62831.5}{50} = 1256.6\,mg/L
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Result:** The sample has $TH = 1256.6\,mg/L$, which is **very hard water** (6× the BIS permissible limit). Mandatory softening is required.

> *[Mark split: 2 standardisation + 1 step 3 calculation + 1 final answer + 1 comment on classification.]*

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU students LOSE marks in hardness problems:**
> 1. **Forgetting the × 1000 factor** when converting mL to L — silent killer of 1 mark.
> 2. **Using molecular weight (100.09) with normality formula** OR **equivalent weight (50) with molarity formula** — examiners are strict about this; both work but you must state which basis you are using.
> 3. **Not mentioning the buffer pH (10.0 ± 0.1)** in the procedure — this is a 1-mark kill.
> 4. **Forgetting to subtract for blank** (if any reagent blank is significant, typically 0.05–0.1 mL).
> 5. **Not classifying the water** (soft / moderately hard / hard / very hard) — KTU examiners expect a concluding statement.
> 6. **Writing "murexide turns blue"** — it actually turns **violet/purple**, not blue. EBT is the one that turns blue.
> 7. **Not stating "1 mL of 0.01 M EDTA ≡ 1 mg CaCO₃"** in the viva — this is the single most important relationship.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Last-Minute Rapid Revision Checklist (Print and Read Before Exam):**

- ✅ Hardness = total Ca²⁺ + Mg²⁺ (as CaCO₃ equivalent) in **mg/L**.
- ✅ **Temporary hardness** = removed by boiling (bicarbonates).
- ✅ **Permanent hardness** = removed by EDTA / lime-soda / ion exchange (chlorides, sulphates).
- ✅ EDTA is a **hexadentate ligand** forming **1:1** complex with Ca²⁺/Mg²⁺.
- ✅ Buffer for TH = **NH₄Cl + NH₄OH at pH 10**.
- ✅ Buffer for Ca²⁺ only = **NaOH at pH 12** (Mg²⁺ masked as Mg(OH)₂).
- ✅ **EBT** = wine-red → sky-blue at pH 10 (used for TH).
- ✅ **Murexide** = pink → violet at pH 12 (used for Ca²⁺ only).
- ✅ **Master formula:** $TH = \dfrac{V_{EDTA} \times M_{EDTA} \times 100.09 \times 1000}{V_{sample}}$
- ✅ **Alternative form:** $TH = \dfrac{V_{EDTA} \times N_{EDTA} \times 50 \times 1000}{V_{sample}}$
- ✅ **1 mL of 0.01 M EDTA ≡ 1 mg of CaCO₃ ≡ 0.4 mg Ca²⁺ ≡ 0.243 mg Mg²⁺.**
- ✅ **MH = TH − CH.**
- ✅ BIS drinking water limit = **200 mg/L** (desirable), 600 mg/L (max permissible).
- ✅ Classification: 0–60 soft, 61–120 moderate, 121–180 hard, > 180 very hard.
- ✅ **Masking agents:** Triethanolamine (for Fe, Al, Mn); Na₂S (for Cu, Zn).
- ✅ **Primary standard for EDTA:** Anhydrous CaCO₃ (AR grade).
- ✅ EDTA is a **secondary standard** — always standardise before use.
- ✅ **Equivalents** at endpoint: $N_{EDTA} V_{EDTA} = N_{hardness} V_{sample}$.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Estimation of Total Solids and Dissolved Solids Content of Wastewater Sample
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCHL307 - CHEMICAL TECHNOLOGY AND ENVIRONMENTAL ENGINEERING LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Estimation of Total Solids and Dissolved Solids Content of Wastewater Sample

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Total Solids (TS)** in a wastewater sample refer to the **residue remaining after a known volume of the sample has been evaporated to dryness at a specified temperature (103–105 °C)**, expressed in **mg/L**. Total Solids mathematically comprise two operationally defined fractions: **Total Suspended Solids (TSS)** and **Total Dissolved Solids (TDS)**.

The KTU 2024 Scheme lab manual specifies the following standard operational definitions as per **APHA Standard Methods for the Examination of Water and Wastewater (23rd Edition, 2017)**:

| Parameter | Operational Definition | Drying Temperature |
|-----------|------------------------|-------------------|
| **Total Solids (TS)** | Residue left after evaporation of an unfiltered sample | 103–105 °C |
| **Total Dissolved Solids (TDS)** | Residue left after evaporation of the **filtered** sample | 103–105 °C or 180 °C |
| **Total Suspended Solids (TSS)** | Residue retained on a **standard glass-fiber filter** (1.5 µm pore size), dried to constant weight | 103–105 °C |
| **Total Volatile Solids (TVS)** | Loss in weight of TS/TDS/TSS residue after ignition at **550 ± 50 °C** | — |
| **Total Fixed Solids (TFS)** | Weight remaining after ignition | — |

Mathematically, the mass balance is:

$$\text{Total Solids (TS)} = \text{Total Suspended Solids (TSS)} + \text{Total Dissolved Solids (TDS)}$$

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Tea Strainer" Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy:** Imagine preparing a cup of *masala chai* (Indian spiced tea). The liquid you drink is the **dissolved fraction** (sugar, tea compounds, milk solids) — the things that passed through the tea strainer. The wet, soggy tea leaves and spice residue caught in the strainer are the **suspended solids** — things too big to pass through. The sum of *what passed through* + *what got caught* = the **total solids** you originally put into the cup. This mental image will help you remember which fraction is filtered (TDS) and which is retained (TSS).

## 1.3 Why This Experiment Matters in KTU Examinations

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This experiment is a **mandatory Module Experiment 2** in the KTU 2024 B.Tech Chemical Engineering curriculum. According to **CO₂ (Apply standard analytical techniques for the characterization of water and wastewater)**, students must demonstrate competence in gravimetric estimation techniques. The exam pattern typically requires you to:
> 1. State the **principle and theory** (≈ 4 marks)
> 2. Explain the **procedure with safety precautions** (≈ 4 marks)
> 3. Perform the **calculation with units** and analyze results (≈ 4 marks)
> 4. Discuss **sources of error and environmental significance** (≈ 2 marks)

## 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Reference Values

- **Standard evaporation temperature:** **103–105 °C** (elimination of mechanically occluded water, but not loss of volatile organic matter)
- **Ignition temperature for Fixed/Volatile Solids:** **550 ± 50 °C**
- **Density of pure water at 20 °C:** **ρ = 998.2 kg/m³** (used for volume-mass conversions)
- **Standard filter pore size:** **1.5 µm** (Glass Fiber Filter, Whatman Grade 934-AH or equivalent)
- **Minimum detectable mass:** **0.5 mg** (analytical balance sensitivity)
- **Typical TDS in domestic wastewater:** **250–850 mg/L**
- **Permissible TDS for drinking water (BIS IS 10500:2012):** **500 mg/L** (acceptable limit), **2000 mg/L** (permissible limit in absence of alternative source)
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Solids in Wastewater — The Hierarchical View

> [!NOTE]
> When you pour a beaker of municipal wastewater into a gravity settling column for 1 hour, you get a **visual segregation** that mirrors the analytical classification used in the lab. This hierarchy is the conceptual skeleton of the experiment.

### 2.1.1 The Three Operational Fractions

1. **Total Solids (TS):** Everything left behind when the entire sample is evaporated.
2. **Total Dissolved Solids (TDS):** The "invisible" fraction — particles, ions, and molecules smaller than ~1.5 µm that pass through a glass-fiber filter. Comprises:
   - **Inorganic salts:** Na⁺, Ca²⁺, Mg²⁺, Cl⁻, SO₄²⁻, HCO₃⁻
   - **Dissolved organic matter:** Humic acids, sugars, small proteins
3. **Total Suspended Solids (TSS):** The "settleable and floatable" fraction retained on the filter. Subdivided into:
   - **Settleable Solids:** Removed by quiescent settling (Imhoff cone test)
   - **Non-settleable Solids:** Colloidal particles (0.001–1 µm) and supracolloidal solids (1–100 µm)

## 2.2 The Gravimetric Principle — Why Drying at 103–105 °C?

The choice of **103–105 °C** is not arbitrary; it represents a thermodynamic compromise:

- **Above 100 °C:** Mechanical water (held in pores of dried residue) and water of crystallization are driven off completely.
- **Below 105 °C:** Volatile organic compounds and decomposition products of bicarbonates are **not** significantly lost.
- **Above 105 °C:** Thermal decomposition of CaCO₃ → CaO + CO₂ begins to introduce negative error.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Bicarbonate decomposition is a major error source:**
> $$\text{Ca(HCO}_3)_2 \xrightarrow{\Delta} \text{CaCO}_3 + \text{H}_2\text{O} + \text{CO}_2 \uparrow$$
> This means TS measured at 103 °C will **overestimate** true dissolved inorganic carbon because some CO₂ is lost as gas, leaving behind a lighter residue.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet & Master Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The 6 Golden Formulas of Module Experiment 2** — Memorize these and the units, and you can solve any KTU Part A or Part B numerical on this topic.

| # | Parameter | Formula | Units | Boundary Conditions |
|---|-----------|---------|-------|---------------------|
| 1 | **Total Solids (TS)** | $\text{TS} = \dfrac{(W_2 - W_1) \times 1000}{V}$ | mg/L | $W_2 > W_1$ (must be positive) |
| 2 | **Total Dissolved Solids (TDS)** | $\text{TDS} = \dfrac{(W_4 - W_3) \times 1000}{V_f}$ | mg/L | Filtrate must be crystal clear |
| 3 | **Total Suspended Solids (TSS)** | $\text{TSS} = \text{TS} - \text{TDS}$ | mg/L | By mass balance |
| 4 | **TSS (Direct method)** | $\text{TSS} = \dfrac{(W_f - W_e) \times 1000}{V_s}$ | mg/L | Filter pre-weighed after drying |
| 5 | **Total Volatile Solids (TVS)** | $\text{TVS} = \dfrac{(W_d - W_a) \times 1000}{V}$ | mg/L | $W_d$ = mass before ignition, $W_a$ = mass after |
| 6 | **Total Fixed Solids (TFS)** | $\text{TFS} = \text{TS} - \text{TVS}$ | mg/L | TFS + TVS = TS |

**Symbol Legend (KTU standard notation):**

- $W_1$ = mass of empty evaporating dish (mg)
- $W_2$ = mass of dish + dried residue (unfiltered sample) (mg)
- $W_3$ = mass of empty dish (for filtrate) (mg)
- $W_4$ = mass of dish + dried filtrate residue (mg)
- $V$ = volume of sample taken (mL)
- $V_f$ = volume of filtrate evaporated (mL)
- $V_s$ = volume of sample filtered (mL)
- $W_f$ = mass of filter + dried residue (mg)
- $W_e$ = mass of empty dried filter (mg)
- $W_d$ = mass of dish + residue before ignition (mg)
- $W_a$ = mass of dish + residue after ignition (mg)

## 2.4 Engineering Significance — Where This Lab Data Goes

| Application Area | How TS/TDS Data is Used |
|------------------|-------------------------|
| **Sewage Treatment Plant Design** | Determines sludge handling capacity and digester volume |
| **Industrial Effluent Monitoring** | Compliance with CPCB/SPCB discharge norms |
| **Reverse Osmosis Plant Design** | Feed water TDS dictates membrane selection & recovery |
| **Agricultural Irrigation Suitability** | Salinity hazard (EC) and Sodium Adsorption Ratio (SAR) are functions of TDS |
| **Boiler Feedwater Quality** | TDS < 100 mg/L for high-pressure boilers (prevents scale) |
| **River Water Quality Modeling** | TDS is a key state variable in QUAL2K and MIKE models |
| **Drinking Water Treatment** | Coagulant dose optimization in conventional treatment |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the TS Mass Balance Equation

We start from the **law of conservation of mass** applied to an open evaporating dish in a drying oven.

### Step 1: Initial System Mass

Let $M_i$ be the total initial mass of the system (evaporating dish + wastewater sample). The dish has mass $M_d$ and the sample has mass $M_s$, so:

$$M_i = M_d + M_s$$

In our KTU notation, $M_d = W_1$ and $M_s = \rho \cdot V$ (where $\rho$ is the sample density in g/mL).

### Step 2: Post-Evaporation System Mass

After heating in the oven at 103–105 °C to **constant weight** (defined as a mass change of < 0.5 mg between successive weighings separated by 1 hour of additional drying), the system mass is:

$$M_f = M_d + M_r$$

where $M_r$ is the mass of the **non-volatile residue** — the material that did not escape as vapor. By our notation, $M_f = W_2$.

### Step 3: Mass of Volatile Fraction (Water Lost)

The water and other volatile compounds that evaporated are:

$$M_v = M_i - M_f = (M_d + M_s) - (M_d + M_r) = M_s - M_r$$

### Step 4: Mass of Solids Recovered

The non-volatile residue mass — which **is** the total solids we are measuring — is:

$$M_r = M_f - M_d = W_2 - W_1$$

### Step 5: Conversion to Concentration (mg/L)

Standard environmental engineering reporting requires concentration in **mg of solute per liter of solution**. Since $V$ mL of sample contains $W_2 - W_1$ mg of solids:

$$\text{TS (mg/L)} = \frac{\text{Mass of Solids (mg)}}{\text{Volume of Sample (L)}} = \frac{(W_2 - W_1) \text{ mg}}{V \text{ mL}} \times \frac{1000 \text{ mL}}{1 \text{ L}}$$

This yields our **master TS equation**:

$$\boxed{\text{TS (mg/L)} = \frac{(W_2 - W_1) \times 1000}{V}}$$

### Step 6: The TDS Derivation (Identical Logic, Different Sample)

For TDS, we repeat the exact same procedure on a **filtrate** that has been passed through a 1.5 µm glass-fiber filter. The mass balance is unchanged:

$$\boxed{\text{TDS (mg/L)} = \frac{(W_4 - W_3) \times 1000}{V_f}}$$

### Step 7: TSS from the Mass Balance

From the fundamental solids mass balance:

$$\text{TS} = \text{TDS} + \text{TSS}$$

Solving for TSS:

$$\boxed{\text{TSS (mg/L)} = \text{TS} - \text{TDS}}$$

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Worked Numerical Example (KTU Board Pattern)

> [!NOTE]
> **The following worked example mirrors the exact style, data presentation, and mark scheme of a KTU Part B 14-mark question. Practice writing this out in your record book.**

**Problem Statement:**
A wastewater sample is analyzed for total solids. A clean evaporating dish has a mass of **47.8326 g** when empty. A **50 mL** aliquot of well-mixed sample is pipetted into the dish and evaporated to dryness at 103 °C. The dish with residue weighs **47.8612 g**. A second **50 mL** portion of the same sample is filtered through a 1.5 µm glass-fiber filter, and **40 mL** of clear filtrate is evaporated in a pre-weighed dish (mass = **52.1044 g**). After drying and cooling, this dish weighs **52.1226 g**. Calculate **TS, TDS, and TSS** in mg/L.

### Step 1: Identify the Given Data

$$W_1 = 47.8326 \text{ g}, \quad W_2 = 47.8612 \text{ g}, \quad V = 50 \text{ mL}$$

$$W_3 = 52.1044 \text{ g}, \quad W_4 = 52.1226 \text{ g}, \quad V_f = 40 \text{ mL}$$

### Step 2: Calculate Mass of Total Solids Residue

$$\Delta W_{\text{TS}} = W_2 - W_1 = 47.8612 - 47.8326 = 0.0286 \text{ g}$$

Converting to mg (since 1 g = 1000 mg):

$$\Delta W_{\text{TS}} = 0.0286 \times 1000 = 28.6 \text{ mg}$$

### Step 3: Apply the TS Formula

$$\text{TS (mg/L)} = \frac{\Delta W_{\text{TS}} \times 1000}{V} = \frac{28.6 \times 1000}{50}$$

$$\text{TS} = \frac{28600}{50} = 572 \text{ mg/L}$$

### Step 4: Calculate Mass of TDS Residue

$$\Delta W_{\text{TDS}} = W_4 - W_3 = 52.1226 - 52.1044 = 0.0182 \text{ g} = 18.2 \text{ mg}$$

### Step 5: Apply the TDS Formula

$$\text{TDS (mg/L)} = \frac{\Delta W_{\text{TDS}} \times 1000}{V_f} = \frac{18.2 \times 1000}{40}$$

$$\text{TDS} = \frac{18200}{40} = 455 \text{ mg/L}$$

### Step 6: Calculate TSS by Mass Balance

$$\text{TSS} = \text{TS} - \text{TDS} = 572 - 455 = 117 \text{ mg/L}$$

### Step 7: Result Summary Table (Use This Format in Your Record)

| Parameter | Value | Unit |
|-----------|-------|------|
| Total Solids (TS) | **572** | mg/L |
| Total Dissolved Solids (TDS) | **455** | mg/L |
| Total Suspended Solids (TSS) | **117** | mg/L |
| % Dissolved Fraction | $\dfrac{455}{572} \times 100 = 79.5\%$ | % |
| % Suspended Fraction | $\dfrac{117}{572} \times 100 = 20.5\%$ | % |

### Step 8: Environmental Interpretation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A TDS of **455 mg/L** is **below the BIS acceptable limit of 500 mg/L** but indicates a moderately mineralized wastewater typical of **domestic sewage mixed with mild industrial discharge**. The TSS of **117 mg/L** is high enough to require **primary sedimentation** before biological treatment (typical raw sewage TSS is 100–350 mg/L).

## 3.3 Derivation of Percent Volatile Solids (% VS)

> [!NOTE]
> This is a **bonus derivation** that is often asked as a 3-mark Part A question.

After determining TS, the dish with residue is ignited in a **muffle furnace at 550 ± 50 °C for 15–20 minutes**. The organic fraction oxidizes to CO₂ and H₂O and escapes. Let:

- $W_d$ = mass of dish + residue **before** ignition (mg)
- $W_a$ = mass of dish + residue **after** ignition (mg)

The **loss on ignition** is the volatile (organic) fraction:

$$\text{Loss on Ignition} = W_d - W_a \text{ (mg)}$$

The **percent volatile solids** is:

$$\%\text{VS} = \frac{(W_d - W_a)}{(W_d - W_1)} \times 100$$

This dimensionless ratio expresses the **organic content** of the total solids, critical for designing **anaerobic digesters** in wastewater treatment plants.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Solids Classification Hierarchy

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Wastewater Sample] --> B[Total Solids TS]
    B --> C[Filter through 1.5 um Glass Fiber Filter]
    C --> D[Filtrate]
    C --> E[Residue on Filter]
    D --> F[Evaporate at 103 to 105 C]
    F --> G[Total Dissolved Solids TDS]
    E --> H[Dry at 103 to 105 C]
    H --> I[Total Suspended Solids TSS]
    B --> J[Optional Ignition at 550 C]
    J --> K[Volatile Solids VS Organic Fraction]
    J --> L[Fixed Solids FS Inorganic Fraction]
    G --> M[TDS Fraction Inorganic Salts and Dissolved Organics]
    M --> N[Sodium Calcium Magnesium]
    M --> O[Chloride Sulfate Bicarbonate]
    I --> P[TSS Fraction Settleable and Colloidal]
    P --> Q[Remove by Primary Sedimentation]
    P --> R[Remove by Coagulation Flocculation]
    style A fill:#e1f5ff,stroke:#0066cc
    style B fill:#fff4e1,stroke:#cc6600
    style G fill:#e8f5e9,stroke:#2e7d32
    style I fill:#fce4ec,stroke:#c2185b
    style K fill:#f3e5f5,stroke:#6a1b9a
    style L fill:#f3e5f5,stroke:#6a1b9a
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Flowchart — Standard Laboratory Procedure

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Step 1: Clean Evaporating Dish] --> S2[Step 2: Dry in Oven at 103 C and Cool in Desiccator]
    S2 --> S3[Step 3: Weigh Empty Dish to Constant Mass W1]
    S3 --> S4[Step 4: Pipette 25 to 50 mL Well Mixed Sample]
    S4 --> S5[Step 5: Evaporate on Steam Bath to Avoid Splashing]
    S5 --> S6[Step 6: Transfer to Oven at 103 to 105 C]
    S6 --> S7[Step 7: Dry to Constant Weight Approximately 1 to 2 Hours]
    S7 --> S8[Step 8: Cool in Desiccator for 30 Minutes]
    S8 --> S9[Step 9: Weigh Dish plus Residue W2]
    S9 --> S10[Step 10: Calculate TS equals W2 minus W1 times 1000 divided by V]
    S10 --> P1[Parallel Branch: TDS Determination]
    S10 --> P2[Parallel Branch: TSS by Difference]
    P1 --> F1[Step A1: Filter Separate 50 mL Sample]
    F1 --> F2[Step A2: Collect 25 to 40 mL Clear Filtrate]
    F2 --> F3[Step A3: Evaporate in Pre Weighed Dish W3]
    F3 --> F4[Step A4: Dry Cool Weigh W4]
    F4 --> F5[Step A5: Calculate TDS equals W4 minus W3 times 1000 divided by Vf]
    P2 --> T1[Step B1: TSS equals TS minus TDS]
    style S1 fill:#bbdefb
    style S10 fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20
    style F5 fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20
    style T1 fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram — Equipment & Glassware Setup

```mermaid

        A1[Beaker with Wastewater]

    end
    subgraph B[Filtration Assembly]

        B2[Whatman GF C Filter 1.5 um]

    end
    subgraph C[Evaporation Station]
        C1[Steam Bath]
        C2[Hot Air Oven 103 to 105 C]
        C3[Evaporating Dish Porcelain]

    end
    subgraph D[Weighing Station]
        D1[Analytical Balance 0.1 mg Sensitivity]
        D2[Weighing Boat or Dish Holder]
    end
    A1 --> A2
    A2 --> A3
    A3 --> B1
    B1 --> B2
    B2 --> B3
    B3 --> B4

    C1 --> C2
    C2 --> C3
    C3 --> C4
    C4 --> D1
    D1 --> D2
    style A fill:#fff9c4
    style B fill:#c5e1a5

```

## 4.4 Mermaid Decision Tree — Error Diagnosis in the Lab

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1[Did you get reproducible weights within 0.5 mg?] -->|No| Q2[Did you dry to constant weight?]
    Q2 -->|No| Q3[Return sample to oven for 1 more hour]
    Q2 -->|Yes| Q4[Was the desiccator sealed properly?]
    Q4 -->|No| Q5[Re weigh after 30 min in desiccator]
    Q4 -->|Yes| Q6[Check analytical balance calibration]
    Q1 -->|Yes| Q7[Is the calculated TS reasonable? 100 to 1000 mg/L for sewage]
    Q7 -->|No| Q8[Did you use the correct V?]
    Q8 -->|No| Q9[Recalculate with correct sample volume]
    Q8 -->|Yes| Q10[Is the sample well mixed? Settling may have occurred]
    Q10 --> Q11[Re sample with proper agitation]
    Q7 -->|Yes| Q12[Result is valid. Report with correct significant figures]
    style Q12 fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20
    style Q3 fill:#fff9c4
    style Q5 fill:#fff9c4
    style Q6 fill:#fff9c4
    style Q9 fill:#fff9c4
    style Q11 fill:#fff9c4
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📘 PART A — SHORT ANSWER QUESTIONS (2 × 3 = 6 Marks)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam – May 2023, Model Paper]`
