**Define Total Solids (TS), Total Dissolved Solids (TDS), and Total Suspended Solids (TSS). State the standard drying temperature for TS estimation and explain why this temperature is chosen.**

#### Model Answer (3 marks):

> **TS (Total Solids):** The residue remaining after a known volume of an unfiltered wastewater sample is evaporated and dried to constant weight at **103–105 °C**. It represents the sum of all dissolved and suspended matter in the sample. *(1 mark)*

> **TDS (Total Dissolved Solids):** The residue remaining after a filtered sample (passed through a 1.5 µm glass-fiber filter) is evaporated to dryness at 103–105 °C. It comprises all substances in molecular or ionic solution that pass through the filter. *(1 mark)*

> **TSS (Total Suspended Solids):** The portion of total solids retained on a 1.5 µm glass-fiber filter after filtration, dried to constant weight at 103–105 °C. Mathematically, **TSS = TS − TDS**. *(0.5 marks)*

> **Reason for 103–105 °C:** This temperature is high enough to drive off mechanically occluded water and water of crystallization but low enough to minimize the loss of volatile organic compounds and the thermal decomposition of bicarbonates (CaCO₃ is stable below 105 °C). *(0.5 marks)*

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]`

**Differentiate between Total Volatile Solids (TVS) and Total Fixed Solids (TFS). What is the practical significance of determining volatile solids in wastewater treatment?**

#### Model Answer (3 marks):

> **Total Volatile Solids (TVS):** The weight loss when the dried TS/TDS/TSS residue is ignited at **550 ± 50 °C** for a fixed time (15–20 min) in a muffle furnace. TVS represents the **organic (combustible) fraction** of the solids. *(1 mark)*

> **Total Fixed Solids (TFS):** The residue remaining after ignition at 550 ± 50 °C; represents the **inorganic (non-combustible) fraction**, primarily mineral salts and oxides. *(1 mark)*

> **Practical Significance:** TVS is a proxy for the **organic loading** of wastewater and is used to: (a) estimate the **biodegradable fraction** of sludge; (b) design **anaerobic digesters** — TVS determines digester volume and methane yield; (c) assess **biological treatability** of industrial effluents; (d) calculate **F/M ratio** (Food-to-Microorganism) in activated sludge systems. *(1 mark)*

---

---

## 📗 PART B — LONG ANSWER QUESTIONS (Internal Choice, 1 × 14 = 14 Marks)

---

### **Question A** `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Module 2]`

**a)** Describe with a neat block diagram, the experimental procedure for the estimation of **Total Solids and Total Dissolved Solids** in a wastewater sample. List the apparatus and chemicals required. State at least **four** significant precautions. *(7 marks)*

**b)** In an experiment, an evaporating dish has an empty weight of **52.7418 g**. A **25 mL** aliquot of well-mixed wastewater is added and evaporated to dryness. After cooling in a desiccator, the dish with residue weighs **52.7765 g**. A second **100 mL** sample is filtered, and **50 mL** of the clear filtrate is evaporated in a pre-weighed dish (empty mass = **48.2310 g**). The final mass is **48.2628 g**. Calculate **TS, TDS, and TSS** in mg/L, and comment on the suitability of this water for **agricultural irrigation** as per the **FAO irrigation water guidelines**. *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution:

**Part (a) — Procedure, Apparatus & Precautions (7 marks)**

**Principle (1 mark):**
Total Solids are determined gravimetrically. A known volume of well-mixed, unfiltered wastewater is evaporated in a pre-weighed porcelain evaporating dish and dried to constant weight in a hot air oven at 103–105 °C. The increase in weight of the dish gives the mass of total solids. For TDS, the procedure is repeated using the **filtrate** obtained by passing the wastewater through a 1.5 µm glass-fiber filter.

**Apparatus & Chemicals Required (1.5 marks):**

| Item | Specification |
|------|--------------|
| Hot air oven | Thermostatically controlled, 103–105 °C |
| Analytical balance | Sensitivity 0.1 mg |
| Porcelain evaporating dishes | 100 mL capacity |
| Desiccator | With silica gel / CaCl₂ desiccant |
| Glass-fiber filters | Whatman Grade 934-AH, 1.5 µm pore size |
| Vacuum filtration assembly | Funnel, flask, pump |
| Volumetric pipettes | 25 mL, 50 mL (Class A) |
| Steam bath / water bath | For initial evaporation |
| Wastewater sample | Well-mixed, representative |
| Distilled water | For rinsing |

**Procedure for TS (1.5 marks):**
1. Clean and ignite the evaporating dish at 550 °C for 1 hour to remove organic residue. Cool in desiccator.
2. Weigh the empty dish to constant weight: **W₁** (record to 0.0001 g).
3. Pipette **25–50 mL** of well-mixed sample into the dish.
4. Evaporate to dryness on a **steam bath** (to prevent splattering and crust formation).
5. Transfer the dish to a hot air oven pre-heated to **103–105 °C**.
6. Dry for at least **1 hour**, cool in desiccator for 30 min, and weigh.
7. Repeat drying-cooling-weighing cycle until **constant weight** is obtained (successive weighings differ by < 0.5 mg). Record as **W₂**.

**Procedure for TDS (1 mark):**
1. Take a separate 50–100 mL portion of well-mixed sample.
2. Filter through a pre-rinsed **1.5 µm glass-fiber filter** under vacuum.
3. Pipette **25–50 mL** of clear filtrate into a pre-weighed evaporating dish (**W₃**).
4. Repeat the evaporation and drying procedure as for TS. Record final weight as **W₄**.

**Precautions (2 marks):**
1. **Drying to constant weight** is essential — incomplete drying causes underestimation, over-drying can volatilize organic matter and cause underestimation.
2. The **desiccator** must be tightly closed during cooling; exposure to humid air will cause moisture pickup and **positive error**.
3. The sample must be **well-mixed (homogenized)** immediately before pipetting, as suspended solids settle rapidly in quiescent wastewater, leading to non-representative sub-sampling.
4. **Evaporate on a steam bath first** before transferring to the oven to prevent bumping, splashing, and crust formation, all of which cause material loss.
5. Use **Class A glassware** and **rinse pipettes** with the sample before final delivery to avoid dilution error.
6. Do not ignite the dish for TS measurement at 550 °C — this is reserved for the **optional Volatile Solids** determination.
7. Handle the dish with **tongs or tissue paper** during weighing — fingerprints add measurable mass.

---

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 marks)**

**Given Data:**

$$W_1 = 52.7418 \text{ g}, \quad W_2 = 52.7765 \text{ g}, \quad V = 25 \text{ mL}$$

$$W_3 = 48.2310 \text{ g}, \quad W_4 = 48.2628 \text{ g}, \quad V_f = 50 \text{ mL}$$

**Step 1: Mass of TS Residue** *(0.5 marks)*

$$\Delta W_{\text{TS}} = W_2 - W_1 = 52.7765 - 52.7418 = 0.0347 \text{ g} = 34.7 \text{ mg}$$

**Step 2: TS Concentration** *(1 mark)*

$$\text{TS} = \frac{34.7 \times 1000}{25} = \frac{34700}{25} = 1388 \text{ mg/L}$$

**Step 3: Mass of TDS Residue** *(0.5 marks)*

$$\Delta W_{\text{TDS}} = W_4 - W_3 = 48.2628 - 48.2310 = 0.0318 \text{ g} = 31.8 \text{ mg}$$

**Step 4: TDS Concentration** *(1 mark)*

$$\text{TDS} = \frac{31.8 \times 1000}{50} = \frac{31800}{50} = 636 \text{ mg/L}$$

**Step 5: TSS by Mass Balance** *(0.5 marks)*

$$\text{TSS} = \text{TS} - \text{TDS} = 1388 - 636 = 752 \text{ mg/L}$$

**Step 6: Final Tabulated Results** *(1 mark)*

| Parameter | Value | Unit |
|-----------|-------|------|
| Total Solids (TS) | **1388** | mg/L |
| Total Dissolved Solids (TDS) | **636** | mg/L |
| Total Suspended Solids (TSS) | **752** | mg/L |
| % Dissolved | 45.8% | % |
| % Suspended | 54.2% | % |

**Step 7: Suitability for Agricultural Irrigation** *(2.5 marks)*

> According to the **FAO Irrigation Water Quality Guidelines (Ayers & Westcot, 1985)**:
>
> | Parameter | TDS (mg/L) | Restriction on Use |
> |-----------|-----------|---------------------|
> | No restriction | < 450 | Can be used on most crops |
> | Slight to moderate restriction | 450 – 2000 | Careful management needed |
> | Severe restriction | > 2000 | Generally unsuitable |

Since the TDS of **636 mg/L** falls in the **450–2000 mg/L** range, this water has a **slight to moderate restriction on use** for irrigation. Specific concerns:

- **Salinity hazard:** Electrical conductivity (EC) can be approximated as **EC (dS/m) ≈ TDS (mg/L) / 640 ≈ 0.99 dS/m** — this is on the higher side for salt-sensitive crops like beans, carrots, and onions.
- **Suitability:** Suitable for **tolerant crops** (cotton, sugarcane, barley, wheat) with adequate drainage.
- **TSS of 752 mg/L is high** — suspended solids would clog drip irrigation emitters and furrows; **sedimentation pre-treatment** is essential.
- **Recommendation:** This water requires **primary sedimentation** to reduce TSS below 50 mg/L before drip irrigation; **salinity monitoring** of soil EC over multiple crop cycles is advised.

---

### **Question B** `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Module 2]`

**a)** What are the **sources of error** in the gravimetric determination of total solids? Discuss how each error affects the result and the **corrective measures** to be taken. *(7 marks)*

**b)** A textile industry effluent is being assessed for compliance with the **CPCB general discharge standards (TDS ≤ 2100 mg/L for discharge into inland surface water)**. A laboratory analysis gives the following data: Mass of empty dish = **63.2542 g**; mass of dish + dried residue (from 20 mL unfiltered sample) = **63.2846 g**; mass of empty dish for filtrate = **58.9017 g**; mass of dish + dried filtrate (from 25 mL filtrate obtained by filtering 100 mL sample) = **58.9293 g**. Calculate TDS and determine whether the effluent **complies with CPCB norms**. What additional treatment do you recommend? *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution:

**Part (a) — Sources of Error and Corrective Measures (7 marks)**

| # | Source of Error | Effect on Result | Corrective Measure |
|---|----------------|------------------|---------------------|
| 1 | **Incomplete drying** (sample removed from oven before constant weight is reached) | **Underestimation** of TS (residual moisture adds to dish mass) | Dry, cool, and weigh repeatedly until two successive weighings agree within **0.5 mg** |
| 2 | **Over-drying / thermal decomposition** (bicarbonate decomposition, loss of volatile organics above 105 °C) | **Underestimation** of TS | Strictly maintain **103–105 °C**; do not exceed 105 °C |
| 3 | **Sample not well-mixed** before pipetting (settled suspended solids) | **Under- or over-estimation** depending on which layer is pipetted | Homogenize sample thoroughly with a **magnetic stirrer** at 500 rpm; pipette while stirring |
| 4 | **Moisture absorption** during cooling in a leaky or newly opened desiccator | **Overestimation** (hygroscopic salts like CaCl₂ absorb atmospheric moisture) | Use a **freshly regenerated desiccator** with active silica gel; cool for exactly 30 min; weigh rapidly after removal |
| 5 | **Loss of residue by splattering** during evaporation on direct flame or improperly controlled oven | **Underestimation** of TS | Evaporate on a **steam bath first** to remove bulk water; only then transfer to oven |
| 6 | **Dirty or scratched dish surface** holding residual contaminants | Variable error — both positive and negative | Use **chemically clean, dedicated porcelain dishes**; ignite at 550 °C before use |
| 7 | **Improper filter pore size** (>1.5 µm allows fine particles to pass; smaller pores clog rapidly) | Affects TSS directly, but propagates error into TDS via mass balance | Use standardized **Whatman Grade 934-AH** or equivalent glass-fiber filters |
| 8 | **Balance drift / uncalibrated balance** | Random error | **Calibrate the analytical balance** with a standard 100 mg or 200 mg check weight before use |
| 9 | **Insufficient filtration volume** leading to filter cake clogging and incomplete separation | Underestimation of TDS, overestimation of TSS | Filter an **adequate volume**; if filtrate is not crystal clear, refilter or use a larger filter area |
| 10 | **Volatilization of ammonium salts and low-MW organics** during drying | Slight underestimation | Note as a known limitation; report TS as "TS at 103–105 °C" |

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 marks)**

**Given Data:**

$$W_1 = 63.2542 \text{ g}, \quad W_2 = 63.2846 \text{ g}, \quad V = 20 \text{ mL}$$

$$W_3 = 58.9017 \text{ g}, \quad W_4 = 58.9293 \text{ g}, \quad V_f = 25 \text{ mL}$$

**Step 1: TS Calculation** *(1.5 marks)*

$$\Delta W_{\text{TS}} = 63.2846 - 63.2542 = 0.0304 \text{ g} = 30.4 \text{ mg}$$

$$\text{TS} = \frac{30.4 \times 1000}{20} = 1520 \text{ mg/L}$$

**Step 2: TDS Calculation** *(2 marks)*

$$\Delta W_{\text{TDS}} = 58.9293 - 58.9017 = 0.0276 \text{ g} = 27.6 \text{ mg}$$

$$\text{TDS} = \frac{27.6 \times 1000}{25} = 1104 \text{ mg/L}$$

**Step 3: TSS by Mass Balance** *(1 mark)*

$$\text{TSS} = 1520 - 1104 = 416 \text{ mg/L}$$

**Step 4: Tabulated Results** *(0.5 marks)*

| Parameter | Value | Unit |
|-----------|-------|------|
| Total Solids (TS) | 1520 | mg/L |
| Total Dissolved Solids (TDS) | **1104** | mg/L |
| Total Suspended Solids (TSS) | 416 | mg/L |

**Step 5: Compliance Check with CPCB Norms** *(1 mark)*

> **CPCB General Discharge Standard (Schedule VI, The Environment (Protection) Rules, 1986, amended 2017):** **TDS ≤ 2100 mg/L** for discharge of industrial effluent into **inland surface waters**.

Calculated TDS = **1104 mg/L** < 2100 mg/L → **The effluent COMPLIES with the CPCB TDS standard** for discharge into inland surface water. ✅

**Step 6: Recommended Treatment** *(1 mark)*

Despite compliance, the effluent has **moderate TDS and high TSS**, and textile effluent typically contains **high color, BOD, and residual dyes**. Recommended treatment train:

1. **Equalization tank** — to homogenize flow and load variations
2. **Primary settling / coagulation-flocculation** — to remove TSS (reduce from 416 to < 100 mg/L)
3. **Biological treatment** — activated sludge process or UASB for BOD reduction
4. **Tertiary treatment** — activated carbon adsorption for color removal; **reverse osmosis (RO)** if TDS is to be reduced further for reuse or zero-liquid-discharge (ZLD)
5. **Sludge handling** — dewatering in filter press; disposal in secured landfill

---

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Top 5 places KTU students LOSE MARKS on this experiment — read carefully:**
>
> 1. **Unit Mismatch:** Writing "TS = 0.0304 g" instead of "TS = 1520 mg/L". Always report in **mg/L**, not grams. Loss: **1–2 marks**.
> 2. **Forgetting the ×1000 factor:** When the sample volume is in mL and the final answer is in mg/L, the conversion factor **1000 mL/L must appear explicitly** in the formula. Loss: **1 mark**.
> 3. **Using total sample volume for TDS calculation:** If you filtered 100 mL and evaporated only 50 mL of filtrate, use **V_f = 50 mL**, NOT 100 mL. This is the most common error. Loss: **2 marks**.
> 4. **Not stating the drying temperature (103–105 °C):** Any answer missing the temperature loses the definition's "completeness" marks. Loss: **0.5–1 mark**.
> 5. **Ignoring significant figures:** With an analytical balance (0.1 mg = 0.0001 g), your readings have **4 decimal places**. Report final answers to **at most 4 significant figures**. Loss: **0.5 mark**.
> 6. **Confusing TDS with Electrical Conductivity (EC):** EC is measured in µS/cm or dS/m; TDS is in mg/L. They are related but **not identical**. EC × (0.55–0.70) ≈ TDS (mg/L). Loss: **1 mark** in interpretation questions.

---

## 🔁 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **Last-minute revision — bookmark this section!**

- ✅ **TS = TDS + TSS** (master mass balance — never forget)
- ✅ **TS formula:** $(W_2 - W_1) \times 1000 / V$, where V is in **mL**, answer in **mg/L**
- ✅ **TDS formula:** $(W_4 - W_3) \times 1000 / V_f$, where $V_f$ is **volume of filtrate evaporated**, not the volume filtered
- ✅ **Drying temperature:** **103–105 °C** (always quote this range)
- ✅ **Filter specification:** Glass-fiber filter, **1.5 µm pore size**, Whatman Grade **934-AH** or equivalent
- ✅ **Constant weight:** Two successive weighings agree within **0.5 mg** after 1 hour of additional drying
- ✅ **Cool in desiccator** for **30 minutes** before weighing (no shorter, no longer)
- ✅ **Steam bath first**, then oven — prevents splattering and bumping
- ✅ **Sample must be well-mixed** (homogenized) before sub-sampling
- ✅ **BIS drinking water limit for TDS:** 500 mg/L (acceptable), 2000 mg/L (permissible in absence of alternative)
- ✅ **CPCB discharge limit for TDS (textile/inland surface water):** 2100 mg/L
- ✅ **CPCB discharge limit for TSS:** 100 mg/L (inland surface water)
- ✅ **Ignition at 550 ± 50 °C** is for **Volatile Solids** determination — do NOT ignite the TS dish unless asked for VS
- ✅ **Bicarbonate decomposition** is a known positive error at higher temperatures
- ✅ **Hygroscopic salts** (e.g., CaCl₂) absorb moisture — keep desiccator sealed
- ✅ **TSS calculation method:** Always prefer **mass balance (TS − TDS)** unless direct filtration weighing is specifically required
- ✅ **Percent volatile solids (%VS)** indicates **organic fraction**; used for digester design
- ✅ **Volumetric pipette of Class A grade** must be used for accurate volume measurement
- ✅ **Glassware must be rinsed with the sample** before final delivery (no dilution by residual distilled water in the pipette)
- ✅ **Environmental significance:** High TDS = brackish water (corrosion, scaling, salt stress to plants); High TSS = BOD load, sludge volume, turbidity, light blockage in rivers
- ✅ **Industrial effluents** typically have TDS in 1000–5000 mg/L range; **domestic sewage** is usually 250–850 mg/L
- ✅ **Reject values** in duplicate analyses should not differ by more than **5%** for TS, **10%** for TDS (KTU precision standard)

> **END OF MODULE EXPERIMENT 2 NOTES — PCCHL307**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Determination of BOD of Wastewater Sample
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCHL307 - CHEMICAL TECHNOLOGY AND ENVIRONMENTAL ENGINEERING LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Determination of BOD of Wastewater Sample

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Biochemical Oxygen Demand (BOD)** is defined as the amount of dissolved oxygen (DO) required by aerobic microorganisms to biologically oxidize the organic matter present in a water or wastewater sample under controlled conditions of temperature (20 °C) and time (5 days), expressed in milligrams of O₂ consumed per litre of sample (mg/L).

The standard notation used universally is **BOD₅ (20 °C, 5 days)**, which represents the 5-day carbonaceous biochemical oxygen demand measured at an incubation temperature of **20 °C**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Related Parameter:** BOD₅ measures only the **carbonaceous (first-stage)** oxygen demand. The nitrogenous oxygen demand (NOD) exerted by nitrifying bacteria usually begins only after 6–8 days, hence the 5-day standard.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Bacteria Buffet"

Imagine a sealed fish tank filled with murky pond water. Inside, invisible aerobic bacteria are like millions of hungry diners sitting at a long buffet table — the buffet is the dissolved organic waste (food scraps, sewage particles, sugars, proteins). To digest this food, the bacteria need **oxygen** (just as humans need oxygen to burn calories). As long as there is food, the bacteria keep eating, and the dissolved oxygen in the tank keeps dropping.

**BOD simply measures "how much food was on the buffet"** by measuring "how much oxygen the diners consumed over a fixed dinner period (5 days at a comfortable room temperature of 20 °C)."

- **High BOD** = A lot of organic "food" in the water → bacteria eat a lot → oxygen crashes → fish suffocate. ❌ Polluted
- **Low BOD** = Very little organic food → bacteria barely eat → oxygen stays high → healthy aquatic life. ✅ Clean

### 1.3 Key Standard Metrics & Constants

| Parameter | Standard Value |
|-----------|----------------|
| Incubation temperature | **20 °C** |
| Incubation duration | **5 days** |
| Standard BOD bottle volume | **300 mL** (or 250 mL) |
| Equivalent weight of O₂ | **8 g/eq** (= 32 / 4) |
| Acceptable residual DO after 5 days | **≥ 1 mg/L, ≤ 7 mg/L** |
| Optimal dilution for BOD₅ reading | **Depletion ≥ 2 mg/L** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why 20 °C and not 25 °C or 30 °C?** The choice of 20 °C was historically adopted because natural water bodies in temperate regions (UK and Northern Europe, where BOD was first standardized in 1908 by the Royal Commission on Sewage Disposal) hover around 18–20 °C. Biological activity roughly doubles for every 10 °C rise (Q₁₀ rule), so temperature control is critical.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Principles — The "Why" Behind Each Step

The BOD test is fundamentally a **bioassay** — a living respiration experiment. It is **not a direct chemical test** like COD; rather, it quantifies only the *biodegradable* fraction of organics that living aerobic microbes can metabolize.

**The Governing Biochemistry (Heterotrophic Respiration):**

$$\text{Organic Matter} \; (\text{C}_x\text{H}_y\text{O}_z) \; + \; \text{O}_2 \xrightarrow{\text{Aerobic Bacteria}} \text{CO}_2 \; + \; \text{H}_2\text{O} \; + \; \text{New Cells} \; + \; \text{Energy}$$

The **rate-limiting reagent** is the **dissolved oxygen** available in the sealed BOD bottle. By measuring DO *before* and *after* a 5-day sealed incubation, we indirectly quantify the food consumed.

### 2.2 Critical Conditions for a Valid BOD Test

- **Sealed environment:** No oxygen can enter from the atmosphere (hence the water-sealed stopper).
- **Aerobic conditions maintained:** Sufficient initial DO must be present so the bacteria do not switch to anaerobic metabolism.
- **Nutrient buffer:** Dilution water is seeded with phosphate buffer (pH 7.2), MgSO₄, CaCl₂, and FeCl₃ to support microbial growth.
- **Saturated initial DO:** Dilution water is aerated to near saturation (~9 mg/L at 20 °C).
- **Suppress nitrification (optional):** Allylthiourea (ATU) is added to inhibit *Nitrosomonas* bacteria so that only carbonaceous BOD is measured.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet — High-Yield Equation Bank

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master these equations.** They account for ~70% of all numerical problems in KTU lab viva and university exams.

**Equation 1: Dissolved Oxygen by Winkler's Iodometric Method**

$$\text{DO} \; (\text{mg/L}) = \frac{V_{\text{titrant}} \times N_{\text{thio}} \times 8000}{V_{\text{aliquot}}}$$

- $V_{\text{titrant}}$ = Volume of 0.025 N Na₂S₂O₃ used (mL)
- $N_{\text{thio}}$ = Normality of sodium thiosulfate (eq/L)
- $8000 = 8 \times 1000$ (equivalent weight of O₂ × mg-to-µg/L conversion)
- $V_{\text{aliquot}}$ = Volume of sample taken for titration (typically 200 mL)

**Equation 2: BOD₅ with Dilution & Seed Correction (Standard APHA Form)**

$$\text{BOD}_5 \; (\text{mg/L}) = \frac{(D_1 - D_2) - (B_1 - B_2)(1 - P) - f(B_3 - B_4)}{P}$$

**Equation 3: BOD₅ without Seed Correction (Most Common Exam Form)**

$$\text{BOD}_5 \; (\text{mg/L}) = \frac{(D_1 - D_2) - (B_1 - B_2)(1 - P)}{P}$$

**Equation 4: BOD₅ without Dilution Water Correction (When B₁ = B₂)**

$$\text{BOD}_5 \; (\text{mg/L}) = \frac{D_1 - D_2}{P}$$

**Equation 5: Dilution Factor / Decimal Volumetric Fraction**

$$P = \frac{V_{\text{sample}}}{V_{\text{sample}} + V_{\text{dilution water}}}$$

**Equation 6: Stoichiometric Equivalence (Winkler Chemistry)**

$$1 \; \text{mol O}_2 \equiv 2 \; \text{mol Mn(OH)}_2 \equiv 2 \; \text{mol I}_2 \equiv 4 \; \text{mol Na}_2\text{S}_2\text{O}_3$$

| Symbol | Definition | Units |
|--------|------------|-------|
| $D_1$ | DO of diluted sample at time $t = 0$ | mg/L |
| $D_2$ | DO of diluted sample after 5 days at 20 °C | mg/L |
| $B_1$ | DO of dilution water at $t = 0$ | mg/L |
| $B_2$ | DO of dilution water after 5 days | mg/L |
| $B_3$ | DO of seed control at $t = 0$ | mg/L |
| $B_4$ | DO of seed control after 5 days | mg/L |
| $P$ | Decimal volumetric fraction of sample | dimensionless |
| $f$ | Ratio of seed in diluted sample to seed in seed control | dimensionless |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Municipal STP Design:** BOD₅ directly determines the size of aeration tanks, MLSS requirements, and F/M ratio in activated sludge plants.
- **Effluent Compliance:** Central Pollution Control Board (CPCB) norms for inland surface water discharge require BOD₅ ≤ **30 mg/L**; for coastal discharge ≤ **100 mg/L**.
- **River Assimilation Capacity:** Streeter–Phelps oxygen sag model uses BOD₅ as the primary input to predict downstream DO deficit.
- **Industrial Effluent Monitoring:** Food, dairy, distillery, and pharma industries report BOD₅ daily for regulatory audit.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Detailed Procedure — Stage-Wise Logical Breakdown

**Stage 1: Preparation of Dilution Water**

Aerated distilled water is fortified with phosphate buffer (pH 7.2), magnesium sulfate, calcium chloride, and ferric chloride to provide essential macronutrients for microbial growth.

**Stage 2: Selection of Dilution Factor (Pre-Estimation Pilot)**

The expected BOD range determines dilution. A rough screening is done by the **Permanganate Value (PV)** test:

| Expected BOD Range (mg/L) | Recommended Dilution |
|---------------------------|----------------------|
| 0 – 10 | No dilution |
| 10 – 50 | 1 : 10 |
| 50 – 200 | 1 : 50 |
| 200 – 1000 | 1 : 250 |
| 1000 – 5000 | 1 : 1000 |

**Stage 3: Filling BOD Bottles**

The dilution water and wastewater sample are carefully mixed in a graduated cylinder without introducing air bubbles (siphoning technique used). Two BOD bottles are filled for each dilution:
- Bottle **A**: DO fixed immediately → gives $D_1$
- Bottle **B**: Sealed, incubated at 20 °C for 5 days → gives $D_2$

**Stage 4: Winkler's Iodometric Method — Chemistry & Equations**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Winkler's Method (1888) — modified by Rideal–Stewart and Azide modification.** This is the reference titration procedure recommended by APHA, IS 3025 (Part 44), and KSPCB.

**Step 4a: Manganous Sulfate Addition**

$$\text{MnSO}_4 + 2\text{NaOH} \longrightarrow \text{Mn(OH)}_2 \downarrow \; (\text{white}) + \text{Na}_2\text{SO}_4$$

**Step 4b: Oxygen Fixation (Alkaline Medium)**

The white precipitate reacts with dissolved O₂ to form a brown-colored manganese oxyhydroxide:

$$2\text{Mn(OH)}_2 + \text{O}_2 \longrightarrow 2\text{MnO(OH)}_2 \downarrow \; (\text{brown})$$

**Step 4c: Acidification with Conc. H₂SO₄**

The brown precipitate dissolves in acid, liberating free iodine from potassium iodide:

$$\text{MnO(OH)}_2 + 2\text{KI} + 2\text{H}_2\text{SO}_4 \longrightarrow \text{MnSO}_4 + \text{I}_2 + \text{K}_2\text{SO}_4 + 3\text{H}_2\text{O}$$

**Step 4d: Iodometric Back-Titration with Sodium Thiosulfate**

$$\text{I}_2 + 2\text{Na}_2\text{S}_2\text{O}_3 \longrightarrow 2\text{NaI} + \text{Na}_2\text{S}_4\text{O}_6$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Stoichiometric Linking Chain:** From steps 4b → 4d, every 1 mole of O₂ ultimately liberates 4 moles of thiosulfate. Therefore, **1 mL of 1 N Na₂S₂O₃ ≡ 8 mg of O₂.**

**Step 4e: Starch Indicator Endpoint**

Starch is added near the titration endpoint, forming a deep blue starch–iodine complex. As the last traces of I₂ are reduced, the blue colour vanishes, marking the endpoint.

### 3.2 Exhaustive Numerical Derivation — Worked Example (Model KTU Problem)

> **Problem Statement (KTU Pattern):**
> A domestic wastewater sample (10 mL) is diluted to 250 mL using aerated dilution water in a standard BOD bottle. The initial DO of the diluted sample ($D_1$) is 8.2 mg/L. After 5 days of incubation at 20 °C, the DO ($D_2$) is found to be 4.0 mg/L. The dilution water has $B_1 = 8.5$ mg/L and $B_2 = 8.4$ mg/L after incubation. Calculate the BOD₅ of the wastewater sample. If a 200 mL aliquot of the incubated bottle was titrated against 0.025 N Na₂S₂O₃, find the titre volume.

**Part A: BOD₅ Calculation**

**Step 1:** Identify the dilution factor.

$$P = \frac{V_{\text{sample}}}{V_{\text{total}}} = \frac{10 \; \text{mL}}{250 \; \text{mL}} = 0.04$$

$$1 - P = 1 - 0.04 = 0.96$$

**Step 2:** Substitute into the dilution-corrected BOD equation.

$$\text{BOD}_5 = \frac{(D_1 - D_2) - (B_1 - B_2)(1 - P)}{P}$$

$$\text{BOD}_5 = \frac{(8.2 - 4.0) - (8.5 - 8.4)(0.96)}{0.04}$$

**Step 3:** Compute the numerator bracket.

$$(D_1 - D_2) = 8.2 - 4.0 = 4.2 \; \text{mg/L}$$

$$(B_1 - B_2) = 8.5 - 8.4 = 0.1 \; \text{mg/L}$$

$$(B_1 - B_2)(1 - P) = 0.1 \times 0.96 = 0.096 \; \text{mg/L}$$

**Step 4:** Final computation.

$$\text{BOD}_5 = \frac{4.2 - 0.096}{0.04} = \frac{4.104}{0.04}$$

$$\boxed{\text{BOD}_5 = 102.6 \; \text{mg/L}}$$

**Part B: Titre Volume Calculation**

**Step 1:** Recall the DO formula.

$$\text{DO} = \frac{V_{\text{titrant}} \times N \times 8000}{V_{\text{aliquot}}}$$

**Step 2:** Rearrange to solve for $V_{\text{titrant}}$.

$$V_{\text{titrant}} = \frac{\text{DO} \times V_{\text{aliquot}}}{N \times 8000}$$

**Step 3:** Substitute $D_2 = 4.0$ mg/L, $V_{\text{aliquot}} = 200$ mL, $N = 0.025$.

$$V_{\text{titrant}} = \frac{4.0 \times 200}{0.025 \times 8000} = \frac{800}{200} = 4.0 \; \text{mL}$$

$$\boxed{V_{\text{titrant}} = 4.0 \; \text{mL}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Interpretation:** A BOD₅ of 102.6 mg/L is **strong sewage** (typical domestic raw sewage is 200–400 mg/L after correction). This dilution (1 : 25) consumed 4.2 mg/L of oxygen in 5 days, well within the **2–7 mg/L depletion rule** — confirming a valid test.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Procedure Flow — BOD Determination (Block-Level Functional Flow)

The following Mermaid block renders the complete procedural pipeline from sample collection to final BOD₅ calculation, including the parallel Winkler titration step.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[StepA: Collect wastewater sample] --> B[StepB: Pre-estimate BOD via Permanganate Value test]
    B --> C[StepC: Select dilution factor from standard table]
    C --> D[StepD: Prepare aerated dilution water with phosphate buffer]
    D --> E[StepE: Mix sample and dilution water in graduated cylinder]
    E --> F[StepF: Fill two BOD bottles 300 mL stopper]
    F --> G[StepG: Bottle 1 - Fix DO immediately using MnSO4 and Alkali Iodide Azide]
    F --> H[StepH: Bottle 2 - Seal and incubate at 20 degree C for 5 days]
    G --> I[StepI: Titrate Bottle 1 against 0.025 N Na2S2O3 with starch indicator]
    H --> J[StepJ: Titrate Bottle 2 after 5 days against Na2S2O3]
    I --> K[StepK: Compute D1 mg per L from titre volume]
    J --> L[StepL: Compute D2 mg per L from titre volume]
    K --> M[StepM: Apply BOD5 equation with dilution factor P]
    L --> M
    M --> N[StepN: Report BOD5 value mg per L with validity check]
```

### 4.2 Winkler's Iodometric Chemistry — Reaction Chain Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    ReagentA[MnSO4 Solution] --> React1[Reaction 1 Manganese Hydroxide Precipitation white]
    ReagentB[NaOH and KI Alkali Iodide Azide] --> React1
    React1 --> React2[Reaction 2 Oxygen Fixation brown MnO OH 2]
    DissolvedO2[Dissolved O2 in Sample] --> React2
    React2 --> React3[Reaction 3 Acidification liberates free I2]
    ReagentC[Concentrated H2SO4] --> React3
    React3 --> React4[Reaction 4 Iodometric Titration against Na2S2O3]
    Titrant[0.025 N Sodium Thiosulfate] --> React4
    React4 --> Endpoint[Starch Indicator Pale Yellow Endpoint]
    Endpoint --> Result[Final DO mg per L Calculation]
```

### 4.3 Equipment & Glassware Schematic Layout

```mermaid
block-beta
    columns 3
    BlockA["BOD Incubation Cabinet 20 plus minus 1 degree C"] BlockB["Winkler Titration Stand with Burette"] BlockC["Magnetic Stirrer with Hot Plate"]
    BlockD["Standard 300 mL BOD Glass Stopper Bottles x 6"] BlockE["500 mL Conical Flasks for Titration"] BlockF["Pipettes 1 mL 2 mL 10 mL 25 mL"]
    BlockG["Reagent Bottles MnSO4 Alkali Iodide Azide H2SO4 Starch Thiosulfate"] BlockH["200 mL Graduated Measuring Cylinder"] BlockI["pH Meter and DO Meter for Validation"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Functional Architecture Insight:** The diagram above maps the equipment clusters into **three logical stations** — (1) Biological incubation station, (2) Wet chemistry titration station, and (3) Reagent/calibration station. In actual KTU lab exams, students are often asked to **identify and label** each station.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Question 1]**

**Q1. Define BOD₅. Why is the standard incubation temperature fixed at 20 °C?**

> [!NOTE]
> **Model Answer (3 Marks):**
> **BOD₅ (Biochemical Oxygen Demand, 5-day)** is the amount of dissolved oxygen consumed by aerobic microorganisms in decomposing the biodegradable organic matter present in a water sample, when incubated in the dark at **20 °C for 5 days**, expressed in mg/L. **(1 mark)**
> The temperature of **20 °C** was historically adopted because it approximates the average temperature of natural water bodies in temperate climates, where the BOD test was originally developed (Royal Commission on Sewage Disposal, 1908, UK). **(1 mark)**
> Biological activity is highly temperature-dependent (Q₁₀ ≈ 2 rule), so standardizing at 20 °C ensures **reproducibility and comparability** of results across laboratories and seasons worldwide. **(1 mark)**

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Question 2]**

**Q2. List the reagents used in the Winkler iodometric method for the determination of DO. State the role of each.**

> [!NOTE]
> **Model Answer (3 Marks):**
> 1. **Manganous sulfate (MnSO₄)** — Reacts with NaOH to form Mn(OH)₂ which fixes dissolved O₂ as brown MnO(OH)₂ precipitate. **(1 mark)**
> 2. **Alkali–iodide–azide reagent (NaOH + KI + NaN₃)** — Provides alkaline medium for precipitation and acts as iodide source; sodium azide eliminates nitrite interference. **(1 mark)**
> 3. **Concentrated sulfuric acid (H₂SO₄)** — Acidifies the fixed sample, dissolving the precipitate and liberating free I₂ equivalent to bound O₂. **(1 mark)**
> 4. *(Bonus reagent, if asked)* — **Starch indicator** and **0.025 N sodium thiosulfate** for back-titration.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question A, 14 Marks]**

### Question A

**(a) Explain with balanced chemical equations the principle and procedure for the determination of Dissolved Oxygen by Winkler's iodometric method.** **[7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**Principle (3 Marks):**
The Winkler method is based on the oxidation of manganous hydroxide [Mn(OH)₂] by dissolved oxygen to form brown manganic oxyhydroxide [MnO(OH)₂]. On acidification, this precipitate liberates free iodine (I₂) in an amount stoichiometrically equivalent to the original dissolved O₂. The liberated iodine is titrated against a standard reducing agent, sodium thiosulfate (Na₂S₂O₃), using starch as an indicator.

**Balanced Equations (4 Marks):**

$$\text{MnSO}_4 + 2\text{NaOH} \longrightarrow \text{Mn(OH)}_2 \downarrow \; (\text{white}) + \text{Na}_2\text{SO}_4$$

$$2\text{Mn(OH)}_2 + \text{O}_2 \longrightarrow 2\text{MnO(OH)}_2 \downarrow \; (\text{brown})$$

$$\text{MnO(OH)}_2 + 2\text{HI} \longrightarrow \text{Mn(OH)}_2 + \text{I}_2 + \text{H}_2\text{O}$$

(Equivalently expressed with H₂SO₄:)

$$\text{MnO(OH)}_2 + 2\text{KI} + 2\text{H}_2\text{SO}_4 \longrightarrow \text{MnSO}_4 + \text{I}_2 + \text{K}_2\text{SO}_4 + 3\text{H}_2\text{O}$$

$$\text{I}_2 + 2\text{Na}_2\text{S}_2\text{O}_3 \longrightarrow 2\text{NaI} + \text{Na}_2\text{S}_4\text{O}_6$$

**Stoichiometric Link:** 1 mol O₂ ≡ 2 mol I₂ ≡ 4 mol Na₂S₂O₃ → 1 mL of 1 N Na₂S₂O₃ = 8 mg O₂.

---

**(b) A wastewater sample of 15 mL is diluted to 300 mL using aerated dilution water. The DO of the diluted sample initially is 8.0 mg/L and after 5 days is 3.6 mg/L. The DO of the dilution water initially is 8.5 mg/L and after 5 days is 8.3 mg/L. Calculate the BOD₅ of the wastewater sample.** **[7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Identify dilution factor.**

$$P = \frac{V_{\text{sample}}}{V_{\text{total}}} = \frac{15}{300} = 0.05$$

$$1 - P = 0.95$$

**Step 2: List the given values.**

$$D_1 = 8.0 \; \text{mg/L}, \quad D_2 = 3.6 \; \text{mg/L}$$

$$B_1 = 8.5 \; \text{mg/L}, \quad B_2 = 8.3 \; \text{mg/L}$$

**Step 3: Substitute in the dilution-corrected BOD₅ equation.**

$$\text{BOD}_5 = \frac{(D_1 - D_2) - (B_1 - B_2)(1 - P)}{P}$$

$$\text{BOD}_5 = \frac{(8.0 - 3.6) - (8.5 - 8.3)(0.95)}{0.05}$$

$$\text{BOD}_5 = \frac{4.4 - (0.2)(0.95)}{0.05}$$

$$\text{BOD}_5 = \frac{4.4 - 0.19}{0.05} = \frac{4.21}{0.05}$$

$$\boxed{\text{BOD}_5 = 84.2 \; \text{mg/L}}$$

**Validity Check:** DO depletion = $D_1 - D_2 = 4.4$ mg/L (within 2–7 mg/L rule ✓); Residual DO = 3.6 mg/L (≥ 1 mg/L ✓).

---

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question B, 14 Marks — Alternative Choice]**

### Question B

**(a) Describe the procedure for determining BOD₅ of a domestic wastewater sample using the dilution method. Mention the validity criteria for accepting the test result.** **[7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**Apparatus Required (1 Mark):** BOD incubator (20 ± 1 °C), 300 mL BOD bottles with ground-glass stoppers, graduated cylinders, pipettes, BOD incubator, Winkler titration assembly.

**Reagents Required (1 Mark):** Phosphate buffer (pH 7.2), MgSO₄, CaCl₂, FeCl₃, MnSO₄, alkali–iodide–azide reagent, concentrated H₂SO₄, 0.025 N Na₂S₂O₃, starch indicator.

**Procedure (4 Marks):**
1. **Pre-estimate BOD** via Permanganate Value test or COD-BOD correlation to choose dilution.
2. **Prepare dilution water** in a 10 L bucket — aerate tap water for 30 minutes, add 1 mL each of phosphate buffer, MgSO₄, CaCl₂, FeCl₃ per litre.
3. **Select dilution** (e.g., 1 : 100, 1 : 50) based on expected strength; siphon mixture into two BOD bottles without air bubbles.
4. **Bottle 1** — Add 2 mL MnSO₄ + 2 mL alkali-iodide-azide, shake, then 2 mL conc. H₂SO₄. Titrate 200 mL aliquot with 0.025 N Na₂S₂O₃ → gives $D_1$.
5. **Bottle 2** — Seal with water seal, incubate at 20 °C for 5 days in dark. After 5 days, fix and titrate similarly → gives $D_2$.
6. **Compute BOD₅** using $\text{BOD}_5 = [(D_1 - D_2) - (B_1 - B_2)(1-P)] / P$.

**Validity Criteria (1 Mark):**
- Initial DO of diluted sample should be **≥ 7 mg/L** and ≤ saturation.
- Final DO after 5 days should be **≥ 1 mg/L**.
- DO depletion $(D_1 - D_2)$ should be **≥ 2 mg/L**.
- pH should remain between **6.5 – 7.5**.

---

**(b) Differentiate between BOD and COD. Explain the significance of the BOD₅/COD ratio for an industrial effluent.** **[7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**Differentiation Table (4 Marks):**

| Parameter | BOD₅ | COD |
|-----------|------|-----|
| Definition | O₂ consumed by aerobic biological oxidation in 5 days at 20 °C | O₂ consumed by strong chemical oxidant (K₂Cr₂O₇) under reflux in 2 hours |
| Oxidizing agent | Aerobic microorganisms | Potassium dichromate (acidic, reflux) |
| Time | 5 days | 2 hours |
| Determines | Biodegradable organics only | Total organics (biodegradable + non-biodegradable) |
| Test type | Bioassay (biological) | Wet chemical oxidation |
| Value | Always ≤ COD | Always ≥ BOD |

**Significance of BOD₅/COD Ratio (3 Marks):**
- If **BOD₅/COD > 0.5** → Effluent is **highly biodegradable** (e.g., domestic sewage); suitable for biological treatment (ASP, trickling filter).
- If **BOD₅/COD = 0.3 – 0.5** → Moderately biodegradable; may need pre-treatment.
- If **BOD₅/COD < 0.3** → Mostly **non-biodegradable** (e.g., textile dyes, pesticides, pharma waste); biological treatment alone is **insufficient** → needs physico-chemical methods (adsorption, Fenton, ozonation, membrane filtration).
- A ratio close to **0** indicates predominantly **inert or toxic** organics — biological treatment will fail.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting the dilution water correction term $(B_1 - B_2)(1-P)$.** If you only use $(D_1 - D_2)/P$, your BOD will be overestimated by up to 10–15%. **Cost: 2 marks deducted per occurrence.**
> 2. **Swapping $D_1$ and $D_2$.** Always remember $D_1$ = initial (time = 0) and $D_2$ = final (after 5 days). Reversing them gives a **negative BOD**.
> 3. **Not balancing the Winkler reactions.** Examiners want **balanced chemical equations** — not just one-line summaries. Practice writing them clearly with state symbols.
> 4. **Using 250 mL instead of 300 mL bottle volume** without restating the calculation. Mention the bottle volume explicitly.
> 5. **Reporting titre volume in litres instead of mL.** The 8000 factor already accounts for the unit conversion — keep $V$ in **mL**.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist**

- BOD measures **biodegradable** organic strength; COD measures **total** organic strength. **COD ≥ BOD always.**
- Standard conditions: **5 days, 20 °C, dark, sealed bottle.** Deviations invalidate the test.
- The **Winkler titration** is iodometric back-titration; **1 mL of 1 N Na₂S₂O₃ ≡ 8 mg O₂** (memorize this).
- **Equation to memorize cold:** $\text{BOD}_5 = [(D_1 - D_2) - (B_1 - B_2)(1-P)] / P$.
- The dilution factor **$P = V_{\text{sample}} / V_{\text{total}}$** is always a **decimal fraction less than 1**.
- **Validity checks:** Depletion ≥ 2 mg/L; Residual DO ≥ 1 mg/L; Initial DO ≤ saturation (~9 mg/L at 20 °C).
- **Winkler Interferences & Solutions:**
  * **Nitrite (NO₂⁻):** Suppressed by **sodium azide (NaN₃)** — Azide Modification.
  * **Ferrous iron (Fe²⁺):** Suppressed by adding **KCN** or using potassium fluoride.
  * **Suspended solids:** Settle before titration or use the **Aluminum chloride flocculation modification**.
- **Seeded BOD:** Required when the wastewater lacks sufficient microbial population (e.g., industrial effluents with toxicants). Domestic sewage usually does not need seeding.
- **Nitrification suppression:** Allylthiourea (ATU) at 0.5 mg/L blocks *Nitrosomonas* — gives **carbonaceous BOD only**.
- **BOD₅ vs BOD₇ / BOD₃ conversions:** $\text{BOD}_5 \approx 1.16 \times \text{BOD}_3$ (only for standard domestic sewage — not industrial).
- **Streeter–Phelps Equation:** Critical river water quality model uses BOD₅ as input — $D_t = (k_L / k_r) \cdot L_0 \cdot (e^{-k_r t} - e^{-k_L t}) + D_0 \cdot e^{-k_L t}$.
- **Unit consistency:** Always write **mg/L** (equivalent to **ppm** for dilute aqueous samples).
- **CPCB Discharge Norms (Memorize):** Inland surface water → BOD₅ ≤ 30 mg/L; Sewer discharge → ≤ 350 mg/L; Coastal → ≤ 100 mg/L.
- **Why BOD is a bioassay, not a direct test:** Because it depends on live microorganisms — results vary with seed quality, temperature, and incubation — precision is typically **±15–20%**, unlike COD which is **±5%**.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Determination of COD of Wastewater Sample
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCHL307 - CHEMICAL TECHNOLOGY AND ENVIRONMENTAL ENGINEERING LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Determination of COD of Wastewater Sample

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Chemical Oxygen Demand (COD)** is defined as the amount of **oxygen required (in milligrams per litre)** to chemically oxidize the entire organic matter (both biodegradable and non-biodegradable) present in a wastewater sample under controlled conditions, using a strong chemical oxidizing agent — typically **potassium dichromate (K₂Cr₂O₇)** in a strongly acidic (50% H₂SO₄) medium, with the reaction carried out under **reflux for 2 hours at 148 °C**.

COD is a **crucial pollution indicator** because it quantifies the total chemical oxidation load — including substances that are resistant to biological degradation (such as lignin, cellulose, and certain synthetic organics) — which the simpler BOD test cannot capture.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Distinction from BOD:** COD is *always greater than or equal to* BOD, because BOD only measures the *biologically oxidizable* fraction over 5 days, while COD measures *all chemically oxidizable* organics in roughly 3 hours.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

Imagine you are a doctor assessing a patient’s health. You do not just check the visible symptoms (like fever = BOD) — you also order a **complete blood test that reveals hidden infections, toxins, and internal organ stress** (the deeper, complete metabolic checkup = COD).

- **BOD** = "How much food can the bacteria in the water eat in 5 days?"
- **COD** = "How much *total fuel* is hidden in this water, even the parts the bacteria cannot touch?"

### Real-World Analogy: The Kitchen Sink Test 🧪
Think of a kitchen sink filled with greasy water:
- The **biological bacteria** can only "eat" the soft buttery fats slowly (this is BOD).
- But a **strong chemical cleaner (like dichromate)** instantly burns off *everything* — the grease, the oil, the dried chilli stains — leaving nothing behind. The amount of "burning oxygen" required to destroy it all is the **COD**.

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Standard Value |
|---|---|
| Reflux temperature | **148 °C** (boiling point of ~96% H₂SO₄ + water azeotrope) |
| Reflux duration | **2 hours** |
| Oxidizing agent | **K₂Cr₂O₇ (0.25 N or 0.0417 M)** |
| Catalyst | **Ag₂SO₄** (for straight-chain aliphatic compounds) |
| Chloride masking agent | **HgSO₄** |
| Titrant | **Ferrous Ammonium Sulfate (FAS), 0.1 N** |
| Indicator | **Ferroin (1,10-phenanthroline ferrous complex)** |
| Equivalent weight of O₂ | **8 g/eq** (since O₂ gains 4 electrons: 32 ÷ 4) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** The dichromate reflux method is the **standard method prescribed by IS: 3025 (Part 58) and APHA Standard Methods (5220-B)**, and is the method recommended in the **KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus** for the PCCHL307 laboratory.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Underlying Principle — Step-by-Step Logic

The COD test is based on the **redox reaction** where dichromate ion (Cr₂O₇²⁻), in strongly acidic solution, oxidizes almost all organic compounds to CO₂ and H₂O, while itself being reduced from **Cr(VI) → Cr(III)**.

### The Governing Oxidation–Reduction Reaction

$$\text{Cr}_2\text{O}_7^{2-} + 14\text{H}^+ + 6\text{e}^- \longrightarrow 2\text{Cr}^{3+} + 7\text{H}_2\text{O}$$

Every organic compound (generalized as CₐHᵦOᵧNδ) gets fully oxidized:

$$\text{C}_a\text{H}_b\text{O}_c\text{N}_d + n\text{Cr}_2\text{O}_7^{2-} + (8n + c)\text{H}^+ \longrightarrow a\text{CO}_2 + \frac{(b + 8n - c)}{2}\text{H}_2\text{O} + d\text{NH}_4^+ + 2n\text{Cr}^{3+}$$

### Why Dichromate? The "Why" Behind the Choice

1. **Powerful oxidizer:** Standard reduction potential **E° = +1.33 V**, strong enough to oxidize ~95–100% of organic matter.
2. **Self-indicating in excess:** Excess Cr(VI) is yellow-orange; Cr(III) is green — the colour contrast aids endpoint detection.
3. **Stable in acid:** Unlike KMnO₄, dichromate does not decompose in H₂SO₄.
4. **Quantitative and reproducible.**

### The Role of Each Reagent (The "How" Decoded)

| Reagent | Function |
|---|---|
| **K₂Cr₂O₇ (0.25 N)** | The oxidizing agent; supplies Cr(VI) to consume organic carbon. |
| **H₂SO₄ (concentrated)** | Provides H⁺ for the redox reaction; creates reflux azeotrope at 148 °C. |
| **Ag₂SO₄** | Catalyst; oxidizes straight-chain aliphatic alcohols and acids that dichromate alone cannot attack. |
| **HgSO₄** | Chelates Cl⁻ as [HgCl₄]²⁻ to prevent chloride interference (Cl⁻ would otherwise consume dichromate and falsely inflate COD). |
| **FAS (0.1 N)** | Standard titrant; reduces the *excess* unreacted dichromate after reflux. |
| **Ferroin indicator** | Redox indicator; oxidized form is pale blue, reduced form is red — sharp endpoint. |

### Endpoint Chemistry — Back-Titration Logic

Because the sample contains a *known excess* of dichromate, the amount of **organic matter** is calculated *indirectly* by measuring how much dichromate was **left over** after oxidation:

$$\text{Cr}_2\text{O}_7^{2-}_{(\text{excess})} + 6\text{Fe}^{2+} + 14\text{H}^+ \longrightarrow 2\text{Cr}^{3+} + 6\text{Fe}^{3+} + 7\text{H}_2\text{O}$$

The volume of FAS used gives, by stoichiometry, the residual dichromate — and the **difference from the blank** equals the dichromate that was actually consumed by organics.

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master this single equation — it is the heart of the entire experiment and worth 7–8 marks in any KTU viva or ESE.**

$$\boxed{\text{COD (mg/L)} = \frac{(V_b - V_s) \times N \times 8 \times 1000}{V}}$$

Where each symbol is defined strictly:

| Symbol | Meaning | Typical Unit |
|---|---|---|
| $V_b$ | Volume of FAS titrant consumed by the **blank** (no sample) | mL |
| $V_s$ | Volume of FAS titrant consumed by the **sample** | mL |
| $N$ | Normality of the FAS titrant | eq/L (typically **0.1 N**) |
| $8$ | Equivalent weight of oxygen $\left(\dfrac{32}{4}\right)$ | g/eq |
| $1000$ | Conversion factor from mL to L | mL/L |
| $V$ | Volume of the wastewater sample taken | mL (typically **20 mL**) |

### Derivation of the Factor "8"

$$\text{O}_2 + 4\text{H}^+ + 4\text{e}^- \longrightarrow 2\text{H}_2\text{O}$$

$$\text{Equivalent weight of O}_2 = \frac{\text{Molecular weight}}{\text{Valency}} = \frac{32}{4} = 8 \text{ g/eq}$$

### Supporting Formula — Calculation of FAS Normality

$$N_{\text{FAS}} = \frac{\text{Strength (g/L)}}{\text{Equivalent weight}} = \frac{\text{Strength}}{392.16}$$

(Ferrous ammonium sulfate hexahydrate, **Fe(NH₄)₂(SO₄)₂·6H₂O**, has equivalent weight **392.16 g/eq**.)

### Chloride Interference Correction (If required, for high-Cl⁻ samples)

$$\text{Corrected COD} = \text{Measured COD} - \left(\text{Cl}^- \text{ concentration in mg/L} \times \frac{8}{35.5}\right)$$

Or, more rigorously, the chloride itself consumes oxygen:

$$\text{COD due to Cl}^- = [\text{Cl}^-] \times \frac{1}{2.5} \quad \text{(approximate, in mg/L)}$$

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Field | Application of COD |
|---|---|
| **Municipal STP design** | Sizing of aeration tanks, sludge loadings, sludge digesters. |
| **Industrial effluent monitoring** | Compliance with **PCB/CPCB** discharge norms (typical limit: **< 250 mg/L** for inland surface discharge). |
| **Textile & Dye industry** | Tracks removal efficiency of dye-laden wastewater. |
| **Food & Pharma** | Quantifies strength of high-organic effluents. |
| **Toxicity assessment** | Non-biodegradable COD indicates recalcitrant pollutants needing tertiary treatment. |
| **Plant performance check** | COD removal efficiency $(\%)= \dfrac{\text{COD}_{\text{in}}-\text{COD}_{\text{out}}}{\text{COD}_{\text{in}}} \times 100$ |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Stoichiometric Derivation of the COD Formula

### Starting from the Fundamentals

Consider the back-titration reaction:

$$\text{Cr}_2\text{O}_7^{2-} + 6\text{Fe}^{2+} + 14\text{H}^+ \longrightarrow 2\text{Cr}^{3+} + 6\text{Fe}^{3+} + 7\text{H}_2\text{O}$$

Let us define:

$$\begin{aligned}
V_b &= \text{volume of FAS used for the blank titration (mL)} \\
V_s &= \text{volume of FAS used for the sample titration (mL)} \\
N   &= \text{normality of FAS solution (eq/L)} \\
V   &= \text{volume of wastewater sample (mL)}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 1:** Equivalents of FAS used in the blank titration:

$$E_b = V_b \times N \times 10^{-3} \text{ eq (converting mL to L)}$$

**Step 2:** Equivalents of FAS used in the sample titration:

$$E_s = V_s \times N \times 10^{-3} \text{ eq}$$

**Step 3:** Equivalents of dichromate that reacted with the **organic matter in the sample**:

$$E_{\text{organic}} = E_b - E_s = (V_b - V_s) \times N \times 10^{-3} \text{ eq}$$

**Step 4:** Since 1 equivalent of oxidant ≡ 1 equivalent of O₂ demand:

$$\text{Equivalents of O}_2 \text{ consumed} = (V_b - V_s) \times N \times 10^{-3}$$

**Step 5:** Mass of O₂ consumed (in grams):

$$\text{Mass of O}_2 = (V_b - V_s) \times N \times 10^{-3} \times 8 \text{ g}$$

**Step 6:** Expressing per litre of sample (V mL = V/1000 L):

$$\text{COD (mg/L)} = \frac{\text{Mass of O}_2 \times 1000}{V/1000} = \frac{(V_b - V_s) \times N \times 8 \times 1000}{V}$$

$$\boxed{\text{COD (mg/L)} = \frac{(V_b - V_s) \times N \times 8000}{V}}$$

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (Full Step-by-Step)

> [!NOTE]
> **Sample KTU Numerical Problem (3 marks typical, but here solved in full board-exam style for 14 marks)**

**Given Data from a Lab Notebook:**

- Volume of wastewater sample, $V = 20 \text{ mL}$
- Normality of FAS, $N = 0.1 \text{ N}$
- Volume of K₂Cr₂O₇ added to each flask = 10 mL of 0.25 N
- Blank reading, $V_b = 24.5 \text{ mL}$
- Sample reading, $V_s = 14.0 \text{ mL}$
- Sample taken for titration = 20 mL

**Find:** COD of the wastewater in mg/L.

### Solution

**Step 1:** Compute the difference in FAS volumes:

$$\begin{aligned}
\Delta V &= V_b - V_s \\
         &= 24.5 - 14.0 \\
         &= 10.5 \text{ mL}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2:** Apply the COD formula:

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{COD} &= \frac{(V_b - V_s) \times N \times 8000}{V} \\
           &= \frac{10.5 \times 0.1 \times 8000}{20} \\
           &= \frac{8400}{20} \\
           &= 420 \text{ mg/L}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3:** Interpretation:

Since the standard **CPCB inland discharge limit** is 250 mg/L, this wastewater is **above permissible limits** and requires treatment (typically biological treatment such as activated sludge process) before disposal.

### Alternate Numerical — With Chloride Interference

Suppose the chloride concentration in the same sample is **[Cl⁻] = 500 mg/L**.

**Step 1:** Calculate COD due to Cl⁻:

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{COD}_{\text{Cl}^-} &= [\text{Cl}^-] \times \frac{1}{2.5} \\
                        &= 500 \times 0.4 \\
                        &= 200 \text{ mg/L}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2:** Calculate true organic COD:

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{COD}_{\text{organic}} &= \text{COD}_{\text{measured}} - \text{COD}_{\text{Cl}^-} \\
                            &= 420 - 200 \\
                            &= 220 \text{ mg/L}
\end{aligned}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner’s Trick:** The 2.5 factor comes from the stoichiometry of dichromate oxidation of Cl⁻ to Cl₂. The 2.5 g of Cl⁻ consumes 1 g of O₂. Always state this derivation explicitly in your KTU answer book.

## 3.3 Analytical Decision Matrix — Common Pitfalls in Calculations

| Condition | What to do | Common Mistake |
|---|---|---|
| $V_s > V_b$ | COD may be **negative** — implies insufficient dichromate was added. Use a more dilute sample. | Reporting a negative COD without comment. |
| Sample is highly coloured | Carry out a **ferroin correction blank** (sample + indicator, no FAS). | Ignoring intrinsic sample colour at the endpoint. |
| Sample has Cl⁻ > 2000 mg/L | Add **10× more HgSO₄** in a fresh aliquot, or use the 0–1500 mg/L range sealed-tube method. | Forgetting to add HgSO₄; falsely reporting high COD. |
| Sample contains volatile organics | Reflux under a **Liebig condenser** to prevent loss. | Losing toluene/benzene — underestimating COD. |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Experimental Setup — Block Architecture Flow

The COD reflux apparatus is best represented as an **interconnected reagent-flow + thermal-control block diagram** to satisfy KTU lab-record requirements.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Wastewater Sample 20 mL] --> B[Round Bottom Reflux Flask 250 mL]
    C[HgSO4 0.4 g] --> B
    D[Ag2SO4 crystals 0.75 g] --> B
    E[K2Cr2O7 0.25 N, 10 mL] --> B
    F[H2SO4 conc. 30 mL with Ag2SO4] --> B
    B --> G[Liebig Condenser]
    G --> H[Heating Mantle 148 C]
    H --> I[Reflux 2 Hours]
    I --> J[Cool to Room Temperature]
    J --> K[Wash Condensate into Flask]
    K --> L[Add Ferroin Indicator 2 drops]
    L --> M[Titrate with FAS 0.1 N]
    M --> N{Endpoint Reached?}
    N -- No --> M
    N -- Yes Blue Green to Reddish Brown --> O[Note Volume V sample]
    P[Distilled Water Blank 20 mL] --> Q[Same Procedure as Sample]
    Q --> R[Note Volume V blank]
    O --> S[Apply COD Formula]
    R --> S
    S --> T[Final COD mg per L]
```

## 4.2 Procedural Flowchart — COD Determination Logic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Start: Collect Wastewater] --> S2{Sample Volume}
    S2 -- High COD suspected --> S3[Dilute the sample]
    S2 -- Normal range --> S4[Take 20 mL aliquot]
    S3 --> S4
    S4 --> S5[Add HgSO4 to mask Cl-]
    S5 --> S6[Add 10 mL K2Cr2O7 0.25 N]
    S6 --> S7[Add 30 mL H2SO4 with Ag2SO4]
    S7 --> S8[Connect condenser]
    S8 --> S9[Reflux 2 hours at 148 C]
    S9 --> S10[Cool and titrate with FAS]
    S10 --> S11[Use Ferroin indicator]
    S11 --> S12[Record titre value V sample]
    S12 --> S13[Run parallel blank to get V blank]
    S13 --> S14[Apply formula COD 8000 N Vb minus Vs divided by V]
    S14 --> S15[Report COD in mg per L]
    S15 --> S16[End]
```

## 4.3 Reagent Hierarchy Map

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph OX[Oxidizing System]
        O1[K2Cr2O7 0.25 N]
        O2[Cr VI to Cr III reduction]
        O3[Consumes organic carbon]
    end
    subgraph CAT[Catalytic System]
        C1[Ag2SO4 catalyst]
        C2[Aids aliphatic oxidation]
    end
    subgraph INT[Interference Suppressors]
        I1[HgSO4 chelator]
        I2[Masks Cl- to HgCl4 2-]
    end
    subgraph TIT[Back Titration System]
        T1[FAS 0.1 N titrant]
        T2[Fe 2+ reduces excess Cr2O7 2-]
        T3[Ferroin redox indicator]
    end
    O1 --> O2 --> O3
    C1 --> C2
    I1 --> I2
    T1 --> T2
    T2 --> T3
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Practical Note for the KTU Lab Record:** Always draw a **neatly labelled diagram** of the reflux assembly — including the **heating mantle, round-bottom flask, Liebig condenser, and water inlet/outlet** — in your record (3–4 marks reserved for this in the lab viva).

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model]
**Define Chemical Oxygen Demand (COD). Why is COD preferred over BOD for routine industrial effluent monitoring?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** Chemical Oxygen Demand is the amount of oxygen, expressed in mg/L, required to chemically oxidize the organic matter in water using a strong oxidizing agent (K₂Cr₂O₇) under acidic reflux conditions for 2 hours at 148 °C.

**Why preferred over BOD (2 marks):**

1. **Speed:** COD results are available in **3 hours** versus **5 days** for BOD.
2. **Comprehensive:** COD captures both **biodegradable and non-biodegradable** organics; BOD captures only the biodegradable fraction.
3. **Reproducibility:** Dichromate oxidation is **stoichiometric and reliable**, while BOD depends on microbial acclimatization.
4. **Toxicity tolerance:** BOD fails in the presence of toxic substances (which kill microbes); COD works regardless.

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model]
**What is the role of (a) Ag₂SO₄ and (b) HgSO₄ in the COD determination?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**(a) Ag₂SO₄ — Catalyst (1.5 marks):**
Silver sulphate acts as a **catalyst** that assists dichromate in oxidizing **straight-chain aliphatic alcohols and fatty acids** which are otherwise difficult to oxidize. It is dissolved in the concentrated H₂SO₄ before addition.

**(b) HgSO₄ — Chloride masking agent (1.5 marks):**
Chloride ions interfere by being oxidized to chlorine, **falsely inflating the COD reading**. HgSO₄ forms a stable, non-oxidizable complex **[HgCl₄]²⁻** with chloride, preventing it from reacting with dichromate. The standard addition is **0.4 g HgSO₄ per 20 mL sample** (sufficient for up to 2000 mg/L Cl⁻).

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer (14 Marks) — Module Internal Choice

### Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Model Paper Style]

**(a)** Explain the principle and procedure for the determination of COD of a wastewater sample by the dichromate reflux method. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** 20 mL of a wastewater sample required 16.2 mL of 0.1 N FAS for titration against excess K₂Cr₂O₇. The blank titration consumed 25.0 mL of the same FAS. Calculate the COD of the sample. Comment on whether the sample meets the CPCB discharge norm of 250 mg/L. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Principle (3 marks):**
The COD test is based on the oxidation of organic matter in the wastewater by a known excess of **potassium dichromate (K₂Cr₂O₇)** in a **50% sulphuric acid medium**, with **silver sulphate** as catalyst and **mercuric sulphate** to mask chloride interference. The reaction is carried out under **reflux for 2 hours at 148 °C**. The excess dichromate remaining after oxidation is back-titrated with **standard Ferrous Ammonium Sulfate (FAS)** using **ferroin** as an indicator.

**Reagents Required (2 marks):**
1. Standard K₂Cr₂O₇ (0.25 N, 10 mL)
2. Conc. H₂SO₄ with Ag₂SO₄ (30 mL)
3. HgSO₄ (0.4 g)
4. FAS (0.1 N) — titrant
5. Ferroin indicator (1,10-phenanthroline ferrous sulphate)

**Procedure (2 marks):**
1. Take 20 mL of wastewater in a 250 mL round-bottom flask.
2. Add 0.4 g HgSO₄, swirl to dissolve.
3. Add 10 mL of 0.25 N K₂Cr₂O₇.
4. Slowly add 30 mL of H₂SO₄–Ag₂SO₄ reagent with continuous swirling.
5. Connect to a Liebig condenser, reflux for **2 hours at 148 °C**.
6. Cool to room temperature; wash down the condenser with distilled water.
7. Add 2–3 drops of ferroin indicator.
8. Titrate the excess dichromate with **0.1 N FAS** until the colour changes from **blue-green to reddish-brown**.
9. Record the titre value as $V_s$.
10. Repeat the entire procedure with **20 mL distilled water** as the blank; record as $V_b$.

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Step 1 (1 mark):** Identify the given data:
- $V = 20 \text{ mL}$
- $N = 0.1 \text{ N}$
- $V_b = 25.0 \text{ mL}$
- $V_s = 16.2 \text{ mL}$

**Step 2 (1 mark):** Compute the difference:

$$\begin{aligned}
V_b - V_s &= 25.0 - 16.2 \\
          &= 8.8 \text{ mL}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 (2 marks):** Apply the COD formula:

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{COD} &= \frac{(V_b - V_s) \times N \times 8000}{V} \\
           &= \frac{8.8 \times 0.1 \times 8000}{20} \\
           &= \frac{7040}{20} \\
           &= 352 \text{ mg/L}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 (1 mark):** Compute percentage excess over CPCB limit:

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{\% Excess} &= \frac{352 - 250}{250} \times 100 \\
                &= 40.8 \%
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 5 (2 marks):** Conclusion and treatment recommendation:
The COD value of **352 mg/L exceeds the CPCB inland discharge norm of 250 mg/L by ~41%**. The wastewater **does not meet** the discharge standard and requires **biological treatment** (e.g., activated sludge process, which typically achieves 70–90% COD removal) followed by **tertiary polishing** (sand filtration / activated carbon) before disposal.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Internal Choice

**(a)** With the help of a labelled diagram, describe the experimental setup for COD determination. List the safety precautions to be observed during the experiment. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Differentiate between BOD and COD. A municipal wastewater sample gives a BOD of 180 mg/L and a COD of 420 mg/L. Calculate the BOD/COD ratio and comment on the biodegradability of the wastewater. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Labelled Diagram (3 marks):** A standard reflux assembly with:

```
        [Water In]
            |
            v
   ___|____________________|___
  |          |              |
  |   Liebig Condenser      |
  |__________________|______|
                     |
              (vapour path)
                     |
            _________v_________
           |                   |
           |  Round-Bottom     |
           |  Flask 250 mL     |
           |  with sample      |
           |___________________|
                     |
            _________v_________
           |  Heating Mantle   |
           |   (~148 °C)       |
           |___________________|
```

**Key components to label:** Heating mantle, round-bottom flask, sample reagents, Liebig condenser (with water inlet and outlet), tripod stand, clamp.

**Procedure summary (2 marks):** Reflux 2 h → cool → back-titrate with FAS → record volumes.

**Safety precautions (2 marks):**
1. Always add **concentrated H₂SO₄ slowly** to water (not vice versa) — the addition is exothermic and corrosive.
2. Wear **acid-resistant gloves, goggles, and a lab coat** — the H₂SO₄–K₂Cr₂O₇ mixture is highly corrosive and oxidizing.
3. Handle **HgSO₄ with care** — it is highly toxic; avoid skin contact and inhalation.
4. Work in a **fume hood** — dichromate vapours and acid mists are toxic and carcinogenic (Cr(VI) is a Group 1 carcinogen).
5. Never pipette **acid or dichromate by mouth** — use a bulb.
6. Allow the reflux flask to **cool to room temperature** before titration to prevent thermal errors.

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Comparison Table (3 marks):**

| Parameter | BOD | COD |
|---|---|---|
| Full form | Biochemical Oxygen Demand | Chemical Oxygen Demand |
| Principle | Microbial oxidation | Chemical oxidation by K₂Cr₂O₇ |
| Duration | 5 days | 2–3 hours |
| Measures | Biodegradable organics only | Total organics (bio + non-bio) |
| Magnitude | Lower | Higher (COD ≥ BOD) |
| Apparatus | BOD bottles, incubator | Reflux assembly, titration |
| Toxicity tolerance | Inhibited by toxic waste | Unaffected by toxicity |
| Correlation | BOD/COD ratio ≈ 0.5–0.8 for domestic sewage | Indicates treatment feasibility |

**Step 1 (1 mark):** Compute the BOD/COD ratio:

$$\begin{aligned}
\frac{\text{BOD}}{\text{COD}} &= \frac{180}{420} \\
                              &= 0.4286
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2 (2 marks):** Interpretation:

| Ratio | Biodegradability |
|---|---|
| **> 0.5** | **Highly biodegradable** — biological treatment is highly effective. |
| **0.3 – 0.5** | **Moderately biodegradable** — typical of municipal sewage after partial stabilization. |
| **< 0.3** | **Poorly biodegradable** — contains significant non-biodegradable / toxic matter. |

**Step 3 (1 mark):** Conclusion:
With a **BOD/COD ratio of 0.43**, the wastewater is **moderately biodegradable**. Conventional **biological treatment** (e.g., activated sludge, trickling filter) can be applied with **good expected COD removal (65–80%)**. However, advanced treatment may be needed to bring the COD down to the discharge standard of 250 mg/L.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner’s Valuation Warning & Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Zones (each costs 1–2 marks in the KTU answer book):**
>
> 1. **Unit mismatch:** Writing COD as “mg” instead of **“mg/L”** — KTU strict penalizes unit omissions.
> 2. **Forgetting the 8 / 8000 factor:** A frequent mistake is to omit the **equivalent weight of O₂**. Always write the formula with all terms and substitute numerically.
> 3. **Reversing $V_b$ and $V_s$:** The blank is always *higher* (more FAS needed) than the sample, because no organics consumed dichromate in the blank. If you get a negative COD, you have inverted them.
> 4. **Skipping the chloride interference note:** For samples from coastal or saline regions, **mention HgSO₄ and chloride masking explicitly** — KTU examiners allot 1 mark for this awareness.
> 5. **Forgetting to write the reflux time and temperature** in the procedure — that’s a 1-mark deduction in the viva.
> 6. **Not mentioning the colour change** (blue-green → reddish-brown) at the endpoint — examiners want the indicator behaviour.
> 7. **Final comment missing:** Always end your numerical answer with a **comparative statement** (e.g., “above CPCB limit” / “requires biological treatment”) — this fetches the last 1–2 marks in KTU valuation.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember ✍️

> [!TIP]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist — Pin this in your lab record!**

- **COD definition:** mg/L of O₂ required to oxidize organics using K₂Cr₂O₇ in **50% H₂SO₄**, **reflux 2 h at 148 °C**.
- **Golden formula:** $\text{COD} = \dfrac{(V_b - V_s) \times N \times 8000}{V}$
- **Why 8000?** $= 8 \text{ (eq. wt. of O}_2) \times 1000 \text{ (mL→L)}$
- **Sample volume:** 20 mL (standard)
- **Blank reading > sample reading** (always check this sanity).
- **Catalyst:** Ag₂SO₄ → for aliphatic organics.
- **Masking agent:** HgSO₄ → for Cl⁻ interference (forms [HgCl₄]²⁻).
- **Titrant:** 0.1 N FAS (Ferrous Ammonium Sulfate).
- **Indicator:** Ferroin; endpoint = **blue-green → reddish-brown**.
- **Equivalent weight of O₂:** **8 g/eq** (because O₂ gains 4 electrons: 32/4).
- **Equivalent weight of FAS:** **392.16 g/eq**.
- **COD ≥ BOD always** — never the reverse.
- **BOD/COD ratio:**
  - > 0.5 → easily biodegradable
  - 0.3–0.5 → moderately biodegradable
  - < 0.3 → poorly biodegradable (toxic/recalcitrant)
- **CPCB inland discharge norm:** **≤ 250 mg/L** COD.
- **Removal efficiency formula:** $\eta = \dfrac{\text{COD}_{\text{in}} - \text{COD}_{\text{out}}}{\text{COD}_{\text{in}}} \times 100\%$
- **Safety:** Cr(VI) is carcinogenic; work in a fume hood; add H₂SO₄ slowly.
- **Standard reference methods:** **IS 3025 (Part 58)**, **APHA 5220-B**.
- **Common equipment:** Heating mantle, Liebig condenser, 250 mL round-bottom flask, burette, pipette.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU Board Tip:** The COD experiment is one of the **highest-scoring lab experiments** in PCCHL307 because of its clear-cut formula and short procedure. Memorize the **principle, the 5 reagents with their roles, the procedure steps in order, the colour change at the endpoint, and the formula with units**. Together, these guarantee full marks in both **viva (10 marks)** and **record (15 marks)** evaluations.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Analysis of Oil and Grease in Wastewater Sample
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCHL307 - CHEMICAL TECHNOLOGY AND ENVIRONMENTAL ENGINEERING LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Analysis of Oil and Grease in Wastewater Sample

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Aligned)

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (Standard Methods 5520 B/C – APHA):** *Oil and grease* is operationally defined as any **non-polar material** (hydrocarbons, fatty acids, soaps, fats, waxes, oils, and related compounds) that is **soluble in an organic solvent of low polarity** (typically **n-hexane** or **petroleum ether**, 30 °C – 60 °C boiling range) and is **insoluble in water**.

In environmental engineering, oil and grease (O&G) is a **collective parameter**, not a single chemical species. It is a *bulk descriptor* that quantifies all extractable hydrophobic organic matter present in a wastewater sample.

## 1.2 Physical & Chemical Identity

| Property | Typical Value |
| :--- | :--- |
| Density range of oils | **0.85 – 0.95 g/cm³** (lighter than water, hence float) |
| Polarity | **Non-polar / Hydrophobic** |
| Solubility in water | **Negligible** (< 1 mg/L for most petroleum oils) |
| Solubility in n-hexane | **Freely miscible** |
| Standard method reference | **APHA Standard Methods 5520 B (Partition-Gravimetric)** |
| Indian standard | **IS 3025 (Part 39): 1989 (Reaffirmed 2014)** |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy – The "Dirty Dish" Mental Model

> [!TIP]
> **Imagine you just finished frying samosas in a kadai.** The used cooking oil in the pan will **never mix with water** no matter how hard you stir. But the moment you pour a small quantity of **kerosene or petrol (organic solvent)** into the pan, the oil instantly dissolves into it. This is exactly what we do in the lab – we use a *grease-loving solvent* (n-hexane) to "lift" oil and grease out of the water, then weigh how much was lifted. The water becomes clean, the solvent becomes dirty, and we evaporate the solvent to find out **how much oil/grease was originally in the water**.

## 1.4 Why is Oil & Grease Measured?

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Significance (KTU High-Yield Point):**
> - Forms a **floating layer** on water surfaces → blocks **oxygen transfer** from atmosphere.
> - Smothers **aquatic flora and fauna** (suffocation of fish).
> - Causes **clogging of sewers, pumps, and STP inlet screens**.
> - Interferes with **biological treatment** (forms scum in activated sludge).
> - Discharged oil films are **fire hazards** in industrial effluents.
> - **BIS discharge limit (industrial effluent):** **10 mg/L** into inland surface waters.
> - **EPA effluent guideline (Oil & Gas sector):** **29 mg/L** monthly average.

## 1.5 Major Sources of Oil & Grease in Wastewater

- **Petroleum refineries** (crude oil, lubricants, diesel).
- **Food processing industries** (dairy, edible oil, meat processing).
- **Metal working industries** (cutting oils, coolants, quenching oils).
- **Tanneries and leather industries** (animal fats).
- **Vehicle service stations** and workshops.
- **Domestic sewage** (cooking oils, soap residues).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Principle of the Experiment

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Principle:** When an immiscible, non-polar organic solvent (n-hexane) is brought into intimate contact with an acidified aqueous wastewater sample, the **non-polar oil and grease molecules preferentially partition from the polar aqueous phase into the non-polar organic phase** due to *like-dissolves-like* (polarity compatibility) and the *hydrophobic effect*. The solvent is then evaporated and the residue (oil + grease + any solvent-soluble non-volatile matter) is **weighed gravimetrically**.

## 2.2 Classification of Analytical Methods

KTU syllabus recognizes **three standard methods** under APHA 5520:

| Method | Code | Solvent Used | Typical Application | Recovery |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Partition-Gravimetric** | 5520 B | **n-Hexane** | Most common in academic labs (KTU) | Good for petroleum oils |
| **Partition-Infrared** | 5520 C | **n-Hexane** (then IR) | Industrial online monitoring | High |
| **Soxhlet Extraction** | 5520 D | **Petroleum ether / n-Hexane** | Solid samples, sludges, soils | Highest (prolonged contact) |

## 2.3 Reagent & Chemical Requirements (Step-Wise Logic)

> [!TIP]
> **Why each reagent is added – the "Why" behind every chemical step:**

- **Hydrochloric acid (1:1 HCl)** → Acidifies the sample to **pH < 2**.
  - **Why?** Converts fatty acid salts (soaps) into free fatty acids, which are then **extractable into n-hexane**. Without acidification, the *soap fraction is lost* (it stays in water as a polar salt).
  - Also **breaks oil-water emulsions** that are often stabilized at near-neutral pH.

- **n-Hexane (C₆H₁₄, BP 69 °C, non-polar)** → Primary extracting solvent.
  - **Why n-hexane and not benzene/chloroform?** Low toxicity, high volatility (easy to evaporate), excellent selectivity for hydrocarbons, and **EPA 1664** mandates n-hexane since Freon (CFC-113) was banned under the **Montreal Protocol** (ozone-depleting substance).

- **Anhydrous Sodium Sulfate (Na₂SO₄)** → Drying agent.
  - **Why?** The hexane extract carries traces of water. Water droplets cause **erroneous high weights** (false positives). Na₂SO₄ absorbs the moisture without dissolving in hexane.

- **Methyl orange indicator** → Confirms pH is below 2 (turns red).

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master Formula for O&G (mg/L):**

$$
C_{\text{O\&G}} \;=\; \frac{(W_2 - W_1) \times 1000}{V_{\text{sample}}}
$$

Where:
- $C_{\text{O\&G}}$ = Concentration of oil and grease in **mg/L**
- $W_1$ = Mass of empty evaporating dish / beaker (in **g**)
- $W_2$ = Mass of dish + dried residue after evaporation (in **g**)
- $V_{\text{sample}}$ = Volume of wastewater sample taken (in **mL**)
- The factor **1000** converts **g/mL → mg/L** (since $1 \text{ g/mL} = 10^6 \text{ mg/L} = 10^6 \text{ mg} / 10^3 \text{ L} = 1000 \text{ mg/L}$ when sample is in mL).

| Supporting Formula / Conversion | Expression | Use |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Density of water (reference) | $\rho_{\text{water}} = 1.0 \text{ g/cm}^3$ at 4 °C | Relates mL ↔ g for aqueous samples |
| Mass of residue (g) | $M_r = W_2 - W_1$ | Direct gravimetric difference |
| Per cent recovery (QC check) | $\%R = \dfrac{C_{\text{measured}}}{C_{\text{spiked}}} \times 100$ | Spike-recovery quality assurance |
| Method Detection Limit (MDL) | $0.5 \text{ mg/L}$ (typical) | Lowest confidently quantifiable value |
| Unit conversion | $1 \text{ mg/L} = 1 \text{ ppm}$ (for water) | Useful in exam numericals |
| Relative deviation | $\vert C_{\text{duplicate}_1} - C_{\text{duplicate}_2} \vert \le 10\%$ | QC acceptance criteria |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** The $V_{\text{sample}}$ in the formula is in **mL** but the final answer must be in **mg/L**. Many students forget the 1000 conversion factor and report values 1000× too small. Always cross-check by **dimensional analysis**.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Sewage Treatment Plants (STPs):** Used to monitor **primary skimming tank** efficiency and **dissolved air flotation (DAF)** performance.
- **Oil refineries:** Compliance testing against **PCB/CPCB** consent conditions.
- **Marine bilge water monitoring:** O&G < 15 mg/L is the **IMO MARPOL 73/78** discharge limit.
- **Aerospace & metal cutting industries:** Detects loss of cutting fluid in effluents.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations, Procedure & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Detailed Laboratory Procedure (KTU Exam Description Standard)

> [!NOTE]
> **Apparatus Required:** Separating funnel (2 L, with Teflon stopcock), vacuum rotary evaporator / water bath, analytical balance (readability 0.1 mg), oven (103 °C), desiccator, Whatman 1 PS phase-separation filter paper (or glass wool + Na₂SO₄ column), 250 mL beaker (pre-weighed).

### Step 1 – Sample Collection & Preservation
- Collect wastewater in a **wide-mouth glass bottle** (plastic leaches O&G from cap liners → contamination).
- Fill bottle **completely** (no headspace) – prevents oil from sticking to walls above the waterline.
- **Acidify in situ** to pH < 2 with 1:1 HCl (use methyl orange as external indicator on a drop plate – turns red).
- Store at **4 °C**; analyse within **28 days**.

### Step 2 – Sample Preparation
- Allow sample to reach **room temperature (25 ± 2 °C)**.
- If sample has visible free oil, decant or pipette carefully.
- **Measure volume** $V_{\text{sample}}$ accurately in a graduated cylinder (typical: **1000 mL**).

### Step 3 – Acidification Check
- Verify pH is still < 2 using methyl orange.
- If needed, add **2–3 drops more of 1:1 HCl**.

### Step 4 – Liquid–Liquid Extraction
- Transfer sample to a **2 L separating funnel**.
- Add **30 mL of n-hexane** (measured with a clean graduated cylinder).
