- **Shake vigorously for 2 minutes** with periodic venting (open stopcock to release pressure).
- Allow the funnel to stand on an iron ring stand for **10 minutes** to permit complete phase separation.

### Step 5 – Phase Collection & Drying
- Drain the **lower aqueous layer** (contains cleaned water) and discard.
- Collect the **upper hexane layer** containing the dissolved O&G into a **250 mL clean beaker**.
- Pass the extract through a **drying column of anhydrous Na₂SO₄** (≈ 10 g packed in a small funnel with glass wool plug) to remove any entrained water.
- **Re-extract the aqueous phase** with a second 30 mL portion of n-hexane to improve recovery → combine both extracts.

### Step 6 – Solvent Evaporation
- Place the beaker on a **pre-heated water bath at 70 – 80 °C** (well below n-hexane BP of 69 °C, but acceptable with hood ventilation; ideally use a rotary evaporator at 40 °C under reduced pressure).
- Evaporate to **near-dryness** under a **fume hood** (n-hexane vapours are toxic and flammable).
- Place beaker in a **hot air oven at 103 °C for 30 minutes** to drive off residual solvent.

### Step 7 – Weighing
- Transfer beaker to a **desiccator** for 30 minutes to cool to room temperature.
- Weigh on an **analytical balance** to the nearest **0.0001 g**. Record as $W_2$.
- **Pre-weighed empty beaker mass** is $W_1$ (record prior to use after drying at 103 °C and cooling).

### Step 8 – Calculation & Result

Apply the master formula and report in **mg/L**.

## 3.2 Exhaustive Worked Numerical (KTU Board Style)

### Solved Example 1 – Single Determination

> A wastewater sample of **500 mL** is analyzed for oil and grease. The empty evaporating beaker mass $W_1 = 78.6421$ g. After extraction, evaporation, and drying, the beaker with residue mass $W_2 = 78.6512$ g. Calculate the O&G concentration in mg/L.

**Solution (Step-by-Step – examiner-style):**

$$
M_r \;=\; W_2 - W_1 \;=\; 78.6512 \text{ g} - 78.6421 \text{ g}
$$

$$
M_r \;=\; 0.0091 \text{ g} \;=\; 9.1 \text{ mg} \quad (\text{since } 1 \text{ g} = 1000 \text{ mg})
$$

Substitute into the master formula:

$$
C_{\text{O\&G}} \;=\; \frac{(W_2 - W_1) \times 1000}{V_{\text{sample}}} \;=\; \frac{0.0091 \times 1000}{500}
$$

$$
C_{\text{O\&G}} \;=\; \frac{9.1}{500} \;=\; 0.0182 \text{ mg/L}
$$

**Inference:** The O&G level is **far below the BIS limit of 10 mg/L**, indicating acceptable effluent quality.

---

### Solved Example 2 – Spike Recovery Calculation (Quality Control)

> An untreated wastewater sample gives O&G = 22.5 mg/L. A **spiked sample** (same matrix + 10 mg/L standard oil) gives O&G = 31.0 mg/L. Calculate the **percent recovery**.

**Solution:**

$$
C_{\text{expected spiked}} \;=\; C_{\text{sample}} + C_{\text{spike}} \;=\; 22.5 + 10.0 \;=\; 32.5 \text{ mg/L}
$$

$$
\%R \;=\; \frac{C_{\text{measured spiked}}}{C_{\text{expected spiked}}} \times 100 \;=\; \frac{31.0}{32.5} \times 100
$$

$$
\%R \;=\; 95.38 \,\%
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Acceptable recovery range (KTU/USEPA):** **80 % – 120 %**. Since 95.38 % is within the band, the analytical run is **validated**.

---

### Solved Example 3 – Duplicate Precision Check

> Two replicate measurements gave O&G values of **18.4 mg/L** and **17.2 mg/L**. Determine if the precision is acceptable.

**Solution:**

$$
\text{Relative Percent Difference (RPD)} \;=\; \frac{\vert C_1 - C_2 \vert}{\text{Mean}} \times 100
$$

$$
\text{Mean} \;=\; \frac{18.4 + 17.2}{2} \;=\; 17.8 \text{ mg/L}
$$

$$
\text{RPD} \;=\; \frac{\vert 18.4 - 17.2 \vert}{17.8} \times 100 \;=\; \frac{1.2}{17.8} \times 100
$$

$$
\text{RPD} \;=\; 6.74 \,\%
$$

> **Result:** RPD is below the **10 % acceptance threshold** ⇒ precision is acceptable.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix – Sources of Error & Remediation

| Error Source | Effect on Result | Mitigation |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Incomplete phase separation | Low O&G (under-reporting) | Allow 15 min standing; use saturated NaCl to break emulsions |
| Inadequate acidification | Loss of fatty-acid (soap) fraction | Verify pH < 2 with methyl orange |
| Wet solvent extract | False-high O&G | Dry over anhydrous Na₂SO₄ |
| Incomplete solvent evaporation | False-high O&G (residual solvent) | Oven dry at 103 °C for 30 min |
| Beaker not cooled in desiccator | False-high (warm air buoyancy) | Cool 30 min in desiccator |
| Volatile light oils (e.g., gasoline) | Under-reporting (loss during evaporation) | Use rotary evaporator at 40 °C under vacuum |
| Plastic container used for sample | Contamination from plasticizers | Use **glass** with **Teflon-lined cap** |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture – Analytical Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Wastewater Sample Collection in Glass Bottle] --> B[Acidification to pH less than 2 using HCl 1:1]
    B --> C[Storage at 4 degrees Celsius for max 28 days]
    C --> D[Measure 1000 mL Sample in Graduated Cylinder]
    D --> E[Transfer to 2 L Separating Funnel]
    E --> F[Add 30 mL n-Hexane and Shake for 2 Minutes]
    F --> G[Allow Phase Separation for 10 Minutes]
    G --> H[Collect Upper Hexane Layer into Pre-weighed Beaker]
    H --> I[Pass Extract through Anhydrous Na2SO4 Column]
    I --> J[Re-extract Aqueous Phase with Fresh 30 mL Hexane]
    J --> K[Combine Both Hexane Extracts]
    K --> L[Evaporate Solvent on Water Bath under Fume Hood]
    L --> M[Oven Dry at 103 degrees Celsius for 30 Minutes]
    M --> N[Cool in Desiccator for 30 Minutes]
    N --> O[Weigh Beaker with Residue on Analytical Balance]
    O --> P[Apply Formula: O and G equals W2 minus W1 times 1000 divided by V]
    P --> Q[Report Result in mg per L and Compare with BIS 10 mg per L Standard]
    Q --> R{Q and G less than 10 mg per L?}
    R -- Yes --> S[Effluent Compliant with Discharge Norms]
    R -- No --> T[Effluent Non-Compliant: Requires Further Treatment]
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram – Apparatus Interconnection

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph SamplingUnit[Sampling and Preservation]
        A1[Glass Winchester Bottle 1 L] --> A2[1:1 HCl Acidification]
        A2 --> A3[Refrigerator 4 degrees Celsius]
    end
    subgraph ExtractionUnit[Extraction Assembly]
        B1[2 L Separating Funnel on Stand] --> B2[30 mL n-Hexane]
        B2 --> B3[Glass Wool Plug]
        B3 --> B4[Anhydrous Na2SO4 Column]
    end
    subgraph EvaporationUnit[Evaporation and Weighing]
        C1[Water Bath 70 to 80 degrees Celsius] --> C2[Hot Air Oven 103 degrees Celsius]
        C2 --> C3[Desiccator with Silica Gel]
        C3 --> C4[Analytical Balance 0.0001 g readability]
    end
    A3 --> B1
    B4 --> C1
    C4 --> D((Final Report mg per L))
```

## 4.3 Schematic – Phase Separation Concept

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Acidified Wastewater containing O and G] -->|Pour into funnel| P2[Add 30 mL n-Hexane]
    P2 -->|Shake 2 min and vent| P3[Two Immiscible Layers Formed]
    P3 --> P4[Upper Layer: n-Hexane plus Dissolved O and G]
    P3 --> P5[Lower Layer: Aqueous Phase with Inorganics]
    P4 -->|Drain through Na2SO4| P6[Dry Hexane Extract]
    P5 -->|Discard or re-extract| P7[Wastewater Treated]
    P6 -->|Evaporate at 70 degrees C| P8[Solvent Free Residue]
    P8 -->|Weigh gravimetrically| P9[O and G Mass in mg]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A – Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Define oil and grease as applied to wastewater analysis. Why is the term operational rather than absolute?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Definition (2 marks):** Oil and grease is the collective gravimetric parameter representing all non-polar, organic substances (hydrocarbons, fats, oils, waxes, fatty acids) in a water sample that are **extractable into n-hexane under acidic conditions (pH < 2)** and **non-volatile at 103 °C**.
- **Operational nature (1 mark):** It is termed *operational* because the value depends entirely on the **solvent used, pH, extraction time, and evaporation temperature**. Different solvents recover different fractions – hence it is a method-defined parameter, not a chemically absolute species.

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**State the principle of the partition-gravimetric method for oil and grease estimation.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- The **acidified wastewater sample** (pH < 2) is extracted with a **non-polar solvent (n-hexane)** in a separating funnel.
- The **non-polar oil and grease molecules partition into the hexane phase** due to *like-dissolves-like* polarity compatibility, while polar inorganic salts remain in the aqueous phase.
- The hexane extract is dried over anhydrous Na₂SO₄, the solvent is **evaporated**, and the **residue is weighed gravimetrically**.
- The mass of residue per unit volume of sample = O&G concentration in **mg/L**.

---

## 5.2 Part B – Long Answer Questions (Module Internal Choice – 14 Marks)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Pattern:** Each Part-B question carries **14 marks**, split as **(a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks**. Students answer **one full question** (either Option A or Option B).

---

### Option A – 14 Marks `[KTU University Exam – July 2024]`

**Q.A (a) [7 Marks]** Describe in detail the **procedure for the determination of oil and grease in a given wastewater sample by the partition-gravimetric method**. List the chemicals and apparatus required.

**Model Answer (7 marks):**

1. **Apparatus (2 marks):** 2 L separating funnel with Teflon stopcock, 1000 mL glass Winchester bottle, 250 mL pre-weighed beaker, water bath, hot air oven (103 °C), desiccator, analytical balance (0.0001 g), glass wool, Na₂SO₄ drying column, fume hood.

2. **Reagents (2 marks):** n-Hexane (extraction solvent), 1:1 HCl (acidification), anhydrous Na₂SO₄ (drying), methyl orange indicator (pH check).

3. **Procedure (3 marks):**
   - Acidify sample to pH < 2 with HCl; store at 4 °C.
   - Measure 1000 mL sample into separating funnel; add 30 mL n-hexane.
   - Shake 2 min, vent, allow 10 min phase separation.
   - Collect upper hexane layer, dry over Na₂SO₄ column into pre-weighed beaker $W_1$.
   - Re-extract aqueous layer with another 30 mL hexane; combine extracts.
   - Evaporate solvent on water bath under fume hood, then oven-dry at 103 °C for 30 min.
   - Cool in desiccator 30 min; weigh to get $W_2$.
   - Apply formula $C_{\text{O\&G}} = \dfrac{(W_2 - W_1) \times 1000}{V_{\text{sample}}}$ in mg/L.

---

**Q.A (b) [7 Marks]** A **750 mL** wastewater sample was analyzed for O&G. The empty beaker mass was **102.4523 g** and the beaker + residue mass was **102.4612 g**. Compute the O&G concentration in mg/L and comment on its compliance with **BIS effluent standards**.

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

$$
W_2 - W_1 \;=\; 102.4612 - 102.4523 \;=\; 0.0089 \text{ g} \;=\; 8.9 \text{ mg}
$$

$$
C_{\text{O\&G}} \;=\; \frac{8.9 \times 1000}{750} \;=\; 11.87 \text{ mg/L}
$$

> **Comparison with BIS Standard:**
> The **BIS permissible limit for industrial effluent** discharged into inland surface water is **10 mg/L** (IS 2490).
> Measured value = **11.87 mg/L > 10 mg/L** ⇒ **Non-compliant**.
> **Recommendation:** Install a **dissolved air flotation (DAF)** unit or **oil skimmer** as tertiary treatment to bring the value below 10 mg/L.

---

### Option B – 14 Marks `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]`

**Q.B (a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **significance of oil and grease determination in industrial wastewater**. Discuss the various **sources and environmental impacts**.

**Model Answer (7 marks):**

1. **Sources (3 marks):**
   - Petroleum refineries → crude/diesel/lubricants.
   - Food & dairy industries → animal fats, vegetable oils.
   - Metal working → cutting oils, coolants.
   - Tanneries, edible oil refineries, vehicle workshops.

2. **Environmental Impacts (2 marks):**
   - Forms **floating oil film** → blocks atmospheric reaeration, reduces **dissolved oxygen (DO)**, kills aquatic life.
   - Clogs **sewer lines, pumps, bar screens**, interferes with biological treatment (scum formation in activated sludge).
   - **Fire hazard** in industrial sumps and oil refineries.

3. **Significance of Monitoring (2 marks):**
   - Statutory **regulatory compliance** (CPCB / SPCB / BIS / EPA).
   - Process **performance evaluation** of skimming tanks, DAF, API separators.
   - **Effluent trading** and consent-to-operate renewal.

---

**Q.B (b) [7 Marks]** Compare the **partition-gravimetric method (APHA 5520 B)** with the **Soxhlet extraction method (APHA 5520 D)** for oil and grease determination. Under what conditions is each method preferred?

**Model Answer (7 marks):**

| Parameter | Partition-Gravimetric (5520 B) | Soxhlet Extraction (5520 D) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Solvent** | n-Hexane (30 mL × 2 extractions) | Petroleum ether or n-Hexane (150–200 mL) |
| **Sample state** | **Liquid** wastewater | **Solid/semi-solid** (sludge, soil, sediment) |
| **Principle** | Single-stage liquid–liquid partition | Continuous reflux extraction (6–8 hours) |
| **Extraction time** | 15–20 min (incl. settling) | 6–8 hours (overnight possible) |
| **Equipment** | Separating funnel | Soxhlet apparatus + thimble + condenser |
| **Recovery efficiency** | 80–95% (good for free oils) | **>95%** (best for adsorbed/occluded oils) |
| **Solvent consumption** | Low (60 mL) | High (200 mL) |
| **KTU lab preference** | **Yes – most common** | Used for solid samples only |
| **Sensitivity** | ~5 mg/L (gravimetric) | ~1 mg/L (gravimetric, with larger mass) |

> [!TIP]
> **Conclusion:** For **routine liquid wastewater** analysis in the KTU lab, **5520 B** is preferred (rapid, low solvent). For **sludge characterization** or **soil contamination** studies (e.g., oil spill assessment), **5520 D Soxhlet** is chosen for higher recovery.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Zones in KTU Valuation:**
> 1. **Forgetting the 1000 conversion factor** in the formula → 1000× error (lose 2 marks in numerical).
> 2. **Not stating WHY acidification is done** (pH < 2 frees fatty acids from soaps + breaks emulsions) → lose 1 mark in theory.
> 3. **Using $V_{\text{sample}}$ in litres instead of mL** in the formula → wrong answer (loses 1 mark).
> 4. **Confusing "n-hexane" with "petroleum ether"** in the procedure description → partial deduction.
> 5. **Failing to mention the role of anhydrous Na₂SO₄** in drying the extract → lose 1 mark.
> 6. **Not reporting the result with correct units (mg/L)** in the final line → lose 0.5 mark.
> 7. **Omitting desiccator cooling** in the procedure → 0.5 mark deduction (warm beakers give false-low mass).
> 8. **Comparing with the BIS limit** is mandatory in compliance questions – skipping the comparison loses 1 mark.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Bulletins – Must Memorize for KTU 2024 ESE:**

- [ ] **Master formula:** $C_{\text{O\&G}} = \dfrac{(W_2 - W_1) \times 1000}{V_{\text{sample in mL}}}$ in **mg/L**.
- [ ] **Solvent of choice (current standard):** **n-Hexane** (Freon-113 banned).
- [ ] **Sample pH requirement:** **pH < 2** (use 1:1 HCl + methyl orange).
- [ ] **Drying agent:** **Anhydrous Na₂SO₄** (NOT CaCl₂ – reacts with oils).
- [ ] **Oven drying temperature:** **103 °C for 30 min**.
- [ ] **Desiccator cooling time:** **30 min minimum** (to room temperature).
- [ ] **BIS effluent standard:** **10 mg/L** (IS 2490 Part I, inland surface water).
- [ ] **EPA method code:** **EPA 1664** (n-Hexane extractable material – HEM).
- [ ] **APHA method code:** **Standard Methods 5520 B** (Partition-Gravimetric).
- [ ] **Sample container:** **Glass bottle, no headspace, acidified, 4 °C**.
- [ ] **Sample holding time:** **28 days** (acidified).
- [ ] **Volume typically taken:** **1000 mL** (or 500 mL for high-strength effluents).
- [ ] **Solvent volume per extraction:** **30 mL × 2** extractions.
- [ ] **Shaking time:** **2 minutes** with periodic venting.
- [ ] **Phase settling time:** **10 minutes** for clear meniscus.
- [ ] **Units of O&G:** **mg/L** (equivalent to **ppm** for aqueous samples).
- [ ] **Acceptable recovery (QC):** **80 % – 120 %**.
- [ ] **Acceptable RPD between duplicates:** **≤ 10 %**.
- [ ] **Detection limit:** **~0.5 mg/L** (gravimetric) / **0.1 mg/L** (infrared, 5520 C).
- [ ] **Why operational definition?** Because recovery depends on solvent, pH, time, temperature – not an absolute chemical quantity.
- [ ] **Why n-hexane over chloroform?** Non-toxic, non-ozone-depleting, selective for hydrocarbons.
- [ ] **Industrial sources:** Refineries, food processing, metal working, tanneries, transport workshops.
- [ ] **Environmental effect:** Oxygen blockage, aquatic toxicity, sewer clogging, fire hazard.
- [ ] **Treatment methods:** Oil skimmers, API separators, DAF units, coalescers.
- [ ] **Glassware rule:** Always use **pre-weighed, oven-dried, desiccator-cooled** beaker.
- [ ] **Order of operations:** Acidify → Extract → Dry → Evaporate → Oven dry → Cool → Weigh.
- [ ] **Dimensional sanity check:** Residue in mg / sample in mL × 1000 ⇒ **mg/L** ✓.
- [ ] **Mention the standard comparison** in every numerical answer (BIS 10 mg/L).
- [ ] **Volatile oils** (gasoline) may be **under-reported** due to evaporation – this is a known limitation.
- [ ] **Free vs emulsified oil:** Free oil floats; emulsified oil requires acidification + salt to break.
- [ ] **Soxhlet method** is reserved for **solid samples** (sludges, soils).
- [ ] **Fume hood is mandatory** when handling n-hexane (volatile, flammable, narcotic).
- [ ] **Balance readability:** At least **0.0001 g** (analytical balance, not top-pan).
- [ ] **Report O&G to 1 decimal place** in mg/L (e.g., 11.9 mg/L).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Preparation of Biodiesel
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCHL307 - CHEMICAL TECHNOLOGY AND ENVIRONMENTAL ENGINEERING LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Preparation of Biodiesel

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus)

**Biodiesel** is defined as a mono-alkyl ester of long-chain fatty acids derived from a renewable lipid feedstock, such as vegetable oil or animal fat. It is produced industrially and in the laboratory via a chemical reaction called **Transesterification**, in which triglycerides (the main constituent of oils/fats) react with a short-chain alcohol (commonly **methanol**) in the presence of a suitable catalyst (typically a homogeneous base catalyst such as **sodium hydroxide (NaOH)** or **potassium hydroxide (KOH)**) to yield Fatty Acid Methyl Esters (FAME), which is biodiesel, and glycerol as a by-product.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Board Definition:** Biodiesel is a non-petroleum-based diesel fuel comprising a mix of mono-alkyl esters of long-chain fatty acids, produced by the transesterification of vegetable oils or animal fats with methanol. Its cetane number, energy content, and viscosity profile make it functionally analogous to petro-diesel.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard reference abbreviation:** FAME = **Fatty Acid Methyl Ester** (e.g., methyl oleate, methyl stearate, methyl palmitate).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a heavy, greasy cooking oil (like coconut oil or sunflower oil) being too thick and sludgy to flow easily through a modern diesel engine. Now think of biodiesel preparation as a **"molecular tailoring job"**:

- The thick, long-chain triglyceride (a "tripod" molecule with 3 long fatty "legs") is **chopped up** by methanol (the "scissor" molecule).
- Methanol slices the tripod into **3 thin, single fatty-acid methyl ester "rods"** + **glycerol backbone** (the leftover).
- These 3 thin rods = **biodiesel**, and they are now slim enough to flow through fuel injectors.

So, in simple words:

> **Biodiesel preparation = dismantling one big fat molecule into 3 small, engine-friendly fuel molecules using alcohol.**

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics (Bolded)

- **Density of biodiesel:** ≈ **0.86 – 0.90 g/cm³** at 15 °C
- **Kinematic viscosity of biodiesel:** ≈ **3.5 – 5.0 mm²/s** at 40 °C
- **Flash point of biodiesel:** **> 130 °C** (much safer than petro-diesel)
- **Molecular weight of methanol (CH₃OH):** **32.04 g/mol**
- **Molecular weight of glycerol (C₃H₈O₃):** **92.09 g/mol**
- **Average molecular weight of a generic triglyceride:** ≈ **800 – 900 g/mol**
- **Standard reaction temperature:** **55 – 65 °C** (close to the boiling point of methanol, **64.7 °C**)
- **Standard reaction time:** **1 – 2 hours**
- **Molar ratio of methanol to oil:** **6 : 1** (stoichiometric is 3:1; excess alcohol drives equilibrium forward)
- **Typical catalyst loading (NaOH):** **0.5 – 1.0 % (w/w) of oil**
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Transesterification Reaction – Step-by-Step Logic

The overall reaction of transesterification proceeds in **three reversible, consecutive steps**, where triglycerides are progressively converted to diglycerides, monoglycerides, and finally glycerol, releasing one methyl ester molecule at each step.

### Step 1 — Triglyceride → Diglyceride
The catalyst (e.g., methoxide ion, CH₃O⁻, generated *in situ* from NaOH + CH₃OH) attacks the carbonyl carbon of one fatty acid chain. The chain detaches as a methyl ester, leaving a **diglyceride**.

### Step 2 — Diglyceride → Monoglyceride
A second methoxide attack releases a second methyl ester, leaving a **monoglyceride**.

### Step 3 — Monoglyceride → Glycerol
A third methoxide attack releases the final methyl ester, leaving free **glycerol**.

### Why Excess Alcohol?
- The forward reaction is **equilibrium-limited** (3 forward reactions vs 3 reverse).
- A **6:1 molar ratio** (methanol:oil) pushes Le Chatelier's equilibrium toward the product side.
- Excess alcohol is later recovered (methanol is cheaper than oil), so it is industrially practical.

### Why NaOH/KOH (Base Catalysis)?
- Base catalysis is **~4000× faster** than acid catalysis at moderate temperatures.
- Feedstock **must be dry** (< 0.06 % water) — water + oil = **saponification** (soap formation, which destroys yield).
- Free Fatty Acid (FFA) content must be **< 2 %** — otherwise, perform a pre-esterification with H₂SO₄.

## 2.2 Laboratory Procedure Flow

1. **Pretreatment of oil:** Filter, heat to 110 °C for 15 min (drives off moisture), cool to 60 °C.
2. **Preparation of methoxide solution:** Mix NaOH pellets into methanol in a beaker; stir until fully dissolved (exothermic).
3. **Reaction:** Pour methoxide solution into preheated oil in a 3-necked round-bottom flask fitted with a **reflux condenser**, **thermometer**, and **mechanical stirrer**.
4. Maintain **60 ± 2 °C**, stir at 600–800 rpm, for **~90 minutes**.
5. Transfer the mixture to a **separating funnel** and allow to stand for 8–12 hours (overnight is ideal).
6. Two distinct layers form:
   - **Top layer:** Crude biodiesel (FAME) — light yellow.
   - **Bottom layer:** Glycerol + soap + residual catalyst (denser).
7. **Washing:** Drain glycerol. Wash biodiesel with warm distilled water (50 °C) 2–3 times to remove residual catalyst/soap. Stop when wash water is clear and pH ≈ 7.
8. **Drying:** Heat washed biodiesel to 105 °C for 30 min to remove dissolved water.
9. **Weigh** the final product → Calculate % yield.

> [!NOTE]
> **Visual cue during reaction:** A clear, golden, single-phase liquid turning into a cloudy, biphasic mixture confirms transesterification has occurred.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Typical Value / Unit | Notes |
|---|----------|---------|----------------------|-------|
| 1 | Theoretical methanol required | $V_{MeOH} = \dfrac{3 \times M_{MeOH} \times m_{oil}}{M_{oil}}$ | mL | Stoichiometric basis (3:1 molar) |
| 2 | Practical methanol volume (6:1 ratio) | $V_{practical} = 2 \times V_{MeOH}$ | mL | Doubles the stoichiometric amount |
| 3 | Catalyst (NaOH) mass | $m_{NaOH} = 0.01 \times m_{oil}$ | g | 1 % w/w of oil (typical lab value) |
| 4 | % Yield of Biodiesel | $\% \text{Yield} = \dfrac{m_{biodiesel,\,actual}}{m_{oil}} \times 100$ | % | m_biodiesel actual / m_oil × 100 |
| 5 | % Conversion of Oil | $\% \text{Conv} = \dfrac{m_{FAME}}{m_{FAME,\,theoretical}} \times 100$ | % | FAME = Fatty Acid Methyl Ester |
| 6 | Saponification Value | $SV = \dfrac{56.1 \times V_{KOH} \times N_{KOH}}{m_{oil}}$ | mg KOH/g oil | Indicates average mol. weight of oil |
| 7 | Acid Value | $AV = \dfrac{56.1 \times V_{KOH} \times N_{KOH}}{m_{oil}}$ | mg KOH/g oil | Must be < 2 for base catalysis |
| 8 | Density | $\rho = \dfrac{m}{V}$ | g/cm³ | Compare against standard 0.86 – 0.90 |
| 9 | Flash point correction | — | > 130 °C | Compared to petro-diesel ≈ 55 °C |
| 10 | Molecular weight of generic triglyceride | $M_{oil} \approx \dfrac{3 \times 56.1 \times 1000}{SV}$ | g/mol | Inverse of saponification value |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Vertical pipe safeguard applied:** In the formulas above, the absolute value / divisibility operator is rendered as plain text (e.g., "per") or LaTeX fractions to avoid breaking the markdown table pipe `|` syntax.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Renewable fuel:** Reduces dependence on imported crude oil (India imports ~85 % of its petroleum).
- **Lower emissions:** ~ **60 % less CO**, **50 % less particulate matter**, near-zero **SO₂**.
- **Biodegradable:** Fully degrades in ~28 days vs petro-diesel (infinite).
- **Feedstock flexibility:** Waste cooking oil, jatropha, pongamia, neem, algae, tallow.
- **Petro-diesel blend standards:** **B5** (5 % biodiesel), **B20**, **B100** — per **BIS 15607 : 2022** (India).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Calculation of Methanol Volume (Stoichiometric)

**Given:**
- Volume of oil used, $V_{oil} = 250 \text{ mL}$
- Density of oil, $\rho_{oil} = 0.92 \text{ g/cm}^3$ (typical for refined sunflower/coconut oil)
- Molecular weight of oil (generic), $M_{oil} = 884 \text{ g/mol}$ (typical triglyceride)
- Molecular weight of methanol, $M_{MeOH} = 32.04 \text{ g/mol}$
- Density of methanol, $\rho_{MeOH} = 0.792 \text{ g/cm}^3$
- Molar ratio desired: **3 : 1** (stoichiometric) → practical **6 : 1**

**Step 1 — Mass of oil:**

$$m_{oil} = V_{oil} \times \rho_{oil} = 250 \text{ mL} \times 0.92 \text{ g/cm}^3$$

$$m_{oil} = 230 \text{ g}$$

**Step 2 — Moles of oil (triglyceride):**

$$n_{oil} = \dfrac{m_{oil}}{M_{oil}} = \dfrac{230}{884} = 0.2602 \text{ mol}$$

**Step 3 — Moles of methanol needed (stoichiometric, 3 mol CH₃OH per 1 mol oil):**

$$n_{MeOH,\,stoich} = 3 \times n_{oil} = 3 \times 0.2602 = 0.7805 \text{ mol}$$

**Step 4 — Mass of methanol:**

$$m_{MeOH} = n_{MeOH,\,stoich} \times M_{MeOH} = 0.7805 \times 32.04 = 25.01 \text{ g}$$

**Step 5 — Volume of methanol (stoichiometric):**

$$V_{MeOH,\,stoich} = \dfrac{m_{MeOH}}{\rho_{MeOH}} = \dfrac{25.01}{0.792} = 31.58 \text{ mL}$$

**Step 6 — Practical volume (doubling for 6:1 ratio):**

$$V_{MeOH,\,practical} = 2 \times 31.58 = 63.16 \text{ mL} \approx 63 \text{ mL}$$

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Calculation of % Yield of Biodiesel

**Given (lab data):**
- Mass of oil taken = **230 g**
- Mass of biodiesel obtained (after washing + drying) = **201.5 g**

**Step 1 — Write the formula:**

$$\% \text{Yield} = \dfrac{m_{biodiesel,\,actual}}{m_{oil}} \times 100$$

**Step 2 — Substitute values:**

$$\% \text{Yield} = \dfrac{201.5}{230} \times 100$$

$$\% \text{Yield} = 0.8761 \times 100 = 87.61 \,\%$$

**Step 3 — Interpretation:**

> A yield of **87 – 95 %** is considered excellent in a KTU lab setting. Loss is due to washing, entrained glycerol, and mechanical transfer losses.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Theoretical Mass of Biodiesel (Mass Balance)

**From stoichiometry:**
1 mole of oil (884 g) → 3 moles of FAME (3 × ~286 g) + 1 mole of glycerol (92 g).

**Verification by mass balance:**

$$m_{oil} = 3 \times m_{FAME} + m_{glycerol}$$

$$884 = 3 \times 286.5 + 92 = 859.5 + 92 = 951.5$$

(The slight imbalance is due to the *molecular weights of the fatty acid chains themselves*; for oleic-acid–derived FAME, $M_{FAME} \approx 296.5$ g/mol, giving a near-perfect balance.)

**For our 230 g oil sample (theoretical biodiesel mass):**

$$m_{FAME,\,theoretical} = 230 \times \dfrac{3 \times 296.5}{884} = 231.5 \text{ g}$$

% Conversion:

$$\% \text{Conv} = \dfrac{201.5}{231.5} \times 100 = 87.04 \,\%$$

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Saponification Value (Quality Check)

Saponification value (SV) helps us verify the *molecular weight* of the oil.

**Procedure:** Titrate 1 g of oil with 0.5 N KOH after saponification.

$$SV = \dfrac{56.1 \times V_{KOH} \times N_{KOH}}{m_{oil}}$$

**Example:** If $V_{KOH} = 4.2 \text{ mL}$, $N_{KOH} = 0.5$, $m_{oil} = 1.0$ g:

$$SV = \dfrac{56.1 \times 4.2 \times 0.5}{1.0} = 117.8 \text{ mg KOH/g oil}$$

**Interpretation:** A *low* SV → *high* molecular weight oil (good for biodiesel). Typical range: **180 – 210 mg KOH/g** for most vegetable oils.

> [!NOTE]
> **Cross-check formula:** $M_{oil} = \dfrac{3 \times 56.1 \times 1000}{SV} = \dfrac{168300}{SV}$
> For $SV = 190$, $M_{oil} = 885.8$ g/mol ✓
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart — Biodiesel Preparation Process

    H --> I[Step 8: Settle 8-12 hours]

    I --> K[Bottom Layer: Glycerol + Soap]
    K --> L[Step 9: Drain Glycerol Byproduct]

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Common Marks Lost Area:**
> 1. Students often write **"biodiesel is ethanol + oil"** — this is **WRONG**. Methanol (CH₃OH) is the standard alcohol. Ethanol is used only in ethanol-blended biodiesel research.
> 2. Confusing **transesterification** with **esterification**. Esterification converts *free fatty acids* using acid catalyst; transesterification converts *triglycerides* using base catalyst. KTU values precision.
> 3. Forgetting that **glycerol is the denser, bottom layer** in the separating funnel (not the top). Carrying the wrong layer ruins 2–3 marks.
> 4. Not mentioning the role of the **reflux condenser** (prevents methanol loss at 60 °C; methanol boils at 64.7 °C).
> 5. Skipping the **drying step (105 °C / 30 min)** — water in biodiesel causes corrosion and engine knock.

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks each)

### Question 1. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model]`
**Define biodiesel. Write the balanced chemical equation for its preparation from triglycerides.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** Biodiesel is a mono-alkyl ester of long-chain fatty acids, derived from vegetable oils or animal fats, produced by **transesterification** with a short-chain alcohol in the presence of a catalyst.

**Equation (2 marks):**

$$\text{Triglyceride} + 3 \text{CH}_3\text{OH} \xrightarrow{\text{NaOH},\,60^\circ\text{C}} 3 \text{FAME (Biodiesel)} + \text{Glycerol}$$

$$\text{R-COOCH}_2\text{-CH(OOCR}_2\text{)-CH}_2\text{-OOCR}_3 + 3 \text{CH}_3\text{OH} \rightarrow 3 \text{R-COOCH}_3 + \text{C}_3\text{H}_5(\text{OH})_3$$

---

### Question 2. `[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model]`
**List any six raw materials / feedstocks used for biodiesel production. Why is methanol preferred over ethanol?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Feedstocks (3 marks – ½ each):**
1. Soybean oil
2. Jatropha oil
3. Pongamia (karanja) oil
4. Waste cooking oil
5. Sunflower oil
6. Animal tallow / microalgal oil

**Methanol preference (1 mark):**
- **Shorter chain (C1)** → faster reaction kinetics.
- **Cheaper and more available** industrially (derived from natural gas).
- **Forms less stable emulsions** with glycerol, easing separation.
- **Lower boiling point** facilitates easy recovery.

---

## 5.2 Part B — ESE Module Internal Choice (14 Marks each)

### **Question A (14 marks)** — `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model]`

**(a)** Describe the **laboratory preparation of biodiesel from refined sunflower oil** with a neat labelled diagram of the apparatus. Mention the role of the reflux condenser. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Apparatus (2 marks):** 3-necked round-bottom flask (500 mL), mechanical stirrer, Liebig condenser, thermometer (0–100 °C), heating mantle, separating funnel, beakers.

**Procedure (4 marks):**
1. Take **250 mL** of refined sunflower oil in the RB flask.
2. Heat gently to **110 °C for 15 min** to remove moisture (critical — water causes saponification).
3. Cool to **~60 °C**.
4. In a separate beaker, dissolve **2.3 g NaOH pellets** (1 % w/w of oil) in **63 mL methanol** (6:1 molar ratio) to form sodium methoxide.
5. Transfer methoxide into the oil via the addition funnel.
6. Stir at **600–800 rpm**, maintain **60 ± 2 °C** for **90 min** under reflux.
7. Transfer contents to a separating funnel; allow to stand **8–12 h** for layer separation.
8. Drain lower glycerol layer; wash upper FAME layer with **warm distilled water (50 °C)** until pH = 7.
9. Dry biodiesel at **105 °C for 30 min** to remove traces of water.

**Role of reflux condenser (1 mark):** Methanol boils at **64.7 °C**; the condenser condenses escaping methanol vapour back into the flask, preventing loss of reactant and maintaining the 6:1 molar ratio.

**(b)** What is the **percentage yield** of biodiesel if 230 g of oil produces 198 g of pure biodiesel? Comment on the result. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Formula (2 marks):**

$$\% \text{Yield} = \dfrac{m_{biodiesel,\,actual}}{m_{oil}} \times 100$$

**Substitution (2 marks):**

$$\% \text{Yield} = \dfrac{198}{230} \times 100 = 86.09 \,\%$$

**Commentary (3 marks):**
- A yield of **86 %** lies in the *acceptable laboratory range* (good lab yield: 80–95 %).
- Losses arise from: (i) incomplete reaction, (ii) entrained glycerol in biodiesel, (iii) washing-water losses, (iv) transfer losses to glassware.
- For higher yield, increase methanol:oil ratio to **9:1** or use **co-solvent (tert-butanol)**.

---

### **Question B (14 marks)** — `[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model]`

**(a)** Explain the **mechanism of base-catalysed transesterification** with a stepwise description. Why is **excess methanol** used even though stoichiometry requires only 3 moles per mole of oil? **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Mechanism (5 marks — 1 mark per key step):**

1. **Catalyst activation:** NaOH + CH₃OH → CH₃O⁻ (methoxide) + Na⁺ + H₂O.
2. **Nucleophilic attack:** Methoxide oxygen attacks the **electrophilic carbonyl carbon** of the triglyceride, forming a **tetrahedral intermediate**.
3. **Rearrangement:** The intermediate collapses, expelling the **diglyceride anion**.
4. **Proton transfer:** Diglyceride anion deprotonates a fresh methanol molecule → **diglyceride + methoxide** (catalyst regenerated).
5. The cycle repeats twice more, ultimately producing **3 moles of FAME + 1 mole of glycerol**.

**Why excess methanol? (2 marks):**
- The reaction is **reversible** (3 forward, 3 reverse reactions).
- Per **Le Chatelier's principle**, doubling the methanol shifts equilibrium toward the product side.
- Excess methanol is **recovered by distillation** post-reaction (boiling point 64.7 °C is well below FAME's), making it economically efficient.

**(b)** A lab uses 200 mL of coconut oil (density 0.92 g/mL, molecular weight 638 g/mol). Calculate: **(i)** the stoichiometric methanol volume, **(ii)** the practical methanol volume for 6:1 ratio, and **(iii)** the theoretical mass of FAME produced. **Assume:** $M_{MeOH} = 32$ g/mol, $M_{FAME} \approx 244$ g/mol, $\rho_{MeOH} = 0.79$ g/mL. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**(i) Stoichiometric methanol (3 marks):**

$$m_{oil} = 200 \times 0.92 = 184 \text{ g}$$

$$n_{oil} = \dfrac{184}{638} = 0.2884 \text{ mol}$$

$$n_{MeOH} = 3 \times 0.2884 = 0.8652 \text{ mol}$$

$$m_{MeOH} = 0.8652 \times 32 = 27.69 \text{ g}$$

$$V_{MeOH,\,stoich} = \dfrac{27.69}{0.79} = 35.05 \text{ mL}$$

**(ii) Practical methanol (2 marks):**

$$V_{MeOH,\,practical} = 2 \times 35.05 = 70.10 \text{ mL}$$

**(iii) Theoretical FAME mass (2 marks):**

$$n_{FAME} = 3 \times n_{oil} = 0.8652 \text{ mol}$$

$$m_{FAME} = 0.8652 \times 244 = 211.1 \text{ g}$$

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **Core reaction:** Triglyceride + 3 Methanol → 3 FAME (Biodiesel) + Glycerol.
- **Catalyst of choice:** NaOH (1 % w/w of oil) or KOH.
- **Molar ratio:** **6 : 1** (methanol : oil) — practical; **3 : 1** — stoichiometric.
- **Reaction temperature:** **60 ± 2 °C** (just below methanol's BP of 64.7 °C).
- **Reaction time:** **~90 minutes** under stirring.
- **Why reflux?** To prevent methanol loss.
- **Layer separation:** **Top = Biodiesel (FAME)**, **Bottom = Glycerol**.
- **Washing:** With warm distilled water at 50 °C, repeat until pH = 7.
- **Drying:** 105 °C for 30 min in a hot-air oven.
- **% Yield formula:** $\%Y = \dfrac{m_{FAME}}{m_{oil}} \times 100$
- **Precautions:**
  - Oil must be **dry** (water → soap).
  - **FFA < 2 %** (else pre-esterify with H₂SO₄).
  - Add catalyst *to methanol*, *not* methanol to catalyst (exothermic).
- **Quality checks:** Density 0.86–0.90 g/cm³, Viscosity 3.5–5.0 mm²/s, Flash point > 130 °C, Acid value < 0.5 mg KOH/g.
- **Industrial blend standard (India):** **BIS 15607 : 2022** for B5–B100 blends.
- **By-product utility:** Crude glycerol → pharmaceuticals, cosmetics, explosives (nitroglycerin), animal feed.
- **Glycerol density** (1.261 g/cm³) is **greater** than FAME density (~0.88 g/cm³) → **biodiesel floats on top**.

> [!TIP]
> **Last-minute memory hook:** *"**Triglyceride + 3 MeOH → 3 FAME + Glycerol; 60 °C, NaOH, 6:1, 90 min, top layer is biodiesel.**"* Memorise this single line and you've covered 70 % of the theory part.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Preparation of Magnetite Particle
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCHL307 - CHEMICAL TECHNOLOGY AND ENVIRONMENTAL ENGINEERING LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Preparation of Magnetite Particle

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Magnetite (Fe₃O₄)** is a mixed-valence iron oxide belonging to the spinel ferrite family, possessing the chemical formula Fe²⁺Fe³⁺₂O₄. It is the most magnetic of all naturally occurring minerals on Earth and forms the structural basis for a wide class of synthetic superparamagnetic nanoparticles used in environmental remediation, biomedical applications, and catalysis.

In the context of the **PCCHL307 – Chemical Technology and Environmental Engineering Laboratory**, the term *Preparation of Magnetite Particles* refers specifically to the **wet chemical synthesis (co-precipitation route)** of nano- to micro-sized magnetite particulates from aqueous solutions of ferrous (Fe²⁺) and ferric (Fe³⁺) salt precursors, typically **ferrous sulfate heptahydrate (FeSO₄·7H₂O)** and **ferric chloride hexahydrate (FeCl₃·6H₂O)**, under a controlled alkaline environment maintained by **sodium hydroxide (NaOH)** or **ammonium hydroxide (NH₄OH)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim Intent):**
> "Synthesis of magnetite (Fe₃O₄) nano/microparticles by chemical co-precipitation of Fe²⁺ and Fe³⁺ ions in 1:2 molar ratio under inert/oxygen-free alkaline conditions, followed by washing, drying, and characterization."

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy (Plain-English Explanation)

Imagine you are making a **chocolate-vanilla marble cake**, but instead of food ingredients, you are mixing two different iron "flavors" (Fe²⁺ and Fe³⁺) in water. When you pour a strong base (like NaOH) into this mixture under a protective nitrogen blanket (so the oxygen in the air does not "spoil" the flavor by converting Fe²⁺ into rust-colored Fe³⁺ hydroxide), a black magnetic sludge precipitates out. This black sludge, when washed, dried, and ground, is **magnetite powder**.

The "magnetic marble" analogy is powerful: each particle is essentially an **ordered crystal lattice** where Fe²⁺ and Fe³⁺ ions occupy specific sites (tetrahedral and octahedral) inside a cubic close-packed oxygen framework. This ordered arrangement is what gives magnetite its extraordinary magnetic responsiveness — it can be **pulled out of solution with a simple bar magnet**, making it invaluable for water purification and heavy-metal adsorption.

## 1.3 Key Physical & Chemical Constants (Bolded as Required)

| Property | Value / Description |
| :--- | :--- |
| **Chemical Formula** | **Fe₃O₄** (or **FeO·Fe₂O₃**) |
| **Molar Mass** | **231.533 g/mol** |
| **Crystal Structure** | **Inverse Spinel (Cubic, Fd-3m space group)** |
| **Density** | **5.17 g/cm³** |
| **Color** | **Black (in bulk) / Blackish-brown (in nano form)** |
| **Curie Temperature (Tᶜ)** | **858 K (585 °C)** |
| **Saturation Magnetization (Mₛ)** | **~92 emu/g (bulk); ~30–80 emu/g (nano)** |
| **Standard Molar Ratio (Fe²⁺ : Fe³⁺)** | **1 : 2** |
| **Required pH for Precipitation** | **≥ 9.0 (typically 10–12)** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why is the 1:2 molar ratio sacrosanct?**
> Magnetite has the formula Fe²⁺Fe³⁺₂O₄. To get one formula unit, you need **one Fe²⁺** and **two Fe³⁺** ions. If the ratio is off, you end up with impurities: excess Fe²⁺ → wüstite (FeO); excess Fe³⁺ → maghemite (γ-Fe₂O₃) or goethite (α-FeOOH).

## 1.4 Relevance to Environmental Engineering (Contextual Hook)

In environmental engineering, magnetite is a **workhorse material** for:
- **Adsorption of heavy metals** (As³⁺, As⁵⁺, Cr⁶⁺, Pb²⁺, Cd²⁺) from contaminated water.
- **Removal of organic dyes** (methylene blue, congo red) via magnetic separation.
- **Catalyst support** for Fenton-like oxidation of organic pollutants.
- **Soil remediation** via injection of magnetic nano-adsorbents.

The experiment thus bridges **synthesis → application**, training students in both unit operations and environmental utility.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Governing Chemistry — Why Co-Precipitation Works

The co-precipitation of magnetite is fundamentally an **acid-base neutralization accompanied by a redox-stable precipitation event**. The reaction proceeds in aqueous medium and can be written as:

$$
\text{Fe}^{2+} + 2\,\text{Fe}^{3+} + 8\,\text{OH}^{-} \longrightarrow \text{Fe}_{3}\text{O}_{4} \downarrow + 4\,\text{H}_{2}\text{O}
$$

### 2.1.1 Step-Wise Reaction Logic (Bulleted Breakdown)

- **Step 1 — Dissolution of Precursor Salts:**
  FeSO₄·7H₂O and FeCl₃·6H₂O dissolve in deoxygenated distilled water, releasing free Fe²⁺ and Fe³⁺ ions. An inert atmosphere (N₂ gas purging) is essential to prevent oxidation of Fe²⁺ to Fe³⁺ by dissolved O₂.

- **Step 2 — Addition of Alkali (NaOH/NH₄OH):**
  The alkali raises the pH sharply above 9. This drives the supersaturation of iron hydroxides, which then undergo a **dehydration-condensation** pathway to form the mixed-metal oxide (magnetite).

- **Step 3 — Nucleation and Growth:**
  At pH > 9, two simultaneous equilibria are operative: Fe(OH)₂ (white-green) and Fe(OH)₃ (reddish-brown) form transiently. They co-precipitate and **dehydrate in situ** to yield the black Fe₃O₄ nuclei. These nuclei grow into larger particles unless capped by a surfactant.

- **Step 4 — Digestion (Aging):**
  The suspension is held at 50–80 °C for 30–60 min. This Ostwald-ripening step improves crystallinity and magnetic ordering.

- **Step 5 — Separation, Washing, Drying:**
  The black precipitate is separated using a **permanent bar magnet** (external magnetic field), washed repeatedly with deionized water and ethanol to remove residual salts (Na₂SO₄, NaCl), and finally dried in a hot-air oven at 60–80 °C.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Examiner Insight:** Many students lose marks for skipping the "inert atmosphere" step. KTU 2024 scheme explicitly tests whether students understand **why** N₂/Ar purging is mandatory. The answer: to keep Fe²⁺ from oxidizing to Fe³⁺ (which would shift the product to γ-Fe₂O₃ / Fe(OH)₃ instead of Fe₃O₄).

## 2.2 The "Why" Behind the 1:2 Stoichiometry

Each Fe₃O₄ unit cell contains:
- **8 Fe²⁺ ions** in octahedral sites
- **16 Fe³⁺ ions** split equally (8 in tetrahedral + 8 in octahedral)
- **32 O²⁻ ions** in cubic close packing

Thus the Fe²⁺ : Fe³⁺ ratio is **8 : 16 = 1 : 2**. Any deviation from this in the precursor solution produces a non-stoichiometric, weakly magnetic, or oxidized product.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield Cheat Sheet)

> **Critical formatting rule:** No vertical pipe symbol `|` is used inside table cells. All absolute-value or magnitude notations use `\vert` instead.

| # | Formula / Quantity | Expression | Units | Remarks |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | **Stoichiometric Requirement** | $n_{\text{Fe}^{2+}} = \frac{1}{3}\,n_{\text{total Fe}}$ and $n_{\text{Fe}^{3+}} = \frac{2}{3}\,n_{\text{total Fe}}$ | mol | Based on Fe₃O₄ formula |
| 2 | **Mass of FeSO₄·7H₂O needed** | $m = n_{\text{Fe}^{2+}} \times 278.01$ | g | Molar mass = **278.01 g/mol** |
| 3 | **Mass of FeCl₃·6H₂O needed** | $m = n_{\text{Fe}^{3+}} \times 270.30$ | g | Molar mass = **270.30 g/mol** |
| 4 | **Theoretical Yield of Fe₃O₄** | $m_{\text{theo}} = n_{\text{Fe}^{2+}} \times 231.53$ | g | 1 mol Fe²⁺ → 1 mol Fe₃O₄ |
| 5 | **Percent Yield** | $\%\text{Yield} = \dfrac{m_{\text{actual}}}{m_{\text{theoretical}}} \times 100$ | % | Always < 100 % in practice |
| 6 | **Particle Size (XRD, Scherrer)** | $D = \dfrac{K\lambda}{\beta\cos\theta}$ | nm | $K = 0.9$, $\lambda = 0.154\,\text{nm}$ (Cu Kα) |
| 7 | **Saturation Magnetization Estimate** | $M_s \approx 92 \left(1 - \dfrac{6t}{D}\right)$ | emu/g | Surface dead-layer model |
| 8 | **Surface Area (BET)** | $SSA = \dfrac{6000}{\rho \cdot D}$ | m²/g | For spherical, $\rho = 5.17$ g/cm³ |
| 9 | **Molarity of Fe²⁺ Solution** | $M = \dfrac{m_{\text{salt}}}{M_{\text{salt}} \times V_{\text{soln}}}$ | mol/L | Used for dilution prep |
| 10 | **Reaction Heat (ΔH°)** | $\Delta H^{\circ}_{\text{rxn}} \approx -111.5$ | kJ/mol | Exothermic precipitation |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world engineering utility:** The Scherrer equation (row 6) is what allows environmental engineers to predict the **adsorption kinetics** of magnetite — smaller particles (high D⁻¹) have higher surface area and faster heavy-metal uptake.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Laboratory Procedure (with Full Mass-Balance Calculation)

> **Hypothetical Working Scale (KTU-Standard):** Synthesize **1.0 g of Fe₃O₄** as the target.

### 3.1.1 Step A — Required Moles of Fe₃O₄

Starting from the target mass of 1.0 g:

$$
n_{\text{Fe}_3\text{O}_4} = \frac{m}{M} = \frac{1.0\ \text{g}}{231.53\ \text{g/mol}}
$$

$$
n_{\text{Fe}_3\text{O}_4} = 4.319 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{mol}
$$

**Textual interpretation:** To produce 1.0 g of pure magnetite, we need 4.319 × 10⁻³ moles of the product.

### 3.1.2 Step B — Required Moles of Fe²⁺ and Fe³⁺

By the 1:2 stoichiometry, **1 mol of Fe₃O₄ requires 1 mol of Fe²⁺ and 2 mol of Fe³⁺**:

$$
n_{\text{Fe}^{2+}} = 1 \times 4.319 \times 10^{-3} = 4.319 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{mol}
$$

$$
n_{\text{Fe}^{3+}} = 2 \times 4.319 \times 10^{-3} = 8.638 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{mol}
$$

**Textual interpretation:** We need roughly 4.32 millimoles of ferrous ions and 8.64 millimoles of ferric ions. This 1:2 ratio is non-negotiable.

### 3.1.3 Step C — Required Mass of Precursor Salts

For **FeSO₄·7H₂O** (M = 278.01 g/mol):

$$
m_{\text{FeSO}_4\cdot 7\text{H}_2\text{O}} = n_{\text{Fe}^{2+}} \times M_{\text{salt}} = 4.319 \times 10^{-3} \times 278.01
$$

$$
m_{\text{FeSO}_4\cdot 7\text{H}_2\text{O}} = 1.2007\ \text{g} \approx 1.20\ \text{g}
$$

For **FeCl₃·6H₂O** (M = 270.30 g/mol):

$$
m_{\text{FeCl}_3\cdot 6\text{H}_2\text{O}} = n_{\text{Fe}^{3+}} \times M_{\text{salt}} = 8.638 \times 10^{-3} \times 270.30
$$

$$
m_{\text{FeCl}_3\cdot 6\text{H}_2\text{O}} = 2.3347\ \text{g} \approx 2.33\ \text{g}
$$

**Textual interpretation:** Dissolve 1.20 g of ferrous sulfate heptahydrate and 2.33 g of ferric chloride hexahydrate in 50 mL of deoxygenated distilled water.

### 3.1.4 Step D — Required Volume of NaOH (Molarity Method)

Assume we use **2.0 M NaOH** as the precipitating base. The reaction demands **8 mol of OH⁻ per mol of Fe₃O₄**:

$$
n_{\text{OH}^-} = 8 \times 4.319 \times 10^{-3} = 0.03455\ \text{mol}
$$

$$
V_{\text{NaOH}} = \frac{n_{\text{OH}^-}}{M_{\text{NaOH}}} = \frac{0.03455}{2.0} = 0.01728\ \text{L}
$$

$$
V_{\text{NaOH}} \approx 17.3\ \text{mL}
$$

**Textual interpretation:** Slowly add ~17.3 mL of 2.0 M NaOH dropwise under continuous stirring and N₂ bubbling until pH reaches ~10–11.

### 3.1.5 Step E — Theoretical Mass of Byproduct (Washing Justification)

The total dissolved salt byproducts are:
- **Na₂SO₄** from FeSO₄ + NaOH
- **NaCl** from FeCl₃ + NaOH
- **Excess NaOH** unreacted

These are ionic and **must be removed by magnetic decantation + washing with DI water + ethanol** to obtain pure Fe₃O₄.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Effect of Process Variables

| Variable | Effect on Particle Size | Effect on Magnetization | Optimal Range |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **pH** | Smaller at very high pH (rapid nucleation) | Decreases if goethite forms | **9.5 – 11.0** |
| **Temperature** | Larger at higher T (Ostwald ripening) | Improves crystallinity | **50 – 80 °C** |
| **Fe²⁺ : Fe³⁺ ratio** | Deviation causes impurities | Maximum at 1:2 | **Exactly 1:2** |
| **Stirring speed** | Smaller at high RPM (shear breaks nuclei) | Marginal effect | **600 – 1000 rpm** |
| **NaOH addition rate** | Smaller at fast addition (more nuclei) | Marginal effect | **Slow dropwise** |
| **Inert atmosphere** | Prevents oxidation | Critical for Mₛ | **Continuous N₂/Ar** |

## 3.3 Exhaustive Derivation of Theoretical Yield

The general percent-yield formula is:

$$
\%\text{Yield} = \frac{m_{\text{actual}}}{m_{\text{theoretical}}} \times 100
$$

**Derivation chain:**

$$
m_{\text{theoretical}} = n_{\text{Fe}^{2+}} \times M_{\text{Fe}_3\text{O}_4}
$$

Because the **limiting reagent** is typically Fe²⁺ (since Fe³⁺ is in double quantity and is harder to lose):

$$
m_{\text{theoretical}} = 4.319 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{mol} \times 231.53\ \text{g/mol} = 1.000\ \text{g}
$$

Suppose, in practice, you obtain **0.86 g** of dry black powder:

$$
\%\text{Yield} = \frac{0.86}{1.000} \times 100 = 86.0\ \%
$$

**Textual interpretation:** An 86 % yield is excellent for a co-precipitation lab-scale synthesis. Losses arise from incomplete precipitation, transfer losses on glassware, and oxidation side-reactions.

## 3.4 Particle Size Estimation via Scherrer Equation (XRD Method)

The Scherrer equation relates the X-ray diffraction peak broadening to crystallite size:

$$
D = \frac{K\lambda}{\beta \cos\theta}
$$

**Worked example (typical values for lab-synthesized magnetite):**

$$
K = 0.9,\quad \lambda = 0.15406\ \text{nm},\quad \beta = 0.015\ \text{rad},\quad 2\theta = 35.5^{\circ}\ (\theta = 17.75^{\circ})
$$

$$
\cos\theta = \cos(17.75^{\circ}) = 0.9524
$$

$$
D = \frac{0.9 \times 0.15406}{0.015 \times 0.9524}
$$

$$
D = \frac{0.13865}{0.014286} = 9.71\ \text{nm}
$$

**Textual interpretation:** The crystallite size is approximately **9.7 nm**, confirming that the product is a **nanoparticulate magnetite** (i.e., in the 1–100 nm regime).

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Process Flow Diagram — Co-Precipitation of Magnetite

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[Start: Target 1.0 g Fe3O4] --> A1[Weigh FeSO4·7H2O 1.20 g]
    A1 --> A2[Weigh FeCl3·6H2O 2.33 g]
    A2 --> A3[Dissolve in 50 mL deoxygenated DI water]
    A3 --> A4[Purge with N2 gas for 15 min]
    A4 --> B1[Heat to 60 °C with stirring 800 rpm]
    B1 --> B2[Add 2.0 M NaOH dropwise until pH 10]
    B2 --> C1[Black precipitate forms]
    C1 --> C2[Age digest for 45 min at 60 °C]
    C2 --> D1[Separate using bar magnet]
    D1 --> D2[Wash with DI water 3 times]
    D2 --> D3[Wash with ethanol 2 times]
    D3 --> E1[Dry in oven at 70 °C for 12 h]
    E1 --> E2[Grind to fine black powder]
    E2 --> F1[Characterize XRD SEM VSM]
    F1 --> F2[Store in airtight vial]
    F2 --> A5[Yield calculation and reporting]
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Why Each Block Exists

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph PRECURSOR_PREP[PRECURSOR PREPARATION]
        P1[Weighing balance] --> P2[Volumetric flask]
        P2 --> P3[Deoxygenation unit]
    end

    subgraph REACTION_ZONE[REACTION ZONE]
        R1[Three-neck round-bottom flask] --> R2[Magnetic stirrer with hot plate]
        R2 --> R3[Thermometer and pH probe]
        R3 --> R4[Dropping funnel with NaOH]
    end

    subgraph SEPARATION[SOLID LIQUID SEPARATION]
        S1[External NdFeB bar magnet] --> S2[Decantation]
        S2 --> S3[Vacuum filtration optional]
    end

    subgraph POST_TREATMENT[POST TREATMENT]
        PT1[DI water wash] --> PT2[Ethanol wash]
        PT2 --> PT3[Hot air oven]
        PT3 --> PT4[Mortar and pestle grinding]
    end

    subgraph CHARACTERIZATION[CHARACTERIZATION]
        C1[XRD for phase purity] --> C2[SEM for morphology]
        C2 --> C3[VSM for magnetization]
        C3 --> C4[BET for surface area]
    end

    PRECURSOR_PREP --> REACTION_ZONE
    REACTION_ZONE --> SEPARATION
    SEPARATION --> POST_TREATMENT
    POST_TREATMENT --> CHARACTERIZATION
```

## 4.3 Inverse-Spinel Crystal Structure of Magnetite (Schematic Mapping)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph UNIT_CELL[Fe3O4 Unit Cell Fd-3m]
        O[O2- ions cubic close packed 32 per cell]
        TD[Tetrahedral A sites 8 Fe3+ ions]
        OC[Octahedral B sites 8 Fe2+ plus 8 Fe3+ ions]
        O --> TD
        O --> OC
    end

    subgraph MAGNETIC_ORDER[Magnetic ordering]
        A_SPIN[Fe3+ A site spin up]
        B_FE3[Fe3+ B site spin down]
        B_FE2[Fe2+ B site spin up]
        NET[Net magnetic moment from Fe2+ ions only]
    end

    UNIT_CELL --> MAGNETIC_ORDER
    A_SPIN --> NET
    B_FE3 --> NET
    B_FE2 --> NET
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Why does this matter for environmental engineering?**
> The net magnetic moment per formula unit comes solely from the Fe²⁺ ions (since the Fe³⁺ spins cancel antiferromagnetically). This is why the **1:2 ratio is critical** — any deviation reduces the net moment and weakens magnetic separability during water-treatment operations.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Scheme]**
**"Why is an inert atmosphere (N₂/Ar purging) maintained during the synthesis of magnetite by co-precipitation?"**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- (1 mark) The inert atmosphere prevents oxidation of ferrous (Fe²⁺) ions to ferric (Fe³⁺) ions by atmospheric O₂.
- (1 mark) If Fe²⁺ gets oxidized, the 1:2 molar ratio of Fe²⁺:Fe³⁺ is disturbed, and the product shifts from Fe₃O₄ toward γ-Fe₂O₃ (maghemite) or α-FeOOH (goethite).
- (1 mark) These impurities are weakly magnetic or non-magnetic, defeating the purpose of synthesizing magnetite for environmental separation applications.

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Scheme]**
**"What is the significance of the 1:2 molar ratio of Fe²⁺ to Fe³⁺ ions in magnetite synthesis?"**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- (1 mark) The chemical formula of magnetite is Fe²⁺Fe³⁺₂O₄, demanding a 1:2 stoichiometry of ferrous to ferric ions in the precursor.
- (1 mark) This ratio places Fe³⁺ ions in both tetrahedral (8) and octahedral (8) sites, while Fe²⁺ ions occupy only octahedral (8) sites, yielding the inverse spinel structure.
- (1 mark) Any deviation produces non-stoichiometric impurities (FeO, Fe₂O₃, FeOOH) with degraded magnetic saturation, reducing separability in environmental applications.

## 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks)

> **KTU 2024 Rule:** Each Part B question carries 14 marks and offers internal choice between two full questions. Each sub-part is typically 7 marks.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Scheme]**
**"Describe the laboratory preparation of magnetite (Fe₃O₄) particles by the co-precipitation method. Include the reaction, apparatus, and procedural steps."**

**Model Solution:**

**Apparatus (2 marks):** Three-neck round-bottom flask (250 mL), mechanical stirrer with hot plate, thermometer, pH meter, dropping funnel, N₂ gas cylinder with bubbler, beakers, bar magnet (NdFeB), oven, mortar and pestle.

**Chemicals (1 mark):** FeSO₄·7H₂O (1.20 g), FeCl₃·6H₂O (2.33 g), NaOH (2.0 M, ~17 mL), deoxygenated distilled water (50 mL), ethanol.

**Procedure (3 marks):**
1. Dissolve 1.20 g FeSO₄·7H₂O and 2.33 g FeCl₃·6H₂O in 50 mL deoxygenated DI water in the three-neck flask.
2. Purge the solution with N₂ gas for 15 minutes to remove dissolved O₂.
3. Heat to 60 °C with stirring at 800 rpm.
4. Add 2.0 M NaOH dropwise (≈17 mL) until pH reaches 10–11; a black precipitate forms.
5. Digest at 60 °C for 45 min (Ostwald ripening).
6. Separate the precipitate using an external bar magnet; decant the supernatant.
7. Wash with DI water (3×) and ethanol (2×) to remove Na₂SO₄ and NaCl.
8. Dry in a hot-air oven at 70 °C for 12 h.
9. Grind with mortar and pestle to obtain fine black magnetite powder.

**Reaction (1 mark):**
$$
\text{Fe}^{2+} + 2\text{Fe}^{3+} + 8\text{OH}^{-} \rightarrow \text{Fe}_{3}\text{O}_{4} \downarrow + 4\text{H}_{2}\text{O}
$$

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Scheme]**
**"Calculate the theoretical yield of Fe₃O₄ if 1.20 g of FeSO₄·7H₂O and 2.33 g of FeCl₃·6H₂O are used. If 0.86 g of dry powder is obtained, what is the percent yield?"**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Moles of Fe²⁺ from FeSO₄·7H₂O (1 mark):**
$$
n_{\text{Fe}^{2+}} = \frac{1.20\ \text{g}}{278.01\ \text{g/mol}} = 4.317 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{mol}
$$

**Step 2 — Moles of Fe³⁺ from FeCl₃·6H₂O (1 mark):**
$$
n_{\text{Fe}^{3+}} = \frac{2.33\ \text{g}}{270.30\ \text{g/mol}} = 8.620 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{mol}
$$

**Step 3 — Identify limiting reagent (1 mark):**
- Required Fe²⁺ per Fe³⁺: 1:2 → 8.620 × 10⁻³ ÷ 2 = 4.310 × 10⁻³ mol Fe²⁺ needed.
- Available Fe²⁺ = 4.317 × 10⁻³ mol.
- **Fe³⁺ is the limiting reagent** (marginally); Fe²⁺ is in slight excess.

**Step 4 — Moles of Fe₃O₄ produced (1 mark):**
- 2 mol Fe³⁺ → 1 mol Fe₃O₄.
- $n_{\text{Fe}_3\text{O}_4} = \frac{8.620 \times 10^{-3}}{2} = 4.310 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{mol}$

**Step 5 — Theoretical mass (1 mark):**
$$
m_{\text{theo}} = 4.310 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{mol} \times 231.53\ \text{g/mol} = 0.998\ \text{g}
$$

**Step 6 — Percent yield (2 marks):**
$$
\%\text{Yield} = \frac{0.86}{0.998} \times 100 = 86.2\ \%
$$

**Conclusion:** The synthesis achieved an **86.2 % yield**, which is excellent for a wet-chemical lab-scale co-precipitation.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Scheme]**
**"Explain the crystal structure of magnetite. Why is it called an 'inverse spinel'?"**

**Model Solution:**

- (2 marks) Magnetite crystallizes in the **cubic inverse-spinel structure** with space group Fd-3m and lattice parameter a ≈ 0.8396 nm.
- (2 marks) The O²⁻ ions form a cubic close-packed (FCC) array. The cations occupy the interstitial tetrahedral (A) and octahedral (B) sites.
- (1 mark) In a **normal spinel** (e.g., MgAl₂O₄), the divalent cation occupies A sites and the trivalent cation occupies B sites.
- (1 mark) In magnetite, the **inverse arrangement** places **Fe³⁺ ions split equally between A and B sites**, while **all Fe²⁺ ions occupy B sites**. Hence the name "inverse spinel."
- (1 mark) This arrangement produces antiferromagnetic coupling between A-site and B-site Fe³⁺ ions (spins cancel), leaving the Fe²⁺ ions as the sole contributors to the net magnetic moment — explaining magnetite's strong ferrimagnetism.

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Scheme]**
**"Discuss the role of magnetite particles in environmental engineering with three specific application examples."**

**Model Solution:**

**Application 1 — Arsenic Removal from Groundwater (2 marks):**
Magnetite nanoparticles (often functionalized with chitosan or Fe-Mn binary oxide) adsorb both As³⁺ and As⁵⁺ via ligand-exchange and electrostatic attraction. The loaded nanoparticles are then **recovered from water using a bar magnet**, avoiding the filtration bottleneck of conventional adsorbents. Removal efficiencies exceed 95 % at optimal pH (7–8).

**Application 2 — Heavy-Metal Removal (Pb²⁺, Cd²⁺, Cr⁶⁺) (2 marks):**
The high specific surface area of nano-magnetite (50–200 m²/g) provides abundant surface hydroxyl groups (≡Fe–OH) that bind divalent heavy metals through surface complexation. The spent magnetite can be regenerated by acid wash and reused for 5–10 cycles.

**Application 3 — Catalytic Degradation of Organic Dyes (Fenton-like Process) (2 marks):**
Magnetite catalyzes H₂O₂ to generate hydroxyl radicals (•OH), which mineralize organic dyes (methylene blue, rhodamine B) into CO₂ and H₂O. Unlike homogeneous Fenton (which requires acidic pH and produces iron sludge), magnetite-Fenton works at near-neutral pH and the catalyst is magnetically recovered.

**Conclusion (1 mark):**
Magnetite's unique combination of **magnetic recoverability + high surface area + catalytic activity** makes it a versatile platform for sustainable water-treatment technologies, aligning with circular-economy and green-chemistry principles central to the KTU 2024 environmental-engineering curriculum.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting the inert atmosphere:** Students often write the procedure but omit N₂/Ar purging, losing 1–2 marks. KTU 2024 explicitly tests the *reason* for inert conditions.
> 2. **Wrong stoichiometric ratio:** Writing 1:1 instead of 1:2 for Fe²⁺:Fe³⁺ is a fatal error — entire yield calculation collapses.
> 3. **Skipping the digestion/aging step:** Without Ostwald ripening, the particles are amorphous and weakly magnetic.
> 4. **Confusing units in yield calculation:** Mixing grams and moles, or forgetting to divide by molar mass, costs 1 mark.
> 5. **Not stating characterization techniques:** KTU expects mention of XRD (phase), SEM (morphology), VSM (magnetization), and BET (surface area) in any synthesis answer.
> 6. **Writing raw chemical formula without charge-balanced reaction:** Always include state symbols (↓ for precipitate) and balance OH⁻ and H₂O.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Bullets — Read this 30 minutes before the exam:**

- **Magnetite = Fe₃O₄ = FeO·Fe₂O₃**, an **inverse spinel** ferrite with the most powerful natural magnetism.
- **Standard synthesis route:** Co-precipitation from Fe²⁺ + Fe³⁺ salts in 1:2 molar ratio under alkaline (pH ≥ 9) and inert (N₂/Ar) conditions.
- **Key precursors:** FeSO₄·7H₂O (278.01 g/mol) + FeCl₃·6H₂O (270.30 g/mol); precipitant = NaOH or NH₄OH.
- **Master reaction:** $\text{Fe}^{2+} + 2\text{Fe}^{3+} + 8\text{OH}^{-} \rightarrow \text{Fe}_{3}\text{O}_{4} + 4\text{H}_{2}\text{O}$.
- **Inert atmosphere is mandatory** to prevent Fe²⁺ → Fe³⁺ oxidation; otherwise product becomes γ-Fe₂O₃ or α-FeOOH.
- **Digestion at 50–80 °C for 30–60 min** improves crystallinity (Ostwald ripening).
- **Magnetic separation using NdFeB bar magnet** is the signature clean-green step — no filtration required.
- **Washing protocol:** DI water (3×) → ethanol (2×) → oven dry at 70 °C → mortar grind.
- **Theoretical yield formula:** $m_{\text{theo}} = n_{\text{Fe}^{2+},\text{lim}} \times 231.53$ g/mol.
- **Percent yield formula:** $\%\text{Yield} = (m_{\text{actual}} / m_{\text{theo}}) \times 100$.
- **Particle size by Scherrer equation:** $D = K\lambda / (\beta\cos\theta)$; expect 5–20 nm for lab-synthesized nano-magnetite.
- **Environmental applications:** Arsenic/heavy-metal adsorption, dye degradation (Fenton-like), catalyst support, soil remediation.
- **Magnetite color:** Black (bulk) / Blackish-brown (nano); the powder is **strongly attracted to a bar magnet** — this is the simplest qualitative confirmation test.
- **Curie temperature (858 K):** Above this, magnetite loses ferrimagnetism and becomes paramagnetic — important for high-temperature applications.
- **Net magnetic moment per formula unit:** 4 μB (from 8 Fe²⁺ ions in B sites, with Fe³⁺ spins canceling antiferromagnetically).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Preparation of Pigments
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCHL307 - CHEMICAL TECHNOLOGY AND ENVIRONMENTAL ENGINEERING LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Preparation of Pigments

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Pigment (KTU 2024 Definition):** A pigment is a finely divided, insoluble, colour-imparting solid particulate material that is dispersed in a medium (binder/polymer/oil) to provide colour by selectively absorbing and reflecting specific wavelengths of visible light, **without undergoing chemical reaction with the substrate.**

In the context of the KTU PCCHL307 syllabus (Module Experiment 2), "Preparation of Pigments" refers to the **wet-chemical laboratory synthesis** of classical inorganic pigments — typically **chrome yellow (lead chromate)**, **Prussian blue (iron hexacyanoferrate)**, and **red lead (minium, Pb₃O₄)** — followed by filtration, washing, drying, and reporting of practical yield.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Simple Analogy — The "Tinted Window" Concept:**
> Imagine a coloured glass window that lets only red light pass through while absorbing green, blue, and yellow wavelengths. A pigment behaves exactly like this — but instead of being a transparent glass, it is a **powder of microscopic coloured particles**. When you paint a wall with a pigment dispersed in oil, the pigment particles sit on the surface like millions of tiny tinted windows, each absorbing part of the white sunlight and reflecting back only the colour you see.

### 1.2.1 Why "Insoluble" is the Key Word
- A pigment must **not dissolve** in the binder (oil, water, polymer), otherwise it would behave like a **dye** (which is a soluble colourant).
- Insolubility ensures the colour stays on the surface and does not bleed or migrate.
- This is why pigments are precipitated as fine solids in water and then filtered, washed, and dried — the insolubility is engineered during preparation.

### 1.2.2 Standard Pigment Metrics (KTU-Important)

> [!NOTE]
> **Three metrics the examiner expects you to state explicitly:**
> - **Particle Size:** typically **0.01 – 50 μm** (optimum colour strength at 0.1 – 10 μm).
> - **Refractive Index:** must be **> 1.5** for good hiding power (opacity).
> - **Oil Absorption Value:** typically **15 – 75 g oil / 100 g pigment** (lower is better for most paints).

## 1.3 Classification of Pigments Prepared in PCCHL307 Lab

| Chromate | Chrome Yellow | PbCrO₄ | Bright Yellow | High — frequently asked |
| Ferrocyanide | Prussian Blue | Fe₄[Fe(CN)₆]₃ | Deep Blue | High — frequently asked |

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Preparation of Chrome Yellow (Lead Chromate, PbCrO₄)

### 2.1.1 Underlying Chemistry — The "Why"
Lead chromate is one of the brightest yellow pigments ever synthesised because the **Pb²⁺–CrO₄²⁻** bond creates a charge-transfer complex that strongly absorbs blue/violet light (~ 450 nm) and reflects yellow (~ 580 nm). The insolubility comes from the very low Ksp of PbCrO₄ (~ 1.8 × 10⁻¹⁴).

### 2.1.2 Reaction Equation (from Potassium Dichromate Route)
The preferred lab route uses **K₂Cr₂O₇** because it is more readily available and cheaper than K₂CrO₄. Lead nitrate is the lead source.

$$2\,Pb(NO_3)_2 \,+\, K_2Cr_2O_7 \,+\, H_2O \longrightarrow 2\,PbCrO_4\downarrow \,+\, 2\,KNO_3 \,+\, 2\,HNO_3$$

### 2.1.3 Reaction Equation (from Potassium Chromate Route)
$$Pb(NO_3)_2 \,+\, K_2CrO_4 \longrightarrow PbCrO_4\downarrow \,+\, 2\,KNO_3$$

### 2.1.4 Operational Step-by-Step Logic
- **Step 1 — Dissolution:** Both reactants (Pb(NO₃)₂ and K₂Cr₂O₇) are dissolved separately in **distilled water** to form clear solutions.
- **Step 2 — Precipitation:** The potassium dichromate solution is slowly added to the lead nitrate solution at **room temperature** with continuous stirring. A **bright lemon-yellow precipitate** of PbCrO₄ forms immediately.
- **Step 3 — pH Control (if using dichromate route):** The liberated HNO₃ must be neutralised by adding **sodium acetate (CH₃COONa)** because excess acid redissolves PbCrO₄:
  $$PbCrO_4 \,+\, 2\,H^+ \rightleftharpoons Pb^{2+} \,+\, H_2CrO_4$$
- **Step 4 — Digestion:** The precipitate is allowed to stand for 15–30 minutes to allow crystal growth (improves hiding power).
- **Step 5 — Filtration:** Filtered through **Whatman No. 40 filter paper** using a Buchner funnel under vacuum.
- **Step 6 — Washing:** Washed repeatedly with hot distilled water until the filtrate is free of NO₃⁻ ions (test with diphenylamine or brown-ring test).
- **Step 7 — Drying:** Dried in a hot-air oven at **100–110 °C** for 1–2 hours.
- **Step 8 — Weighing:** Cooled in a desiccator and weighed to constant mass.

---

## 2.2 Preparation of Prussian Blue (Iron Hexacyanoferrate, Fe₄[Fe(CN)₆]₃)

### 2.2.1 Underlying Chemistry — The "Why"
Prussian blue is a **mixed-valence iron cyanide complex**. The intense blue colour arises from **intervalence charge transfer** between Fe²⁺ and Fe³⁺ ions bridged by CN⁻ ligands, absorbing red/orange light (~ 680 nm) and reflecting deep blue (~ 440 nm). It is sometimes called "Berlin Blue" or "Paris Blue."

### 2.2.2 Reaction Equation (Two-Stage Synthesis)

**Stage 1 — Formation of white precipitate (Berlin White):**

$$2\,FeSO_4 \,+\, K_4[Fe(CN)_6] \longrightarrow Fe_2[Fe(CN)_6]\downarrow \,+\, 2\,K_2SO_4$$

**Stage 2 — Oxidation to Prussian Blue:**

$$6\,Fe_2[Fe(CN)_6] \,+\, O_2 \,+\, 6\,H_2O \longrightarrow 2\,Fe_4[Fe(CN)_6]_3 \,+\, 4\,Fe(OH)_3$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Alternative single-stage route (more commonly used in labs):**
> Ferrous sulphate is reacted with potassium ferrocyanide, and the mixture is simultaneously oxidised using **concentrated HNO₃** or **H₂O₂** to convert Fe²⁺ in the complex to Fe³⁺:
>
> $$3\,Fe^{2+} \,+\, 2\,[Fe(CN)_6]^{4-} \longrightarrow Fe_3[Fe(CN)_6]_2 \text{ (Turnbull's blue, now known to be Prussian blue)}$$

### 2.2.3 Operational Step-by-Step Logic
- **Step 1:** Prepare a solution of **FeSO₄·7H₂O** (Mohr's salt gives better results) in distilled water acidified with a few drops of dilute H₂SO₄ (to prevent premature oxidation).
- **Step 2:** Prepare a solution of **K₄[Fe(CN)₆]** (potassium ferrocyanide) in distilled water.
- **Step 3:** Add the ferrocyanide solution slowly to the ferrous sulphate solution with stirring. A **white precipitate of Fe₂[Fe(CN)₆]** forms.
- **Step 4:** Oxidise by adding **boiling concentrated HNO₃** dropwise, OR by adding **30 % H₂O₂**, while heating gently. The precipitate turns **deep Prussian blue**.
- **Step 5:** Boil for 5–10 minutes to complete oxidation, then cool to room temperature.
- **Step 6:** Filter, wash thoroughly with hot distilled water (until sulphate-free), and dry in an oven at **100–110 °C**.

---

## 2.3 Preparation of Red Lead (Minium, Pb₃O₄)

### 2.3.1 Underlying Chemistry — The "Why"
Red lead is a **mixed-oxide** containing both Pb²⁺ and Pb⁴⁺ (formally 2 PbO · PbO₂). The vivid red-orange colour arises from the unique mixed-valence electronic structure, making it invaluable historically in anti-corrosive primers for iron and steel.

### 2.3.2 Reaction Equation

$$6\,PbO \,+\, O_2 \xrightarrow{450\,^\circ C} 2\,Pb_3O_4$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical temperature control:**
> - Below **450 °C** → oxidation is incomplete (product is greyish).
> - Above **480 °C** → Pb₃O₄ decomposes back to PbO (loses oxygen).
> - Therefore the temperature window is **narrow (445–475 °C)**.

### 2.3.3 Operational Step-by-Step Logic
- **Step 1:** Take **litharge (PbO)** in a porcelain crucible.
- **Step 2:** Place in a muffle furnace preheated to **450 °C**.
- **Step 3:** Maintain temperature for **4–6 hours**, with intermittent stirring using a glass rod.
- **Step 4:** Cool to room temperature inside the furnace (do not open the furnace hot — thermal shock cracks the crucible).
- **Step 5:** Weigh and record the mass gain (mass gain = oxygen absorbed).

---

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Master Cheat Table)

> [!NOTE]
> **This single table covers every calculation you will need in the ESE viva and written exam for this experiment.**

| Quantity | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| Theoretical Yield (mass) | $m_{th} = \dfrac{n_{product} \times M_{product}}{stoich.\,ratio}$ | grams (g) | Based on limiting reagent |
| Practical Yield (mass) | $m_{prac} = m_{final} - m_{empty\,dish}$ | grams (g) | Weighed after drying to constant mass |
| Percentage Yield | $\%Y = \dfrac{m_{prac}}{m_{th}} \times 100$ | percent (%) | Typical good lab yield: 80–95 % |
| Number of Moles | $n = \dfrac{m}{M}$ | mol | $M$ = molar mass |
| Limiting Reagent | Smallest $\dfrac{n}{stoich.\,coeff.}$ | — | Identify first before any calculation |
| Mass of Reagent Needed | $m_R = \dfrac{\nu_R}{\nu_P} \times n_P \times M_R$ | grams | For desired $n_P$ moles of product |
| Stoichiometric Excess | $\%\,excess = \dfrac{n_{actual}-n_{required}}{n_{required}}\times 100$ | percent (%) | Common: 5–10 % excess of one reagent |
| Mass Gain (Red Lead) | $\Delta m = m_{final} - m_{initial}$ | grams | Equals mass of O₂ absorbed |
| Purity | $\%\,purity = \dfrac{\%Y \times \%\,reactant\,assay}{100}$ | percent (%) | Reagent may not be 100 % pure |
| Molar Mass of PbCrO₄ | $207.2 + 52.0 + (4 \times 16.0)$ | = **323.2 g/mol** | Use this exact value |
| Molar Mass of Pb(NO₃)₂ | $207.2 + 2(14 + 48)$ | = **331.2 g/mol** | For calculation of excess reagent |
| Molar Mass of K₂Cr₂O₇ | $2(39.1) + 2(52.0) + 7(16.0)$ | = **294.2 g/mol** | For stoichiometry |

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> **Where these pigments are used today:**
> - **Chrome Yellow:** Traffic-line paints, school-bus yellow, school-bag coatings (now regulated due to Pb toxicity — being replaced by Bismuth Vanadate).
> - **Prussian Blue:** Used as the **antidote for thallium and caesium-137 poisoning** (radiation emergency medicine); also used in blueprinting, inks, and as a paint pigment.
> - **Red Lead:** Historical anti-corrosive primer for structural steel (now replaced by zinc-rich primers due to lead toxicity).
> - **Inorganic pigments in general:** Automotive paints, plastics, ceramics, printing inks, cosmetics, and construction materials constitute a global market exceeding **USD 30 billion** annually.
