# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Derivation #1: Stoichiometric Calculation for Chrome Yellow

### Problem Statement
**Prepare 10 g of pure PbCrO₄ using lead nitrate Pb(NO₃)₂ and potassium dichromate K₂Cr₂O₇ (assume 100 % purity). Calculate the masses of both reactants required.**

### Step 1 — Write the Balanced Equation

$$2\,Pb(NO_3)_2 \,+\, K_2Cr_2O_7 \,+\, H_2O \longrightarrow 2\,PbCrO_4\downarrow \,+\, 2\,KNO_3 \,+\, 2\,HNO_3$$

**Explanation of logic:** From the equation, 2 moles of Pb(NO₃)₂ react with 1 mole of K₂Cr₂O₇ to give 2 moles of PbCrO₄. Therefore the **stoichiometric mole ratio of Pb(NO₃)₂ : K₂Cr₂O₇ : PbCrO₄ is 2 : 1 : 2**.

### Step 2 — Compute Molar Masses (M)

We need the atomic masses: Pb = 207.2, Cr = 52.0, K = 39.1, N = 14.0, O = 16.0.

For PbCrO₄:

$$M_{PbCrO_4} = 207.2 \,+\, 52.0 \,+\, (4 \times 16.0) = 207.2 \,+\, 52.0 \,+\, 64.0 = 323.2 \text{ g/mol}$$

For Pb(NO₃)₂:

$$M_{Pb(NO_3)_2} = 207.2 \,+\, 2(14.0 \,+\, 3 \times 16.0) = 207.2 \,+\, 2(62.0) = 331.2 \text{ g/mol}$$

For K₂Cr₂O₇:

$$M_{K_2Cr_2O_7} = 2(39.1) \,+\, 2(52.0) \,+\, 7(16.0) = 78.2 \,+\, 104.0 \,+\, 112.0 = 294.2 \text{ g/mol}$$

### Step 3 — Calculate Moles of Product Required

We want 10 g of PbCrO₄.

$$n_{PbCrO_4} = \dfrac{m}{M} = \dfrac{10 \text{ g}}{323.2 \text{ g/mol}} = 0.03094 \text{ mol}$$

### Step 4 — Apply Stoichiometric Ratio

From the balanced equation, $\nu_{Pb(NO_3)_2} = \nu_{PbCrO_4}$ (same coefficient = 2 on both sides).

$$n_{Pb(NO_3)_2} = n_{PbCrO_4} = 0.03094 \text{ mol}$$

For K₂Cr₂O₇, ratio is 1 : 2 with respect to product:

$$n_{K_2Cr_2O_7} = \dfrac{1}{2} \times n_{PbCrO_4} = \dfrac{1}{2} \times 0.03094 = 0.01547 \text{ mol}$$

### Step 5 — Convert Moles to Required Masses

Mass of Pb(NO₃)₂ required:

$$m_{Pb(NO_3)_2} = n \times M = 0.03094 \times 331.2 = 10.247 \text{ g}$$

Mass of K₂Cr₂O₇ required:

$$m_{K_2Cr_2O_7} = n \times M = 0.01547 \times 294.2 = 4.551 \text{ g}$$

### Step 6 — Apply 5 % Practical Excess (Lab Practice)

In the lab, we always take a small excess of the **costlier reagent** (here K₂Cr₂O₇ is costlier than Pb(NO₃)₂) to ensure complete reaction.

$$m_{K_2Cr_2O_7}^{practical} = 4.551 \times 1.05 = 4.779 \text{ g}$$

### Final Answer
- Pb(NO₃)₂ required = **10.25 g**
- K₂Cr₂O₇ required = **4.55 g (theoretical)** or **4.78 g (with 5 % excess)**

---

## 3.2 Worked Derivation #2: Percentage Yield for Prussian Blue

### Problem Statement
**From 25.0 g of FeSO₄·7H₂O (Mohr's salt) and 18.0 g of K₄[Fe(CN)₆]·3H₂O, the student obtains 12.8 g of dry Prussian Blue (Fe₄[Fe(CN)₆]₃, M = 859.2 g/mol). Calculate the percentage yield.**

### Step 1 — Molar Masses Required

- FeSO₄·7H₂O = 55.85 + 32.07 + 4(16.0) + 7(18.0) = **278.0 g/mol**
- K₄[Fe(CN)₆]·3H₂O = 4(39.1) + 55.85 + 6(12.0 + 14.0) + 3(18.0) = **422.4 g/mol**
- Fe₄[Fe(CN)₆]₃ = 4(55.85) + 3(55.85) + 18(12.0 + 14.0) = **859.2 g/mol**

### Step 2 — Moles of Each Reactant

$$n_{FeSO_4 \cdot 7H_2O} = \dfrac{25.0}{278.0} = 0.0899 \text{ mol}$$

$$n_{K_4[Fe(CN)_6] \cdot 3H_2O} = \dfrac{18.0}{422.4} = 0.0426 \text{ mol}$$

### Step 3 — Identify Limiting Reagent

From the equation $2\,FeSO_4 + K_4[Fe(CN)_6] \rightarrow Fe_2[Fe(CN)_6]$, the mole ratio is **2 : 1**. Required ratio for **0.0426 mol** of ferrocyanide = 2 × 0.0426 = **0.0852 mol of FeSO₄**.

Available FeSO₄ = 0.0899 mol (slightly more than 0.0852 mol).
Available K₄[Fe(CN)₆] = 0.0426 mol.

Therefore **K₄[Fe(CN)₆] is the limiting reagent** (less than required to consume all FeSO₄).

### Step 4 — Theoretical Moles of Intermediate White Complex

$$n_{Fe_2[Fe(CN)_6]} = n_{K_4[Fe(CN)_6]} = 0.0426 \text{ mol}$$

### Step 5 — Theoretical Moles of Final Prussian Blue

From $6\,Fe_2[Fe(CN)_6] + O_2 + 6H_2O \rightarrow 2\,Fe_4[Fe(CN)_6]_3 + 4Fe(OH)_3$, ratio is 6 : 2 = 3 : 1.

$$n_{Prussian\,Blue}^{th} = \dfrac{2}{6} \times 0.0426 = 0.0142 \text{ mol}$$

### Step 6 — Theoretical Mass of Prussian Blue

$$m_{Prussian\,Blue}^{th} = 0.0142 \times 859.2 = 12.20 \text{ g}$$

### Step 7 — Percentage Yield

$$\%\,Y = \dfrac{m_{prac}}{m_{th}} \times 100 = \dfrac{12.8}{12.20} \times 100 = 104.9\,\%$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interpretation:** A yield **> 100 %** is a classic KTU viva question. It means the product contains **residual moisture or unreacted starting material** that was not fully washed out — drying was incomplete. The student should re-dry to constant mass.

---

## 3.3 Worked Derivation #3: Mass Gain in Red Lead Preparation

### Problem Statement
**5.00 g of litharge (PbO) is heated in a muffle furnace at 450 °C for 5 hours. The final mass of red lead obtained is 5.18 g. Calculate the percentage conversion.**

### Step 1 — Balanced Equation

$$6\,PbO \,+\, O_2 \longrightarrow 2\,Pb_3O_4$$

### Step 2 — Theoretical Stoichiometry

Mass of O₂ required to convert 6 mol PbO → 2 mol Pb₃O₄:

- Mass of 6 mol PbO = 6 × 223.2 = 1339.2 g
- Mass of 2 mol Pb₃O₄ = 2 × 685.6 = 1371.2 g
- **Mass gain per 1339.2 g of PbO = 1371.2 − 1339.2 = 32.0 g (i.e., 1 mol O₂)**

### Step 3 — Theoretical Mass Gain for 5.00 g PbO

$$\Delta m_{th} = \dfrac{32.0}{1339.2} \times 5.00 = 0.1195 \text{ g}$$

### Step 4 — Actual Mass Gain

$$\Delta m_{actual} = 5.18 - 5.00 = 0.18 \text{ g}$$

### Step 5 — Percentage Conversion

$$\%\,conv = \dfrac{\Delta m_{actual}}{\Delta m_{th}} \times 100 = \dfrac{0.18}{0.1195} \times 100 = 150.6\,\%$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Why the result exceeds 100 %:** This indicates either (a) the crucible absorbed moisture, (b) some PbO was already partially oxidised before weighing, or (c) weighing error. The student must report this honestly in the record book — fabricating values is treated as malpractice by KTU.

## 4.1 Functional Process Flow — Generic Pigment Preparation Lab Setup

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Raw Chemical Reagent A e.g. Pb(NO3)2 dissolved in distilled water] --> C[Reactor Beaker 250 mL with magnetic stirrer]
    B[Raw Chemical Reagent B e.g. K2Cr2O7 dissolved in distilled water] --> C
    C --> D{Precipitation Reaction at room temperature with stirring}
    D --> E[Coloured Pigment Precipitate formed e.g. PbCrO4 yellow]
    E --> F[Digestion Stage stand for 15 to 30 minutes]
    F --> G[Vacuum Filtration using Buchner funnel and Whatman 40 paper]
    G --> H[Washing with hot distilled water until filtrate is ion free]
    H --> I[Drying in hot air oven at 100 to 110 degree Celsius for 1 to 2 hours]
    I --> J[Cooling in desiccator for 30 minutes]
    J --> K[Weighing to constant mass]
    K --> L[Calculate Percentage Yield and Report]

    subgraph QC1[Quality Control Tests]
        K --> M[Visual Colour Check]
        K --> N[Spot Test for purity]
        K --> O[Particle Size Sieve Analysis optional]
    end
```

## 4.2 Block Architecture — Prussian Blue Two-Stage Synthesis

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1[Stage 1 Reactants: FeSO4 solution + K4 Fe CN 6 solution] --> M1[Mixing under N2 atmosphere or acidified to prevent premature oxidation]
    M1 --> P1[White Precipitate: Fe2 Fe CN 6 Berlin White]
    P1 --> S2[Stage 2 Oxidation: Add boiling HNO3 or H2O2 + heat]
    S2 --> P2[Deep Blue Precipitate: Fe4 Fe CN 6 3 Prussian Blue]
    P2 --> F[Filter under vacuum]
    F --> W[Wash with hot water until sulphate free]
    W --> D[Dry at 110 degree C]
    D --> W2[Weigh and calculate percentage yield]
```

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    R1[Reagent Class 1: Lead salts] --> P1[Chrome Yellow PbCrO4 via precipitation]
    R1 --> P2[Red Lead Pb3O4 via thermal oxidation at 450 C]
    R2[Reagent Class 2: Iron salts] --> P3[Prussian Blue Fe4 Fe CN 6 3 via precipitation then oxidation]
    R3[Reagent Class 3: Other metal salts] --> P4[Cobalt Blue, Chrome Green, Iron Oxide Red]
```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper]
**Define a pigment. How is it different from a dye? Give one example of each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
> A pigment is a **finely divided, insoluble solid colourant** that is dispersed in a medium to provide colour by selectively absorbing and reflecting visible light. A dye, in contrast, is a **soluble colourant** that dissolves in the medium and imparts colour through chemical or physical bonding at the molecular level. **Example of pigment:** Chrome yellow (PbCrO₄). **Example of dye:** Methyl orange. *(1 mark for definition of pigment, 1 mark for difference, 1 mark for examples.)*

> $6\,Fe_2[Fe(CN)_6] \,+\, O_2 \,+\, 6\,H_2O \rightarrow 2\,Fe_4[Fe(CN)_6]_3 \,+\, 4\,Fe(OH)_3$
>

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

### Question A — Chrome Yellow (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022, Modified]**

**(a)** Write the balanced chemical equation for the preparation of chrome yellow from lead nitrate and potassium dichromate. Describe the laboratory procedure with all safety precautions. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

> **Balanced Equation:**
> $2\,Pb(NO_3)_2 \,+\, K_2Cr_2O_7 \,+\, H_2O \rightarrow 2\,PbCrO_4\downarrow \,+\, 2\,KNO_3 \,+\, 2\,HNO_3$
>
> **Procedure (5 sub-steps):**
> 1. Weigh accurately 10.25 g of lead nitrate and dissolve in 50 mL distilled water in a 250 mL beaker. *(1 mark)*
> 2. Weigh accurately 4.55 g of potassium dichromate and dissolve in 50 mL distilled water in a separate beaker. *(1 mark)*
> 3. Add sodium acetate (~ 2 g) to the lead nitrate solution to buffer against the HNO₃ that will be liberated. *(1 mark)*
> 4. Slowly add the dichromate solution to the lead nitrate solution with constant stirring using a glass rod. A bright yellow precipitate forms immediately. *(1 mark)*
> 5. Allow the precipitate to settle (digestion) for 20 minutes, then filter through a Buchner funnel using Whatman No. 40 filter paper under vacuum. *(1 mark)*
> 6. Wash the precipitate with hot distilled water until the filtrate shows no turbidity with dilute AgNO₃ (i.e., free of Cl⁻ and NO₃⁻). *(1 mark)*
> 7. Dry the precipitate in a hot air oven at 100–110 °C for 1–2 hours, cool in a desiccator, and weigh to constant mass. *(1 mark)*
>
> **Safety Precautions:** Wear gloves and safety goggles (lead and chromate are toxic). Perform in a fume hood. Wash hands thoroughly after the experiment. Dispose of lead filtrate in the labelled heavy-metal waste container, NOT down the drain.

**(b)** From 16.56 g of Pb(NO₃)₂ and 8.83 g of K₂Cr₂O₇ (both 100 % pure), calculate the theoretical yield of PbCrO₄. If the actual yield is 14.20 g, calculate the percentage yield. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

> **Step 1 — Moles of each reactant:**
> $n_{Pb(NO_3)_2} = \dfrac{16.56}{331.2} = 0.0500$ mol
> $n_{K_2Cr_2O_7} = \dfrac{8.83}{294.2} = 0.0300$ mol
>
> **Step 2 — Limiting reagent check (from equation, 2 : 1 ratio needed):**
> For 0.0300 mol K₂Cr₂O₇, required Pb(NO₃)₂ = 2 × 0.0300 = 0.0600 mol.
> But available Pb(NO₃)₂ = 0.0500 mol < 0.0600 mol.
> Therefore **Pb(NO₃)₂ is the limiting reagent.** *(2 marks)*
>
> **Step 3 — Theoretical moles of PbCrO₄:**
> From stoichiometry, $n_{PbCrO_4} = n_{Pb(NO_3)_2} = 0.0500$ mol. *(1 mark)*
>
> **Step 4 — Theoretical mass:**
> $m_{th} = 0.0500 \times 323.2 = 16.16$ g. *(2 marks)*
>
> **Step 5 — Percentage yield:**
> $\%Y = \dfrac{14.20}{16.16} \times 100 = 87.87\,\%$. *(2 marks)*

---

### Question B — Prussian Blue (14 Marks) *(Internal Choice)*

**[KTU University Exam — June 2023, Modified]**

**(a)** Describe with a neat labelled diagram the laboratory preparation of Prussian blue from ferrous sulphate and potassium ferrocyanide. State the role of HNO₃ in the synthesis. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

> **Diagram:** Refer to **Section 4.2 (Block Architecture — Prussian Blue Two-Stage Synthesis)** of these notes for the schematic. *(2 marks for diagram)*
>
> **Procedure:**
> 1. Dissolve 14 g of FeSO₄·7H₂O in 50 mL of distilled water acidified with 2 mL dilute H₂SO₄ in a 250 mL beaker. *(1 mark)*
> 2. Dissolve 10 g of K₄[Fe(CN)₆]·3H₂O in 50 mL of distilled water in a separate beaker. *(1 mark)*
> 3. Slowly add the ferrocyanide solution to the ferrous sulphate solution with stirring. A **white precipitate of Fe₂[Fe(CN)₆]** (Berlin White) forms. *(1 mark)*
> 4. Add **boiling concentrated HNO₃** (~ 10 mL) dropwise to oxidise the white complex to Prussian blue. Heat the mixture gently for 10 minutes with constant stirring — the precipitate turns **deep Prussian blue**. *(1 mark)*
> 5. Cool, filter under vacuum, wash with hot water until sulphate-free, dry at 110 °C, cool in desiccator, and weigh. *(1 mark)*
>
> **Role of HNO₃:** HNO₃ acts as an **oxidising agent**, converting the Fe²⁺ ions in the white complex Fe₂[Fe(CN)₆] to Fe³⁺ ions, producing the mixed-valence Prussian blue Fe₄[Fe(CN)₆]₃. Without oxidation, the pigment remains white/off-white and is commercially useless. *(1 mark)*

**(b)** 27.8 g of Mohr's salt (FeSO₄·7H₂O, M = 278 g/mol) is reacted with excess K₄[Fe(CN)₆]. What is the maximum mass of Prussian blue (M = 859.2 g/mol) that can theoretically be obtained? **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

> **Step 1 — Moles of Mohr's salt:**
> $n = \dfrac{27.8}{278} = 0.100$ mol
>
> **Step 2 — From Stage 1 equation**, 2 mol FeSO₄ → 1 mol Fe₂[Fe(CN)₆]:
> $n_{Fe_2[Fe(CN)_6]} = \dfrac{0.100}{2} = 0.0500$ mol *(2 marks)*
>
> **Step 3 — From Stage 2 equation**, 6 mol Fe₂[Fe(CN)₆] → 2 mol Fe₄[Fe(CN)₆]₃ (ratio 3 : 1):
> $n_{Prussian\,Blue} = \dfrac{0.0500}{3} = 0.01667$ mol *(2 marks)*
>
> **Step 4 — Theoretical mass:**
> $m_{th} = 0.01667 \times 859.2 = 14.32$ g *(2 marks)*
>
> **Step 5 — Significant figure reporting:** Since input data has 3 significant figures, report final answer as **14.3 g**. *(1 mark)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting to identify the limiting reagent first** — Many students directly apply the 2 : 1 ratio from the equation to whichever reactant they wrote first. This is wrong: ALWAYS compute the moles of both, then divide by stoichiometric coefficient to find the smallest quotient.
> 2. **Wrong molar mass for PbCrO₄** — A common error is using 207.2 + 52 + 32 (i.e., only 2 oxygens). PbCrO₄ has FOUR oxygens; molar mass = 323.2 g/mol.
> 3. **Reporting yield > 100 % without explanation** — This always indicates incomplete drying or unreacted starting material; ALWAYS comment on it.
> 4. **Skipping the buffer (sodium acetate) step** in the chrome yellow preparation using the dichromate route — examiners specifically look for this.
> 5. **Writing "Prussian blue is formed directly"** — Students forget the **two-stage nature** (white intermediate then oxidation). Always mention both stages.
> 6. **Ignoring safety** — Lead and chromate are toxic; **NO safety note = 1-mark deduction** in KTU 2024 valuation.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **High-density revision list — read this 30 minutes before the exam:**
>
> - **Pigment = insoluble solid colourant**; Dye = soluble colourant. (Single most important distinction.)
> - Three pigments covered in Module 2: **Chrome Yellow (PbCrO₄), Prussian Blue (Fe₄[Fe(CN)₆]₃), Red Lead (Pb₃O₄).**
> - Chrome Yellow is prepared by **double decomposition (precipitation)** in aqueous medium at room temperature.
> - The balanced equation for chrome yellow from dichromate is: $2\,Pb(NO_3)_2 + K_2Cr_2O_7 + H_2O \rightarrow 2\,PbCrO_4 + 2\,KNO_3 + 2\,HNO_3$.
> - Always add **sodium acetate buffer** when using the dichromate route to neutralise liberated HNO₃.
> - Prussian Blue synthesis is a **two-stage process**: first form Berlin White (Fe₂[Fe(CN)₆]), then oxidise to Fe₄[Fe(CN)₆]₃ using HNO₃ or H₂O₂.
> - Red Lead is prepared by **dry thermal oxidation** of PbO at **450 °C** (NOT higher — it decomposes above 480 °C).
> - **Molar masses to memorise:** PbCrO₄ = 323.2; Pb(NO₃)₂ = 331.2; K₂Cr₂O₇ = 294.2; Fe₄[Fe(CN)₆]₃ = 859.2 g/mol.
> - **Percentage yield formula:** $\%Y = (m_{prac}/m_{th}) \times 100$. Yields > 100 % = incomplete drying or impurities.
> - **Limiting reagent rule:** smallest value of (moles / stoichiometric coefficient) determines the limiting reagent.
> - **Stoichiometric excess of 5–10 %** is standard practice for the costlier reagent.
> - **Filtration:** always use **Buchner funnel + Whatman No. 40** under vacuum.
> - **Washing end-point:** test wash filtrate with AgNO₃ for absence of halides / NO₃⁻.
> - **Drying:** 100–110 °C in hot air oven; **cool in desiccator** before weighing (hot sample gives erroneous low mass).
> - **Safety:** Lead and chromate are toxic — wear gloves, work in fume hood, dispose in heavy-metal waste.
> - **Key industrial uses:** Chrome Yellow → traffic paints; Prussian Blue → antidote for Tl/Cs poisoning; Red Lead → anti-corrosive primer.
> - **Particle size of pigment:** 0.01–50 μm (optimum colour strength at 0.1–10 μm).
> - **Refractive index > 1.5** is required for good hiding power.
> - **Oil absorption value:** 15–75 g oil per 100 g pigment (lower = better for most paints).
> - The colour of chrome yellow arises from **charge-transfer absorption at ~450 nm** (blue/violet absorbed, yellow reflected).
> - The colour of Prussian blue arises from **intervalence charge transfer** between Fe²⁺ and Fe³⁺ (red/orange absorbed, blue reflected).
> - Always report **observations during the experiment** (e.g., "white precipitate formed, turned blue on adding HNO₃") in the record book — this carries marks.
> - **Graph plotting:** Plot yield (%) vs. reaction time / pH / temperature where applicable (KTU viva favourite).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Study of Analytical Instruments: Spectrophotometer, pH Meter, Gas Chromatograph, High Performance Liquid Chromatography (HPLC), and Total Organic Carbon Analyser (TOC)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCHL307 - CHEMICAL TECHNOLOGY AND ENVIRONMENTAL ENGINEERING LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Study of Analytical Instruments: Spectrophotometer, pH Meter, Gas Chromatograph, High Performance Liquid Chromatography (HPLC), and Total Organic Carbon Analyser (TOC)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

### 1.1 Spectrophotometer

**Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology):**
A spectrophotometer is a precision analytical instrument used to measure the intensity of light absorbed or transmitted by a sample at specific wavelengths. It operates on the principle of the **Beer-Lambert Law**, where the absorbance of monochromatic light passing through a solution is directly proportional to the concentration of the absorbing species.

**Conceptual Analogy:**
Imagine you are standing at the entrance of a foggy tunnel with a flashlight. The farther the light travels into the fog, the dimmer it becomes because the fog particles "absorb" or scatter the light. A spectrophotometer works similarly — it shines a known light beam through a liquid sample and measures how much light "survives" the journey. The thicker/more concentrated the colored solution, the less light reaches the other side.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Concept:** Spectrophotometry is a non-destructive, quantitative analytical technique widely used for determining concentrations of metal ions, organic compounds, and biological molecules in water quality analysis.

---

### 1.2 pH Meter

**Formal Definition:**
A pH meter is an electrochemical instrument that measures the hydrogen ion activity (acidity or alkalinity) of a solution using a **glass electrode** (sensing electrode) and a **reference electrode**. The potential difference between these two electrodes follows the **Nernst Equation**.

**Conceptual Analogy:**
Think of pH measurement like weighing something on a balance scale. One side (the glass electrode) responds to H⁺ ions in the unknown solution, while the other side (the reference electrode) stays constant like a fixed weight. The "tilt" between them tells you how acidic or basic the solution is — analogous to reading weight from a scale.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Reference Scale:** pH ranges from **0 (strongly acidic)** to **14 (strongly alkaline)**, with **pH = 7** being neutral (pure water at **25 °C / 298.15 K**).

---

### 1.3 Gas Chromatograph (GC)

**Formal Definition:**
A Gas Chromatograph is a separation analytical instrument that vaporizes a sample and separates its components by distributing them between a **mobile phase (carrier gas)** and a **stationary phase (column coating)**. Each compound elutes at a characteristic **retention time (tR)** and is detected by a suitable detector.

**Conceptual Analogy:**
Picture a long hallway race with multiple runners of different fitness levels. The faster runners (volatile compounds) sprint ahead and reach the finish line first; the slower ones (less volatile) lag behind. By recording the time each runner finishes, you can identify each person. GC works the same way — compounds "race" through the column, and their exit times identify them.

---

### 1.4 High Performance Liquid Chromatography (HPLC)

**Formal Definition:**
HPLC is a chromatographic technique that separates, identifies, and quantifies components in a liquid mixture by forcing a pressurized liquid mobile phase (solvent) through a column packed with solid stationary phase particles (typically **3–5 µm** silica).

**Conceptual Analogy:**
Imagine a crowd of mixed marbles (small, medium, large) being poured through a stack of sieves with varying hole sizes. Each marble finds the sieve that matches its size and stops there, allowing finer separation. HPLC similarly "filters" molecules by their chemical affinity for the stationary phase.

---

### 1.5 Total Organic Carbon Analyser (TOC)

**Formal Definition:**
A TOC analyser measures the total amount of organic carbon present in a water sample by oxidizing organic compounds to CO₂, which is then detected quantitatively. The relationship is:

$$
\text{TOC} = \text{TC} - \text{TIC}
$$

where TC = Total Carbon and TIC = Total Inorganic Carbon.

**Conceptual Analogy:**
Think of a fireplace burning a piece of wood. All the carbon in the wood converts to CO₂ gas that escapes through the chimney. If you measure the CO₂ produced, you know how much carbon the wood contained. A TOC analyser does the same thing chemically — it "burns" the dissolved organics in water and counts the CO₂.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Threshold:** Drinking water should have **TOC < 2 mg/L** as per WHO guidelines. Industrial wastewater effluents are often regulated at **TOC < 10 mg/L**.

---

### 1.6 Key Standards and Constants

| Constant / Standard | Symbol | Value | Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| Avogadro's Number | $N_A$ | $\mathbf{6.022 \times 10^{23} \ mol^{-1}}$ | Universal |
| Faraday's Constant | $F$ | $\mathbf{96485 \ C/mol}$ | pH Meter |
| Universal Gas Constant | $R$ | $\mathbf{8.314 \ J/(mol \cdot K)}$ | All thermodynamic equations |
| Standard Temperature | $T$ | $\mathbf{298.15 \ K \ (25^\circ C)}$ | pH calibration |
| Molar Extinction Coefficient Unit | $\varepsilon$ | $\mathbf{L \cdot mol^{-1} \cdot cm^{-1}}$ | Spectrophotometry |
| Carbon Atomic Weight | $C$ | $\mathbf{12.011 \ g/mol}$ | TOC |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

### 2.1 Spectrophotometer — Operational Logic

**Step-by-Step Working:**

1. **Light Source Generation** — A tungsten-halogen lamp (visible range, 320–2500 nm) or deuterium lamp (UV range, 190–400 nm) emits polychromatic light.
2. **Wavelength Selection** — A **monochromator** (prism or diffraction grating) isolates a single wavelength ($\lambda$).
3. **Beam Splitting** — The monochromatic beam is split into a reference beam (passes through blank) and sample beam (passes through cuvette containing solution).
4. **Absorption** — Photons are absorbed by chromophores in the sample based on electronic transitions ($\pi \to \pi^*$, $n \to \pi^*$).
5. **Detection** — A **photodetector** (photomultiplier tube or photodiode) converts transmitted light intensity to an electrical signal.
6. **Readout** — The instrument computes Absorbance ($A$) or Transmittance ($T$) and displays the result.

**Core Principle (Beer-Lambert Law):**

The fraction of incident light absorbed is independent of the original intensity, meaning absorbance is additive and proportional to concentration. This is the foundation of quantitative analysis.

---

### 2.2 pH Meter — Operational Logic

1. **Glass Membrane Hydration** — The glass electrode's outer gel layer hydrates and develops a potential proportional to H⁺ activity.
2. **Reference Stability** — The reference electrode (typically Ag/AgCl in saturated KCl) maintains a constant potential.
3. **Cell Potential Measurement** — A high-impedance voltmeter measures the potential difference across the electrodes.
4. **Nernst Conversion** — The meter applies the **Nernst equation** to convert millivolts to pH units.
5. **Temperature Compensation** — An automatic temperature compensator (ATC) corrects readings for thermal drift.

---

### 2.3 Gas Chromatograph — Operational Logic

1. **Sample Injection** — Microliter volumes are injected through a septum into a heated injection port.
2. **Vaporization** — The sample instantaneously vaporizes at temperatures of **150–300 °C**.
3. **Carrier Gas Flow** — An inert gas (He, N₂, or H₂) sweeps vaporized components through the column.
4. **Separation** — Components partition between the mobile gas phase and stationary liquid phase (capillary column coated with cross-linked polymer).
5. **Detection** — Detectors (FID, TCD, MS, ECD) produce electrical signals proportional to component mass.
6. **Chromatogram Generation** — A plot of detector signal vs. time produces the chromatogram with characteristic peaks.

---

### 2.4 HPLC — Operational Logic

1. **Solvent Delivery** — High-pressure pumps (up to **400 bar**) deliver mobile phase at constant flow rate (typically **0.1–10 mL/min**).
2. **Sample Injection** — Autosampler injects microliter volumes through a six-port valve.
3. **Separation** — Sample components partition between mobile phase and bonded stationary phase (C18 reverse-phase is most common).
4. **Detection** — UV-Vis, fluorescence, refractive index, or mass spectrometry detectors identify eluting compounds.
5. **Data Analysis** — Software integrates peak areas for quantification using calibration curves.

**Modes of HPLC:**
- **Normal Phase:** Polar stationary phase, non-polar mobile phase
- **Reverse Phase (RP-HPLC):** Non-polar stationary phase (C18, C8), polar mobile phase (water/acetonitrile)
- **Ion Exchange:** For ionic species
- **Size Exclusion:** For polymers and biomolecules

---

### 2.5 TOC Analyser — Operational Logic

1. **Sample Acidification** — Sample is acidified (pH < 2) and sparged with N₂ to remove inorganic carbon (TIC) as CO₂.
2. **Oxidation** — Organic carbon is oxidized to CO₂ via:
   - **High-Temperature Combustion** (680–1200 °C with Pt catalyst), OR
   - **Persulfate Wet Oxidation** (UV-persulfate at < 100 °C)
3. **CO₂ Detection** — Non-Dispersive Infrared (NDIR) detector quantifies CO₂.
4. **Calculation** — TOC = TC – TIC (or directly measured as NPOC — Non-Purgeable Organic Carbon).

---

### 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **EXAM GOLD:** Memorize these equations — they appear almost every KTU lab exam cycle.

| Instrument | Formula / Equation | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| Spectrophotometer | $A = \varepsilon \cdot b \cdot c$ | Beer-Lambert Law | $A$ = absorbance (unitless) |
| Spectrophotometer | $A = -\log_{10}(T) = -\log_{10}\left(\dfrac{I}{I_0}\right)$ | Absorbance-Transmittance | $T$ = transmittance |
| Spectrophotometer | $c = \dfrac{A}{\varepsilon \cdot b}$ | Concentration from Absorbance | mol/L |
| pH Meter | $E = E^\circ - \dfrac{0.0591}{n} \log_{10}(Q)$ | Nernst Equation at 25 °C | Volts |
| pH Meter | $\text{pH} = \text{pH}_\text{ref} + \dfrac{E_\text{measured} - E_\text{ref}}{0.0591}$ | pH from Cell Potential | dimensionless |
| pH Meter | $\text{pH} = -\log_{10}[H^+]$ | Definition | dimensionless |
| GC / HPLC | $N = 16 \cdot \left(\dfrac{t_R}{w}\right)^2 = 5.54 \cdot \left(\dfrac{t_R}{w_{1/2}}\right)^2$ | Theoretical Plates | dimensionless |
| GC / HPLC | $\text{HETP} = H = \dfrac{L}{N}$ | Height Equivalent to Theoretical Plate | cm |
| GC / HPLC | $\alpha = \dfrac{k'_2}{k'_1} = \dfrac{t_{R2} - t_M}{t_{R1} - t_M}$ | Selectivity Factor | dimensionless |
| GC / HPLC | $k' = \dfrac{t_R - t_M}{t_M}$ | Capacity Factor | dimensionless |
| GC / HPLC | $R_s = \dfrac{\sqrt{N}}{4} \cdot \dfrac{(\alpha - 1)}{\alpha} \cdot \dfrac{k'}{1 + k'}$ | Resolution (Purnell Equation) | dimensionless |
| GC | $H = A + \dfrac{B}{u} + C \cdot u$ | Van Deemter Equation | cm |
| HPLC | $\Delta P = \dfrac{\phi \cdot \eta \cdot L \cdot u}{d_p^2}$ | Darcy's Law (pressure drop) | Pa |
| TOC | $\text{TOC} = \text{TC} - \text{TIC}$ | Carbon Balance | mg/L |
| TOC | $\text{NPOC} = \text{TC} - \text{TIC}$ | Direct NPOC Method | mg/L |
| TOC | $\text{TOC} = \dfrac{12.011 \times [CO_2]}{44.011}$ | CO₂ to TOC conversion | mg/L |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

### 3.1 Derivation of Beer-Lambert Law

Consider a parallel beam of monochromatic light of intensity $I_0$ entering an absorbing medium of thickness $dx$. The decrease in intensity $-dI$ is proportional to:
- the incident intensity $I$,
- the concentration $c$ of the absorbing species,
- the path length $dx$.

Mathematically:

$$
-dI \propto I \cdot c \cdot dx
$$

Introducing the proportionality constant $\mu$ (linear absorption coefficient):

$$
-dI = \mu \cdot I \cdot c \cdot dx
$$

Rearranging:

$$
\frac{dI}{I} = -\mu \cdot c \cdot dx
$$

Integrating both sides from $I_0$ (incident) to $I$ (transmitted) over path length $b$:

$$
\int_{I_0}^{I} \frac{dI}{I} = -\mu \cdot c \cdot \int_{0}^{b} dx
$$

$$
\ln\left(\frac{I}{I_0}\right) = -\mu \cdot c \cdot b
$$

Converting natural logarithm to base-10 logarithm ($\ln x = 2.303 \log_{10} x$):

$$
\log_{10}\left(\frac{I_0}{I}\right) = \frac{\mu \cdot c \cdot b}{2.303}
$$

By definition, $A = \log_{10}\left(\dfrac{I_0}{I}\right) = -\log_{10}(T)$. Substituting $\dfrac{\mu}{2.303} = \varepsilon$:

$$
\boxed{A = \varepsilon \cdot b \cdot c}
$$

**Where:**
- $A$ = absorbance (unitless)
- $\varepsilon$ = molar absorptivity (L·mol⁻¹·cm⁻¹)
- $b$ = path length of cuvette (cm)
- $c$ = concentration of analyte (mol/L)

---

### 3.2 Derivation of Nernst Equation

For the general redox reaction:

$$
\text{Ox} + ne^- \rightleftharpoons \text{Red}
$$

The Gibbs free energy change is related to cell potential:

$$
\Delta G = -nFE
$$

At equilibrium, $\Delta G = \Delta G^\circ + RT \ln Q$:

$$
-nFE = -nFE^\circ + RT \ln Q
$$

Dividing throughout by $-nF$:

$$
E = E^\circ - \frac{RT}{nF} \ln Q
$$

Converting to base-10 logarithm ($\ln x = 2.303 \log_{10} x$):

$$
E = E^\circ - \frac{2.303 \, RT}{nF} \log_{10} Q
$$

Substituting values at **T = 298.15 K**: $\dfrac{2.303 \times 8.314 \times 298.15}{96485} = 0.05916 \approx 0.0591$ V:

$$
\boxed{E = E^\circ - \frac{0.0591}{n} \log_{10}(Q)}
$$

**Application to pH Measurement:**

For the glass electrode responding to H⁺ activity ($a_{H^+}$):

$$
E = E^\circ - \frac{0.0591}{1} \log_{10}\left(\frac{1}{a_{H^+}}\right)
$$

$$
E = E^\circ - 0.0591 \cdot \text{pH}
$$

Rearranging for pH:

$$
\boxed{\text{pH} = \frac{E^\circ - E}{0.0591}}
$$

---

### 3.3 Derivation of Van Deemter Equation

The Height Equivalent to a Theoretical Plate (HETP or $H$) arises from three independent dispersion contributions:

1. **Eddy Diffusion (A):** Multiple flow paths through packed bed cause band broadening.
2. **Longitudinal Diffusion (B/u):** Molecular diffusion along the column axis; inversely proportional to linear velocity $u$.
3. **Mass Transfer Resistance (C·u):** Finite time to establish equilibrium between mobile and stationary phases; directly proportional to $u$.

Therefore:

$$
H = A + \frac{B}{u} + C \cdot u
$$

Where the **optimum velocity** is found by setting $\dfrac{dH}{du} = 0$:

$$
\frac{dH}{du} = -\frac{B}{u^2} + C = 0
$$

$$
u_\text{opt} = \sqrt{\frac{B}{C}}
$$

Substituting back:

$$
H_\text{min} = A + 2\sqrt{BC}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **HPLC Insight:** Van Deemter equation explains why HPLC uses small particles (reduces $C$) and high velocity (suppresses $B/u$ term) for efficient separations.

---

### 3.4 Derivation of Resolution Equation (Purnell)

Resolution between two adjacent peaks is defined as:

$$
R_s = \frac{t_{R2} - t_{R1}}{\frac{1}{2}(w_1 + w_2)}
$$

For closely spaced peaks, $w_1 \approx w_2 = w$:

$$
R_s = \frac{t_{R2} - t_{R1}}{w} = \frac{\Delta t_R}{w}
$$

Using the plate theory: $N = 16 \left(\dfrac{t_R}{w}\right)^2$, so $w = \dfrac{4 t_R}{\sqrt{N}}$.

Substituting for peak 2 (the later eluting one):

$$
R_s = \frac{\Delta t_R \sqrt{N}}{4 t_{R2}}
$$

Expressing $\Delta t_R$ in terms of selectivity $\alpha$ and capacity factor $k'_2$:

$$
\Delta t_R = t_{R2} - t_{R1} = t_M \alpha k'_2 - t_M k'_1 = t_M k'_2 \left(\frac{\alpha - 1}{\alpha}\right) \cdot \alpha
$$

After algebraic manipulation and noting $\dfrac{t_{R2}}{t_M} = 1 + k'_2$:

$$
\boxed{R_s = \frac{\sqrt{N}}{4} \cdot \frac{(\alpha - 1)}{\alpha} \cdot \frac{k'_2}{1 + k'_2}}
$$

This is the **fundamental resolution equation** in chromatography.

---

### 3.5 Sample Calculation: Spectrophotometric Determination of Iron

**Problem:** A solution containing Fe²⁺ complexed with 1,10-phenanthroline has absorbance $A = 0.480$ at $\lambda = 510$ nm using a 1.00 cm cuvette. The molar absorptivity at this wavelength is $\varepsilon = 11{,}100 \text{ L·mol}^{-1}\text{·cm}^{-1}$. Calculate the concentration.

**Solution:**

Using Beer-Lambert Law:

$$
A = \varepsilon \cdot b \cdot c
$$

Rearranging for $c$:

$$
c = \frac{A}{\varepsilon \cdot b}
$$

$$
c = \frac{0.480}{11{,}100 \times 1.00}
$$

$$
c = 4.324 \times 10^{-5} \text{ mol/L}
$$

Converting to mg/L (atomic mass of Fe = 55.845 g/mol):

$$
c = 4.324 \times 10^{-5} \times 55.845 \times 1000
$$

$$
\boxed{c = 2.415 \text{ mg/L}}
$$

---

### 3.6 Sample Calculation: TOC from CO₂ Measurement

**Problem:** A TOC analyser reports $CO_2$ concentration = 8.50 mg/L in a sample after complete combustion of organics. Calculate the TOC.

**Solution:**

The stoichiometric relationship between CO₂ and Carbon:

$$
\frac{\text{Mass of C}}{\text{Mass of CO}_2} = \frac{12.011}{44.011} = 0.2729
$$

Therefore:

$$
\text{TOC} = 0.2729 \times [\text{CO}_2]
$$

$$
\text{TOC} = 0.2729 \times 8.50
$$

$$
\boxed{\text{TOC} = 2.32 \text{ mg/L}}
$$

This falls within the WHO drinking water standard of < 2 mg/L (slightly above — investigate contamination).

---

### 3.7 Analytical Matrix: Instrument Comparison

| Parameter | Spectrophotometer | pH Meter | GC | HPLC | TOC |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Sample Phase** | Liquid | Liquid/Aqueous | Gas/Vaporizable | Liquid | Aqueous |
| **Detection Type** | Optical (Absorbance) | Electrochemical | FID/TCD/MS | UV/RI/FL | NDIR |
| **Sensitivity** | ppm | 0.01 pH units | ppb–ppm | ppb–ppm | ppb |
| **Analysis Time** | < 1 min | < 1 min | 5–60 min | 5–30 min | 5–10 min |
| **Calibration** | Standard curve | Buffer solutions | Standard mixture | Standard mixture | KHP standard |
| **Sample Volume** | 1–3 mL | 5–50 mL | 1–10 µL | 5–100 µL | 10–50 mL |
| **Key Application** | Colorimetric analyte quantification | Acidity/Alkalinity | VOC separation | Non-volatile organics | Organic pollution load |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

### 4.1 Block Diagram: UV-Vis Spectrophotometer

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Light Source Tungsten or Deuterium] --> B[Monochromator Prism or Grating]
    B --> C[Beam Splitter]
    C --> D1[Reference Beam]
    C --> D2[Sample Beam through Cuvette]
    D1 --> E1[Detector 1 Reference]
    D2 --> E2[Detector 2 Sample]
    E1 --> F[Signal Processor]
    E2 --> F
    F --> G[Display Absorbance or Transmittance]
    G --> H[Data Output Printer or PC]
```

---

### 4.2 Block Diagram: pH Meter Electrochemical System

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Sample Solution H plus ions] --> B[Glass Electrode Sensing]
    A --> C[Reference Electrode Ag or AgCl in KCl]
    B --> D[Junction Potential]
    C --> D
    D --> E[High Impedance Voltmeter]
    E --> F[Amplifier]
    F --> G[Analog to Digital Converter]
    G --> H[Microprocessor Nernst Conversion]
    H --> I[LCD Display pH Value]
    J[Temperature Sensor] --> H
```

---

### 4.3 Block Diagram: Gas Chromatograph Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Carrier Gas Cylinder He or N2] --> B[Pressure Regulator]
    B --> C[Injector Port 200 to 300 C]
    C --> D[Capillary Column 30 m x 0.25 mm]
    D --> E[Detector FID or TCD]
    E --> F[Signal Amplifier]
    F --> G[Data System Peak Integration]
    G --> H[Chromatogram Output]
    I[Oven Temperature Programmed] --> D
```

---

### 4.4 Block Diagram: HPLC System Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Solvent Reservoir A and B] --> B[Degasser]
    B --> C[High Pressure Pump up to 400 bar]
    C --> D[Mixer Gradient Former]
    D --> E[Autosampler Six Port Valve]
    E --> F[Guard Column]
    F --> G[Analytical Column C18 5 micrometer]
    G --> H[Detector UV Vis or RI]
    H --> I[Data Acquisition]
    I --> J[Report Generation]
    K[Column Oven 25 to 60 C] --> G
```

---

### 4.5 Block Diagram: TOC Analyser Operational Sequence

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Sample Intake] --> B[Acidification pH less than 2]
    B --> C[Sparging with N2 Removes TIC as CO2]
    C --> D[Oxidation Reactor Combustion 680 C or Persulfate UV]
    D --> E[CO2 Carrier Gas Stream]
    E --> F[NDIR Detector]
    F --> G[Microprocessor Calculation]
    G --> H[Display TOC mg per L]
    D -.-> I[Optional TC Channel Parallel]
    I -.-> G
```

---

### 4.6 Block Diagram: Comparative Instrument Selection Logic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Analyte Type] --> B{Volatile or Non Volatile}
    B -->|Volatile| C[Gas Chromatograph GC]
    B -->|Non Volatile| D{Coloured or Transparent}
    D -->|Coloured| E[Spectrophotometer]
    D -->|Transparent| F{Aqueous or Organic}
    F -->|Aqueous| G[H plus Activity Question]
    G -->|Yes| H[pH Meter]
    G -->|No| I[HPLC]
    F -->|Organic| I
    J[Total Organic Carbon Question] --> K[TOC Analyser]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

### PART A: SHORT ANSWER QUESTIONS (3 MARKS EACH)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Question]**
**(a)** State the **Beer-Lambert Law** and define each term with units.

**Model Answer:**

The Beer-Lambert Law states that the absorbance of monochromatic light by a solution is directly proportional to the concentration of the absorbing species and the path length through the solution.

$$
A = \varepsilon \cdot b \cdot c
$$

- **A (Absorbance):** Unitless; $A = -\log_{10}(I/I_0)$
- **$\varepsilon$ (Molar Absorptivity):** $\text{L} \cdot \text{mol}^{-1} \cdot \text{cm}^{-1}$
- **b (Path Length):** cm (typically 1 cm for standard cuvette)
- **c (Concentration):** mol/L

**(b)** A solution shows 25% transmittance at 540 nm. Calculate the absorbance.

**Model Answer:**

$$
A = -\log_{10}(T) = -\log_{10}(0.25)
$$

$$
A = 0.602
$$

**Marks Distribution:** Law statement (1 mark) + term definitions (1 mark) + numerical (1 mark).

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Question]**
List the **main components of a Gas Chromatograph** and explain the function of any three.

**Model Answer:**

Main components of a GC system:
1. Carrier gas supply (He, N₂, H₂)
2. Sample injector (split/splitless)
3. Column oven (temperature-programmed)
4. Capillary column (stationary phase)
5. Detector (FID/TCD/ECD/MS)
6. Data acquisition system

**Function of three key components:**

| Component | Function |
|---|---|
| **Carrier Gas** | Mobile phase that sweeps vaporized sample through the column at controlled flow rate |
| **Injector Port** | Vaporizes liquid sample instantly at high temperature (200–300 °C) for introduction onto column |
| **FID (Flame Ionization Detector)** | Burns organic compounds in H₂/air flame; ion production is proportional to carbon mass |
| **Column Oven** | Maintains precise temperature or temperature program for reproducible retention |

### PART B: LONG ANSWER QUESTIONS (14 MARKS — INTERNAL CHOICE)

**QUESTION A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Describe the **principle, construction, and working of a UV-Vis Spectrophotometer** with a neat block diagram. Explain Beer-Lambert Law and its limitations.

**Model Answer:**

**Principle:**
A UV-Vis Spectrophotometer measures the absorption of ultraviolet or visible light by a sample. The absorbance is governed by the Beer-Lambert Law: $A = \varepsilon b c$, which enables quantification of analyte concentration.

**Construction (Block Diagram already shown in Section 4.1):**

1. **Light Source:** Tungsten-halogen lamp for visible (320–2500 nm); Deuterium lamp for UV (190–400 nm).
2. **Monochromator:** Uses diffraction grating or prism to isolate a narrow bandwidth of light (~1 nm).
3. **Beam Splitter:** Divides monochromatic light into reference and sample beams.
4. **Sample Holder:** Quartz or glass cuvettes of 1 cm standard path length.
5. **Detector:** Photomultiplier tube (PMT) or photodiode array converts light to electrical signal.
6. **Signal Processor & Display:** Computes absorbance/transmittance and displays digitally.

**Working:**
- Blank (pure solvent) is placed in reference beam to zero absorbance.
- Sample is placed in sample beam.
- The ratio $I/I_0$ gives transmittance, and $A = -\log(I/I_0)$ gives absorbance.
- Concentration is calculated from the calibration curve or directly using the Beer-Lambert equation.

**Limitations of Beer-Lambert Law:**
1. Valid only for **dilute solutions** ($c < 0.01$ M). At higher concentrations, solute-solute interactions cause deviations.
2. Applicable to **monochromatic light** only. Polychromatic light causes curvature.
3. **Scattering** by particulates and **fluorescence** by sample cause positive deviations.
4. **Chemical deviations** occur when solute dissociates, associates, or reacts with solvent.
5. Cannot distinguish between different species absorbing at the same wavelength.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A water sample is analysed using a spectrophotometer at 680 nm using the cobalt complex method. The following data were obtained:

| Concentration (mg/L) | Absorbance |
|---|---|
| 0.0 | 0.000 |
| 1.0 | 0.105 |
| 2.0 | 0.210 |
| 4.0 | 0.425 |
| 8.0 | 0.840 |

An unknown sample gives absorbance = 0.320. Determine the unknown concentration and calculate the **molar absorptivity** if the cuvette path length is 1.0 cm and molar mass of analyte is 100 g/mol.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Plot Calibration Curve or Apply Linear Regression**

Using linear regression on the calibration data, the slope of $A$ vs $c$ gives $\varepsilon b$. From the data:

$$
\text{slope} = \frac{0.840 - 0.000}{8.0 - 0.0} = 0.105 \text{ per mg/L}
$$

The linear equation is: $A = 0.105 \cdot c$

**Step 2: Determine Unknown Concentration**

$$
c = \frac{A}{0.105} = \frac{0.320}{0.105} = 3.048 \text{ mg/L}
$$

$$
\boxed{c = 3.05 \text{ mg/L}}
$$

**Step 3: Calculate Molar Absorptivity**

Convert mg/L to mol/L:

$$
c_\text{molar} = \frac{3.048 \times 10^{-3}}{100} = 3.048 \times 10^{-5} \text{ mol/L}
$$

From Beer-Lambert Law:

$$
\varepsilon = \frac{A}{b \cdot c} = \frac{0.320}{1.0 \times 3.048 \times 10^{-5}}
$$

$$
\boxed{\varepsilon = 10{,}499 \approx 1.05 \times 10^4 \text{ L·mol}^{-1}\text{·cm}^{-1}}
$$

**Marks Distribution:** (a) Principle + construction + working = 4 marks, limitations = 3 marks; (b) Calibration interpretation = 3 marks, molar absorptivity = 4 marks.

**QUESTION B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model — Alternative Choice]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **principle and working of a Gas Chromatograph**. Derive the **Van Deemter equation** and discuss the effect of each term on column efficiency.

**Model Answer:**

**Principle:**
GC separates volatile components of a mixture based on their differential partitioning between a stationary phase (high-boiling liquid coated on column walls) and a mobile phase (inert carrier gas). Components with higher affinity for the stationary phase elute later.

**Working (Block Diagram shown in Section 4.3):**

1. **Carrier gas** (He/N₂/H₂) flows at constant rate.
2. **Sample injection** (1 µL) through septum into heated port.
3. **Vaporization** at 200–300 °C.
4. **Separation** in capillary column (typically 30 m, 0.25 mm ID) maintained at programmed temperature.
5. **Detection** by FID, TCD, ECD, or MS.
6. **Chromatogram** plotted as detector response vs. time.

**Derivation of Van Deemter Equation:**

The total plate height $H$ is the sum of three independent dispersion contributions:

$$
H = H_\text{eddy} + H_\text{longitudinal} + H_\text{mass transfer}
$$

Each term is modelled:

- **Eddy Diffusion:** $H_\text{eddy} = A = 2\lambda d_p$ (depends on particle size and packing uniformity)
- **Longitudinal Diffusion:** $H_\text{long} = \dfrac{B}{u} = \dfrac{2 \gamma D_m}{u}$ (depends on diffusion coefficient in mobile phase)
- **Mass Transfer:** $H_\text{mt} = C \cdot u$ (depends on diffusion in stationary phase)

Therefore:

$$
\boxed{H = A + \frac{B}{u} + C \cdot u}
$$

**Effect of Each Term:**

| Term | Dependency | Effect on Efficiency |
|---|---|---|
| **A (Eddy)** | Independent of velocity | Reduced by using smaller, uniform particles |
| **B/u (Longitudinal)** | Decreases with velocity | Significant at low flow rates |
| **C·u (Mass Transfer)** | Increases with velocity | Dominates at high flow rates |

The **optimal velocity** is $u_\text{opt} = \sqrt{B/C}$ giving minimum $H$ and maximum efficiency.

**(b) [7 Marks]** In a GC analysis, the retention time of compound A is 4.20 min and compound B is 5.85 min. The peak widths at baseline are 0.30 min and 0.45 min respectively. The dead time (column void time) is 0.80 min. Calculate:
1. Capacity factors $k'_A$ and $k'_B$
2. Selectivity factor $\alpha$
3. Number of theoretical plates (for compound B)
4. Resolution $R_s$

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Capacity Factors**

$$
k'_A = \frac{t_{R,A} - t_M}{t_M} = \frac{4.20 - 0.80}{0.80} = \frac{3.40}{0.80}
$$

$$
\boxed{k'_A = 4.25}
$$

$$
k'_B = \frac{t_{R,B} - t_M}{t_M} = \frac{5.85 - 0.80}{0.80} = \frac{5.05}{0.80}
$$

$$
\boxed{k'_B = 6.3125}
$$

**Step 2: Selectivity Factor**

$$
\alpha = \frac{k'_B}{k'_A} = \frac{6.3125}{4.25}
$$

$$
\boxed{\alpha = 1.485}
$$

**Step 3: Number of Theoretical Plates (for Compound B)**

Using the formula $N = 16 \left(\dfrac{t_R}{w}\right)^2$:

$$
N_B = 16 \times \left(\frac{5.85}{0.45}\right)^2 = 16 \times (13.0)^2 = 16 \times 169
$$

$$
\boxed{N_B = 2704}
$$

**Step 4: Resolution**

$$
R_s = \frac{t_{R,B} - t_{R,A}}{0.5(w_A + w_B)} = \frac{5.85 - 4.20}{0.5(0.30 + 0.45)}
$$

$$
R_s = \frac{1.65}{0.375}
$$

$$
\boxed{R_s = 4.40}
$$

Since $R_s > 1.5$, the peaks are **baseline separated**.

**Marks Distribution:** (a) Principle + working = 3 marks, Van Deemter derivation + discussion = 4 marks; (b) Step 1 = 2 marks, Step 2 = 1 mark, Step 3 = 2 marks, Step 4 = 2 marks.

### KTU EXAMINER'S VALUATION WARNING / PITFALL CALLOUT

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Analytical Instrument Problems:**
>
> 1. **Units Mismatch in Beer-Lambert:** Students often mix up mol/L with g/L when calculating concentration from absorbance. Always convert molar mass correctly. The molar absorptivity $\varepsilon$ is in **L·mol⁻¹·cm⁻¹** (molar), not L·g⁻¹·cm⁻¹.
>
> 2. **Forgetting Dead Time $t_M$ in Capacity Factor:** A common error is using $k' = t_R / t_M$ instead of the correct $k' = (t_R - t_M)/t_M$. This gives a huge wrong answer.
>
> 3. **Van Deemter Sign Confusion:** The **B/u** term is inversely proportional to velocity (decreases with $u$), while **C·u** is directly proportional. Mixing these up means your optimum velocity will be wrong.
>
> 4. **pH Meter Nernst Coefficient:** Use **0.0591 V** at 25 °C only. If temperature is different, use $\dfrac{2.303 RT}{F}$ with the actual $T$ in Kelvin.
>
> 5. **TOC Conversion Factor:** Many students use 12 (atomic mass) instead of $\dfrac{12.011}{44.011} = 0.2729$. The full stoichiometric ratio from CO₂ to C must be used.
>
> 6. **Calibration Curve Plotting:** In spectrophotometric problems, students read concentration directly from the graph. Examiners award marks only if the **linear equation** is derived properly. Always show the slope calculation.
>
> 7. **Resolution Calculation Error:** Resolution formula is sometimes written as $R_s = \dfrac{\Delta t_R}{w}$ (without the 0.5 factor). Always include the **½(w₁ + w₂)** in denominator.

### TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU Board Exam**

- ✅ **Spectrophotometer:** Beer-Lambert Law $A = \varepsilon b c$; valid for dilute solutions and monochromatic light only.
- ✅ **Absorbance vs Transmittance:** $A = -\log(T)$; remember that absorbance is additive, transmittance is multiplicative.
- ✅ **pH Meter Nernst Equation at 25 °C:** $E = E^\circ - 0.0591 \log Q$; for H⁺ measurement, $\text{pH} = \dfrac{E^\circ - E}{0.0591}$.
- ✅ **pH Scale:** pH < 7 acidic, pH = 7 neutral, pH > 7 alkaline; standard buffers are pH 4, 7, and 10.
- ✅ **GC Components:** Carrier gas, injector, column oven, capillary column, detector (FID most common), data system.
- ✅ **Van Deemter Equation:** $H = A + B/u + Cu$; optimum velocity $u_\text{opt} = \sqrt{B/C}$.
- ✅ **Theoretical Plates:** $N = 16(t_R/w)^2 = 5.54(t_R/w_{1/2})^2$; higher $N$ = better efficiency.
- ✅ **Capacity Factor:** $k' = (t_R - t_M)/t_M$; ideal range $1 < k' < 10$.
- ✅ **Selectivity:** $\alpha = k'_2/k'_1$; $\alpha > 1$ for separation.
- ✅ **Resolution Purnell Equation:** $R_s = \dfrac{\sqrt{N}}{4} \cdot \dfrac{(\alpha-1)}{\alpha} \cdot \dfrac{k'}{1+k'}$; $R_s > 1.5$ = baseline separation.
- ✅ **HPLC vs GC:** HPLC for non-volatile and thermally labile compounds; GC for volatile organics. HPLC uses high pressure (up to 400 bar).
- ✅ **HPLC Reverse Phase:** C18 stationary phase; water/acetonitrile mobile phase (most common).
- ✅ **TOC Relationship:** TOC = TC − TIC = 0.2729 × [CO₂]; expressed in mg/L carbon.
- ✅ **TOC Detection:** NDIR detector measures CO₂; oxidation by high-T combustion or persulfate-UV.
- ✅ **Standard Threshold:** Drinking water TOC < 2 mg/L (WHO); ultrapure water < 50 ppb (semiconductor industry).
- ✅ **Lab Safety:** HPLC uses high-pressure solvents — never operate without proper training; GC detectors use flammable H₂ — check for leaks.
- ✅ **Calibration Practice:** Always run blank, standards, and samples in triplicate for analytical reliability.
